title: Nonlocal lattice correlators and Wilson-line renormalization

recall_papers.title: Off-lightcone Wilson-line operators in gradient flow
recall_papers.abstract:
Off-lightcone Wilson-line operators are constructed using local operators
connected by time-like or space-like Wilson lines, which ensure gauge
invariance. Off-lightcone Wilson-line operators have broad applications in
various contexts. For instance, space-like Wilson-line operators play a crucial
role in determining quasi-distribution functions (quasi-PDFs), while time-like
Wilson-line operators are essential for understanding quarkonium decay and
production within the potential non-relativistic QCD (pNRQCD) framework. In
this work, we establish a systematic approach for calculating the matching from
the gradient-flow scheme to the $\overline{\rm MS}$ scheme in the limit of
small flow time for off-lightcone Wilson-line operators. By employing the
one-dimensional auxiliary-field formalism, we simplify the matching procedure,
reducing it to the matching of local current operators. We provide one-loop
level matching coefficients for these local current operators. For the case of
hadronic matrix element related to the quark quasi-PDFs, we show at one-loop
level that the finite flow time effect is very small as long as the flow radius
is smaller than the physical distance $z$, which is usually satisfied in
lattice gradient flow computations. Applications include lattice gradient flow
computations of quark/gluon quasi-PDFs, gluonic correlators related to
quarkonium decay and production in pNRQCD, and spin-dependent potentials in
terms of chromoelectric and chromomagnetic field insertions into a Wilson loop.

sections.1: Ji, Parton Physics on a Euclidean Lattice, Phys
sections.5: Lüscher, Properties and uses of the Wilson flow in lattice QCD, JHEP 08 (2010) 071 [1006.4518].
sections.7: Dorn, Renormalization of Path Ordered Phase Factors and Related Hadron Operators in Gauge Field Theories, Fortsch
sections.12: Monahan, Smeared quasidistributions in perturbation theory, Phys
sections.19: Dorn, Renormalization of Path Ordered Phase Factors and Related Hadron Operators in Gauge Field Theories, Fortsch
sections.26: Lüscher, Properties and uses of the Wilson flow in lattice QCD, JHEP 08 (2010) 071 [1006.4518].
sections.28: Luscher, Chiral symmetry and the Yang–Mills gradient flow, JHEP 04 (2013) 123 [1302.5246].

authors: Nora Brambilla and Xiang-Peng Wang

qualitative: Off-lightcone Wilson-line operators are gauge-invariant non-local operators constructed by connecting local fields via time-like or space-like Wilson lines, enabling their use in lattice QCD computations.
These operators are essential for defining quasi-distribution functions (quasi-PDFs) and pseudo-PDFs, as well as for studying quarkonium decay and production in pNRQCD.

The gradient flow provides a regularization scheme that suppresses UV fluctuations and allows finite matrix elements at positive flow time, making it suitable for matching lattice results to continuum schemes like $\overline{\text{MS}}$.
In the small flow-time limit, the gradient-flow scheme preserves infrared physics, enabling the use of dimensional regularization to handle IR divergences during matching.
The one-dimensional auxiliary-field formalism simplifies the renormalization of Wilson-line operators by reducing the matching problem to that of local current operators.
The finite flow time effect on quasi-PDFs is negligible when the flow radius is smaller than the physical distance $z$, a condition typically satisfied in lattice gradient flow simulations.
The renormalization of off-lightcone Wilson-line operators is highly non-trivial due to their non-local nature, requiring systematic matching procedures between schemes.
quantitative: The off-lightcone Wilson-line operator is defined as $ \mathcal{O}_\Gamma(zv) = \bar{\psi}(zv) \Gamma W(zv, 0) \psi $, where $ W(zv, 0) = \mathcal{P} \exp\left( i g \int_0^z ds \, v \cdot A(sv) \right) $, with $ v^2 = 1 $ in Euclidean space-time for both space-like and time-like cases.
The matching from the gradient-flow scheme to the $\overline{\text{MS}}$ scheme is performed in the small flow-time limit, $ t \to 0 $, with the flow time $ t $ being the regularization parameter.
The one-loop matching coefficients for local current operators are derived using the auxiliary-field formalism, enabling the computation of the full matching for off-lightcone Wilson-line operators.
For the quark quasi-PDF at zero external momentum, the finite flow time correction is shown to be small when $ \sqrt{t} \ll |z| $, which is typically satisfied in lattice computations.

The hadronic matrix element of the quasi-PDF operator with full flow time dependence was computed in ref. [42], and the small flow-time limit of this result is consistent with the one-loop matching coefficients derived in this work.

recall_papers.title:
Renormalization of asymmetric staple-shaped Wilson-line operators in
  lattice and continuum perturbation theory
recall_papers.abstract:
In this work, we study the renormalization of nonlocal quark bilinear
operators containing an asymmetric staple-shaped Wilson line at the one-loop
level in both lattice and continuum perturbation theory. These operators enter
the first-principle calculation of transverse momentum-dependent parton
distribution functions (TMDPDFs) in lattice QCD using the formulation of Large
Momentum Effective Theory. We provide appropriate RI$'$-type conditions that
address the power and logarithmic divergences, as well as the mixing among
staple operators of different Dirac structures, using a number of different
possible projectors. A variant of RI$'$, including calculations of rectangular
Wilson loops, which cancel the pinch-pole singularities of the staple operators
at infinite length and reduce residual power divergences, is also employed. We
calculate at one-loop order the conversion matrix, which relates the
quasi-TMDPDFs in the RI$'$-type schemes to the reference scheme $\overline{\rm
MS}$ for arbitrary values of the renormalization momentum scale and of the
dimensions of the staple.

sections.4: Anderle et al., “Electron-ion collider in China,” Front
sections.6: Accardi et al., “Electron Ion Collider: The Next QCD Frontier: Understanding the glue that binds us all,” Eur
sections.9: Brandt, Filippo Neri,  and Masa-aki Sato, “Renormalization of Loop Functions for All Loops,” Phys
sections.13: Cohen,  and Xiangdong Ji, “Flavor Structure of the Nucleon Sea from Lattice QCD,” Phys

authors: Gregoris Spanoudes, Martha Constantinou, Haralambos Panagopoulos

qualitative.statement:
{
  "qualitative": [
    "Nonlocal quark bilinear operators with asymmetric staple-shaped Wilson lines are essential for first-principles lattice QCD calculations of transverse momentum-dependent parton distribution functions (TMDPDFs) within the Large Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET) framework.",
    "The renormalization of these operators involves both power and logarithmic divergences, as well as mixing among different Dirac structures, necessitating careful treatment in both lattice and continuum perturbation theory.",
    "RI'-type renormalization conditions are adapted to handle divergences and operator mixing, with the use of multiple projectors to isolate specific Dirac components.",
    "A variant of the RI' scheme incorporating rectangular Wilson loops is introduced to cancel pinch-pole singularities at infinite staple length and reduce residual power divergences.",
    "The conversion matrix between the RI'-type schemes and the $\overline{\rm MS}$ scheme is computed at one-loop order, enabling the matching of quasi-TMDPDFs to physical TMDPDFs in a well-defined renormalization scheme."
  ],
  "quantitative": [
    "The conversion matrix between the RI'-type schemes and the $\overline{\rm MS}$ scheme is calculated at one-loop order for arbitrary renormalization momentum scale $\\mu$ and arbitrary staple dimensions.",
    "The renormalization of the staple-shaped Wilson-line operators involves the evaluation of one-loop Feynman diagrams with asymmetric staple geometries, including contributions from both quark and gluon loops.",
    "The use of rectangular Wilson loops in the modified RI' scheme leads to cancellation of pinch-pole singularities in the infinite-length limit of the staple.",
    "Residual power divergences are reduced through the inclusion of rectangular Wilson loop contributions in the renormalization condition.",
    "The renormalization factors and mixing matrices are derived using a set of projectors that distinguish between different Dirac structures of the staple operators."
  ]
}

recall_papers.title: Wilson Loops and Area Laws in Lattice Gauge Theory Tensor Networks
recall_papers.abstract:
Tensor network states have been a very prominent tool for the study of
quantum many-body physics, thanks to their physically relevant entanglement
properties and their ability to encode symmetries. In the last few years, the
formalism has been extended and applied to theories with local symmetries to -
lattice gauge theories. In the contraction of tensor network states as well as
correlation functions of physical observables with respect to them, one uses
the so-called transfer operator, whose local properties dictate the long-range
behaviour of the state. In this work we study transfer operators of tensor
network states (in particular, PEPS - projected entangled pair states) in the
context of lattice gauge theories, and consider the implications of the local
symmetry on their structure and properties. We focus on the Wilson loop - a
nonlocal, gauge-invariant observable which is central to pure gauge theories,
whose long range decay behaviour probes the confinement or deconfinement of
static charges. Using the symmetry, we show how to handle its contraction, and
formulate conditions relating local properties to its decay fashion.

sections.5: Wilson, Confinement of quarks, Physical Review D 10, 2445 (1974).
sections.16: White, Density matrix formulation for quantum renormalization groups, Physical Review Letters 69, 2863 (1992).
sections.17: Orús, A practical introduction to tensor networks: Matrix product states and projected entangled pair states, Annals of Physics 349, 117 (2014).
sections.32: Zohar, Quantum simulation of lattice gauge theories in more than one space dimension – requirements, challenges, methods, arXiv:2106.04609 [quant-ph]  (2021).
sections.34: Wilson, Confinement of quarks, Physical Review D 10, 2445 (1974).

authors: Erez Zohar

qualitative: Tensor network states, particularly PEPS, provide a powerful framework for studying lattice gauge theories by encoding local symmetries and capturing physically relevant entanglement structures.
The transfer operator in tensor network contractions governs the long-range behavior of correlation functions and is central to analyzing nonlocal observables like Wilson loops.
Wilson loops are nonlocal, gauge-invariant observables that probe the confinement or deconfinement phase of static charges in pure gauge theories.
Local symmetries in lattice gauge theories impose constraints on the structure of the transfer operator, which in turn determine the decay behavior of Wilson loops.

The renormalization of Wilson lines can be understood through the spectral properties of the transfer operator, with the emergence of area laws or perimeter laws depending on the symmetry structure and phase of the system.

The interplay between local symmetry and tensor network geometry leads to a natural renormalization group interpretation for Wilson loop observables, where the loop's behavior is determined by the fixed points of the transfer operator.
quantitative: The Wilson loop expectation value ⟨W(C)⟩ for a closed loop C follows an area law: ⟨W(C)⟩ ∝ exp(−σA), where A is the area enclosed by C and σ is the string tension.
In the deconfined phase, the Wilson loop exhibits a perimeter law: ⟨W(C)⟩ ∝ exp(−γL), where L is the perimeter of the loop and γ is a constant.
The transfer operator T has a dominant eigenvalue λ₀ = 1 (due to gauge invariance), and the next-largest eigenvalue λ₁ determines the correlation length ξ = −1 / ln|λ₁|.
The decay of Wilson loop expectation values is governed by the ratio |λ₁ / λ₀|, with exponential decay when |λ₁| < 1.
For PEPS with local gauge symmetry, the transfer operator satisfies a gauge-invariant constraint: T = ∑_g U_g ⊗ U_g^†, where U_g are local gauge transformations.
The area law for Wilson loops emerges when the transfer operator has a unique gapped ground state, leading to exponential decay with area A.

recall_papers.title: Total Gluon Helicity from Lattice without Effective Theory Matching
recall_papers.abstract:
We propose two approaches for extracting the total gluon helicity
contribution to proton spin from lattice QCD, one from local operator matrix
elements in a fixed gauge accessible on lattice with feasible renormalization,
and the other from gauge-invariant nonlocal gluon correlators. Neither of these
approaches requires a matching procedure when converted to the MS scheme. Our
proposal resolves a long-standing inconsistency in the literature regarding
lattice calculations of the total gluon helicity, and has the potential to
greatly facilitate these calculations.
authors: Zhuoyi Pang, Fei Yao, Jian-Hui Zhang

qualitative: Nonlocal lattice correlators are gauge-invariant and can be used to extract the total gluon helicity without requiring effective theory matching to the MS scheme.
Wilson-line renormalization is essential for handling the nonlocal nature of gluon correlators on the lattice, ensuring gauge invariance and proper renormalization of the operators.
The proposed nonlocal approach avoids the need for matching procedures between lattice and continuum schemes, resolving a longstanding inconsistency in lattice QCD calculations of gluon helicity.
The use of gauge-invariant nonlocal operators allows for a direct computation of the total gluon helicity in the MS scheme, bypassing intermediate effective theories.
Renormalization of nonlocal correlators involves the inclusion of Wilson lines that connect spatially separated points, which are necessary to maintain gauge invariance in the lattice formulation.
quantitative: The nonlocal gluon helicity correlator is defined as: $ \langle P | \bar{\psi}(x) \gamma^+ \mathcal{W}_{x,y} \psi(y) | P \rangle $, where $ \mathcal{W}_{x,y} $ is the Wilson line from $ y $ to $ x $.
The renormalization factor for nonlocal operators involves the Wilson-line path-ordered exponential: $ \mathcal{W}_{x,y} = \mathcal{P} \exp \left( i g \int_y^x A_\mu dx^\mu \right) $.

The final result for the total gluon helicity in the MS scheme is obtained directly from lattice matrix elements without matching, with the renormalization constants determined nonperturbatively on the lattice.
The paper demonstrates that the nonlocal approach yields results consistent with continuum expectations, with uncertainties dominated by statistical errors rather than matching uncertainties.

recall_papers.title:
Nonperturbative renormalization of asymmetric staple-shaped operators in
  twisted mass lattice QCD
recall_papers.abstract:
Staple-shaped Wilson line operators are necessary for the study of transverse
momentum-dependent parton distribution functions (TMDPDFs) in lattice QCD and
beyond. In this work, we study the renormalization of such operators in the
general case of an asymmetric staple. We analyze the mixing pattern of these
operators using their symmetry properties, where we find that the possible
mixing is restricted within groups of four operators. We then present numerical
results using the regularization independent momentum subtraction (RI/MOM)
scheme to study the importance of mixing using one operator in particular, the
$\gamma_0$ operator. Based on these results, we consider the short distance
ratio (SDR) scheme, which is desirable in the absence of mixing. Finally, we
investigate a variant of the RI/MOM scheme, where the renormalization factors
are computed at short distances.
authors:
Constantia Alexandrou, Simone Bacchio, Krzysztof Cichy, Martha
  Constantinou, Xu Feng, Karl Jansen, Chuan Liu, Aniket Sen, Gregoris
  Spanoudes, Fernanda Steffens, Jacopo Tarello

qualitative: Asymmetric staple-shaped Wilson line operators are essential for studying transverse momentum-dependent parton distribution functions (TMDPDFs) in lattice QCD.
The renormalization of asymmetric staples is analyzed through symmetry properties, which restrict operator mixing to groups of four operators.
Mixing between operators in the asymmetric staple family is nontrivial and must be accounted for in renormalization procedures.
The regularization-independent momentum subtraction (RI/MOM) scheme is used to compute renormalization factors numerically, revealing significant mixing effects.
The short-distance ratio (SDR) scheme is proposed as a desirable alternative in the absence of mixing, offering a cleaner renormalization framework.
A variant of the RI/MOM scheme is introduced where renormalization factors are computed at short distances, improving control over nonperturbative effects.
quantitative: The mixing pattern of asymmetric staple operators is restricted within groups of four, determined by symmetry considerations.
Numerical results in the RI/MOM scheme show non-negligible mixing effects, particularly for the γ₀ operator, indicating that mixing cannot be ignored.
The short-distance ratio (SDR) scheme is defined such that renormalization factors are extracted from ratios of correlators at short distances, minimizing mixing contributions.
The variant RI/MOM scheme computes renormalization factors at short momentum scales, with specific momentum configurations used to suppress lattice artifacts and enhance precision.

recall_papers.title: Transverse Momentum Distributions from Lattice QCD without Wilson Lines
recall_papers.abstract:
The transverse-momentum-dependent distributions (TMDs), which are defined by
gauge-invariant 3D parton correlators with staple-shaped lightlike Wilson
lines, can be calculated from quark and gluon correlators fixed in the Coulomb
gauge on a Euclidean lattice. These quantities can be expressed
gauge-invariantly as the correlators of Coulomb-gauge-dressed fields, which
reduce to the standard TMD correlators under principal-value prescription in
the infinite boost limit. In the framework of Large-Momentum Effective Theory,
a quasi-TMD defined from such correlators in a large-momentum hadron state can
be matched to the TMD via a factorization formula, whose exact form is derived
using Soft Collinear Effective Theory and verified at one-loop order. Compared
to the currently used gauge-invariant correlators, this new method can
substantially improve statistical precision and simplify renormalization for
the time-reversal-even TMDs, which will greatly enhance the predicative power
of lattice QCD in the non-perturbative region.
authors: Yong Zhao

qualitative: The transverse-momentum-dependent distributions (TMDs) can be computed on a Euclidean lattice using gauge-invariant correlators defined in the Coulomb gauge, avoiding the need for explicit Wilson lines.

The Coulomb-gauge (CG) quasi-TMD is defined via correlators of Coulomb-gauge-dressed quark fields, which are gauge-invariant and reduce to standard TMDs in the infinite boost limit under a principal-value prescription.

The gauge-invariant dressing of the quark field, ΨC(b) = UC(b)ψ(b), is constructed via a gauge transformation that rotates the gauge field into the Coulomb gauge, with UC(b) determined order-by-order in the strong coupling g.
In the Coulomb gauge (∇→⋅A→=0), the dressing factors ωm vanish for all m, simplifying the correlator to the standard CG form without non-local Wilson-line contributions.

Under an infinite Lorentz boost along the z-direction, the dressing factor UC(b) reduces to a path-ordered lightlike Wilson line 𝒲±n†(b), leading to the standard TMD correlator involving a lightlike Wilson line.

The orientation of the lightlike Wilson line (determined by boundary conditions at bz=±∞) affects time-reversal-odd TMDs (e.g., Sivers function), but not time-reversal-even TMDs or the factorization formula for quasi-TMDs.
For TMDs with time-reversal odd components, an additional transverse gauge link 𝒲⟂ is required at infinity to ensure full gauge invariance, though it is not essential for the current framework.

The renormalization of the CG quasi-TMD involves only the quark wave function renormalization, which is multiplicative, simplifying the renormalization procedure compared to standard gauge-invariant correlators.
This method improves statistical precision and simplifies renormalization for time-reversal-even TMDs, enhancing the predictive power of lattice QCD in the non-perturbative regime.
quantitative: CG quasi-TMD definition: B(x,b⟂,μ,Pz) = Pz ∫_{-∞}^{∞} dbz/(2π) e^{i x Pz bz} × (1/(2 Pt)) ⟨P| ψ¯(b) γt ψ |∇→⋅A→=0 |P⟩
Coulomb gauge condition: ∇→⋅A→ = 0
Gauge transformation for quark field: ΨC(b) = UC(b) ψ(b)
Gauge transformation for gauge field: A→C = UC A→ UC⁻¹ + (i/g) UC ∇→ UC⁻¹, with ∇→⋅A→C = 0
Dressing factor expansion: UC = 1 + ∑_{m=1}^∞ (-i g)^m / m! ω^m
First-order solution: ω₁ = 1/∇² (∇→⋅A→)
Second-order solution: ω₂ = 2! / ∇² [∇→⋅(ω₁† ∇→ ω₁) + [∇→ ω₁, A→]]
Infinite boost limit: ω₁(b) → 1/(∇₊ ± 0) A₊ = ∫_{∓∞}^{b⁻} db₁⁻ A₊(b₁⁻, b⟂)
Infinite boost limit: ω₂(b) → 2! ∫_{∓∞}^{b⁻} ∫_{∓∞}^{b₁⁻} db₁⁻ db₂⁻ A₊(b₂⁻, b⟂) A₊(b₁⁻, b⟂)
Light-cone coordinates: b± = (bt ± bz)/√2
Wilson line limit: UC(b) → 𝒲±n†(b) = ℘ exp[i g ∫_{b⁻}^{∓∞} db₁⁻ A₊(b₁⁻, b⟂)]
Standard TMD correlator: Oγ+(b) = ψ¯(b) 𝒲±n(b) γ+ 𝒲±n† ψ
Light-cone gauge limit: Oγ+(b)|A₊=0 = ψ¯(b) 𝒲⟂(∓∞⁻; b⟂, 0⟂) γ+ ψ
CG quasi-TMD for T-odd TMDs: OγtC(b)|∇→⋅A→=0 = ψ¯(b) 𝒲⟂(±∞z; b⟂, 0⟂) γt ψ

sorted_papers.title: Off-lightcone Wilson-line operators in gradient flow
sorted_papers.abstract:
Off-lightcone Wilson-line operators are constructed using local operators
connected by time-like or space-like Wilson lines, which ensure gauge

various contexts. For instance, space-like Wilson-line operators play a crucial

this work, we establish a systematic approach for calculating the matching from

sorted_papers.reason:
论文完全匹配查询核心需求。研究聚焦非局域格点关联函数（非光锥威尔逊线）与威尔逊线重整化，与“非局域格点关联函数和威尔逊线重整化”高度契合。方法上采用梯度流方案，实现从梯度流到$\overline{\rm MS}$的匹配，直接回应重整化需求。应用涵盖准PDF、胶子关联函数等，均属非局域格点关联函数范畴。所有关键要素均满足。

约束项检查：  
- C1：研究非局域格点关联函数 — 满足。证据：“Off-lightcone Wilson-line operators”、“lattice gradient flow computations of quark/gluon quasi-PDFs” 对比 “非局域 lattice correlators”  
- C2：涉及威尔逊线重整化 — 满足。证据：“matching from the gradient-flow scheme to the $\overline{\rm MS}$ scheme” 对比 “Wilson-line renormalization”

For instance, space-like Wilson-line operators play a crucial role in determining quasi-distribution function (quasi-PDFs) citebib.bib10 and pseudo-distribution functions (pseudo-PDFs) citebib.bib11, while time-like Wilson-line operators are essential for understanding quarkonium decay and production within the potential non-relativistic QCD (pNRQCD) effective theory citebib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20. Recently, there has been increasing interests in the study of the quasi-PDFs and pseudo-PDFs defined as the hadronic matrix elements of the Wilson operators of the type 𝒪Γ​(z​v)subscript𝒪Γ𝑧𝑣displaystyleOGamma(zv) =displaystyle= ψ¯​(z​v)​Γ​W​(z​v,0)​ψ​,¯𝜓𝑧𝑣Γ𝑊𝑧𝑣0𝜓0displaystylebarpsi(zv)Gamma W(zv,0)psi, 𝒪μ​ν​α​β​(z​v)superscript𝒪𝜇𝜈𝛼𝛽𝑧𝑣displaystyleOmunualphabeta(zv) =displaystyle= g2​Fμ​ν​(z​v)​W​(z​v,0)​Fα​β​,superscript𝑔2superscript𝐹𝜇𝜈𝑧𝑣𝑊𝑧𝑣0superscript𝐹𝛼𝛽0displaystyle g2Fmunu(zv)W(zv,0)Falphabeta, where ψ𝜓psi is a quark field, Fμ​νsuperscript𝐹𝜇𝜈Fmunu is the gluon field strength tensor, ΓΓGamma represents a Dirac structure, v𝑣v is a four-vector satisfying v2=−1superscript𝑣21v2 (or v2=1superscript𝑣21v2=1) for space-like (or time-like) Wilson operators in Minkowski space-time 2In this context and throughout subsequent discussions, we stay in Minkowski space-time. However, a transition to Euclidean space-time is warranted when introducing gradient flow, as it is defined in Euclidean space-time, which indicates v2=1superscript𝑣21v2=1 both for space-like and time-like Wilson-line operators. , z𝑧z is a real number and W​(z​v,0)𝑊𝑧𝑣0W(zv,0) is a straight-line path-ordered Wilson line connecting the two fields, W​(z​v,0)=P​exp⁡(i​g​∫0z𝑑s​v⋅A​(s​v)),𝑊𝑧𝑣0P𝑖𝑔superscriptsubscript0𝑧⋅differential-d𝑠𝑣𝐴𝑠𝑣displaystyle W(zv,0)=textPexpleft(igint0zds,vcdot A(sv)right), in which the fundamental representation and the adjoint representation of the gauge group are assumed for the quark case, and the gluon case, respectively. Without loss of generality, we choose z>0𝑧0z>0 throughout this work. Notably, quasi-PDFs and pseudo-PDFs can be factorized in relation to the standard light-cone parton distribution functions (PDFs) based on the short-distance operator product expansion (OPE) and the large momentum effective theory (LaMET) citebib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23. Employing hadronic matrix elements of off-lightcone Wilson-line operators as opposed to lightlike ones, offers the advantage of being considerably more suitable to lattice computations. However, this convenience comes with the trade-off of the highly non-trivial renormalization of the off-lightcone Wilson-line operators. For a comprehensive overview, we refer to ref. citebib.bib24. Furthermore, the vacuum expectation value of gluonic Wilson-line operators, such as the one defined in eq. , are related to the studies of quarkonium decay and production in the framework of pNRQCD, and the QCD vacuum structure (see ref. citebib.bib25 for a review). The gradient flow was originally introduced as a method to regularize and suppress the ultraviolet (UV) fluctuations citebib.bib26, bib.bib27 in lattice calculations. It has proven useful in lattice QCD scale settings and in lattice calculation of local matrix elements and correlation functions citebib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40. Alongside its noise-reducing effect, gradient flow exhibits the notable property that composite operators at positive flow time remain finite even in the continue limit, provided they are expressed using renormalized physical parameters and fields citebib.bib29. This property renders gradient flow suitable as a matching scheme between lattice and perturbative calculations. It is worth noting that gradient flow does not modify the infrared behavior of operators in the small flow-time limit, allowing the use of dimensional regularization to regulate infrared (IR) divergences when matching from the gradient-flow scheme to MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS-like schemes. The initial proposal to explore quasi-PDFs with the incorporation of gradient flow was presented in ref. citebib.bib41. Subsequently, a one-loop matching calculation was performed, considering full flow time dependencies, for the hadronic matrix element defined using the operator in eq. with zero external momenta, which is related to the quark quasi-PDF citebib.bib42. In this study, we present a systematic approach for calculating the matching from the gradient-flow scheme to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme for Wilson-line operators, such as those defined in eq. and eq. , specifically in the limit of small flow time. Our approach is based on the one-dimensional auxiliary-field formalism, and we provide comprehensive details of the one-loop order matching calculations. For more recent applications of perturbative gradient flow, see refs. citebib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib46, bib.bib47, bib.bib48, bib.bib49, bib.bib50, bib.bib51, bib.bib52, bib.bib53 and the references therein. We structure the remaining content of the paper as follows. In section , we introduce the one-dimensional auxiliary-field formalism and elaborate on its application in the renormalization of Wilson-line operators. In section , we introduce the gradient-flow formalism and implement the small flow-time expansion to the local current operators, yielding the core formulas for our matching calculations. Section is dedicated to the computation of the matching coefficients up to one-loop order. Our summary and conclusions can be found in section . In appendix , we present the detailed matching calculation with full flow time dependence for the hadronic matrix element that is related to quasi-PDFs at zero external momenta. We compare these findings with existing results from ref. citebib.bib42, as well as with the results of section in the small flow-time limit.

content_sections.3 Gradient flow and small flow-time expa: In the following, we work in d𝑑d-dimensional Euclidean space-time with d=4−2​ϵ𝑑42italic-ϵd=4-2epsilon. The gradient flow extends the d𝑑d-dimensional QCD to a (d+1)𝑑1(d+1)-dimensional field theory by introducing an additional coordinate t𝑡t (not to be confused with the time coordinate in Minkowski space-time) of mass dimension −22-2, which is called the flow time citebib.bib28, bib.bib29. In this work, we consider the local current operators defined in eqs. and eq. , which are flowed at the flow time t𝑡t. We use the same symbols for both the flowed and the un-flowed current operators as long as it does not lead to confusions. In the context of Wilson-line operators, we flow the external fields and the Wilson lines at the flow time t𝑡t. Specifically, this means that all the gluon fields directly connecting to the “heavy” auxiliary field lines are flowed at the flow time t𝑡t, while the “heavy” auxiliary fields themselves remain un-flowed. In the regime of small flow time, we employ a “short-distance” OPE for the flowed current operators. Our objective is to derive matching coefficients between renormalized flowed current operators and the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS renormalized un-flowed current operators, 𝒪R​(t)=c𝒪​(t,μ)​𝒪MS¯​(μ)+O​(t),superscript𝒪R𝑡subscript𝑐𝒪𝑡𝜇superscript𝒪¯MS𝜇𝑂𝑡displaystyleOrm R(t)=cO(t,mu)Ooverlinerm MS(mu)+O(t), where we have neglected operator mixing. We work in the massless limit for quark fields and set all the external scales to be zero whenever feasible. This strategic choice allows us to leverage established techniques from HQET for matching calculations. Specifically, we do not need to calculate the un-flowed operators because they yield scaleless integrals to all orders in perturbative calculations in dimensional regularization so that we can obtain the matching coefficients c𝒪​(t,μ)subscript𝑐𝒪𝑡𝜇cO(t,mu) from the renormalized flowed operators simply by subtracting the IR poles. Similarly to the renormalization of current operators, we can break down the matching coefficients c𝒪​(t,μ)subscript𝑐𝒪𝑡𝜇cO(t,mu) for the local current operators into products of field matching coefficients and corresponding vertex matching coefficients. (45d) Here ζ̊ψ​(t,μ)subscript̊𝜁𝜓𝑡𝜇mathringzetapsi(t,mu), ζhvF​(ζhvA)superscriptsubscript𝜁subscriptℎ𝑣𝐹superscriptsubscript𝜁subscriptℎ𝑣𝐴zetahvF(zetahvA) and ζA​(t,μ)subscript𝜁𝐴𝑡𝜇zetaA(t,mu) are the matching coefficients of the ringed fermion field χ̊̊𝜒mathringchi, the “heavy” auxiliary field hvsubscriptℎ𝑣hv in fundamental (adjoint) representation and the flowed gluon field B𝐵B, respectively. Additionally, ζψ​hv​(t,μ)subscript𝜁𝜓subscriptℎ𝑣𝑡𝜇zetapsi hv(t,mu), ζ∥⟂​(t,μ)zeta|perp(t,mu), ζ⟂⟂​(t,μ)subscript𝜁perpendicular-toabsentperpendicular-to𝑡𝜇zetaperpperp(t,mu) and ζF​(t,μ)subscript𝜁𝐹𝑡𝜇zetaF(t,mu) denote matching coefficients stemming from vertex corrections for the corresponding flowed local current operators. We calculate these matching coefficients to one-loop order in section . A key insight arising from our study in section on the multiplicative renormalization of Wilson-line operators is that matching coefficients for Wilson operators in the transition from the gradient-flow scheme to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme can be directly derived from matching coefficients for the corresponding local current operators. This occurs because in the one-dimensional auxiliary-field formalism, combinations of the renormalized local current operators at different space-time points do not introduce additional UV divergences, apart from the subtracted linear divergence, and because of eq. that relates local operators in the small flow-time limit. Therefore, additional matching for these combined local current operators in different space-time points is not needed in the small flow-time limit.
content_sections.4 Computations and results: While the matching coefficients for the fields can be gauge-dependent, it is important to note that the combinations leading to the local current operators under consideration in this work are gauge-invariant. Therefore, we have chosen to employ the Feynman gauge consistently throughout our calculations. Our computations are conducted in the context of Euclidean space-time. In Euclidean space-time, the renormalized Lagrangian ℒhvsubscriptℒsubscriptℎ𝑣Lhv is given by ℒhvE=Zhv​h¯v​(−i​v⋅∂+i​δ​m)​hv+g​Zg​ZA12​Zhv​h¯v​v⋅Aa​Ta​hv,superscriptsubscriptℒsubscriptℎ𝑣Esubscript𝑍subscriptℎ𝑣subscript¯ℎ𝑣⋅𝑖𝑣𝑖𝛿𝑚subscriptℎ𝑣⋅𝑔subscript𝑍𝑔superscriptsubscript𝑍𝐴12subscript𝑍subscriptℎ𝑣subscript¯ℎ𝑣𝑣superscript𝐴𝑎superscript𝑇𝑎subscriptℎ𝑣displaystyleLhvrm E=Zhvbarhv(-ivcdotpartial+idelta m)hv+gZgZAfrac12Zhvbarhvvcdot AaTahv, where the superscript “E” indicates Euclidean space-time. content_sections.4 Computations and results 4.1 δ​m𝛿𝑚\\de: The calculation of the self-energy of the “heavy” auxiliary field at one-loop in gradient flow is similar to that of the heavy quark field in HQET. Setting the external momentum p𝑝p flowing from left to right, the one-loop self-energy diagram shown in figure gives the amplitude ℳ(a)=g2​μ2​ϵ​CF​∫dd​k(2​π)d​1(k⋅v+p⋅v−i​0)​k2​e−2​t​k2,subscriptℳ𝑎superscript𝑔2superscript𝜇2italic-ϵsubscript𝐶𝐹superscript𝑑𝑑𝑘superscript2𝜋𝑑1⋅𝑘𝑣⋅𝑝𝑣𝑖0superscript𝑘2superscript𝑒2𝑡superscript𝑘2displaystyleM(a)=g2tildemu2epsilonCFintfracddk(2pi)dfrac1left(kcdot v+pcdot v-i0right)k2e-2tk2, where μ2​ϵ=(μ2​eγE4​π)ϵsuperscript𝜇2italic-ϵsuperscriptsuperscript𝜇2superscript𝑒subscript𝛾𝐸4𝜋italic-ϵtildemu2epsilon=left(fracmu2egammaE4piright)epsilon and we have chosen the “heavy” auxiliary field to be in fundamental representation. These amplitudes do not give expressions that are exactly proportional to the LO expression of the operator, g​F∥⟂μ​ν​hvgFmunu|perphv even after tensor reduction for the loop integrations. They lead to mixing with the gauge non-invariant operators i​vμ​A⟂ν​(i​v⋅D−i​δ​m)​hv𝑖superscript𝑣𝜇superscriptsubscript𝐴perpendicular-to𝜈⋅𝑖𝑣𝐷𝑖𝛿𝑚subscriptℎ𝑣ivmuAperpnu(ivcdot D-idelta m)hv, whose LO expression is proportional to q∥μ​A⟂νsuperscriptsubscript𝑞∥𝜇superscriptsubscript𝐴perpendicular-to𝜈q|muAperpnu, and thus it is not straightforward to disentangle the mixing from those that give contributions to the operator g​F∥⟂μ​ν​hvgFmunu|perphv. Fortunately, we can extract the contributions to the operator g​F∥⟂μ​ν​hvgFmunu|perphv through the results that are proportional to q⟂ν​A∥μsuperscriptsubscript𝑞perpendicular-to𝜈superscriptsubscript𝐴∥𝜇qperpnuA|mu. If we set t=0𝑡0t=0 in ℳ(e1)∥⟂M(e1)|perp and ℳ(e7)∥⟂M(e7)|perp, we can easily see that the linear divergences coming from ℳ(e7)∥⟂M(e7)|perp and the second term of ℳ(e1)∥⟂M(e1)|perp cancel with d=3−2​ϵ𝑑32italic-ϵd=3-2epsilon. However, in gradient flow scheme, the cancellation of linear divergences are much more complicated. Apparently, the linearly divergences arising from ℳ(e7)∥⟂M(e7)|perp and the second term of ℳ(e1)∥⟂M(e1)|perp do not cancel with d=4−2​ϵ𝑑42italic-ϵd=4-2epsilon and t>0𝑡0t>0. The cancellation of linear divergences in gradient-flow scheme will be restored when diagrams (e2)subscript𝑒2(e2) and (e3)subscript𝑒3(e3) are included. Great simplification can be achieved for our matching calculation by adopting the well-developed method used in the matching calculation in HQET. That is, we focus on the large loop momentum region k>>qmuch-greater-than𝑘𝑞k>>q and expand the amplitudes up to linear order of the external momentum q𝑞q before performing the loop momentum integration. This method is valid in our case because, in the small flow-time limit, the flowed operators share the same IR behavior with the corresponding un-flowed operators and contributions to the matching coefficients come from the UV region. The relationships between the matching coefficients of the local current operators and the matching coefficients of the fields and vertices are provided in eqs. . The one-loop results of ζhvsubscript𝜁subscriptℎ𝑣zetahv, ζ̊ψsubscript̊𝜁𝜓mathringzetapsi, ζψ​hvsubscript𝜁𝜓subscriptℎ𝑣zetapsi hv, ζAsubscript𝜁𝐴zetaA, ζ∥⟂zeta|perp, ζ⟂⟂subscript𝜁perpendicular-toabsentperpendicular-tozetaperpperp and ζFsubscript𝜁𝐹zetaF can be found in eq. , eq. , eq. , eq. , eq. , eq. and eq. , respectively. Implementing those results, we finally obtain cψ​hv​(t,μ)subscript𝑐𝜓subscriptℎ𝑣𝑡𝜇displaystyle cpsi hv(t,mu) =displaystyle= 1−αs4​π​CF​[32​log⁡(2​μ2​t​eγE)+log⁡2+1]+O​(αs2),1subscript𝛼𝑠4𝜋subscript𝐶𝐹delimited-322superscript𝜇2𝑡superscript𝑒subscript𝛾𝐸43221𝑂superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠2displaystyle 1-fracalphas4piCFleft[frac32logleft(2mu2tegammaEright)+fraclog2+1right]+O(alphas2), c∥⟂​(t,μ)displaystyle c|perp(t,mu) =displaystyle= 1+O​(αs2),1𝑂superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠2displaystyle 1+O(alphas2), c⟂⟂​(t,μ)subscript𝑐perpendicular-toabsentperpendicular-to𝑡𝜇displaystyle cperpperp(t,mu) =displaystyle= 1+αs4​π​CA×log⁡(2​μ2​t​eγE)+O​(αs2),1subscript𝛼𝑠4𝜋subscript𝐶𝐴2superscript𝜇2𝑡superscript𝑒subscript𝛾𝐸𝑂superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠2displaystyle 1+fracalphas4piCAtimeslogleft(2mu2tegammaEright)+O(alphas2), cF​(t,μ)subscript𝑐𝐹𝑡𝜇displaystyle cF(t,mu) =displaystyle= 1+αs4​π​CA​[2​log⁡(2​μ2​t​eγE)+78]+O​(αs2).1subscript𝛼𝑠4𝜋subscript𝐶𝐴delimited-22superscript𝜇2𝑡superscript𝑒subscript𝛾𝐸78𝑂superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠2displaystyle 1+fracalphas4piCAleft[2logleft(2mu2tegammaEright)+frac78right]+O(alphas2). As mentioned in the end of section , the matching coefficients for the Wilson-line operators can be directly obtained from the corresponding matching coefficients of the local current operators in the small flow-time limit within the one-dimensional auxiliary-field formalism. In the small flow-time limit, expressing the matching equations for the Wilson-line operators defined in eq. , eq. and eq. as 𝒪ΓR​(z​v,t)superscriptsubscript𝒪ΓR𝑧𝑣𝑡displaystyleOGammarm R(zv,t) =displaystyle= 𝒞ψ​(t,μ)​eδ​m​z​𝒪ΓMS¯​(z​v)+O​(t),subscript𝒞𝜓𝑡𝜇superscript𝑒𝛿𝑚𝑧superscriptsubscript𝒪Γ¯MS𝑧𝑣𝑂𝑡displaystyleCpsi(t,mu)edelta mzOGammaoverlinerm MS(zv)+O(t), 𝒪∥⟂R​(z​v,t)displaystyleO|perprm R(zv,t) =displaystyle= 𝒞∥⟂​(t,μ)​eδ​m​z​𝒪∥⟂MS¯​(z​v)+O​(t),displaystyleC|perp(t,mu)edelta mzO|perpoverlinerm MS(zv)+O(t), 𝒪∥⟂R​(z​v,t)displaystyleO|perprm R(zv,t) =displaystyle= 𝒞⟂⟂​(t,μ)​eδ​m​z​𝒪⟂⟂MS¯​(z​v)+O​(t),subscript𝒞perpendicular-toabsentperpendicular-to𝑡𝜇superscript𝑒𝛿𝑚𝑧superscriptsubscript𝒪perpendicular-toabsentperpendicular-to¯MS𝑧𝑣𝑂𝑡displaystyleCperpperp(t,mu)edelta mzOperpperpoverlinerm MS(zv)+O(t), where we have omitted the Lorentz indices for simplicity, we establish relations 𝒞ψ​(t,μ)subscript𝒞𝜓𝑡𝜇displaystyleCpsi(t,mu) =displaystyle= cψ​hv2​(t,μ),subscriptsuperscript𝑐2𝜓subscriptℎ𝑣𝑡𝜇displaystyle c2psi hv(t,mu), 𝒞∥⟂​(t,μ)displaystyleC|perp(t,mu) =displaystyle= c∥⟂2​(t,μ),displaystyle c2|perp(t,mu), 𝒞⟂⟂​(t,μ)subscript𝒞perpendicular-toabsentperpendicular-to𝑡𝜇displaystyleCperpperp(t,mu) =displaystyle= c⟂⟂2​(t,μ). subscriptsuperscript𝑐2perpendicular-toabsentperpendicular-to𝑡𝜇displaystyle c2perpperp(t,mu). These relations are independent of z𝑧z and external states of the matrix elements constructed using these Wilson-line operators. Regarding the spin-dependent potentials in terms of chromomagnetic field insertions into a Wilson loop, each such insertion will lead to a factor of the matching coefficient cF​(t,μ)subscript𝑐𝐹𝑡𝜇cF(t,mu) for the matching from the gradient-flow scheme to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme.
content_sections.5 Summary and outlook: We have developed a systematic approach for matching from the gradient-flow scheme to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme for Wilson-line operators in the small flow-time limit. This approach extends to various applications, including quasi-PDFs, gluonic correlators that appear in quarkonium decay and production in the framework of pNRQCD and spin-dependent potentials in terms of chromomagnetic field insertions into a Wilson loop. Within the one-dimensional auxiliary-field formalism, the matching from the gradient-flow scheme to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme for the Wilson-line operators discussed in this work can be reduced to the matching for the corresponding local current operators in the small flow-time limit. We match these local current operators from the gradient-flow scheme to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme by comparing perturbative computations in these two schemes. Great simplification for the matching procedure can be achieved by using small flow-time expansion and setting external momenta to be zero whenever possible. In this way, the well-developed method of matching calculation in HQET can be applied. After completing the matching for the local current operators, one automatically obtains the matching coefficients for the corresponding Wilson-line operators in the small flow-time limit, because the combinations of the renormalized local current operators at different spacetime do not introduce further UV divergences, apart from the subtracted linear divergences, due to the multiplicative renormalizability of the Wilson-line operators. The matching for the local current operators can be further decomposed as the matching for the fields and vertices due to the multiplicative renormalizability of the local current operators. We thus compute the matching coefficients at one-loop level for auxiliary “heavy” auxiliary field hvsubscriptℎ𝑣hv, massless quark field ψ𝜓psi, ψ​hv𝜓subscriptℎ𝑣psi hv vertex, gluon field g​A𝑔𝐴gA and the vertices of the local current operators g​F∥⟂μ​ν​hv,g​F⟂⟂μ​ν​hvgFmunu|perphv,,gFmunuperpperphv and g​h¯v​F⟂⟂μ​ν​hv𝑔subscript¯ℎ𝑣subscriptsuperscript𝐹𝜇𝜈perpendicular-toabsentperpendicular-tosubscriptℎ𝑣gbarhvFmunuperpperphv. The one-loop result of the linearly divergent “mass” correction δ​m𝛿𝑚delta m is also obtained. Provided with these results, we give the matching coefficients for the local current operators and the Wilson-line operators in the small flow-time limit. It is worth mentioning that this method is also applicable for matching from lattice regularization schemes to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme in the small lattice spacing limit, albeit with added complexity due to UV and IR divergence treatment citebib.bib68. In the appendix, we have shown that, at one-loop level, for the case of hadronic matrix element defined in eq. at zero external momenta, the finite flow time effect is very small as long as the flow radius is smaller than physical distance z𝑧z, which is usually satisfied in lattice gradient flow computations. With the systematic approach we have developed for the matching from the gradient-flow scheme to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme for the Wilson-line operators, it is straightforward to extend the current study to two-loop order, which would be more helpful for lattice gradient flow computations of matrix elements constructed using the off-lightcone Wilson-line operators. We leave it for future research.
content_sections.5 Summary and outlook Note added: Before suission of this work, we noticed that the one-loop result of the matching coefficient for the gluonic correlator GBsubscript𝐺𝐵GB was recently published in ref. citebib.bib69, which was the same as our one-loop result of 𝒞⟂⟂​(t,μ)subscript𝒞perpendicular-toabsentperpendicular-to𝑡𝜇Cperpperp(t,mu). Vairo for reading the paper and giving very useful comments. The work of N. B. and X. -P. sorted_papers.title:
Renormalization of asymmetric staple-shaped Wilson-line operators in
  lattice and continuum perturbation theory
sorted_papers.abstract:
In this work, we study the renormalization of nonlocal quark bilinear
operators containing an asymmetric staple-shaped Wilson line at the one-loop
level in both lattice and continuum perturbation theory. These operators enter
the first-principle calculation of transverse momentum-dependent parton
distribution functions (TMDPDFs) in lattice QCD using the formulation of Large
Momentum Effective Theory. We provide appropriate RI$'$-type conditions that
address the power and logarithmic divergences, as well as the mixing among
staple operators of different Dirac structures, using a number of different
possible projectors. A variant of RI$'$, including calculations of rectangular
Wilson loops, which cancel the pinch-pole singularities of the staple operators
at infinite length and reduce residual power divergences, is also employed. We
calculate at one-loop order the conversion matrix, which relates the
quasi-TMDPDFs in the RI$'$-type schemes to the reference scheme $\overline{\rm
MS}$ for arbitrary values of the renormalization momentum scale and of the
dimensions of the staple.

sections.4: Anderle et al., “Electron-ion collider in China,” Front
sections.6: Accardi et al., “Electron Ion Collider: The Next QCD Frontier: Understanding the glue that binds us all,” Eur
sections.9: Brandt, Filippo Neri,  and Masa-aki Sato, “Renormalization of Loop Functions for All Loops,” Phys
sections.13: Cohen,  and Xiangdong Ji, “Flavor Structure of the Nucleon Sea from Lattice QCD,” Phys

qualitative.statement:
{
  "qualitative": [
    "Nonlocal quark bilinear operators with asymmetric staple-shaped Wilson lines are essential for first-principles lattice QCD calculations of transverse momentum-dependent parton distribution functions (TMDPDFs) within the Large Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET) framework.",
    "The renormalization of these operators involves both power and logarithmic divergences, as well as mixing among different Dirac structures, necessitating careful treatment in both lattice and continuum perturbation theory.",
    "RI'-type renormalization conditions are adapted to handle divergences and operator mixing, with the use of multiple projectors to isolate specific Dirac components.",
    "A variant of the RI' scheme incorporating rectangular Wilson loops is introduced to cancel pinch-pole singularities at infinite staple length and reduce residual power divergences.",
    "The conversion matrix between the RI'-type schemes and the $\overline{\rm MS}$ scheme is computed at one-loop order, enabling the matching of quasi-TMDPDFs to physical TMDPDFs in a well-defined renormalization scheme."
  ],
  "quantitative": [
    "The conversion matrix between the RI'-type schemes and the $\overline{\rm MS}$ scheme is calculated at one-loop order for arbitrary renormalization momentum scale $\\mu$ and arbitrary staple dimensions.",
    "The renormalization of the staple-shaped Wilson-line operators involves the evaluation of one-loop Feynman diagrams with asymmetric staple geometries, including contributions from both quark and gluon loops.",
    "The use of rectangular Wilson loops in the modified RI' scheme leads to cancellation of pinch-pole singularities in the infinite-length limit of the staple.",
    "Residual power divergences are reduced through the inclusion of rectangular Wilson loop contributions in the renormalization condition.",
    "The renormalization factors and mixing matrices are derived using a set of projectors that distinguish between different Dirac structures of the staple operators."
  ]
}

authors: Gregoris Spanoudes, Martha Constantinou, Haralambos Panagopoulos

content_sections.I Introduction: One of the directions of research in lattice QCD, which has shown rapid progress in the last decade, is the first-principle study of a family of nonperturbative distribution functions (DFs) that describe the internal structure of hadrons: parton distribution functions (PDFs), generalized parton distribution functions (GPDs) and transverse-momentum dependent parton distributions functions (TMDPDFs). All three types of DFs are crucial for a comprehensive understanding of the three-dimensional hadron picture. Calculating DFs from first principles has long been a challenge in Hadron Physics due to their nonperturbative and light-cone nature. The latter does not allow for a direct nonperturbative computation of DFs on Euclidean lattice. In the last decade, a groundbreaking approach by X. Jicitebib.bib1 has overcome this issue. The approach connects Euclidean equal-time correlation functions (referred to as quasi-DFs), which are accessible by lattice simulations, to the physical light-cone DFs using the framework of Large Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET)citebib.bib2. This breakthrough has paved the way for extracting, for the first time, DFs from lattice simulations. Several groups have successfully employed this approach in the calculation of various quark and gluon DFs on the lattice: quark PDFs and GPDs in Refs. citebib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41, bib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib46, bib.bib47, bib.bib48, bib.bib49, bib.bib50, quark TMDPDFs and soft-function in Refs. citebib.bib51, bib.bib52, bib.bib53, bib.bib54, bib.bib55, bib.bib56, bib.bib57, bib.bib58, bib.bib59, bib.bib60, bib.bib61, bib.bib62, bib.bib63, bib.bib64, bib.bib65, bib.bib66, bib.bib67, bib.bib68, gluon PDFs in Refs. citebib.bib69, bib.bib70, bib.bib71, bib.bib72, bib.bib73, bib.bib74, bib.bib75, bib.bib76, bib.bib77, bib.bib78 and hadronic light-cone distribution amplitudes (DA) in Refs. citebib.bib79, bib.bib80, bib.bib7, bib.bib81, bib.bib82. An overview of recent progress in the research of DFs on the lattice can be found in Refs. citebib.bib83, bib.bib84, bib.bib85, bib.bib86, bib.bib87. The goal of these studies is to complement the planned experimental programs for investigating the 3D tomography of the nucleon in major experimental facilities, such as the electron ion colliders of USAcitebib.bib88, bib.bib89 and Chinacitebib.bib90. Theoretical studies can significantly complement the experimental investigations, especially when there are limitations either in the experimental programs or in the phenomenological models used for the analysis of the experimental data. The computation of DFs using Ji’s approach (also called the “quasi-PDFs” approach) involves a three-step process. Firstly, nonperturbative calculations are performed to determine hadron matrix elements of gauge-invariant quark or gluon nonlocal operators. These operators contain path-ordered Wilson lines with specific shapes, such as straight lines for PDFs and staple-shaped lines for TMDPDFs. Secondly, the nonlocal operators are renormalized to establish a connection with physically measurable quantities. This task is challenging compared to the case of local operators, as explained below. Lastly, the renormalized lattice DFs are perturbatively matched to the corresponding physical light-cone DFs using LaMET. One-loop matching formulae have been extracted in the literature for several quasi DFscitebib.bib91, bib.bib92; in some cases a two-loop formula is also availablecitebib.bib93, bib.bib94. In this study, we focus on the implementation of the second step in the quasi-PDFs approach regarding the renormalization of nonlocal operators using one-loop perturbation theory in both continuum and lattice regularizations. While a nonperturbative calculation of the renormalization functions in numerical simulations of lattice QCD is desirable, perturbation theory can give important feedback for the development of an appropriate nonperturbative renormalization prescription, which addresses all kinds of divergences, as well as possible mixing with operators of equal or lower dimension allowed by global symmetries. Most importantly, perturbation theory gives us the matching functions (at a given order) between nonperturbative renormalization schemes and continuum perturbative schemes – primarily MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS – employed in phenomenology. Studies of nonlocal operators with Wilson lines in continuum perturbation theory go back decades, including seminal workcitebib.bib95, bib.bib96, bib.bib97, bib.bib98, bib.bib99, bib.bib100, bib.bib101, bib.bib102, bib.bib103, bib.bib104 for the renormalization of open and closed (loops) Wilson lines, with and without singular points (cusps, self-intersections), and having quark or gluon fields at the endpoints. Lattice studies of nonlocal operators have emerged only in the last decade after the development of the quasi-PDFs approach. The first perturbative lattice calculation of Wilson-line operators was made by our group in Ref. citebib.bib105, to one loop for massless quarks, using the Wilson/clover fermion action and a variety of Symanzik-improved gluon actions. A straight Wilson line with quark fields at the endpoints was employed in order to investigate the renormalization of quark quasi-PDFs. This study showed that the lattice formulation introduces several new complications, such as mixing among operators of equal dimension and different twists, and power-law divergences (even in the absence of mixing with lower-dimensional operators, in contrast to the case of local operators). A number of extensions of this study have been followed by our group regarding the presence of finite quark masscitebib.bib106 and the calculation of one-loop artifacts to all orders in the lattice spacing a𝑎acitebib.bib107. In a different extension of our study, we have considered nonlocal operators with a symmetric staple-shaped Wilson linecitebib.bib108 in order to investigate the renormalization of quark quasi-TMDPDFs. In our current work, we extend further the latter calculation by employing the more general case of an asymmetric staple-shaped Wilson line, where all three segments of the staple can have different lengths. Studies by other groups along these lines have appeared in Refs. citebib.bib109, bib.bib110, bib.bib111, bib.bib112. Staple-shaped nonlocal operators have a wide range of applicability. They enter the analysis of semi-inclusive deep inelastic scattering (SIDIS) processes, as well as the Drell-Yan (DY) processes, in a kinematic region where the photon virtuality is large and the measured transverse momentum of the produced hadron is of the order of ΛQCDsubscriptΛQCDLambdarm QCDcitebib.bib113. In these analyses, the segments of the staple that are parallel to the direction in which the hadron is boosted have an infinite length. Thus, while our study focuses on finite lengths of the staple segments, an extrapolation to infinite limit must be taken in the renormalized matrix elements of these operators. The presence or not of an asymmetry in the shape of the staple operators affects their renormalization. Besides the presence of an additional scale in the renormalization conditions, the mixing pattern is different between symmetric and asymmetric staple operators (see Sec. ). However, this difference is not visible in one-loop lattice perturbation theory, as concluded by the present calculation. As stated before, there are a number of challenges to address in order to renormalize the nonlocal Wilson-line operators of arbitrary shape: 1. Power divergences arise for cutoff regularized theories, such as lattice QCDcitebib.bib98. The divergences depend on the total length (L𝐿L) of the Wilson line and can be absorbed in an exponential factor of the form e−δ​m​Lsuperscript𝑒𝛿𝑚𝐿e-delta mL, where δ​m𝛿𝑚delta m is a dimensionful regularization-dependent quantity whose magnitude diverges linearly with the regulator. 2. Logarithmic divergences arise not only from contact terms but also from the singular points of the Wilson line. In the case of a straight line, singular points are just the endpoints. For operators of different shapes, there can also be cusp divergencescitebib.bib100, which depend on the angle and number of cusps present. In the case of the staple line, there are two cusps of angle π/2𝜋2pi/2. These divergences can be addressed by using typical renormalization schemes, such as RI or ratio schemes. 3. Finite operator mixing arises between Wilson-line operators 𝒪Γsubscript𝒪ΓOGamma with different (products of) Dirac matrices ΓΓGamma depending on the regularization. In the case of straight-line operators, the one-loop computationcitebib.bib105 shows mixing in pairs between nonlocal fermion bilinears of the form (𝒪Γsubscript𝒪ΓOGamma, 𝒪Γ,γν1/2subscript𝒪Γsubscript𝛾subscript𝜈12OGamma,gammanu1/2), where ν1subscript𝜈1hatnu1 is the direction of the straight line, for chirality-breaking actions. Symmetry arguments (reflections, charge conjugation)citebib.bib114 confirm that the one-loop mixing pattern is also valid nonperturbatively. In the case of (symmetric and asymmetric) staple operators, the one-loop computationcitebib.bib108 shows mixing in pairs with a different pattern: (𝒪Γsubscript𝒪ΓOGamma, 𝒪[Γ,γν2]/2subscript𝒪Γsubscript𝛾subscript𝜈22O[Gamma,gammanu2]/2), where ν2subscript𝜈2hatnu2 is the direction of the sides of the staple line. However, symmetry argumentscitebib.bib68 show a wider mixing pattern: quadruplets of operators emerge in the asymmetric case and triplets in the symmetric case when nonchiral fermions are employed. 4. In the case of (symmetric and asymmetric) staple operators, a pinch-pole singularitycitebib.bib57 arises when one takes the infinite limit of lateral sizes of the staple (this limit is included in the definition of TMDPDFs). This singularity – a linear divergence – comes from the gluon exchange between the two parallel (“longitudinal”) segments of the staple. 5. The renormalization functions are (in general) complex in nonminimal subtraction schemes1The presence of an imaginary part in the renormalization functions depends on the exact definition of the renormalization scheme. For example, one can define purely real renormalization conditions by using only the real part of “projected” Green’s functions; in this way, both the real and the imaginary part of the renormalized Green’s functions will be rendered finite. . Nonperturbative renormalization prescriptionscitebib.bib115, bib.bib8, bib.bib116, bib.bib58, bib.bib65, bib.bib68 have already been employed in lattice simulations for both straight-line and staple operators using RI-type schemes. There are also studies of alternative prescriptions, such as the ratio (or short-distance ratio) schemecitebib.bib66, Wilson-line-mass-subtraction schemecitebib.bib117, bib.bib109, RI-xMOM scheme (using the auxiliary-field approachcitebib.bib103, bib.bib109, bib.bib118, bib.bib119, bib.bib120) and a hybrid renormalization schemecitebib.bib121. While a standard RI-type scheme (extended in the presence of mixing) can treat the various types of divergences and mixing of the nonlocal operators at one loop, recent nonperturbative examinationscitebib.bib122, bib.bib66 at different lattice spacings have provided compelling evidence indicating the existence of residual linear divergences in both straight-line and staple operators. In this regard, an alternative version of RI, as suggested in Refs. citebib.bib123, bib.bib65, which includes the computation of vacuum expectation value for rectangular Wilson loops, is also employed in our current work. This prescription (described in Sec. ) is expected to suppress the residual power divergences from the Green’s functions of the staple operators (see Sec. ). The paper is organized as follows: In Sec. , we provide the setup of our calculation, including the definition of the operators under study and the renormalization conditions that we employ throughout, corresponding to four different variants of the RI scheme. Sections and present our main results in dimensional and lattice regularization, respectively. This includes both the renormalization functions and conversion matrices between the RI-type and MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS schemes. In Sec. , we summarize and give some future plans. We have also included three appendices. In Appendix, we provide a list of formulae for the calculation, in dimensional regularization, of one-loop Feynman integrals appropriate to nonlocal operators. Appendix contains the definitions of Feynman-parameter integrals appearing in our results. In Appendix, we collect one-loop results for the renormalization of the quark field in both dimensional and lattice regularizations.

Other discretizations involve smeared gluon links, e. g. , stout, HYP, Wilson flow. We plan to investigate the impact of stout smearing at the one-loop level in future work. There is a total of 16 different operators which can be extracted from Eq. depending on the choice of the Dirac matrix ΓΓGamma inserted between the fermion and antifermion fields: Γ=𝟙Γ𝟙Gamma=openone (scalar S𝑆S), γ5≡γ1​γ2​γ3​γ4subscript𝛾5subscript𝛾1subscript𝛾2subscript𝛾3subscript𝛾4gamma5equivgamma1gamma2gamma3gamma4 (pseudoscalar P𝑃P), γμsubscript𝛾𝜇gammamu (vector Vμsubscript𝑉𝜇Vmu), γ5​γμsubscript𝛾5subscript𝛾𝜇gamma5gammamu (axial vector Aμsubscript𝐴𝜇Amu), σμ​ν≡[γμ,γν]/2subscript𝜎𝜇𝜈subscript𝛾𝜇subscript𝛾𝜈2sigmamunuequiv[gammamu,gammanu]/2 (tensor Tμ​νsubscript𝑇𝜇𝜈Tmunu). Of particular interest is the study of vector, axial-vector, and tensor operators, which correspond to the unpolarized, helicity, and transversity types of TMDPDFs, respectively. Since we are working in one-loop perturbation theory with massless fermions, the flavor content of the fermion fields ψ𝜓psi and ψ¯¯𝜓barpsi is irrelevant for our calculations up to this order. Thus, our one-loop results are valid for both flavor singlet and nonsinglet operators, given that there is no additional mixing with gluon nonlocal operatorscitebib.bib119, bib.bib70.
content_sections.II Calculation setup II.2 Symmetry prope: We examine below the properties of staple operators under symmetry transformations. Similar investigations can be found incitebib.bib65, bib.bib68. Operators with the same behavior under all symmetries can mix among themselves. In this way, one can identify the mixing pattern of the staple operators based on nonperturbative arguments. Since the staple operators depend on two special directions (ν1subscript𝜈1hatnu1 and ν2subscript𝜈2hatnu2) in which the staple is defined, we consider appropriate versions of the symmetry transformations of the QCD action (in Euclidean space) with respect to the special directions. 1. Translational symmetry: The operators are covariant under translations in Euclidean space. Similarly to the case of local operators 𝒪​(x)𝒪𝑥cal O(x) which cannot mix with translated versions of themselves (𝒪​(y)𝒪𝑦cal O(y)), mixing among nonlocal operators involving different paths and shapes for the Wilson line joining the fermion-antifermion pair cannot occurcitebib.bib98, bib.bib100, bib.bib103. 2. Two-dimensional (2D) rotational (or square, on the lattice) symmetry: The operators are covariant under rotations over the two-dimensional (2D) plane transverse to the plane in which the staple is defined. The 16 staple operators are classified into two representations of the 2D rotational group: scalar: S,P,Vν1,Vν2,Aν1,Aν2,Tν1​ν2,Tν3​ν4𝑆𝑃subscript𝑉subscript𝜈1subscript𝑉subscript𝜈2subscript𝐴subscript𝜈1subscript𝐴subscript𝜈2subscript𝑇subscript𝜈1subscript𝜈2subscript𝑇subscript𝜈3subscript𝜈4S,P,Vnu1,Vnu2,Anu1,Anu2,Tnu1nu2,Tnu3nu4, vector: (Vν3,Vν4),(Aν3,Aν4),(Tν3​ν1,Tν4​ν1),(Tν3​ν2,Tν4​ν2)subscript𝑉subscript𝜈3subscript𝑉subscript𝜈4subscript𝐴subscript𝜈3subscript𝐴subscript𝜈4subscript𝑇subscript𝜈3subscript𝜈1subscript𝑇subscript𝜈4subscript𝜈1subscript𝑇subscript𝜈3subscript𝜈2subscript𝑇subscript𝜈4subscript𝜈2(Vnu3,Vnu4),(Anu3,Anu4),(Tnu3nu1,Tnu4nu1),(Tnu3nu2,Tnu4nu2), where (ν1,ν2,ν3,ν4)subscript𝜈1subscript𝜈2subscript𝜈3subscript𝜈4(nu1,nu2,nu3,nu4) correspond to different orthogonal directions in the 4D Euclidean space. The first representation is one-dimensional while the second is two-dimensional reducible (e. g. , (Vν3,Vν4)subscript𝑉subscript𝜈3subscript𝑉subscript𝜈4(Vnu3,Vnu4) splits into two different one-dimensional representations: Vν3+i​Vν4subscript𝑉subscript𝜈3𝑖subscript𝑉subscript𝜈4Vnu3+iVnu4 and Vν3−i​Vν4subscript𝑉subscript𝜈3𝑖subscript𝑉subscript𝜈4Vnu3-iVnu4). Operator mixing can only occur among operators that support the same irreducible representation. The fact that an operator such as Vν3+i​Vν4subscript𝑉subscript𝜈3𝑖subscript𝑉subscript𝜈4Vnu3+iVnu4 can mix with Aν3+i​Aν4subscript𝐴subscript𝜈3𝑖subscript𝐴subscript𝜈4Anu3+iAnu4 but not with Aν3−i​Aν4subscript𝐴subscript𝜈3𝑖subscript𝐴subscript𝜈4Anu3-iAnu4 implies certain relations among the corresponding renormalization and mixing coefficients. Thus, in contrast to the local operators, which are covariant under 4D rotations, the residual rotational symmetry cannot completely prevent mixing between scalar, pseudoscalar, vector, axial-vector, and tensor operators. Also, operators which support the same representation of the 4D rotational group but different representations of the 2D rotational group, e. g. Vν1subscript𝑉subscript𝜈1Vnu1 (or Vν2subscript𝑉subscript𝜈2Vnu2) and Vρ≠(ν1,ν2)subscript𝑉𝜌subscript𝜈1subscript𝜈2Vrhoneq(nu1,nu2), will not share the same renormalization factor (in contrast to the case of local operators); at least, the 2D rotational symmetry prevents the mixing among these operators. 3. Parity (𝒫𝒫P): In Euclidean space, temporal and spatial directions are not distinguished. Thus, parity can be generalized in any direction. The generalized parity transformations 𝒫μsubscript𝒫𝜇Pmu for the fermion and gluon fields with respect to the direction μ𝜇mu are defined below. Charge conjugation does not provide any additional information on the mixing of asymmetric staple operators compared to the previously mentioned symmetries. We conclude that when the fermion action breaks chiral symmetry (e. g. , Wilson/clover fermions, twisted-mass fermions), mixing among the asymmetric staple operators (𝒪Γ,𝒪Γ​γν1​γν2,𝒪Γ​γν1,𝒪Γ​γν2)subscript𝒪Γsubscript𝒪Γsubscript𝛾subscript𝜈1subscript𝛾subscript𝜈2subscript𝒪Γsubscript𝛾subscript𝜈1subscript𝒪Γsubscript𝛾subscript𝜈2(OGamma,OGammagammanu1gammanu2,OGammagammanu1,OGammagammanu2) is allowed. When chiral fermions (e. g. , massless overlap/domain-wall/continuum fermions) are employed, the mixing pattern is minimized to (𝒪Γ,𝒪Γ​γν1​γν2)subscript𝒪Γsubscript𝒪Γsubscript𝛾subscript𝜈1subscript𝛾subscript𝜈2(OGamma,OGammagammanu1gammanu2). In the specific case of symmetric staple operators (y′=ysuperscript𝑦′𝑦yprime=y), the basis of independent operators is reduced (𝒪i=0subscript𝒪𝑖0widetildeOi=0, for i>4𝑖4i>4) and the mixing pattern takes the form: (𝒪Γ,𝒪[Γ,γν1​γν2]/2,𝒪[Γ,γν1]/2,𝒪[Γ,γν2]/2)subscript𝒪Γsubscript𝒪Γsubscript𝛾subscript𝜈1subscript𝛾subscript𝜈22subscript𝒪Γsubscript𝛾subscript𝜈12subscript𝒪Γsubscript𝛾subscript𝜈22(OGamma,O[Gamma,gammanu1gammanu2]/2,O[Gamma,gammanu1]/2,O[Gamma,gammanu2]/2) for nonchiral fermions and (𝒪Γ,𝒪[Γ,γν1​γν2]/2)subscript𝒪Γsubscript𝒪Γsubscript𝛾subscript𝜈1subscript𝛾subscript𝜈22(OGamma,O[Gamma,gammanu1gammanu2]/2) for chiral fermions. Depending on the Dirac matrix ΓΓGamma, one or three out of the three commutators [Γ,γν1]Γsubscript𝛾subscript𝜈1[Gamma,gammanu1], [Γ,γν2]Γsubscript𝛾subscript𝜈2[Gamma,gammanu2], [Γ,γν1​γν2]Γsubscript𝛾subscript𝜈1subscript𝛾subscript𝜈2[Gamma,gammanu1gammanu2] appearing in the mixing pattern of the symmmetric staple operators with nonchiral fermions will be zero, thus leading to a mixing triplet or singlet (multiplicatively renormalizable operator), respectively. Similarly, when [Γ,γν1​γν2]≠0Γsubscript𝛾subscript𝜈1subscript𝛾subscript𝜈20[Gamma,gammanu1gammanu2]neq 0, the mixing pattern of the symmetric staple operators with chiral fermions gives a mixing pair; otherwise it leads to a multiplicatively renormalizable operator. In contrast to the symmetric case, symmetry arguments alone are not sufficient to establish multiplicative renormalization for any asymmetric operator. However, all-order perturbative studies in the continuum show that a multiplicative renormalization can address all the divergences of the nonlocal Wilson-line operatorscitebib.bib98, bib.bib100, bib.bib103. Thus, the mixing which is allowed by symmetries is absent in minimal subtraction schemes. However, it can occur as finite mixing in nonminimal schemes. On the lattice, the mixing is present when employing MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS or any nonperturbative intermediate scheme; while MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS is a minimal continuum scheme, on the lattice, additional finite regularization-dependent terms contribute to the renormalization functions in order to be able to match the continuum MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS-renormalized Green’s functions. In our work, we focus on the wider mixing pattern arising in the case of asymmetric staple operators when a chirality-breaking fermion action is employed. Our goal is to construct a common prescription for renormalizing the staple operators in any regularization (regularization independent). To this end, we consider the following quadruplets of the sixteen independent bilinear operators 𝒪Γsubscript𝒪ΓOGamma (with Γ∈SiΓsubscript𝑆𝑖Gammain Si)3There is freedom in choosing the signs in front of each operator, leading to different conventions. : S1subscript𝑆1displaystyle S1 ≡displaystyleequiv 𝟙,σν𝟙​ν𝟚,γν𝟙,γν𝟚,displaystyle openone , sigmanu1nu2, gammanu1 , gammanu2 , (II.2.33) S2subscript𝑆2displaystyle S2 ≡displaystyleequiv γ5,σν4​ν3,γ5​γν1,γ5​γν2,subscript𝛾5subscript𝜎subscript𝜈4subscript𝜈3subscript𝛾5subscript𝛾subscript𝜈1subscript𝛾5subscript𝛾subscript𝜈2displaystyle gamma5 , sigmanu4nu3, gamma5gammanu1, gamma5gammanu2 , (II.2.34) S3subscript𝑆3displaystyle S3 ≡displaystyleequiv γν3,γ5​γν4,σν3​ν1,σν3​ν2,subscript𝛾subscript𝜈3subscript𝛾5subscript𝛾subscript𝜈4subscript𝜎subscript𝜈3subscript𝜈1subscript𝜎subscript𝜈3subscript𝜈2displaystyle gammanu3, gamma5gammanu4, sigmanu3nu1, sigmanu3nu2 , (II.2.35) S4subscript𝑆4displaystyle S4 ≡displaystyleequiv γν4,γ5​γν3,σν4​ν1,σν4​ν2. subscript𝛾subscript𝜈4subscript𝛾5subscript𝛾subscript𝜈3subscript𝜎subscript𝜈4subscript𝜈1subscript𝜎subscript𝜈4subscript𝜈2displaystyle gammanu4, gamma5gammanu3, sigmanu4nu1, sigmanu4nu2 . (II.2.36) In the present study, we do not consider possible mixing (on the lattice) with higher dimensional operators multiplied by the appropriate power of the lattice spacing. In the case of local operators, such mixing is present only for finite values of the lattice spacing, and it vanishes when taking the continuum limit. However, in the case of nonlocal operators, where power divergences a−n,n∈ℤ+superscript𝑎𝑛𝑛superscriptℤa-n, ninZ+ are present, 𝒪​(a)𝒪𝑎O(a) effects in the bare Green’s functions can contribute to the renormalized Green’s functions at two loops4𝒪​(a​g2)𝒪𝑎superscript𝑔2O(a g2) terms from the bare Green’s function multiplied by 𝒪​(1/a​g2)𝒪1𝑎superscript𝑔2O(1/a g2) terms from the renormalization function lead to 𝒪​(a0​g4)𝒪superscript𝑎0superscript𝑔4O(a0 g4) contributions to the renormalized Green’s function. . Alternatively, one can suppress these unwanted effects in two ways: by removing power divergences from the Green’s function through an appropriate ratio with another Green’s function which has the same power divergences, e. g. , a closed Wilson loop, by subtracting artifacts from the bare Green’s functions calculated in lattice perturbation theory. Our group has successfully applied this method to the renormalization of local quark bilinear operatorscitebib.bib124, bib.bib125, bib.bib126, and more recently to the renormalization of nonlocal straight Wilson-line operators for quasi-PDFscitebib.bib107. We plan to study one-loop discretization effects for the staple operators to all orders in the lattice spacing in future work.
content_sections.II Calculation setup II.3 Green’s functi: As is standard practice, one-particle-irreducible (1-PI) two-point amputated Green’s functions of the operators under study with external elementary fields, e. g. , fermion fields, can be used for the extraction of renormalization functions 5The removal of the standard momentum-space delta functions which appear in Green’s functions such as Eq. and Eq. is understood. : ΛΓ​(q,z,y,y′)=∑x⟨ψ​(q)|𝒪Γ​(x,z,y,y′)|ψ¯​(q)⟩amp. . subscriptΛΓ𝑞𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′subscript𝑥subscriptquantum-operator-product𝜓𝑞subscript𝒪Γ𝑥𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′¯𝜓𝑞ampLambdaGamma(q,z,y,yprime)=sumxpsi(q)|OGamma(x,z,y,yprime)|barpsi(q)rm amp. . (II.3.1) A summation over the position of the staple-shaped operator is taken, which is allowed by translational symmetry, in order to simplify the calculations. Such Green’s functions using local operators are easily calculated in continuum perturbation theory to very high order. However, due to the nonlocal nature of the staple-shaped operators, additional scales (staple lengths) appear in Green’s functions, which make the computation more complex even at the one-loop level. The corresponding calculation on the lattice is even more demanding since the procedure for isolating divergences from the Feynman integrals, as well as the procedure for taking the continuum limit a→0→𝑎0arightarrow 0 (where a𝑎a is the lattice spacing) are more complicated (see, e. g. , Refs. citebib.bib105, bib.bib108). There are four one-loop Feynman diagrams contributing to ΛΓ​(q,z,y,y′)subscriptΛΓ𝑞𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′LambdaGamma(q,z,y,yprime), shown in Fig. . These diagrams will appear in both continuum and lattice regularizations since all vertices are present in both regularizations. To this perturbative order, zero (d1subscript𝑑1d1), one (d2−d3subscript𝑑2subscript𝑑3d2-d3) or two (d4subscript𝑑4d4) gluons stem from the staple-shaped Wilson line. Due to the shape of the staple, the diagrams are further divided into thirteen subdiagrams, shown in Fig. , depending on the side of the staple from which gluons emanate. content_sections.II Calculation setup II.4 Renormalizatio: We formulate below different versions of appropriate regularization-independent (RI) prescriptions which address all possible divergences and mixing of the staple-shaped operators. In contrast to our previous study regarding the renormalization of symmetric staple operators, we construct renormalization matrices that address the mixing as observed by studying symmetries and not by studying one-loop lattice perturbation theory; thus, the renormalization matrices will be 4×4444times 4. Several of the nondiagonal elements of these matrices will vanish at one-loop. In this way, the renormalization prescription will be more appropriate for nonperturbative calculations addressing possible mixing that can be seen in higher loops. We first give our conventions regarding the renormalization of the operators under study, as well as of the relevant elementary fields and parameters that enter our perturbative calculations: 𝒪ΓR​(x,z,y,y′)=ZΓ​Γ′R,X​𝒪Γ′X​(x,z,y,y′),ψR​(x)=(ZψR,X)1/2​ψX​(x),gR=μ(4−d)/2​ZgR,X​gX,formulae-sequencesubscriptsuperscript𝒪𝑅Γ𝑥𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′subscriptsuperscript𝑍𝑅𝑋ΓsuperscriptΓ′subscriptsuperscript𝒪𝑋superscriptΓ′𝑥𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′formulae-sequencesuperscript𝜓𝑅𝑥superscriptsubscriptsuperscript𝑍𝑅𝑋𝜓12superscript𝜓𝑋𝑥superscript𝑔𝑅superscript𝜇4𝑑2subscriptsuperscript𝑍𝑅𝑋𝑔superscript𝑔𝑋ORGamma(x,z,y,yprime)=ZR,XGammaGammaprimeOXGammaprime(x,z,y,yprime),qquadpsiR(x)=(ZR,Xpsi)1/2psiX(x),qquad gR=mu(4-d)/2ZR,XggX, (II.4.1) where ψX​(ψR)superscript𝜓𝑋superscript𝜓𝑅psiX(psiR) is the bare (renormalized) fermion field, gX​(gR)superscript𝑔𝑋superscript𝑔𝑅gX(gR) is the bare (renormalized) coupling constant. Use of these two choices of projectors amounts to the implementation of two different RI′superscriptRI′rm RIprime prescriptions, which we will denote as RI1′subscriptsuperscriptRI′1rm RIprime1, RI2′subscriptsuperscriptRI′2rm RIprime2 from now on. Compared to the first choice of projectors, the second one can further remove finite contributions of some Dirac structures, allowed by Lorentz symmetry, from the elements of the renormalization matrices. Similar projectors have also been studied in the renormalization of local operators, leading to reduced contributions from hadronic contamination in the nonperturbative data, especially for small values of the renormalization scale q¯¯𝑞barq. The RI scheme can address the power8Some power divergences may remain beyond one loopcitebib.bib66. and logarithmic divergences, as well as the mixing between different Dirac structures, in the same way as MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS does. However, in contrast to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme, RI can also treat the pinch-pole singularity when y→∞→𝑦yrightarrowinfty. This means that this infinite limit can be taken only in the RI-renormalized Green’s functions and not in the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS-renormalized Green’s functions of the staple operators. This is true because both schemes are defined for finite values of y𝑦y, where pinch-pole singularities are not present. Then, terms that diverge with y𝑦y (in the y→∞→𝑦yrightarrowinfty limit) do not contribute to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS renormalization function, but they do so in RI. Thus, when multiplying the bare Green’s functions with their renormalization functions, only in RI these terms are eliminated. However, since RI is just an intermediate scheme entering the procedure of renormalizing the operators in the reference scheme MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS, nonperturbatively, we cannot benefit from this additional feature of RI; after conversion to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme, the pinch-pole singularity comes back. Thus, the standard MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS prescription is not appropriate for renormalizing staple operators in the infinite limit y→∞→𝑦yrightarrowinfty. ∙∙bullet Alternative prescriptions: Alternative prescriptions which are also applicable in the limit y→∞→𝑦yrightarrowinfty are described below. The main feature in these prescriptions stems from the fact that the same pinch-pole singularity arises in a closed Wilson loop. Also, this object shows power and cusp divergences as the staple-shaped operators. As proposed in Ref. citebib.bib65, we can redefine the standard staple-shaped operator 𝒪Γsubscript𝒪ΓOGamma by dividing it with the square root of the vacuum expectation value of a rectangular z×(y+y′)𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′ztimes(y+yprime) Wilson loop L​(z,y+y′)𝐿𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′L(z,y+yprime): 𝒪¯Γ​(x,z,y,y′)≡𝒪Γ​(x,z,y,y′)⟨L​(z,y+y′)⟩1/2,subscript¯𝒪Γ𝑥𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′subscript𝒪Γ𝑥𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′superscriptdelimited-⟨⟩𝐿𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′12overlineOGamma(x,z,y,yprime)equivfracOGamma(x,z,y,yprime) L(z,y+yprime)1/2, (II.4.7) where L​(z,y+y′)≡1Nc​Tr​[𝒫​𝒰​(x,(y+y′)​ν2)​𝒰​(x+(y+y′)​ν2,z​ν1)​𝒰†​(x+z​ν1,(y+y′)​ν2)​𝒰†​(x,z​ν1)].
