(II.4.11) Z¯Γ​Γ′R,Xsubscriptsuperscript¯𝑍𝑅𝑋ΓsuperscriptΓ′overlineZR,XGammaGammaprime addresses the remaining end-point divergences, as well as the mixing. In order to extract Z¯Γ​Γ′R,Xsubscriptsuperscript¯𝑍𝑅𝑋ΓsuperscriptΓ′overlineZR,XGammaGammaprime, we need to calculate the one-loop Feynman diagrams of Fig. contributing to ⟨LX​(z,y+y′)⟩delimited-⟨⟩superscript𝐿𝑋𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′ LX(z,y+yprime). Older studies of Wilson loops can be found, e. g. , in Refs. citebib.bib123, bib.bib128 in both continuum and lattice using Wilson gluons. Here, we extend these calculations to the case of Symanzik-improved gluons. For completeness, we have also repeated the continuum calculation. Since the end-point divergences do not depend on the dimensions of the staple10On the contrary, power divergences depend on the total length of the staple line and thus, renormalization functions in ordinary RI must be computed separately for each choice of the dimensions of the staple. , a nonperturbative determination of Z¯Γ​Γ′R,Xsubscriptsuperscript¯𝑍𝑅𝑋ΓsuperscriptΓ′overlineZR,XGammaGammaprime is expected to exhibit a much milder dependence on the staple lengths z,y,y′𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′z,y,yprime which lie in the renormalization window: a≪z≪ΛQCD−1much-less-than𝑎𝑧much-less-thansuperscriptsubscriptΛQCD1all zllLambdarm QCD-1, a≪y≪ΛQCD−1much-less-than𝑎𝑦much-less-thansuperscriptsubscriptΛQCD1all yllLambdarm QCD-1, a≪y′≪ΛQCD−1much-less-than𝑎superscript𝑦′much-less-thansuperscriptsubscriptΛQCD1all yprimellLambdarm QCD-1. In this way it becomes more acceptable to renormalize the modified operators 𝒪¯Γsubscript¯𝒪ΓoverlineOGamma defined at large values of the lengths z,y,y′𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′z,y,yprime, using renormalization functions Z¯Γ​Γ′R​I′,Xsubscriptsuperscript¯𝑍𝑅superscript𝐼′𝑋ΓsuperscriptΓ′overlineZRIprime,XGammaGammaprime defined at smaller values of z,y,y′𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′z,y,yprime within the perturbative region. To distinguish the lengths appearing in the bare operators from the reference lengths appearing in the renormalization functions, we will call the latter as z¯,y¯,y¯′¯𝑧¯𝑦superscript¯𝑦′barz,bary,baryprime. If one is interested in taking the limit y→∞→𝑦yrightarrowinfty, then there are only two relevant lengths: z¯¯𝑧barz and y¯−y¯′¯𝑦superscript¯𝑦′bary-baryprime. Another option that we do not consider in this work is the short-distance ratio (SDR) scheme, described in Ref. citebib.bib66. In this scheme, ratios of hadron matrix elements of the modified operators at different external momenta are considered. All the good features of RI-bar are also valid in the SDR scheme. However, we stress that SDR is valid when operator mixing is absent or negligible.

Also, the term [y/z​tan−1⁡(y/z)+y′/z​tan−1⁡(y′/z)]delimited-𝑦𝑧superscript1𝑦𝑧superscript𝑦′𝑧superscript1superscript𝑦′𝑧[y/ztan-1(y/z)+yprime/ztan-1(yprime/z)] appearing in s0subscript𝑠0s0 (Eq. ) gives rise to a pinch-pole singularity (linear divergence) in the limit y→∞→𝑦yrightarrowinfty for fixed values of (y−y′)𝑦superscript𝑦′(y-yprime). This term stems from the sub-diagram d4​esubscript𝑑4𝑒d4e. Our results agree with previous studies in Refs. citebib.bib108, bib.bib123 which consider specific cases of the staple operators. The different Dirac structures appearing in Eqs. ( – ) can be classified into two categories: the structures corresponding to the tree-level Green’s functions of the staple operators that belong to the same mixing set according to symmetries (structures multiplied by Σi,1subscriptΣ𝑖1Sigmai,1 or Σi,2subscriptΣ𝑖2Sigmai,2), and all the remaining structures. The former structures are multiplied by regularization-dependent form factors (Σi,jsubscriptΣ𝑖𝑗Sigmai,j), while the form factors of the latter structures do not depend on regularization. This is confirmed by comparing our one-loop results in both continuum and lattice regularizations (Eq. ). In particular, the bare Green’s functions in the continuum consist of two instead of four structures of the first category because chiral symmetry is preserved (see discussion in Sec. ). On the other hand, the lattice bare Green’s functions at one loop also contain a pair of the four first-category structures, which is different from that obtained in DR (see Sec. ). Since the lattice regularization that we employ breaks chiral symmetry, we expect that the missing two structures of the first category will appear in higher loops. By taking into account the classification of the Dirac structures in the Green’s functions, we conclude that a renormalization prescription that considers a wider mixing pattern, i. e. , mixing among all 16 independent operators of different ΓΓGamma matrices, is not an optimal choice for renormalizing the staple operators. In this case, several form factors Σi,jsubscriptΣ𝑖𝑗Sigmai,j will contribute to the 16×16161616times 16 mixing matrix, including pure dependence on the regularization-independent form factors Σi,j>2subscriptΣ𝑖𝑗2Sigmai,j>2 in some nondiagonal elements. Given that we are interested in matching bare lattice Green’s functions to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme (through an intermediate nonperturbative scheme), in which the regularization-independent form factors cannot contribute, it is more economic to consider an intermediate renormalization prescription that includes only the minimum number of operators necessary for disentangling the mixing occurring in the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme on the lattice. In our study, we consider such a minimal set of operators as dictated by symmetries. The one-loop renormalized Green’s functions in the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme can be obtained by removing the 𝒪​(1/ε)𝒪1𝜀O(1/varepsilon) term in Eq. . Also, the renormalized Green’s functions in any variant of the RI scheme can be obtained by imposing the corresponding renormalization conditions to the above results. The extraction of the conversion functions among these schemes (RI variants and MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS) is then straightforward; they are given in Eqs. (, – ) for the schemes under study. Specific combinations of the form factors Σi,jsubscriptΣ𝑖𝑗Sigmai,j contribute in the renormalization conditions of Eq. for each choice of projectors 𝒫Γsuperscriptsubscript𝒫Γdelimited-1PGamma and 𝒫Γsuperscriptsubscript𝒫Γdelimited-2PGamma. The latter choice has the advantage that in most cases, only Σi,1subscriptΣ𝑖1Sigmai,1 and Σi,2subscriptΣ𝑖2Sigmai,2 survive, which multiply the relevant structures for the study of mixing. (III.3.1) The conversion matrices are regularization (X) independent, and thus, our results in DR are also applicable to the lattice. They can be used in lattice simulations in order to translate the renormalized hadron matrix elements of the staple operators, such as the quasi-beam functioncitebib.bib68 and the soft functioncitebib.bib62, from the intermediate RI-type schemes to MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS. (III.3.38) Another property of the conversion matrices comes from the combination of 𝒫μsubscript𝒫𝜇Pmu, 𝒯μ,𝒞subscript𝒯𝜇𝒞Tmu,C symmetries: one can prove (see Eq. ) that the real (imaginary) parts of the conversion matrices are even (odd) under (z→−z→𝑧𝑧zrightarrow-z, y→−y→𝑦𝑦yrightarrow-y, y′→−y′→superscript𝑦′superscript𝑦′yprimerightarrow-yprime). This is confirmed by our one-loop computation. We illustrate our results for the conversion matrices in the plots of Figs. ( – ) by employing certain values of the free parameters used in lattice simulations: μ¯=2¯𝜇2barmu=2 GeV, β=1𝛽1beta=1 (Landau gauge), a​q¯=(2​πL​n1,2​πL​n2,2​πL​n3,2​πT​(n4+12))𝑎¯𝑞2𝜋𝐿subscript𝑛12𝜋𝐿subscript𝑛22𝜋𝐿subscript𝑛32𝜋𝑇subscript𝑛412abarq=(frac2piLn1,frac2piLn2,frac2piLn3,frac2piT(n4+frac12)), where a𝑎a is the lattice spacing, L3×Tsuperscript𝐿3𝑇L3times T is the lattice volume and ni∈ℤsubscript𝑛𝑖ℤniinZ. Following simulations by ETMC, we choose isotropic momentum scales in the spatial directions (n1=n2=n3subscript𝑛1subscript𝑛2subscript𝑛3n1=n2=n3) and a nonzero twist of 1/2121/2 in the temporal component; the latter choice is compatible with the antiperiodic boundary conditions applied on the fermion fields in the temporal direction. As an example, we apply n1=n2=n3=4subscript𝑛1subscript𝑛2subscript𝑛34n1=n2=n3=4, n4=5subscript𝑛45n4=5, L=32𝐿32L=32, T=64𝑇64T=64 and a=0.09𝑎0.09a=0.09 fm. We note that specific choices of nisubscript𝑛𝑖ni can lead to a vanishing imaginary part. In particular, by setting to zero the two momentum components parallel to the staple segments, the one-loop expression for the conversion matrices becomes purely real. However, such a choice of momentum gives rise to unwanted Lorentz noninvariant contributions in the nonperturbative calculations. Thus, in our example, we follow the common practice of employing democratic momenta with reduced Lorentz noninvariant contributions at the cost of introducing an imaginary part in the RI-type renormalization matrices and the conversion matrices. In Figs. ( – ), we examine the dependence of some representative conversion matrix elements on the Wilson-line scales y𝑦y, z𝑧z, and y−y′𝑦superscript𝑦′y-yprime (in lattice units), respectively. In particular, we plot the real and imaginary parts of the 2×2222times 2 block CΓ​Γ′MS¯,RI′subscriptsuperscript𝐶¯MSsuperscriptRI′ΓsuperscriptΓ′Coverlinerm MS,rm RIprimeGammaGammaprime, (Γ,Γ′=γν1,γν2)formulae-sequenceΓsuperscriptΓ′subscript𝛾subscript𝜈1subscript𝛾subscript𝜈2(Gamma,Gammaprime=gammanu1,gammanu2), for the four renormalization prescriptions: RI1′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′1prime1, RI2′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′2prime2, RI1′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′1prime1-bar, RI2′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′2prime2-bar. As shown in Fig. , the real part of the diagonal elements (first plot in the left column and second plot in the right column) has an almost linear (flat) dependence on y/a𝑦𝑎y/a in the RIi′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′𝑖primei (RIi′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′𝑖primei-bar) schemes due to the presence (absence) of the pinch-pole singularity. As expected, the convergent behavior of RIi′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′𝑖primei-bar appears for large values of y/a𝑦𝑎y/a (≳6greater-than-or-equivalent-toabsent6gtrsim 6). There are no significant differences in the real diagonal elements between schemes with index 1 and 2. The real part of the nondiagonal elements (first plot in the second column and second plot in the first column of Fig. ) also converges for large values of y/a𝑦𝑎y/a (≳8greater-than-or-equivalent-toabsent8gtrsim 8) for all RI-type schemes. Note that the nondiagonal elements are identical between RIi′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′𝑖primei and RIi′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′𝑖primei-bar schemes (for the same i𝑖i) at one loop. Differences between schemes with index 1 and 2 are now visible: the real nondiagonal elements for the RI2′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′2prime2 scheme have smaller absolute values compared to RI1′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′1prime1, which are closer to zero. The relative size of the real nondiagonal elements compared to the diagonal ones is ∼20%similar-toabsentpercent20sim 20%. The imaginary part of the conversion matrix elements (the four plots in the last two rows of Fig. ) gives a much milder contribution compared to the real one (≲10%less-than-or-similar-toabsentpercent10lesssim 10%). The imaginary (diagonal and nondiagonal) elements for RIi′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′𝑖primei-bar coincide with the corresponding elements for RIi′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′𝑖primei (for the same i𝑖i) at one loop. A plateau is observed at large values of y/a𝑦𝑎y/a; a more stable behavior is seen for the nondiagonal elements. As in the case of real nondiagonal elements, noticeable distinctions can also be spotted in the imaginary parts between schemes with index 1 and 2. However, there is no consistent pattern regarding which scheme yields smaller contributions, as it varies for each element. Similar conclusions are extracted from Fig. by considering the dependence of the conversion matrix elements on z/a𝑧𝑎z/a. Here, we observe a convergent dependence for z/a≳6greater-than-or-equivalent-to𝑧𝑎6z/agtrsim 6 for all conversion matrix elements, except for the imaginary diagonal parts (the four plots in the last two rows of Fig. ). Now, the real diagonal parts have flat behavior in both RIi′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′𝑖primei and RIi′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′𝑖primei-bar schemes since the pinch-pole singularity does not arise for large values of z/a𝑧𝑎z/a. Examining the dependence of the conversion matrix elements on (y−y′)/a𝑦superscript𝑦′𝑎(y-yprime)/a in Fig. , we conclude that a more flat behavior is observed for smaller values of (y−y′)/a𝑦superscript𝑦′𝑎(y-yprime)/a, while for larger values the elements decrease (in most cases) rapidly. An almost linear dependence on (y−y′)/a𝑦superscript𝑦′𝑎(y-yprime)/a with a negative slope is obtained in the real diagonal parts of the RIi′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′𝑖primei schemes (see the first plot in the first column and the second plot in the second column of Fig. ), coming from the pinch-pole divergent term. In summary, the conversion matrices exhibit significant contributions from the real diagonal components, whereas the imaginary diagonal and nondiagonal elements make comparatively milder yet perceptible contributions. Safer conclusions by comparing the different types of renormalization schemes can be obtained when combining the conversion matrices with the nonperturbative data.

Bigr],, (IV.1.1) where Uplaq. subscript𝑈plaqUrm plaq. and Urect. subscript𝑈rectUrm rect. are the standard 4-link “plaquette”, and 6-link “2×1212times1 rectangle” Wilson loops. We selected three of the most common choices of the Symanzik coefficients cisubscript𝑐𝑖ci, called Wilson, Tree-Level Symanzik, and Iwasaki gluon actions, as shown in Table . Since we consider mass-independent renormalization schemes, we set the quark mass equal to zero. Consequently, the results from this study are also applicable to the twisted mass fermions citebib.bib131 in the chiral limit. One should, however, keep in mind that, in going from the twisted basis to the physical basis, operator identifications are modified (e. g. , the scalar density, under “maximal twist”, turns into a pseudoscalar density, etc. ). The results for the one-loop lattice bare Green’s functions of the asymmetric staple operators ΛΓLRsuperscriptsubscriptΛΓLRLambdaGammarm LR are presented below in terms of the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS-renormalized Green’s functions, derived by the corresponding calculation in DR. The methodology for calculating the one-loop momentum integrals on the lattice is described in Refs. citebib.bib105, bib.bib108. Here, we cite results for a general Wilson-line lattice operator with n𝑛n cusps [(n+1)𝑛1(n+1) segments] and no self-intersections, as calculated in our previous study considering symmetric staplescitebib.bib108. We confirm that the formula constructed in the latter publication gives the correct result for the difference between the bare lattice Green’s functions and the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS-renormalized Green’s functions, also in the case of asymmetric staple operators studied in this work. Thus, for a general Wilson-line lattice operator we have: δ​ΛΓ𝛿subscriptΛΓdisplaystyledeltaLambdaGamma ≡displaystyleequiv ΛΓLR​(q,r,n,ℓ1,…,ℓn+1,νi,νf)−ΛΓMS¯​(q,r,n,ℓ1,…,ℓn+1,νi,νf)superscriptsubscriptΛΓLR𝑞𝑟𝑛subscriptℓ1…subscriptℓ𝑛1subscript𝜈𝑖subscript𝜈𝑓superscriptsubscriptΛΓ¯MS𝑞𝑟𝑛subscriptℓ1…subscriptℓ𝑛1subscript𝜈𝑖subscript𝜈𝑓displaystyleLambdaGammarm LR(q,r,n,ell1,ldots,elln+1,hatnui,hatnuf)-LambdaGammaoverlinerm MS(q,r,n,ell1,ldots,elln+1,hatnui,hatnuf) (IV.1.2) where r𝑟r is the 4-vector that connects the two endpoints of the Wilson line, ℓjsubscriptℓ𝑗ellj is the length of the jthsuperscript𝑗thjrm th straight-line segment and l≡∑j=1n+1ℓj𝑙superscriptsubscript𝑗1𝑛1subscriptℓ𝑗lequivsumj=1n+1ellj is the total length of the Wilson line, cS​Wsubscript𝑐𝑆𝑊cSW is the clover coefficient in the fermion action, and νi​(νf)subscript𝜈𝑖subscript𝜈𝑓hatnui (hatnuf) is the direction of the Wilson line in the initial (final) endpoint. The first two terms in the curly brackets of Eq. come from the sum of Feynman diagrams d2subscript𝑑2d2 and d3subscript𝑑3d3, while the last term comes from diagram d4subscript𝑑4d4. The expression for the case under study (asymmetric staple operator with two cusps) can be extracted by setting n=2𝑛2n=2, l=|z|+|y|+|y′|𝑙𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′l=|z|+|y|+|yprime|, νi=sgn​(y)​ν2subscript𝜈𝑖sgn𝑦subscript𝜈2hatnui=rm sgn(y) hatnu2, and νf=−sgn​(y′)​ν2subscript𝜈𝑓sgnsuperscript𝑦′subscript𝜈2hatnuf=-rm sgn(yprime) hatnu2. The corresponding MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS-renormalized Green’s function ΛΓMS¯superscriptsubscriptΛΓ¯MSLambdaGammaoverlinerm MS can be read from Eqs. ( – ) by removing 1/ε1𝜀1/varepsilon terms. Conclusions from this calculation are summarized below: 1. Linear divergence: On the lattice, there is a linear divergence, which depends on the length of the Wilson line and the gluon action that is employed. This divergence comes from diagrams contributing to the Wilson-line self-energy. At one loop, the contributing diagram is only d4subscript𝑑4d4. 2. Logarithmic divergences: In both regularizations (DR and lattice), there are end-point logarithmic divergences, coming from diagrams d2subscript𝑑2d2 and d3subscript𝑑3d3, and cusp and contact logarithmic divergences, coming from diagram d4subscript𝑑4d4. The coefficients in front of these divergences are regularization-independent. 3. Operator mixing: Diagrams d2subscript𝑑2d2 and d3subscript𝑑3d3 give rise (upon summation) to the Dirac structure Γ′=(Γ​ν̸i+ν̸f​Γ)/2superscriptΓ′Γsubscriptitalic-ν̸𝑖subscriptitalic-ν̸𝑓Γ2Gammaprime=(Gammahatnotnui+hatnotnufGamma)/2, which differs from the tree-level structure ΓΓGamma of the operator. This indicates that operator mixing is present: In order to remove this additional structure, we need to renormalize the Wilson-line operators 𝒪Γsubscript𝒪ΓOGamma and 𝒪Γ′subscript𝒪superscriptΓ′OGammaprime as a doublet by introducing a 2×2222times 2 mixing renormalization matrix. However, as concluded by symmetries, the employment of 4×4444times 4 mixing matrices for renormalizing quadruplets of asymmetric staple-shaped operators is expected to be required at higher loops. The mixing contributions at one loop depend solely on the direction of the Wilson line entering the endpoints, regardless of the shape of the Wilson line. Also, the coefficient α2+α3​cSWsubscript𝛼2subscript𝛼3subscript𝑐SWalpha2+alpha3 crm SW in front of the structure Γ′superscriptΓ′Gammaprime depends on 𝓇𝓇r and cS​Wsubscript𝑐𝑆𝑊cSW; in particular, α2subscript𝛼2alpha2 vanishes when 𝓇=0𝓇0r=0. Thus, the one-loop mixing contributions originate from the chirality-breaking parts of the fermion action. As concluded by symmetries, these specific contributions are expected to be absent when a chiral-fermion action is employed. 4. Finite contributions: Diagram d1subscript𝑑1d1 is identical in DR and lattice regularization (up to discretization effects 𝒪​(a)𝒪𝑎O(a)) giving no contribution to the difference δ​ΛΓ𝛿subscriptΛΓdeltaLambdaGamma. Finite contributions of diagrams d2subscript𝑑2d2 and d3subscript𝑑3d3 stem only from the endpoints of the Wilson line. Any parts of a segment that do not include the endpoints give finite contributions which differ between DR and lattice regularization only by discretization effects. In diagram d4subscript𝑑4d4, the finite contributions come from the cusps and the straight-line segments of the Wilson line; they depend on both the number of cusps (n𝑛n) and the number of segments (n+1𝑛1n+1). Hence, the exact shape of the Wilson line does not affect the one-loop continuum and lattice Green’s functions of the Wilson-line operators differently; the only additional contributions on the lattice depend on the total length, the number of cusps, and the direction of the Wilson line entering the endpoints. content_sections.V Summary and Future plans: In this work, we present an extensive and comprehensive study of the renormalization of nonlocal quark bilinear operators featuring an asymmetric staple-shaped Wilson line. This project is motivated by the increased interest in studying TMDPDFs from lattice QCD using novel approaches, such as large momentum effective theory and short-distance factorization, which require matrix elements of the operators under study. More details can be found in the TMD Handbookcitebib.bib133. The analysis is based on a one-loop perturbative calculation of Green’s functions of such operators in both lattice and continuum (dimensional) regularizations. Based on our results, we identify the mixing pattern of these operators and propose renormalization prescriptions applicable to perturbative and nonperturbative data. More precisely, we discuss RI-type conditions by using different projectors that effectively address power and logarithmic divergences, as well as the finite mixing among operators with different Dirac structures. We have systematically analyzed the mixing patterns of these operators, leveraging symmetry arguments for both chiral and nonchiral fermions. We also introduce a variant of the RI prescription, which removes the pinch-pole singularities inherent in staple operators of infinite length by incorporating rectangular Wilson loops. This strategy also eliminates residual power divergences. Another novel aspect of this work is the extraction of the conversion factors to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme using the results in dimensional regularization. Our calculations are performed for arbitrary values of the renormalization momentum scale and the spatial dimensions of the staple. This ensures their applicability across a broad spectrum of nonperturbative investigations that may use the results of this work. Potential future work includes an ambitious extension of this calculation to two-loop perturbation theory. This direction has the potential to offer valuable insights and improvements to ongoing nonperturbative investigations. By performing higher-order loop calculations, we aim to refine the renormalization procedure and eliminate sources of systematic uncertainties. Additionally, we plan to calculate one-loop lattice discretization effects across a range of staple lengths and momentum scales, which has significant potential for enhancing nonperturbative estimates, particularly at short distances. Other possible directions include calculations with different lattice formulations, which might be interesting for the TMD community. Acknowledgements. G. S. and H. P. acknowledge financial support from the European Regional Development Fund and the Republic of Cyprus through the Research and Innovation Foundation under contract number EXCELLENCE/0421/0025. M. C. acknowledges financial support from the U. S. Department of Energy, Office of Nuclear Physics, Early Career Award under Grant No. DE-SC0020405. M. C. is grateful for the hospitality of the University of Cyprus during the completion of this manuscript under the project 3D-nucleon, ID number EXCELLENCE/0421/0043, co-financed by the European Regional Development Fund and the Republic of Cyprus through the Research and Innovation Foundation.

sorted_papers.title: Wilson Loops and Area Laws in Lattice Gauge Theory Tensor Networks
sorted_papers.abstract:
Tensor network states have been a very prominent tool for the study of
quantum many-body physics, thanks to their physically relevant entanglement
properties and their ability to encode symmetries. In the last few years, the
formalism has been extended and applied to theories with local symmetries to -
lattice gauge theories. In the contraction of tensor network states as well as
correlation functions of physical observables with respect to them, one uses
the so-called transfer operator, whose local properties dictate the long-range
behaviour of the state. In this work we study transfer operators of tensor
network states (in particular, PEPS - projected entangled pair states) in the
context of lattice gauge theories, and consider the implications of the local
symmetry on their structure and properties. We focus on the Wilson loop - a
nonlocal, gauge-invariant observable which is central to pure gauge theories,
whose long range decay behaviour probes the confinement or deconfinement of
static charges. Using the symmetry, we show how to handle its contraction, and
formulate conditions relating local properties to its decay fashion.

sections.5: Wilson, Confinement of quarks, Physical Review D 10, 2445 (1974).
sections.16: White, Density matrix formulation for quantum renormalization groups, Physical Review Letters 69, 2863 (1992).
sections.17: Orús, A practical introduction to tensor networks: Matrix product states and projected entangled pair states, Annals of Physics 349, 117 (2014).
sections.32: Zohar, Quantum simulation of lattice gauge theories in more than one space dimension – requirements, challenges, methods, arXiv:2106.04609 [quant-ph]  (2021).
sections.34: Wilson, Confinement of quarks, Physical Review D 10, 2445 (1974).

qualitative: Tensor network states, particularly PEPS, provide a powerful framework for studying lattice gauge theories by encoding local symmetries and capturing physically relevant entanglement structures.
The transfer operator in tensor network contractions governs the long-range behavior of correlation functions and is central to analyzing nonlocal observables like Wilson loops.
Wilson loops are nonlocal, gauge-invariant observables that probe the confinement or deconfinement phase of static charges in pure gauge theories.
Local symmetries in lattice gauge theories impose constraints on the structure of the transfer operator, which in turn determine the decay behavior of Wilson loops.

The renormalization of Wilson lines can be understood through the spectral properties of the transfer operator, with the emergence of area laws or perimeter laws depending on the symmetry structure and phase of the system.

The interplay between local symmetry and tensor network geometry leads to a natural renormalization group interpretation for Wilson loop observables, where the loop's behavior is determined by the fixed points of the transfer operator.
quantitative: The Wilson loop expectation value ⟨W(C)⟩ for a closed loop C follows an area law: ⟨W(C)⟩ ∝ exp(−σA), where A is the area enclosed by C and σ is the string tension.
In the deconfined phase, the Wilson loop exhibits a perimeter law: ⟨W(C)⟩ ∝ exp(−γL), where L is the perimeter of the loop and γ is a constant.
The transfer operator T has a dominant eigenvalue λ₀ = 1 (due to gauge invariance), and the next-largest eigenvalue λ₁ determines the correlation length ξ = −1 / ln|λ₁|.
The decay of Wilson loop expectation values is governed by the ratio |λ₁ / λ₀|, with exponential decay when |λ₁| < 1.
For PEPS with local gauge symmetry, the transfer operator satisfies a gauge-invariant constraint: T = ∑_g U_g ⊗ U_g^†, where U_g are local gauge transformations.
The area law for Wilson loops emerges when the transfer operator has a unique gapped ground state, leading to exponential decay with area A.

authors: Erez Zohar

content_sections.I Introduction: In recent years, tensor network states citebib.bib1 have been a very prominent tool, rooted in quantum information science, for the study of quantum many body systems and especially strongly correlated physics. In particular, Matrix Product States (MPS) citebib.bib2, bib.bib3 enable to study numerically and analytically physically relevant states, e. g. ground states of local many body Hamiltonians (that is, states exhibiting an entanglement entropy area law). In higher spatial dimensions, MPS generalize to PEPS - Projected Entangled Pair States citebib.bib3, bib.bib4. These are useful for the description of strongly correlated physics in two spatial dimensions and more. PEPS (and MPS) are constructed out of the contraction of local building blocks (tensors). They satisfy, by construction, the entanglement entropy area law (focusing on the physically relevant part of the Hilbert space) and allows the state to depend on very few local parameters and hence making it feasible for computations (compared with arbitrary states in the exponentially large Hilbert space). Furthermore, it also allows one to encode symmetries already on the single tensor level. By properly parametrizing the local tensors, a global symmetry of the whole PEPS under a symmetry group can be imposed citebib.bib5, bib.bib6. This way, one can generate families of ansatz states in which the symmetry group of the studied model is encoded by construction. While originally used mostly in the context of condensed matter physics, MPS and PEPS have recently been extended to the study of particle physics too - in particular, to lattice gauge theories, aiming at solving long standing open, non-perturbative questions of the standard model, such as the confinement of quarks citebib.bib7. Due to its running coupling citebib.bib8 Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD) which allows one to use perturbation theory in high energy scales (collider physics) thanks to asymptotic freedom, is strongly interacting in low energies, preventing the use of perturbative methods. Lattice Gauge Theories (LGTs) citebib.bib7 have been introduced to overcome this difficulty, first as tools for lattice regularization of gauge invariant field theories. They quickly became a very successful numerical approach. Combined with quantum Monte Carlo it has been applied to nonperturbative QCD computations, such as the hadronic spectrum citebib.bib9. However, quantum Monte Carlo does not allow for the direct observation of real time dynamics, and faces the fermionic sign problem in several important physical scenarios, not allowing one to probe some of the interesting exotic regions of the QCD phase diagram citebib.bib10, and this requires the use of other methods, with tensor networks being one such approach. The tensor network framework for lattice gauge theories has been rapidly growing in the last few years. For 1+1​d11𝑑1+1d systems, MPS have already been extensively used. This does not only include abstract formalistic descriptions of MPS with a local symmetry citebib.bib11, bib.bib12 or benchmarks of models that can be treated in other ways, such as, but not only, the Schwinger model citebib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20. Successful numerical studies of lattice gauge theories in 1+1​d11𝑑1+1d have been carried out even in scenarios which face the sign problem when approached with conventional methods (such as real time evolution citebib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23 and finite density citebib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27). This was done for both Abelian and non-Abelian models - see citebib.bib28 and references therein for a discussion of that. The application of tensor networks to higher dimensional lattice gauge theories has been discussed as well in the last few years citebib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib38. From the rather more abstract, or formalistic point of view, gauging mechanisms which lift globally invariant PEPS to locally invariant ones by adding a gauge field and entangling it to the matter properly were introduced and discussed citebib.bib39, bib.bib40. For a parallel approach in the action formalism - tensor field theory - which uses tensor networks (but not tensor network states), see citebib.bib41 and references therein. In this work, we will focus on a particular gauging mechanism - the one introduced in citebib.bib40 and used mostly with fermionic matter, for creating gauged Gaussian fermionic PEPS citebib.bib31, bib.bib32: special PEPS constructions which allow for the description of fermionic matter coupled to dynamical gauge fields. Their construction may be seen as a minimal coupling procedure on the level of states, which is not possible in general but could be done in the context of PEPS citebib.bib42. While in general numerical computations are hard and challenging for PEPS in two spatial dimensions and more, it has been shown that, when this particular construction is used, the PEPS may be contracted efficiently (allowing one to extract physical information) when combining with Monte-Carlo methods which do not suffer from the sign problem citebib.bib33. Variational Monte-Carlo then allows to find ground states of lattice gauge theory Hamiltonians when such states are used as ansatz states, which has already been demonstrated and benchmarked for a pure ℤ3subscriptℤ3Z3 lattice gauge theory in 2+1212+1 dimensions citebib.bib37. A question that has to be asked when a PEPS is studied, is how physical information can be extracted from the contracted state - computation of expectation values of observables and correlation functions. Thanks to the special structure of MPS, one may introduce a mathematical object called transfer matrix (or operator) citebib.bib43 to compute efficiently expectation values of observables and correlation functions. This may be extended to two dimensional PEPS, by first contracting the rows, obtaining effectively a chain of the rows which is an MPS, and considering its transfer matrix citebib.bib44. In this work, we will study such transfer operators of lattice gauge theory PEPS in two space dimensions. Gauge theories are special in the sense that they exhibit a local symmetry, responsible to mediating local interactions between the matter fields. This symmetry gives rise to many local constraints. All the physical states are invariant under gauge transformations - local transformations parametrized by the elements of the so-called gauge group. As a result, only gauge invariant observables and correlation functions - those which are invariant under local transformations - give rise to a nonvanishing expectation values. Thus, LGT PEPS admit a very special structure manifested in the local tensors citebib.bib30, bib.bib39, bib.bib40; in this work, we focus on the implications of the local symmetry on the transfer operators, and hence aim at using the symmetry to simplify the PEPS contraction, focusing on pure gauge theories (that is, without dynamical matter). In such scenarios, closed flux loops - usually referred to as the operators which create them, Wilson loops citebib.bib7 - are perhaps the most important observables (and almost the only possible gauge invariant one). The decay rule of large Wilson loops in pure gauge theories serves as probes for confinement of static charges: area law decay implies confinement, while a perimeter law - a deconfined (Coulomb) phase. Confinement implies a gapped, disordered phase, while deconfined phases are massless and ordered citebib.bib45. The local ingredients of the Wilson loop are not gauge invariant - only their combination along the nonlocal path preserves the symmetry. This means, that when computing it for a gauge invariant PEPS, the transfer operator formalism must be extended and modified, requiring the inclusion of various types of transfer matrices which construct this nonlocal observable. The different building blocks will also have special properties citebib.bib32, dictated by the special local symmetry, which will affect the behaviour of the Wilson loop and its decay. In this work, we will study the properties of transfer operators of gauge invariant PEPS. We will see how the symmetry affects the properties of the local tensors, and that thanks to it, some parts of the tensors may be excluded and ignored when a contraction is done (e. g. when combined with some numerical methods). We will also see how that affects the Wilson loop’s decay - that is, how local properties of the tensors dictate the decay of large Wilson loops. Note that PEPS have been previously used for the computation of Wilson loop expectation value in various cases - ℤ2subscriptℤ2Z2 string nets citebib.bib46, bib.bib47, as well as U​𝑈1U citebib.bib31 and S​U​𝑆𝑈2SU citebib.bib32 toy models; here we derive a general framework based on transfer matrix arguments and demonstrate with particular constructions We begin with briefly reviewing important preliminaries from group theory and lattice gauge theory, in section II; move on to formulating gauge invariant PEPS and reviewing their symmetry properties, in section III; in section IV we introduce the transfer operators - after a brief review of their general properties, we formulate the flux-free transfer operators for LGT PEPS, study their properties and use them to calculate the norm; section V focuses on the contraction of Wilson loop expectation values for LGT PEPS, studying the relevant transfer operators and deriving conditions for area and perimeter decay laws; finally, in section VI, we give an explicit illustration, including both analytical and numerical arguments, for a ℤ2subscriptℤ2Z2 lattice gauge theory. Throughout this work the Einstein summation convention (on doubly repeated indices) is assumed unless stated otherwise; with the only exception of irreducible representation indices, whose summation should not be assumed.
content_sections.II Mathematical and Physical Preliminari: Consider a group G𝐺G, which is either a finite or a compact Lie one. Each group element g∈G𝑔𝐺gin G may be represented by different unitary matrices Dj​(g)superscript𝐷𝑗𝑔Djleft(gright), labelled by the group’s irreducible representations (irreps) j𝑗j; the dimension of these matrices is referred to as the irrep dimension, dim​(j)dim𝑗textdimleft(jright) (e. g. 2​j+12𝑗12j+1 for S​U​𝑆𝑈2SU). In the Hilbert space ℋℋH of some quantum mechanical theory, we would like to consider transformations parametrized by the elements of G𝐺G. To do that, for each g∈G𝑔𝐺gin G we introduce a unitary operator θgsubscript𝜃𝑔thetag, and define it by its action on a basis states of the form |j​m⟩ket𝑗𝑚left|jmright. j𝑗j labels the irreducible representations of G𝐺G and m𝑚m is an index labelling all states within this representation - that is, all the states that may be mixed by the transformations θgsubscript𝜃𝑔thetag that act block-diagonally on the irreps: θg​|j​m⟩=|j​m′⟩​Dm′​mj​(g)subscript𝜃𝑔ket𝑗𝑚ket𝑗superscript𝑚′subscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑗superscript𝑚′𝑚𝑔thetagleft|jmright=left|jmprimeright Djmprimemleft(gright) We can hence express θgsubscript𝜃𝑔thetag as θg=⨁𝑗​Dm​nj​(g)​|j​m⟩​⟨j​n|subscript𝜃𝑔𝑗direct-sumsubscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑗𝑚𝑛𝑔ket𝑗𝑚bra𝑗𝑛thetag=undersetjbigoplusDjmnleft(gright)left|jmrightleft jnright| - and therefore the dimension of the irrep j𝑗j, dim​(j)dim𝑗textdimleft(jright), is also the dimension of ℋjsubscriptℋ𝑗Hj, the Hilbert subspace spanned by |j​m⟩ket𝑗𝑚left|jmright, which we call a multiplet. The Hilbert space may be seen as a direct sum of multiplet subspaces ℋ=⨁𝑗​ℋjℋ𝑗direct-sumsubscriptℋ𝑗H=undersetjbigoplusHj In general, quantum Hilbert spaces may contain more than one multiplet carrying the same irreducible representation. These transformations are sometimes referred to as right transformations, since they mix the multiplet elements |j​m⟩ket𝑗𝑚left|jmright, when seen as the components of a dim​(j)dim𝑗textdimleft(jright) dimensional vector, via right matrix multiplication, as shown in . One can also introduce the left transformations θg​|j​m⟩=Dm​m′j​(g)​|j​m′⟩subscript𝜃𝑔ket𝑗𝑚subscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑗𝑚superscript𝑚′𝑔ket𝑗superscript𝑚′tildethetagleft|jmright=Djmmprimeleft(gright)left|jmprimeright Note that the left transformations are not independent from the right ones: for each g∈G𝑔𝐺gin G one may find hℎh such that θg=θhsubscript𝜃𝑔subscript𝜃ℎtildethetag=thetah. We introduce the left transformations separately nevertheless since they will be mathematically convenient later when the PEPS are constructed. When G𝐺G is a compact Lie group, its elements may be uniquely identified in terms of group parameters or coordinates ϕasubscriptitalic-ϕ𝑎phia; then, for each irrep j𝑗j, Dj​(g)=exp​(i​ϕa​(g)​Taj)superscript𝐷𝑗𝑔exp𝑖subscriptitalic-ϕ𝑎𝑔subscriptsuperscript𝑇𝑗𝑎Djleft(gright)=textexpleft(iphialeft(gright)Tjaright) - the parameters ϕa​(g)subscriptitalic-ϕ𝑎𝑔phialeft(gright) depend on the group element, while the generators Tajsubscriptsuperscript𝑇𝑗𝑎Tja depend on the representation. The latter form a set of matrices with dimension dim​(j)dim𝑗textdimleft(jright), satisfying the group’s Lie algebra [Taj,Tbj]=i​fa​b​c​Tcjsubscriptsuperscript𝑇𝑗𝑎subscriptsuperscript𝑇𝑗𝑏𝑖subscript𝑓𝑎𝑏𝑐subscriptsuperscript𝑇𝑗𝑐left[Tja,Tjbright]=ifabcTjc where fa​b​csubscript𝑓𝑎𝑏𝑐fabc are the group’s structure constants. One may also introduce the abstract generators, Jasubscript𝐽𝑎Ja, which are block diagonal in the representations, Ja=⨁𝑗​(Taj)m​n​|j​m⟩​⟨j​n|,subscript𝐽𝑎𝑗direct-sumsubscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝑇𝑎𝑗𝑚𝑛ket𝑗𝑚bra𝑗𝑛Ja=undersetjbigoplusleft(Tajright)mnleft|jmrightleft jnright|, satisfying the algebra [Ja,Jb]=i​fa​b​c​Jcsubscript𝐽𝑎subscript𝐽𝑏𝑖subscript𝑓𝑎𝑏𝑐subscript𝐽𝑐left[Ja,Jbright]=ifabcJc too. The states |j​m⟩ket𝑗𝑚left|jmright are eigenstates of mutually commuting operators: the j𝑗j quantum numbers(s) labelling the irreducible representation (and hence the multiplet) are eigenvalues of the Casimir operators which commute with all the generators; within the representation, the states are labelled by the eigenvalues of a maximal set of mutually commuting generators (Cartan subalgebra) - m𝑚m. Similarly, when the group is finite, j𝑗j labels the irreducible representation while the m𝑚m numbers are obtained from the simultaneous diagonalization of a maximal set of commuting transformations. All the irreps of Abelian groups are one dimensional and thus no m𝑚m indices are required. In the ℤNsubscriptℤ𝑁ZN case, the N𝑁N different irreps are labelled by the integers j=0,…,N−1𝑗0…𝑁1j=0,. . . ,N-1, which label the group elements g=0,…,N−1𝑔0…𝑁1g=0,. . . ,N-1 too, with Dj​(g)=exp​(i​2​πN​j​g)superscript𝐷𝑗𝑔exp𝑖2𝜋𝑁𝑗𝑔Djleft(gright)=textexpleft(ifrac2piNjgright). As a non-Abelian example, consider S​U​𝑆𝑈2SU, whose irreps are labelled by j𝑗j that are non-negative integers and half-integers. Just like gauge theories in the continuum, LGTs describe the interaction of matter particles through gauge fields. In the lattice case, the matter fields reside on the lattice sites, while the gauge fields, mediating the interactions between matter particles, are on the links. One can either discretize both space and time citebib.bib7, as used for Euclidean, Monte-Carlo computations, or discretize only space while keeping time continuous citebib.bib48. The latter corresponds to the Hamiltonian formulation widely used in the context of quantum simulation and tensor networks, including in this work. Since we consider Hamiltonian lattice gauge theory in 2+1212+1 dimensions, our lattice will be two dimensional. As this work focuses on the pure gauge case and matter fields are absent, all the degrees of freedom will reside on the links. We will review the basic ingredients of such models following the conventions of citebib.bib49 and citebib.bib50.Consider a two dimensional lattice, whose sites are labelled by vectors of integers 𝐱∈ℤ2𝐱superscriptℤ2xinZ2. 𝐞isubscript𝐞𝑖hatei denote the unit vectors pointing in directions i=1,2𝑖12i=1,2, and any link is classified by two numbers, (𝐱,i)𝐱𝑖left(x,iright), standing for the beginning of the link and the direction to which it emanates, respectively. Each link (𝐱,i)𝐱𝑖left(x,iright) hosts a local gauge field Hilbert space ℋgauge​(𝐱,i)subscriptℋgauge𝐱𝑖Htextgaugeleft(x,iright), which can be spanned by group element states |g⟩g∈Gsubscriptket𝑔𝑔𝐺leftleft|grightrightgin G labelled by the gauge group elements. These states form a basis of ℋgaugesubscriptℋgaugeHtextgauge, with the orthogonality relation ⟨g′|g⟩=δ​(g′,g),inner-productsuperscript𝑔′𝑔𝛿superscript𝑔′𝑔left gprime|gright=deltaleft(gprime,gright), where δ​(g′,g)𝛿superscript𝑔′𝑔deltaleft(gprime,gright) is the Kronecker delta if G𝐺G is finite, and a Dirac delta distribution in the compact lie case - denoting the Haar measure of G𝐺G by d​g𝑑𝑔dg, ∫𝑑g′​f​(g′)​δ​(g′,g)=f​(g). differential-dsuperscript𝑔′𝑓superscript𝑔′𝛿superscript𝑔′𝑔𝑓𝑔int dgprimefleft(gprimeright)deltaleft(gprime,gright)=fleft(gright). In a sense, using the multiplet states introduced previously, |j​m​n⟩=|j​m⟩⊗|j​n⟩ket𝑗𝑚𝑛tensor-productket𝑗𝑚ket𝑗𝑛left|jmnright=left|jmrightotimesleft|jnright or, ℋgauge=⨁𝑗​ℋj⊗ℋjsubscriptℋgaugetensor-product𝑗direct-sumsubscriptℋ𝑗subscriptℋ𝑗Htextgauge=undersetjbigoplusHjotimesHj where ℋjsubscriptℋ𝑗Hj is the dim​(j)dim𝑗textdimleft(jright) dimensional subspace spanned by the |j​m⟩ket𝑗𝑚left|jmright multiplet states. In a lattice gauge theory, only gauge invariant states and operators are considered physical. If G𝐺G is a compact Lie group, we can formulate the gauge transformations Θg​(𝐱)subscriptΘ𝑔𝐱hatThetagleft(xright) in terms of their generators, 𝒢a​(𝐱)=La​(𝐱,1)+La​(𝐱,2)−Ra​(𝐱−𝐞1,1)−Ra​(𝐱−𝐞2,2)subscript𝒢𝑎𝐱subscript𝐿𝑎𝐱1subscript𝐿𝑎𝐱2subscript𝑅𝑎𝐱subscript𝐞11subscript𝑅𝑎𝐱subscript𝐞22Galeft(xright)=Laleft(x,1right)+Laleft(x,2right)-Raleft(x-hate1,1right)-Raleft(x-hate2,2right) Gauge invariance is then formulated in terms of the Gauss laws 𝒢a​(𝐱)​|ψ⟩=0∀𝐱∈ℤ2,aformulae-sequencesubscript𝒢𝑎𝐱ket𝜓0for-all𝐱superscriptℤ2𝑎Galeft(xright)left|psiright=0quadquadforallxinZ2,a (once again, excluding static charges citebib.bib52). We call this eigenvalue equation the Gauss law, since 𝒢a​(𝐱)subscript𝒢𝑎𝐱Galeft(xright) can clearly be seen as the divergence of electric fields - Lasubscript𝐿𝑎La and Rasubscript𝑅𝑎Ra - on a site. For physical states - the one which satisfy the local constraints - the divergence of electric fields is zero. It is very apparent in the U​𝑈1U case, where it takes the explicit form (E​(𝐱,1)+E​(𝐱,2)−E​(𝐱−𝐞1,1)−E​(𝐱−𝐞2,2))​|ψ⟩𝐸𝐱1𝐸𝐱2𝐸𝐱subscript𝐞11𝐸𝐱subscript𝐞22ket𝜓displaystyleleft(Eleft(x,1right)+Eleft(x,2right)-Eleft(x-hate1,1right)-Eleft(x-hate2,2right)right)left|psiright ≡∇⋅𝐄​(𝐱)​|ψ⟩=0∀𝐱∈ℤ2,aformulae-sequenceabsent⋅∇𝐄𝐱ket𝜓0for-all𝐱superscriptℤ2𝑎displaystyleequivnablacdotEleft(xright)left|psiright=0quadquadforallxinZ2,a In non Abelian cases, the divergence involves left and right electric fields, which is related to the charge carried by non-Abelian gauge bosons citebib.bib48 (e. g. , the colour charged gluon vs the electric neutral photon).Since we deal with gauge invariant states, it is expected that the expectation values of non gauge invariant operators will vanish. Thus, when classifying the phases and behaviour of gauge theories one needs to consider only gauge invariant observables and correlation functions. One option, for compact Lie group, is to compute expectation values of electric field operators and functions thereof (and only of Casimir operators if the group is non-Abelian). Another possible gauge invariant observable is the loop variable, and in particular Wilson Loops citebib.bib7. Even though they are Hilbert space operators, all the elements of Ujsuperscript𝑈𝑗Uj commute - one can see in the definition above that they are all diagonal in the same basis. We define the Wilson loop operator 𝒲​(𝒞)𝒲𝒞Wleft(Cright) as the ordered contraction of group element operators along this closed path, that is 𝒲j​(𝒞)superscript𝒲𝑗𝒞displaystyleWjleft(Cright) =Tr​(∏ℓ∈𝒞​Uj​(ℓ))=absentTrℓ𝒞productsuperscript𝑈𝑗ℓabsentdisplaystyle=textTrleft(undersetellinCprodUjleft(ellright)right)= =Uj​(ℓ1)m1​m2​Uj​(ℓ2)m2​m3​⋯​Uj​(ℓL)mL​m1absentsuperscript𝑈𝑗subscriptsubscriptℓ1subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2superscript𝑈𝑗subscriptsubscriptℓ2subscript𝑚2subscript𝑚3⋯superscript𝑈𝑗subscriptsubscriptℓ𝐿subscript𝑚𝐿subscript𝑚1displaystyle=Ujleft(ell1right)m1m2Ujleft(ell2right)m2m3cdots Ujleft(ellLright)mLm1 It is a trace over the product of the group element operators U𝑈U, seen as matrices, ordered along the closed path 𝒞𝒞C with length L𝐿L (which is simply the number of links along the path). Depending on the orientation of the path, one may have to use U†superscript𝑈†Udagger instead of U𝑈U, on half of the links along the path - those pointing leftwards or downwards (see Fig. ). For simplicity, we will omit the j𝑗j indices below, but obviously the same irrep must be used along the path, otherwise the matrix product is ill defined. Consider U​𝑈1U with j=1𝑗1j=1 as an example; there, 𝒲​(𝒞)=exp​(i​∑ℓ∈𝒞​ϕ​(ℓ))𝒲𝒞exp𝑖ℓ𝒞italic-ϕℓWleft(Cright)=textexpleft(iundersetellinCsumphileft(ellright)right) with half of the phases with a minus sign, according to their orientation. In order to consider the action of the group element operators on representation states, we use the Clebsch-Gordan series and coefficients ⟨J​M​j​m|K​N⟩inner-product𝐽𝑀𝑗𝑚𝐾𝑁left JMjm|KNright citebib.bib55 and obtain Um​m′j​|J​M​M′⟩subscriptsuperscript𝑈𝑗𝑚superscript𝑚′ket𝐽𝑀superscript𝑀′displaystyle Ujmmprimeleft|JMMprimeright =displaystyle= dim​(J)dim​(K)dim𝐽dim𝐾displaystylesqrtfractextdimleft(Jright)textdimleft(Kright) ⟨J​M​j​m|K​N⟩​⟨K​N′|J​M′​j​m′⟩​|K​N​N′⟩inner-product𝐽𝑀𝑗𝑚𝐾𝑁inner-product𝐾superscript𝑁′𝐽superscript𝑀′𝑗superscript𝑚′ket𝐾𝑁superscript𝑁′displaystyleleft JMjm|KNrightleft KNprime|JMprimejmprimerightleft|KNNprimeright - that is, the action of the group element operator Ujsuperscript𝑈𝑗Uj on a state with representation J𝐽J yields states with all representations which are obtained by combining j𝑗j and J𝐽J (more precisely, fusing the two irreps together). Acting with a loop operator hence excites the representations along the loop with respect to that rule. One may truncate the Hilbert space in the representation basis: as long as all the irreducible representations used are taken completely and connected by nonzero Clebsch Gordan coefficients when j𝑗j is added, one may use to define a Ujsuperscript𝑈𝑗Uj operator acting on that truncated space. The transformation properties , and will still hold citebib.bib49, which may make it convenient for some numerical approaches (or quantum simulation implementations citebib.bib56) but, since the group structure will be lost, the group element basis will no longer be defined, making, in particular, and the Fourier transform invalid. In most cases, rectangular Wilson loops are considered. We denote by W​(R1,R2)𝑊subscript𝑅1subscript𝑅2Wleft(R1,R2right) a rectangular loop sized R1×R2subscript𝑅1subscript𝑅2R1times R2 (see Fig. ). Very large Wilson loops of pure gauge theories are a probe for confinement (or deconfinement) of static charges, as introduced by Wilson in citebib.bib7 (see also citebib.bib45, bib.bib57, bib.bib58). In a confining phase, −log⁡⟨W​(R1,R2)⟩∝R1​R2proportional-to𝑊subscript𝑅1subscript𝑅2subscript𝑅1subscript𝑅2-logleft Wleft(R1,R2right)rightpropto R1R2 for R1,R2≫1much-greater-thansubscript𝑅1subscript𝑅21R1,R2gg 1 (area law), while in a deconfined phase −log⁡⟨W​(R1,R2)⟩∝R1+R2proportional-to𝑊subscript𝑅1subscript𝑅2subscript𝑅1subscript𝑅2-logleft Wleft(R1,R2right)rightpropto R1+R2 for R1,R2≫1much-greater-thansubscript𝑅1subscript𝑅21R1,R2gg 1 (perimeter law). In citebib.bib59, Creutz introduced the parameter χ​(R1,R2)=−log⁡(W​(R1,R2)​W​(R1−1,R2−1)W​(R1−1,R2)​W​(R1,R2−1))𝜒subscript𝑅1subscript𝑅2𝑊subscript𝑅1subscript𝑅2𝑊subscript𝑅11subscript𝑅21𝑊subscript𝑅11subscript𝑅2𝑊subscript𝑅1subscript𝑅21chileft(R1,R2right)=-logleft(fracWleft(R1,R2right)Wleft(R1-1,R2-1right)Wleft(R1-1,R2right)Wleft(R1,R2-1right)right) for the detection of static charge confinement. In the general case of ⟨W​(R1,R2)⟩=W0​e−κA​R1​R2−κP​(R1+R2)delimited-⟨⟩𝑊subscript𝑅1subscript𝑅2subscript𝑊0superscript𝑒subscript𝜅𝐴subscript𝑅1subscript𝑅2subscript𝜅𝑃subscript𝑅1subscript𝑅2left Wleft(R1,R2right)right=W0e-kappaAR1R2-kappaPleft(R1+R2right) For large R1,R2subscript𝑅1subscript𝑅2R1,R2, the area factor κAsubscript𝜅𝐴kappaA (called the string tension), should it exist, is the most dominant one. The Creutz parameter χ𝜒chi filters out the contributions of the constant prefactor W0subscript𝑊0W0 and the perimeter coefficient κPsubscript𝜅𝑃kappaP, and thus within a confining phase, χ​(R1,R2)→κA>0→𝜒subscript𝑅1subscript𝑅2subscript𝜅𝐴0chileft(R1,R2right)rightarrowkappaA>0 for R1,R2≫1much-greater-thansubscript𝑅1subscript𝑅21R1,R2gg 1, while in a deconfining one it converges to zero.
content_sections.III Gauge Invariant PEPS: In this work, we use the lattice gauge theory PEPS formalism of citebib.bib40, bib.bib33, with slightly different notations (and restricted to the pure gauge case). First of all, let us review it.
content_sections.III Gauge Invariant PEPS III.1 Review of: Each site 𝐱∈ℤ2𝐱superscriptℤ2xinZ2 of our square, periodic lattice is at the intersection of four legs. The outgoing ones are in the right and up directions, while the left and down directed legs are considered ingoing. We wish, as usual with PEPS, to construct a physical lattice state describing different physical degrees of freedom located on different sites. Each such degree of freedom is described by a local physical Hilbert space: if we had matter, we would fix a physical matter Hilbert space to each lattice site. Here, however, the gauge fields are our only physical degrees of freedom, and they reside on the links. Thus, with each lattice site 𝐱𝐱x we associate two physical Hilbert spaces, located on the outgoing legs. We refer to them as the side (ℋssubscriptℋ𝑠Hs) and top (ℋtsubscriptℋ𝑡Ht) physical Hilbert spaces. These are local gauge field Hilbert spaces (note that the word local here has to do with being defined on a single link, not with the gauge symmetry being local) - that is, either the full ℋgaugesubscriptℋgaugeHtextgauge spaces introduced in Eq. , or truncated versions thereof containing only some representations. When truncating, it is important to make sure that all the |j​m​n⟩ket𝑗𝑚𝑛left|jmnright for an included j𝑗j are present, otherwise no gauge invariance can be imposed, as explained above citebib.bib49, bib.bib40. When constructing a PEPS, in order to connect the local physical building blocks to one physical quantum state, one has to introduce auxiliary or virtual degrees of freedom, on top of the physical ones given by the model we study. These are used merely for the purpose of contraction. On each of the four legs we introduce an auxiliary or virtual Hilbert space, ℋr,ℋu,ℋl,ℋdsubscriptℋ𝑟subscriptℋ𝑢subscriptℋ𝑙subscriptℋ𝑑Hr,Hu,Hl,Hd for the right, up, left and down going legs, respectively. They are spanned by group multiplet states of the form |j​m⟩ket𝑗𝑚left|jmright, as defined in Eq. . One may include all such multiplets, truncate, or include several copies of the same multiplet, which allows to increase the number of variational parameters; but once again, all the states within a multiplet included must be present, and the representations used in the physical spaces must be included (though possibly with a higher multiplicity). For more details about that, refer to citebib.bib40 where the general construction of such states is discussed. On each site, we construct the physical-virtual state |A⟩=Ajr​mr;ju​mu;jl​ml;jd​mdjs​ms​ns;jt​mt​nt​|js​ms​ns;jt​mt​nt⟩​|jr​mr;ju​mu;jl​ml;jd​md⟩∈ℋs×ℋt×ℋr×ℋu×ℋl×ℋdket𝐴subscriptsuperscript𝐴subscript𝑗𝑠subscript𝑚𝑠subscript𝑛𝑠subscript𝑗𝑡subscript𝑚𝑡subscript𝑛𝑡subscript𝑗𝑟subscript𝑚𝑟subscript𝑗𝑢subscript𝑚𝑢subscript𝑗𝑙subscript𝑚𝑙subscript𝑗𝑑subscript𝑚𝑑ketsubscript𝑗𝑠subscript𝑚𝑠subscript𝑛𝑠subscript𝑗𝑡subscript𝑚𝑡subscript𝑛𝑡ketsubscript𝑗𝑟subscript𝑚𝑟subscript𝑗𝑢subscript𝑚𝑢subscript𝑗𝑙subscript𝑚𝑙subscript𝑗𝑑subscript𝑚𝑑subscriptℋ𝑠subscriptℋ𝑡subscriptℋ𝑟subscriptℋ𝑢subscriptℋ𝑙subscriptℋ𝑑left|Aright=Ajsmsns;jtmtntjrmr;jumu;jlml;jdmdleft|jsmsns;jtmtntrightleft|jrmr;jumu;jlml;jdmdrightinHstimesHttimesHrtimesHutimesHltimesHd where the first ket refers to the physical states and the second to the virtual ones (see Fig. ). The coordinate 𝐱𝐱x was omitted for simplicity, but the Hilbert spaces are all associated with particular sites and, in general, the tensors Ajr​mr;ju​mu;jl​ml;jd​mdjs​ms​ns;jt​mt​ntsubscriptsuperscript𝐴subscript𝑗𝑠subscript𝑚𝑠subscript𝑛𝑠subscript𝑗𝑡subscript𝑚𝑡subscript𝑛𝑡subscript𝑗𝑟subscript𝑚𝑟subscript𝑗𝑢subscript𝑚𝑢subscript𝑗𝑙subscript𝑚𝑙subscript𝑗𝑑subscript𝑚𝑑Ajsmsns;jtmtntjrmr;jumu;jlml;jdmd may depend on the position, although we will focus on translationally invariant PEPS and thus they will be independent of 𝐱𝐱x. To contract the PEPS, on each link we introduce the maximally entangled states |B1​(𝐱)⟩ketsubscript𝐵1𝐱displaystyleleft|B1left(xright)right =∑𝑗​|j​m⟩r,𝐱​|j​m⟩l,𝐱+𝐞1absent𝑗subscriptket𝑗𝑚𝑟𝐱subscriptket𝑗𝑚𝑙𝐱subscript𝐞1displaystyle=undersetjsumleft|jmrightr,xleft|jmrightl,x+hate1 |B2​(𝐱)⟩ketsubscript𝐵2𝐱displaystyleleft|B2left(xright)right =∑𝑗​|j​m⟩u,𝐱​|j​m⟩d,𝐱+𝐞2absent𝑗subscriptket𝑗𝑚𝑢𝐱subscriptket𝑗𝑚𝑑𝐱subscript𝐞2displaystyle=undersetjsumleft|jmrightu,xleft|jmrightd,x+hate2 As usual, we construct our PEPS |ψ⟩ket𝜓left|psiright by projecting the virtual states on the legs onto the maximally entangled states, |ψ⟩=⨂𝐱,i​⟨Bi​(𝐱)|⨂𝐱|A​(𝐱)⟩ket𝜓𝐱𝑖tensor-productquantum-operator-productsubscript𝐵𝑖𝐱𝐱tensor-product𝐴𝐱left|psiright=undersetx,ibigotimesleft Bileft(xright)right|undersetxbigotimesleft|Aleft(xright)right Note that ⨂𝐱​|A​(𝐱)⟩𝐱tensor-productket𝐴𝐱undersetxbigotimesleft|Aleft(xright)right in both the physical and virtual spaces, while ⨂𝐱,i​⟨Bi​(𝐱)|𝐱𝑖tensor-productbrasubscript𝐵𝑖𝐱undersetx,ibigotimesleft Bileft(xright)right| is only virtual. Thus the result of this projection, |ψ⟩ket𝜓left|psiright, is still a quantum state, including only physical degrees of freedom - the virtual, or auxiliary ones, are all contracted: this is the standard way to contract PEPS, and a frequently used notation. The physical degrees of freedom are now correlated, and in particular, thanks to maximally entangling nearest neighbours, this guarantees the entanglement entropy area law. One still has some freedom to choose which maximally entangled states to use; the ones that we picked here are invariant under the following group transformations: θgr​(𝐱)​θgl⁣†​(𝐱+𝐞1)​|B1​(𝐱)⟩subscriptsuperscript𝜃𝑟𝑔𝐱subscriptsuperscript𝜃𝑙†𝑔𝐱subscript𝐞1ketsubscript𝐵1𝐱displaystyletildethetargleft(xright)thetaldaggergleft(x+hate1right)left|B1left(xright)right =|B1​(𝐱)⟩absentketsubscript𝐵1𝐱displaystyle=left|B1left(xright)right θgu​(𝐱)​θgd⁣†​(𝐱+𝐞2)​|B2​(𝐱)⟩subscriptsuperscript𝜃𝑢𝑔𝐱subscriptsuperscript𝜃𝑑†𝑔𝐱subscript𝐞2ketsubscript𝐵2𝐱displaystyletildethetaugleft(xright)thetaddaggergleft(x+hate2right)left|B2left(xright)right =|B2​(𝐱)⟩absentketsubscript𝐵2𝐱displaystyle=left|B2left(xright)right (with θg,θgsubscript𝜃𝑔subscript𝜃𝑔thetag,tildethetag defined in , respectively) as depicted in Fig. . This allows to construct states with a global or local symmetry, as we shall now see.
content_sections.III Gauge Invariant PEPS III.2 Imposing: We want our PEPS |ψ⟩ket𝜓left|psiright to be gauge invariant as in with respect to the local gauge transformations defined in . If the local physical-virtual states on each site satisfy (citebib.bib40) Θgs​(𝐱)​Θgt​(𝐱)​|A​(𝐱)⟩=θgl​(𝐱)​θgd​(𝐱)​|A​(𝐱)⟩,subscriptsuperscriptΘ𝑠𝑔𝐱subscriptsuperscriptΘ𝑡𝑔𝐱ket𝐴𝐱subscriptsuperscript𝜃𝑙𝑔𝐱subscriptsuperscript𝜃𝑑𝑔𝐱ket𝐴𝐱displaystyletildeThetasgleft(xright)tildeThetatgleft(xright)left|Aleft(xright)right=thetalgleft(xright)thetadgleft(xright)left|Aleft(xright)right, Θgs​(𝐱)​|A​(𝐱)⟩=θgr​(𝐱)​|A​(𝐱)⟩,subscriptsuperscriptΘ𝑠𝑔𝐱ket𝐴𝐱subscriptsuperscript𝜃𝑟𝑔𝐱ket𝐴𝐱displaystyleThetasgleft(xright)left|Aleft(xright)right=tildethetargleft(xright)left|Aleft(xright)right, Θgt​(𝐱)​|A​(𝐱)⟩=θgu​(𝐱)​|A​(𝐱)⟩∀g∈Gformulae-sequencesubscriptsuperscriptΘ𝑡𝑔𝐱ket𝐴𝐱subscriptsuperscript𝜃𝑢𝑔𝐱ket𝐴𝐱for-all𝑔𝐺displaystyleThetatgleft(xright)left|Aleft(xright)right=tildethetaugleft(xright)left|Aleft(xright)rightquadquadforall gin G where the physical Hilbert spaces are transformed using Θ,ΘΘΘTheta,tildeTheta defined in , and the virtual ones using Θ,ΘΘΘTheta,tildeTheta defined in and respectively; see Fig. . Using the transformation properties of the maximally entangled states one obtains that |ψ⟩ket𝜓left|psiright is gauge invariant. In order to get a more intuitive picture of the symmetry conditions , let us consider the compact Lie group case again. Omitting the coordinate, since we deal we a single coordinate 𝐱𝐱x, let us denote the right and left generators of the physical degrees of freedom by Ras/tsubscriptsuperscript𝑅𝑠𝑡𝑎Rs/ta and Las/tsubscriptsuperscript𝐿𝑠𝑡𝑎Ls/ta. For the virtual degrees of freedom we can also define such operators, but in their case note that they do not commute, since they do not act on separate degrees of freedom (|j​m⟩ket𝑗𝑚left|jmright states, unlike the physical |j​m​n⟩ket𝑗𝑚𝑛left|jmnright states). The conditions can be expressed, using these notations, as Gauss laws: (Las+Lat)​|A⟩=(Ral+Rad)​|A⟩,superscriptsubscript𝐿𝑎𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐿𝑎𝑡ket𝐴superscriptsubscript𝑅𝑎𝑙superscriptsubscript𝑅𝑎𝑑ket𝐴displaystyleleft(Las+Latright)left|Aright=left(Ral+Radright)left|Aright, Ras​|A⟩=Lar​|A⟩,superscriptsubscript𝑅𝑎𝑠ket𝐴superscriptsubscript𝐿𝑎𝑟ket𝐴displaystyle Rasleft|Aright=Larleft|Aright, Rat​|A⟩=Lau​|A⟩∀asuperscriptsubscript𝑅𝑎𝑡ket𝐴superscriptsubscript𝐿𝑎𝑢ket𝐴for-all𝑎displaystyle Ratleft|Aright=Lauleft|Arightquadquadforall a The first condition looks like the familiar physical Gauss law. It implies that the two ingoing representations of the virtual indices must combine to the same representation to which the two physical representations combine: js⊗jt∼jl⊗jdsimilar-totensor-productsubscript𝑗𝑠subscript𝑗𝑡tensor-productsubscript𝑗𝑙subscript𝑗𝑑jsotimes jtsim jlotimes jd . The other two conditions, are different identifying the right constituents of the physical degrees of freedom with the virtual states on the same legs. Combining the first condition with the other two, we can obtain a condition on the four virtual legs: the elements of Ajr​mr;ju​mu;jl​ml;jd​mdjs​ms​ns;jt​mt​ntsubscriptsuperscript𝐴subscript𝑗𝑠subscript𝑚𝑠subscript𝑛𝑠subscript𝑗𝑡subscript𝑚𝑡subscript𝑛𝑡subscript𝑗𝑟subscript𝑚𝑟subscript𝑗𝑢subscript𝑚𝑢subscript𝑗𝑙subscript𝑚𝑙subscript𝑗𝑑subscript𝑚𝑑Ajsmsns;jtmtntjrmr;jumu;jlml;jdmd must vanish, unless jr⊗ju∼jl⊗jd. similar-totensor-productsubscript𝑗𝑟subscript𝑗𝑢tensor-productsubscript𝑗𝑙subscript𝑗𝑑jrotimes jusim jlotimes jd. When the group is Abelian, only the irrep indices remain and the Clebsch-Gordan coefficients are simply Kronecker deltas. One can then formulate jr⊗ju∼jl⊗jdsimilar-totensor-productsubscript𝑗𝑟subscript𝑗𝑢tensor-productsubscript𝑗𝑙subscript𝑗𝑑jrotimes jusim jlotimes jd in a very simple way. For U​𝑈1U, for example, ⟨j1​j2|J⟩=δj1+j2,Jinner-productsubscript𝑗1subscript𝑗2𝐽subscript𝛿subscript𝑗1subscript𝑗2𝐽left j1j2|Jright=deltaj1+j2,J, and the ℤNsubscriptℤ𝑁ZN is the appropriate modular modification, ⟨j1​j2|J⟩=δj1+j2,J​mod​Ninner-productsubscript𝑗1subscript𝑗2𝐽subscript𝛿subscript𝑗1subscript𝑗2𝐽mod𝑁left j1j2|Jright=deltaj1+j2,JtextmodN. We thus obtain, in the U​𝑈1U case, only tensor elements for which jr+ju−jl−jd=0subscript𝑗𝑟subscript𝑗𝑢subscript𝑗𝑙subscript𝑗𝑑0jr+ju-jl-jd=0 may be nonzero (jr+ju−jl−jd=N​ℤsubscript𝑗𝑟subscript𝑗𝑢subscript𝑗𝑙subscript𝑗𝑑𝑁ℤjr+ju-jl-jd=NZ for ℤNsubscriptℤ𝑁ZN). The same applies to non-Abelian groups as well, but since physical states contain the (generally different) m,n𝑚𝑛m,n quantum numbers it is less simple. For S​U​𝑆𝑈2SU, e. g. , if we choose to include only the j=0,1/2𝑗012j=0,1/2 representations, the only non-vanishing tensor elements will be those with an even number of virtual legs (ingoing or outgoing) with j=1/2𝑗12j=1/2, such that a singlet can be formed by combining the contributions of all four legs. The only freedom left in the definition of Ajr​mr;ju​mu;jl​ml;jd​mdjs​ms​ns;jt​mt​ntsubscriptsuperscript𝐴subscript𝑗𝑠subscript𝑚𝑠subscript𝑛𝑠subscript𝑗𝑡subscript𝑚𝑡subscript𝑛𝑡subscript𝑗𝑟subscript𝑚𝑟subscript𝑗𝑢subscript𝑚𝑢subscript𝑗𝑙subscript𝑚𝑙subscript𝑗𝑑subscript𝑚𝑑Ajsmsns;jtmtntjrmr;jumu;jlml;jdmd is to introduce some parameters fjr,ju,jl,jdjsubscriptsuperscript𝑓𝑗subscript𝑗𝑟subscript𝑗𝑢subscript𝑗𝑙subscript𝑗𝑑fjjr,ju,jl,jd which only depend on the representations, and we obtain citebib.bib40: Ajr​mr;ju​mu;jl​ml;jd​mdjs​ms​ns;jt​mt​nt=∑𝑗​fjr,ju,jl,jdj​⟨jl​ml​jd​md|j​m⟩​⟨j​m|js​ms​jt​mt⟩​δjs​jr​δjt​jt​δns,mr​δnt,musubscriptsuperscript𝐴subscript𝑗𝑠subscript𝑚𝑠subscript𝑛𝑠subscript𝑗𝑡subscript𝑚𝑡subscript𝑛𝑡subscript𝑗𝑟subscript𝑚𝑟subscript𝑗𝑢subscript𝑚𝑢subscript𝑗𝑙subscript𝑚𝑙subscript𝑗𝑑subscript𝑚𝑑𝑗subscriptsuperscript𝑓𝑗subscript𝑗𝑟subscript𝑗𝑢subscript𝑗𝑙subscript𝑗𝑑inner-productsubscript𝑗𝑙subscript𝑚𝑙subscript𝑗𝑑subscript𝑚𝑑𝑗𝑚inner-product𝑗𝑚subscript𝑗𝑠subscript𝑚𝑠subscript𝑗𝑡subscript𝑚𝑡subscript𝛿subscript𝑗𝑠subscript𝑗𝑟subscript𝛿subscript𝑗𝑡subscript𝑗𝑡subscript𝛿subscript𝑛𝑠subscript𝑚𝑟subscript𝛿subscript𝑛𝑡subscript𝑚𝑢Ajsmsns;jtmtntjrmr;jumu;jlml;jdmd=undersetjsumfjjr,ju,jl,jdleft jlmljdmd|jmrightleft jm|jsmsjtmtrightdeltajsjrdeltajtjtdeltans,mrdeltant,mu In the following, we will focus on PEPS satisfying the above symmetry properties, with no more than one copy of each irrep in the virtual spaces. This may seem restrictive when attempting to apply the states to real, physically relevant Hamiltonians; here, however, we wish to consider the most minimal constructions which capture the relevant symmetry properties, allowing us to demonstrate our claims and results as accurately as possible. When applied to Hamiltonians as variational ansatz states the states may have to be generalized indeed but in a straight forward way that does not affect the properties we discuss here. For example, as was demonstrated already in the ℤ3subscriptℤ3Z3 case citebib.bib37, several copies of the virtual representations are required in order to use such PEPS in order to variationally find the ground states of the ℤ3subscriptℤ3Z3 Hamiltonian. One could also consider a more general PEPS construction, in which such properties are only satisfied after blocking, for effective sites and effective links. The symmetry conditions described above will hold in this case too - for the blocked tensor network, rather than the original, microscopic” one, and thus what we study here could easily be applied to such cases too. A more general scenario would be with local MPO symmetries citebib.bib4, but this is out of the scope of this work and requires its own, separate discussion.

content_sections.IV Transfer Operators and Norms of PEPS: Before turning to the study of the transfer operator of our gauge invariant PEPS, let us recall what the transfer operator of a PEPS is. First, we briefly review the one dimensional, MPS case citebib.bib43. We strictly focus on the translationally invariant case, since this work is aimed at translational invariant systems; however, the general transfer matrix discussion may be (and has been) generalized to the non-translationally invariant case.

The transfer matrix approach can be generalized to two dimensional PEPS, such as the ones we consider here, constructed in . We assume the system has periodic boundary conditions - a torus of size 𝒩×𝒩𝒩𝒩NtimesN (generalizations to other boundary conditions are straightforward). The local transfer operator of a PEPS on a site is a map from two double virtual Hilbert spaces, associated with the ingoing (left and down) legs, to other two double virtual spaces, directed to the outgoing directions (right and up): T=Tl​l′,r​r′,d​d′,u​u′​|l​l′⟩​⟨r​r′|⊗|d​d′⟩​⟨u​u′|𝑇tensor-productsubscript𝑇𝑙superscript𝑙′𝑟superscript𝑟′𝑑superscript𝑑′𝑢superscript𝑢′ket𝑙superscript𝑙′bra𝑟superscript𝑟′ket𝑑superscript𝑑′bra𝑢superscript𝑢′hatT=Tllprime,rrprime,ddprime,uuprimeleft|llprimerightleft rrprimeright|otimesleft|ddprimerightleft uuprimeright| (note that we use again a convention in which the input vectors are denoted by bras, in accordance with matrix product ordered from left to right in the positive system directions). observables, one may use the entire MPS machinery for computations of norms, expectation values and correlation functions. Naively, one may deduce that correlations in this case decay exponentially as in the MPS case citebib.bib43. However, unlike in the one dimensional, MPS case, here the transfer matrix is a composite object with some internal structure, which can lead to different results. It was shown in citebib.bib60, for example, that two dimensional PEPS can describe critical physics, exhibiting power law contributions.

Then one may use perturbation theory to find the spectrum of E𝐸hatE. The nonperturbed part is ∑μ≤L​λμN​|0​(jμ)⟩​⟨0​(jμ)|⊗|0​(jμ)⟩​⟨0​(jμ)|⊗⋯⊗|0​(jμ)⟩​⟨0​(jμ)|tensor-producttensor-product𝜇𝐿superscriptsubscript𝜆𝜇𝑁ket0subscript𝑗𝜇bra0subscript𝑗𝜇ket0subscript𝑗𝜇bra0subscript𝑗𝜇⋯ket0subscript𝑗𝜇bra0subscript𝑗𝜇undersetmuleq LsumlambdamuNleft|0left(jmuright)rightleft 0left(jmuright)right|otimesleft|0left(jmuright)rightleft 0left(jmuright)right|otimescdotsotimesleft|0left(jmuright)rightleft 0left(jmuright)right| giving rise to zeroth order eigenvectors as before, with corrections which are product vectors as well. Now allow for nonzero weak off diagonal elements in the zeroth block. Perturbation theory is still valid, keeping our eigenvectors close to product states along the row. In fact, as long as the diagonal terms of the zeroth block are significantly stronger (in absolute value) than the rest of the τ0subscript𝜏0tau0 elements, this argument holds. As these other terms get larger and larger, the perturbative description loses its validity and the eigenvectors get farther from being product states along the row. This may be interpreted as the lack or the presence of long-range order: the farther we are from product states along the row, the longer ranged order we have. Since confinement has to do with disorder citebib.bib45, we find here the first hint to detecting area law from the transfer operators. As we shall see later on, indeed, the closer the transfer matrix eigenvectors are to product states, the closer we are to an area law of the Wilson loop.

the ingoing legs form a combined singlet, while both the outgoing legs, separately, belong to the J𝐽J representation (one regular, one conjugate) - see Fig. .

Do we need to use all the elements of the transfer operators for the Wilson loop contraction? The answer is no; we can ignore some of them in the computation, while tiling the different building blocks together, thanks to the local symmetry and the special properties it enforces on the states and the transfer operators, just like we did in the case of the norm. As discussed, each of the local transfer operators used for the contraction, either with or without flux, can be seen as a map between the two ingoing legs to the two outgoing ones. While the ingoing legs form together a multiplet vector of the group, the output is a product of two separate multiplet vectors on the two outgoing legs (see Fig. (b), and (b)). The numerator of the Wilson loop expectation value requires a particular tiling of the transfer operators, closing the loop. Since the output to each direction forms a multiplet vector, this will also be the input of the neighbouring transfer operators in the outgoing directions, and we can restrict all our transfer operators by cutting off all the input options that could not be realized within the Wilson loop tiling. This is done in a very similar way to what did in the norm computation, where we defined τ0subscript𝜏0hattau0 instead of T𝑇hatT. Since our system is translationally invariant, let us identify the lower left corner of the loop with the lower left corner of our system. Let us consider the numerator of the expectation value of the Wilson loop: ⟨ψ|W|ψ⟩=⟨ψ||ψ⟩=Tr​quantum-operator-product𝜓𝑊𝜓quantum-operator-product𝜓𝜓Trdelimited-leftpsiright|Wleft|psiright=leftpsiright|begingatheredincludegraphics[scale=0.2]Wexp. pngendgatheredleft|psiright=textTrleft[begingatheredincludegraphics[scale=0.3]Ttile. pngendgatheredright] Where the trace is on both directions, assuming periodic boundaries (similar results may be easily derived for open boundary conditions); the J,M,N𝐽𝑀𝑁J,M,N indices of the flux-carrying transfer operators have been omitted for simplicity, but it is assumed that they all carry the same irrep J𝐽J (otherwise it would make no physical sense) and that the M,N𝑀𝑁M,N indices are properly connected and summed over along the loop. Using the mapping properties summarized in figures (b), and (b), we can write on each of the outgoing legs its output representation - 00, J𝐽J or J¯¯𝐽overlineJ for the conjugate representations used in the backwards fluxes cases. Therefore, we conclude that a perimeter law will be obtained if the largest relevant (in terms of accessible through Ebsubscript𝐸𝑏hatEb and Etsubscript𝐸𝑡hatEt) eigenvalue of E∥​(R)subscript𝐸parallel-to𝑅hatEparallelleft(Rright) is independent of R𝑅R; an area law is possible if it depends on R𝑅R exponentially. Why only possible? To see why this condition is necessary but not sufficient for the area law to hold, let us consider the following scenario. Previously, we made the assumption that the eigenvectors of the flux-free transfer matrix should be close to product vectors in order to make an area law possible. We also know that the expectation value of the Wilson loop depends on the zeroth flux transfer operators τ0subscript𝜏0hattau0 inside and outside the loop, and some other, flux-carrying transfer operators along the loop. Let us assume that we are, indeed, in a scenario in which the eigenvalues of the transfer matrix are close to product states. Denote as usual the highest eigenvalue of the transfer operator by λ1subscript𝜆1lambda1 . Then the norm, for a large system, will roughly scale as λ1𝒩2superscriptsubscript𝜆1superscript𝒩2lambda1N2: each site contributes a single power of λ1subscript𝜆1lambda1. This is the denominator of the expectation value formula. In the numerator, we will have a contribution of λ1subscript𝜆1lambda1 for each site outside the loop; within the loop, it depends. If the flux carrying transfer operators along the loop take us from the singlet subspace corresponding to λ1subscript𝜆1lambda1 to that of another eigenvalue - denote it by λ′superscript𝜆′lambdaprime - we will have a contribution of λ′superscript𝜆′lambdaprime for each of the sites within the loop, and the Wilson loop’s expectation value will scale as (λ′/λ1)Asuperscriptsuperscript𝜆′subscript𝜆1𝐴left(lambdaprime/lambda1right)A where A𝐴A is the area of the loop (E∥​(R)∝λ′⁣Rproportional-tosubscript𝐸parallel-to𝑅superscript𝜆′𝑅Eparallelleft(Rright)proptolambdaprime R). However, if the flux carrying transfer operators do not take us to another singlet subspace with a different eigenvalue, we will not have an area dependent contribution. In this case, the largest eigenvalue of E∥​(R)subscript𝐸parallel-to𝑅hatEparallelleft(Rright) depends exponentially on R𝑅R (through λ′⁣Rsuperscript𝜆′𝑅lambdaprime R) but an area law is not obtained, which shows us why this condition is necessary but not sufficient. On the other hand, if the eigenvectors of E𝐸hatE are far from product vectors, which means they are governed by some collective, long range effect, we cannot have area-dependent contributions at all.
content_sections.VI Illustration: the ℤ2subscriptℤ2\\math: To conclude and illustrate our discussion, we will show an explicit example, where the gauge group is ℤ2subscriptℤ2Z2. In this case, the group Hilbert space on each link is two dimensional, with representations labelled by j=+,−𝑗j=+,-, which can be simply seen as spins. The group element operators are Hermitian, U=U†=X𝑈superscript𝑈†𝑋U=Udagger=X, and invert the spin, X​|±⟩=|∓⟩𝑋ketplus-or-minusketminus-or-plusXleft|pmright=left|mpright and the group operations ΘΘTheta (no difference between left and right in Abelian groups) are the identity operator as well as Z​|±⟩=±|±⟩𝑍ketplus-or-minusplus-or-minusketplus-or-minusZleft|pmright=pmleft|pmright Gauge transformations are given by Θ​(𝐱)=Z​(𝐱,1)​Z​(𝐱,2)​Z​(𝐱−𝐞1,1)​Z​(𝐱−𝐞1,2)Θ𝐱𝑍𝐱1𝑍𝐱2𝑍𝐱subscript𝐞11𝑍𝐱subscript𝐞12hatThetaleft(xright)=Zleft(x,1right)Zleft(x,2right)Zleft(x-hate1,1right)Zleft(x-hate1,2right) We would like to consider the most general PEPS with translational and rotational invariance, with physical spaces containing all the irreps and virtual ones containing a single copy of each irrep (minimal construction - as explained above, to consider real physical scenarios one will most likely have to generalize in a straight forward manner and add more copies, as was necessary in the ℤ3subscriptℤ3Z3 demonstration of Ref. citebib.bib42). If we consider the |+⟩ketleft|+right states as flux free states, and the |−⟩ketleft|-right as flux carrying, we can interpret α𝛼alpha as the amplitude of having no fluxes going through the site, β𝛽beta as the amplitude of corner flux, γ𝛾gamma - of straight line fluxes and δ𝛿delta - two intersecting flux lines. Here we will be interested in the properties of the transfer operators constructed for such states, and the computation of the Wilson loop expectation value.

seen; the first one is the zeroth block, mixing only projection operators. It depends on α,γ,δ𝛼𝛾𝛿alpha,gamma,delta - the amplitudes for which fluxes do not change directions, and thus the representations are not changed horizontally and vertically on the state, and the on-leg singlets are not flipped on the transfer operators. The second block, where the representation / singlet change, depends on β𝛽beta - the turning (corner) flux amplitude. Furthermore, as the parameter γ𝛾gamma has to do with straight flux lines going through the site, we expect that the larger it gets, the farther the Mμsubscript𝑀𝜇hatMmu operators derived from the zeroth block are from projection operators, and the farther we are from an area law; indeed, as we see, it appears on the off-diagonal terms of the zeroth block, and when γ=0𝛾0gamma=0 the Mμsubscript𝑀𝜇hatMmu operators of the zeroth blocks are projectors.
