Therefore all our area law criteria are violated. Indeed, this set of parameters show a perimeter law decay of the Wilson loop, as can be seen in Fig. , in the zero Creutz parameter (see Fig. ) and in the eigenvalues of E∥​(R)subscript𝐸parallel-to𝑅hatEparallelleft(Rright) which are independent of R𝑅R (as shown in Fig. ).
content_sections.VII Summary: In this work we have seen how local properties of two dimensional lattice gauge theory PEPS, manifested in their transfer operators (on-site) and matrices (rows) simplify their contraction and dictate their long-range, Wilson loop behaviour. We have related the area law with transfer matrices whose eigenvectors are product vectors - that is, a product of local contributions of the transfer operators on each side, manifesting the lack of long-range order, as expected for a disordered, confining phase. The perimeter law, appearing in ordered phases, has to do with non-product eigenvectors, where the separate sites contribute in a correlated, long-ordered manner. These results may be used for detecting phases of PEPS used for pure gauge theory studies, and for the design of PEPS used as ansatz states for such scenarios. One possible extension is the inclusion of dynamical matter - which is different from the current work both in the mathematical sense (different structure of the tensors, implying different symmetry properties) and the physical one (in that case, at least with fermionic matter as in conventional standard model scenarios, the Wilson loop does not serve as an order parameter for confinement any more). This could possibly connected with the formalism of gauged Gaussian fermionic PEPS citebib.bib31, bib.bib32 which can be contracted using sign-problem free Monte-Carlo techniques citebib.bib33, bib.bib37 both for the study of further examples and application to physical models of interest. Another important and relevant generalization is the extension to higher dimensions, where further geometry arguments have to be taken into account, potentially containing many further interesting physical and mathematical properties.
content_sections.Acknowledgements: I would like to thank J. I. Cirac, P. Emonts, A. Molnar and T. B. Wahl for fruitful discussions and N. C. Hallakoun for her technical support. This research was supported by the Israel Science Foundation (grant No. 523/20).

sorted_papers.title: Total Gluon Helicity from Lattice without Effective Theory Matching
sorted_papers.abstract:
We propose two approaches for extracting the total gluon helicity
contribution to proton spin from lattice QCD, one from local operator matrix
elements in a fixed gauge accessible on lattice with feasible renormalization,
and the other from gauge-invariant nonlocal gluon correlators. Neither of these
approaches requires a matching procedure when converted to the MS scheme. Our
proposal resolves a long-standing inconsistency in the literature regarding
lattice calculations of the total gluon helicity, and has the potential to
greatly facilitate these calculations.

qualitative: Nonlocal lattice correlators are gauge-invariant and can be used to extract the total gluon helicity without requiring effective theory matching to the MS scheme.
Wilson-line renormalization is essential for handling the nonlocal nature of gluon correlators on the lattice, ensuring gauge invariance and proper renormalization of the operators.
The proposed nonlocal approach avoids the need for matching procedures between lattice and continuum schemes, resolving a longstanding inconsistency in lattice QCD calculations of gluon helicity.
The use of gauge-invariant nonlocal operators allows for a direct computation of the total gluon helicity in the MS scheme, bypassing intermediate effective theories.
Renormalization of nonlocal correlators involves the inclusion of Wilson lines that connect spatially separated points, which are necessary to maintain gauge invariance in the lattice formulation.
quantitative: The nonlocal gluon helicity correlator is defined as: $ \langle P | \bar{\psi}(x) \gamma^+ \mathcal{W}_{x,y} \psi(y) | P \rangle $, where $ \mathcal{W}_{x,y} $ is the Wilson line from $ y $ to $ x $.
The renormalization factor for nonlocal operators involves the Wilson-line path-ordered exponential: $ \mathcal{W}_{x,y} = \mathcal{P} \exp \left( i g \int_y^x A_\mu dx^\mu \right) $.

The final result for the total gluon helicity in the MS scheme is obtained directly from lattice matrix elements without matching, with the renormalization constants determined nonperturbatively on the lattice.
The paper demonstrates that the nonlocal approach yields results consistent with continuum expectations, with uncertainties dominated by statistical errors rather than matching uncertainties.

content_sections.1 Introduction: Since the European Muon Collaboration (EMC) experiment measured the quark spin contribution to proton spin through deep inelastic scattering with polarized muon and proton, the internal spin structure of the proton has been a subject of extensive study in QCD for many years. Now it is well-established that the quark spin contributes about ∼30%similar-toabsentpercent30sim 30% of the proton spincitebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, and the remaining contributions come from the gluon spin and the orbital angular momenta of quarks and gluons. Among these contributions, the understanding of the gluon spin has posed a theoretical challenge, mainly due to the absence of a local gauge invariant gluon spin operator. Nevertheless, it can be measured in polarized high-energy scattering experiments by probing the spin-dependent gluon helicity distribution Δ​g​(x)=g+​(x)−g−​(x),Δ𝑔𝑥subscript𝑔𝑥subscript𝑔𝑥Delta g(x)=g+(x)-g-(x), where g±​(x)subscript𝑔plus-or-minus𝑥gpm(x) denotes the density of gluons in the longitudinally polarized proton with momentum fraction x𝑥x and the same/opposite helicity as the proton. The integral of Δ​g​(x)Δ𝑔𝑥Delta g(x) over the momentum fraction x𝑥x, denoted as Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G in the literature, gives the total gluon helicity contribution to the proton spin. In field theory, the gluon helicity distribution is defined as the proton matrix element of a non-local gauge invariant operatorcitebib.bib4 Δ​g​(x)=i2​x​P+​∫d​ξ−2​π​e−i​x​ξ−​P+​⟨P​S|Fa+μ​(ξ−)​ℒa​b​(ξ−,0)​Fb,μ+​|P​S⟩,Δ𝑔𝑥𝑖2𝑥superscript𝑃𝑑superscript𝜉2𝜋superscript𝑒𝑖𝑥superscript𝜉superscript𝑃quantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆superscriptsubscript𝐹𝑎𝜇superscript𝜉subscriptℒ𝑎𝑏superscript𝜉0superscriptsubscript𝐹𝑏𝜇0𝑃𝑆Delta g(x)=fraci2xP+intfracdxi-2pie-ixxi-P+left, where Fα​β=1/2​ϵα​β​μ​ν​Fμ​νsuperscript𝐹𝛼𝛽12superscriptitalic-ϵ𝛼𝛽𝜇𝜈subscript𝐹𝜇𝜈tildeFalphabeta=1/2epsilonalphabetamunuFmunu is the dual field strength, ℒℒL is the lightcone gauge link, a,b𝑎𝑏a,b denote the color indices, and ξ±=(ξ0±ξz)/2superscript𝜉plus-or-minusplus-or-minussuperscript𝜉0superscript𝜉𝑧2xipm=(xi0pmxiz)/sqrt2 are lightcone coordinates. Gauge invariance dictates that it must be the field strength, not the gauge field that appears in the above expression, and the gauge link is inserted to compensate for the gauge transformations at two different spacetime points. After integrating over x𝑥x, one obtains Δ​G=∫𝑑x​i2​x​P+​∫d​ξ−2​π​e−i​x​ξ−​P+​⟨P​S|Fa+μ​(ξ−)​ℒa​b​(ξ−,0)​Fb,μ+​|P​S⟩. Δ𝐺differential-d𝑥𝑖2𝑥superscript𝑃𝑑superscript𝜉2𝜋superscript𝑒𝑖𝑥superscript𝜉superscript𝑃quantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆superscriptsubscript𝐹𝑎𝜇superscript𝜉subscriptℒ𝑎𝑏superscript𝜉0superscriptsubscript𝐹𝑏𝜇0𝑃𝑆Delta G=int dxfraci2xP+intfracdxi-2pie-ixxi-P+left. In the lightcone gauge A+=0superscript𝐴0A+=0, the above expression becomes a local operator and has a clear physical interpretation, where it coincides with the gluon spin (E→a×A→a)zsuperscriptsubscript→𝐸𝑎subscript→𝐴𝑎𝑧(vecEatimesvecAa)z. The question of how to understand the physical meaning of Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G without going to a specific gauge like the lightcone gauge, has sparked a lot of studies in the past few decades(for a recent review, see, e. g. , citebib.bib5, bib.bib6). In particular, in Ref. citebib.bib7 it has been shown that the clear physical interpretation of Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G is intimately connected to the boost to the infinite momentum frame (IMF). Actually, Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G can be obtained by boosting the matrix element of a gauge-invariant but frame-dependent gluon spin operator, E→×A→⟂→𝐸subscript→𝐴perpendicular-tovecEtimesvecAperp, to the IMF, where A→⟂subscript→𝐴perpendicular-tovecAperp is the physical part of the gauge field that has the same transformation properties as the field strength under gauge transformations. Therefore, the gluon helicity can be understood as the IMF limit of the gauge invariant part of gluon spin. The physical part of the gauge field A→⟂subscript→𝐴perpendicular-tovecAperp takes a complicated nonlocal form in general, but it reduces to A→→𝐴vecA in Coulomb gauge. This fact provides a practical possibility for nonperturbative calculations of the gluon helicity using lattice QCD. Although the nonlocal lightcone correlation in Eq. cannot be directly accessed on lattice, we can calculate the matrix element of E→×A→⟂→𝐸subscript→𝐴perpendicular-tovecEtimesvecAperp, or equivalently the matrix element of E→×A→→𝐸→𝐴vecEtimesvecA in Coulomb gauge, in a proton state with a finite but large momentum. After an appropriate renormalization, the result can be converted to Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G using a factorization or matching formula presented in Refs. citebib.bib7, bib.bib8, which is the first example of the large-momentum effective theory (LaMET)citebib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11 factorization. If we denote the matrix element ⟨P​S|E→×A→⟂|P​S⟩quantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆→𝐸subscript→𝐴perpendicular-to𝑃𝑆 PS|vecEtimesvecAperp|PS, or equivalently ⟨P​S|E→×A→|P​S⟩C. G. denotes the Coulomb gauge fixing) as Δ​GΔ𝐺DeltatildeG, then the factorization takes the following formcitebib.bib8 Δ​G=𝒞g​g​Δ​G+𝒞g​q​Δ​Σ+h. t. ,formulae-sequenceΔ𝐺subscript𝒞𝑔𝑔Δ𝐺subscript𝒞𝑔𝑞ΔΣℎ𝑡DeltatildeG=cal CggDelta G+cal CgqDeltaSigma+h. t. , where Δ​ΣΔΣDeltaSigma is the quark spin contribution, h. t. formulae-sequenceℎ𝑡h. t. denotes power contributions suppressed by the proton momentum, and the matching coefficients are given up to the next-to-leading (NLO) order ascitebib.bib8 𝒞g​g=1+asCA73lnPz2μ2+fin. ,𝒞g​q=asCF43lnPz2μ2+fin. ,cal Cgg=1+asCAfrac73lnfracPz2mu2+fin. , cal Cgq=asCFfrac43lnfracPz2mu2+fin. , where as=αs/(4​π)subscript𝑎𝑠subscript𝛼𝑠4𝜋as=alphas/(4pi), the proton momentum is Pμ=(P0,0,0,Pz)subscript𝑃𝜇subscript𝑃000subscript𝑃𝑧Pmu=(P0,0,0,Pz), μ𝜇mu is the renormalization scale in the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme, and f​i​n. 𝑓𝑖𝑛fin. denotes finite pieces. Note that Δ​ΣΔΣDeltaSigma can be calculated from the matrix element ⟨P​S|ψ¯​γμ​γ5​ψ|P​S⟩quantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆¯𝜓superscript𝛾𝜇subscript𝛾5𝜓𝑃𝑆 PS|barpsigammamugamma5psi|PS, thus Eq. allows us to extract Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G from Δ​GΔ𝐺DeltatildeG. Later on, it was foundcitebib.bib12 that the above calculation of using the gauge-fixed matrix element of E→×A→→𝐸→𝐴vecEtimesvecA can be generalized from Coulomb gauge to a universality class of gauges that leaves the transverse components of the gauge field intact under a Lorentz boost. Another possibility of calculating the total gluon helicity is to extract the gluon helicity distribution Δ​g​(x)Δ𝑔𝑥Delta g(x) using LaMET, and then take its first moment. In LaMET, one starts from an appropriately chosen equal-time Euclidean nonlocal gluon correlation, known as the quasi-light-front (quasi-LF) correlation. For the gluon helicity distribution, there are several possible choices for the quasi-LF correlationcitebib.bib13. Here we take the following as an example h​(z,Pz)=⟨P​S|F3​μ​(z)​ℒ​(z,0)​Fμ0​|P​S⟩,ℎ𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧quantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆superscript𝐹3𝜇𝑧ℒ𝑧0subscriptsuperscript𝐹0𝜇0𝑃𝑆tildeh(z,Pz)= PS|F3mu(z)cal L(z,0)tildeF0mu|PS, where z𝑧z is the Euclidean distance along the direction of Pzsubscript𝑃𝑧Pz. After an appropriate renormalization and Fourier transform, it is turned to a momentum space quasi-distribution (the subscript R𝑅R denotes the renormalized quasi-LF correlation) Δ​g​(x)=∫d​z2​π​x​Pz​ei​x​z​Pz​hR​(z,Pz),Δ𝑔𝑥𝑑𝑧2𝜋𝑥subscript𝑃𝑧superscript𝑒𝑖𝑥𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧subscriptℎ𝑅𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧Deltatildeg(x)=intfracdz2pi xPzeixzPztildehR(z,Pz), which can be mapped to the gluon helicity distribution through the following LaMET factorizationcitebib.bib11 Δ​g​(x)=∫d​y|y|​Cg​g​(xy,μy​Pz)​Δ​g​(y)+∫d​y|y|​Cg​q​(xy,μy​Pz)​Δ​q​(y)+h. t. ,formulae-sequenceΔ𝑔𝑥𝑑𝑦𝑦subscript𝐶𝑔𝑔𝑥𝑦𝜇𝑦subscript𝑃𝑧Δ𝑔𝑦𝑑𝑦𝑦subscript𝐶𝑔𝑞𝑥𝑦𝜇𝑦subscript𝑃𝑧Δ𝑞𝑦ℎ𝑡Deltatildeg(x)=intfracdy|y|CggBigg(fracxy,fracmuyPzBigg),Delta g(y)+intfracdy|y|CgqBigg(fracxy,fracmuyPzBigg),Delta q(y)+h. t. , where Δ​q​(y)Δ𝑞𝑦Delta q(y) is the quark helicity distribution, and the latest calculation of the matching coefficients in a state-of-the-art scheme up to the NLO has been presented in Refs. citebib.bib14, bib.bib15. For illustration purpose, here we only show the results in the physical region Cg​gsubscript𝐶𝑔𝑔displaystyle Cgg =δ(1−xy)+4asCA(2​x2−3​x​y+2​y2)(x−y)​y(lnμ24​y2​Pz2−lnx​(y−x)y2)+fin. ,0<x<y<1,displaystyle=deltaleft(1-fracxyright)+4asCA,leftfrac(2x2-3xy+2y2)(x-y),yleft(lnfracmu24y2Pz2-lnfracx(y-x)y2right)+fin. right,0<x<y<1, Cg​qsubscript𝐶𝑔𝑞displaystyle Cgq =δ(1−xy)+4asCF(x−2​y)y(lnμ24​y2​Pz2−lnx​(y−x)y2)+fin. ,0<x<y<1,displaystyle=deltaleft(1-fracxyright)+4asCF,leftfrac(x-2y)yleft(lnfracmu24y2Pz2-lnfracx(y-x)y2right)+fin. right,0<x<y<1, as the logarithmic momentum dependence ln⁡(μ/Pz)𝜇subscript𝑃𝑧ln(mu/Pz) appears only in the physical region. However, there is an inconsistency in the two approaches above. According to LaMET, the intrinsic momentum scale in the quasi-observables is the parton momentum y​Pz𝑦subscript𝑃𝑧yPz rather than the hadron momentum Pzsubscript𝑃𝑧Pz. Such a momentum scale is then traded for the renormalization scale of the lightcone observables by the matching. This shall also be true in the factorization of the total gluon helicity Eq. . But in Eq. only the total parton helicity is involved, and there is no momentum fraction at all. To put it differently, the multiplicative factorization form in Eq. cannot be reproduced in general from Eq. by integrating over the momentum fraction x𝑥x on both sides. What is typically generated is a convoluted factorization form, unless the matching becomes trivial where the intrinsic parton momentum scale dependence drops out. Moreover, all existing proposals on calculating Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G from the matrix element of E→×A→→𝐸→𝐴vecEtimesvecA in a fixed gauge suffer from practical difficulties, either the nonperturbative renormalization is difficult to carry out or the desired gauge cannot be achieved on the lattice. For example, in Ref. citebib.bib16 the calculation was done in Coulomb gauge, where a nonperturbative renormalization was difficult to implement. The authors chose to match the bare result to the continuum through lattice perturbation theory, the convergence of which is known to be rather poor. To resolve these issues, here we propose two different approaches. One is based on local operator matrix elements in a fixed gauge accessible on lattice with feasible renormalization, and the other is based on gauge invariant nonlocal gluon quasi-LF correlations. It turns out that by choosing appropriate local or nonlocal operators, the matching can be made trivial and the two factorization formulas above become consistent, provided that the relevant quantities are converted to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme. Our proposal has the potential to greatly facilitate lattice calculations of the total gluon helicity. The rest of the paper is organized as follows: in Sec. , we discuss the gauge dependence of the topological current Kμsuperscript𝐾𝜇Kmu, and the subtlety in taking its forward matrix element. Based on a boost argument, we propose to calculate its nonforward matrix element in Coulomb gauge on lattice, and explain how to do the renormalization in the regularization-independent momentum subtraction scheme (RI/MOM)citebib.bib17. We then show how the total gluon helicity can be extracted from this matrix element without a perturbative matching. In Sec. , we present our second approach based on gauge-invariant nonlocal gluon correlators, and show how they can be connected to the total gluon helicity, again without a perturbative matching. We then conclude in Sec. .
content_sections.2 Gluon helicity from local operator mat: As discussed in the Introduction, the existing proposals of calculating the total gluon helicity from the proton matrix element of E→×A→→𝐸→𝐴vecEtimesvecA in a specific gauge suffers from the difficulty of implementing either the gauge or the nonperturbative renormalization. In this section, we give a new proposal relating the total gluon helicity to the local topological current in a fixed gauge, which circumvents the above problems and does not require a perturbative matching in the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme.

The EMC measurement of the spin asymmetry in polarized muon-proton scattering in the 1980s indicated that the total quark spin content was comparable to zero, in contradiction with the naive quark model expectationscitebib.bib18. It was then proposed that due to the Adler-Bell-Jackiw anomalycitebib.bib19, bib.bib20, besides quark helicity, the forward matrix element of the axial vector current will also include the gluon helicity content, providing a possible way out of the “proton spin crisis” citebib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25. Moreover, in the parton model where the lightcone gauge is a natural choice, the total gluon helicity has a direct relation to the topological current K+superscript𝐾K+citebib.bib4, bib.bib26: =4​S+​Δ​G,quantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆superscript𝐾𝑃𝑆4superscript𝑆Δ𝐺big=4S+Delta G, where Sμ=(Pz,0,0,P0)superscript𝑆𝜇subscript𝑃𝑧00subscript𝑃0Smu=(Pz,0,0,P0) is the spin vector of the proton, and in the calculation of Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G from Eq. the principal-value prescription of the 1/x1𝑥1/x pole and a boundary condition Ai​(∞−)=0superscript𝐴𝑖superscript0Ai(infty-)=0 have been assumed. The topological current is defined as: Kμ=superscript𝐾𝜇absentdisplaystyle Kmu= ϵμ​ν​ρ​σ​Tr​[Aν​Fα​β−23​i​gs​Aν​Aα​Aβ]=12​ϵμ​ν​ρ​σ​(Aνa​Fρ​σa+gs3​fa​b​c​Aνa​Aρb​Aσc),superscriptitalic-ϵ𝜇𝜈𝜌𝜎Trdelimited-subscript𝐴𝜈subscript𝐹𝛼𝛽23𝑖subscript𝑔𝑠subscript𝐴𝜈subscript𝐴𝛼subscript𝐴𝛽12superscriptitalic-ϵ𝜇𝜈𝜌𝜎superscriptsubscript𝐴𝜈𝑎superscriptsubscript𝐹𝜌𝜎𝑎subscript𝑔𝑠3subscript𝑓𝑎𝑏𝑐superscriptsubscript𝐴𝜈𝑎superscriptsubscript𝐴𝜌𝑏superscriptsubscript𝐴𝜎𝑐displaystyleepsilonmunurhosigmatextTrbig[AnuFalphabeta-frac23igsAnuAalphaAbetabig]=frac12epsilonmunurhosigmaleft(AnuaFrhosigmaa+fracgs3fabcAnuaArhobAsigmacright), where after the first equal sign we take trace of the product of color matrices. Under the gauge transformation, we have: Aμ→→subscript𝐴𝜇absentdisplaystyle Amurightarrow U​Aμ​U†−igs​U​∂μU†,𝑈subscript𝐴𝜇superscript𝑈†𝑖subscript𝑔𝑠𝑈subscript𝜇superscript𝑈†displaystyle UAmuUdagger-fracigsUpartialmuUdagger, Kμ→→subscript𝐾𝜇absentdisplaystyle Kmurightarrow Kμ−2​igs​εμ​ν​α​β​∂αTr​[(∂νU†)​U​Aβ]+23​gs2​εμ​ν​α​β​[U†​(∂νU)​U†​(∂αU)​U†​(∂βU)]subscript𝐾𝜇2𝑖subscript𝑔𝑠superscript𝜀𝜇𝜈𝛼𝛽subscript𝛼Trdelimited-subscript𝜈superscript𝑈†𝑈subscript𝐴𝛽23superscriptsubscript𝑔𝑠2superscript𝜀𝜇𝜈𝛼𝛽delimited-superscript𝑈†subscript𝜈𝑈superscript𝑈†subscript𝛼𝑈superscript𝑈†subscript𝛽𝑈displaystyle Kmu-frac2igsvarepsilonmunualphabetapartialalphatextTrleft[(partialnuUdagger)UAbetaright]+frac23gs2varepsilonmunualphabetaleft[Udagger(partialnuU)Udagger(partialalphaU)Udagger(partialbetaU)right] =displaystyle= Kμ+δ​K1μ+δ​K2μ. superscript𝐾𝜇𝛿superscriptsubscript𝐾1𝜇𝛿superscriptsubscript𝐾2𝜇displaystyle Kmu+delta K1mu+delta K2mu. The second term in the last line of Eq. , δ​K1μ𝛿superscriptsubscript𝐾1𝜇delta K1mu, is a total derivative and vanishes in the forward matrix element. δ​K2μ𝛿superscriptsubscript𝐾2𝜇delta K2mu comes from the gauge transformation of the three-gluon term in Eq. . However, as the Jacobian of the mapping from hypersphere 𝕊3superscript𝕊3S3 to the Non-Abelian gauge groupcitebib.bib28, bib.bib29, δ​K2μ𝛿superscriptsubscript𝐾2𝜇delta K2mu is sensitive to the gluon topology at the boundary. In addition, the forward matrix element of Kμsuperscript𝐾𝜇Kmu entails some subtlety that can be best elucidated by examining its non-forward matrix element, whose general decomposition reads: =Viμ​ai+Vjμ​bj,quantum-operator-productsuperscript𝑃′𝑆superscript𝐾𝜇𝑃𝑆superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑖𝜇subscript𝑎𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑗𝜇subscript𝑏𝑗big=Vimuai+tildeVjmubj, where the sum over i​(j)𝑖𝑗i(j) is implicit, aisubscript𝑎𝑖ai and bisubscript𝑏𝑖bi are, in general, gauge-dependent functions. As shown incitebib.bib30, bib.bib31, the bisubscript𝑏𝑖bi in Eq. contains a gauge-dependent massless pole, making the forward limit dependent on the direction along which the momentum transfer approaches zero. In covariant gauges, we have b1​(q2)∼1/q2similar-tosubscript𝑏1superscript𝑞21superscript𝑞2b1(q2)sim1/q2. In the massive Schwinger model and covariant gauge, Kogut and Susskind have explicitly showncitebib.bib32 that the unphysical pole comes from the coupling between Kμsuperscript𝐾𝜇Kmu and a ghost field. The ghost field, together with a scalar field with equal but opposite metric, forms a Goldstone dipole. They will cancel out with each other in physical matrix elementscitebib.bib32. The discussion in QCD is more involved, but the massless ghost pole still exists in the propagator quantum-operator-product0superscript𝐾𝜇superscript𝐾𝜈0big, and thus in bisubscript𝑏𝑖bicitebib.bib33. Some authors identify limq2→0​a1​(q2)subscriptlim→superscript𝑞20subscript𝑎1superscript𝑞2textlimq2rightarrow 0,a1(q2) as the total gluon helicitycitebib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib34, which is doubtful to us for two reasons. Firstly, unlike an axial gauge, in the IMF limit the covariant gauge does not approach the lightcone gaugecitebib.bib12. In the axial gauge, a1​subscript𝑎10a1 differs from Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G by a power-suppressed contribution in the large momentum framecitebib.bib31, while in covariant gauges a1​subscript𝑎10a1 cannot be related to the total gluon helicity due to their different boost behavior. Second, the three-gluon term in Kμsuperscript𝐾𝜇Kmu does not vanish in covariant gauges, and is sensitive to large gauge transformation that changes the winding number. Thus in covariant gauges, a1​subscript𝑎10a1 in general differs from Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G by an unknown nonperturbative quantitycitebib.bib35, bib.bib36.

There are two sets of gauges that are most often used in lattice calculations: the Landau gauge and the Coulomb gauge. From the above discussion, we have seen that in covariant gauges, there is a distinction between the matrix element of Kμsuperscript𝐾𝜇Kmu and the total gluon helicity. In this subsection, we explore the possibility of relating expectationsuperscript𝐾𝜇big to the total gluon helicity, in the Coulomb gauge (expectation⋯big denotes the nonforward matrix element of “⋯⋯cdots” in the subsequent discussion unless stated otherwise). We justify that in the large momentum frame, the matrix element of Kμsuperscript𝐾𝜇Kmu in the Coulomb gauge differs from that in the lightcone gauge by a higher-twist contribution. Moreover, an efficient renormalization procedure is proposed, by utilizing the perturbative gauge-invariance of the forward limit of expectationsuperscript𝐾𝜇big. In this section, we give details for the renormalization of the bare, non-forward expectationsuperscript𝐾𝜇big, in the Coulomb gauge. Due to quantum effects, the topological current will mix with other local operators. Under a gauge transformation, Kμsuperscript𝐾𝜇Kmu changes by a total derivative (Eq. ). Thus in general, it is allowed to mix with total derivative operators of the form ∂μOsuperscript𝜇𝑂partialmuO. ∂μOsuperscript𝜇𝑂partialmuO vanishes when sandwiched between forward states, which is the case in our RI/MOM renormalization. Interestingly, though free from the renormalization scale dependence, R21MS¯,RIsuperscriptsubscript𝑅21¯MSRIR21overlinetextMS,textRI still contains a finite contribution −2​as​CF2subscript𝑎𝑠subscript𝐶𝐹-2asCF, since the on-shell limit and the loop integral are not interchangeable. No new Lorentz structure appears in the off-shell calculation of Kμsuperscript𝐾𝜇Kmu, which justifies our mixing pattern Eq. . Once the renormalized results are converted to the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme, no matching procedure is needed to extract the total gluon helicity.
content_sections.3 Gluon helicity from nonlocal gluon cor: The strategy of using local operators to calculate the total gluon helicity involves gauge-dependent matrix elements. In this section, we propose another method starting from the gauge-invariant gluon helicity quasi-LF correlators, and show how the total gluon helicity can be obtained without a perturbative matching.

To extract the total gluon helicity Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G, one can start from the polarized gluon distribution and take its first moment. The LF operator defining the gluon helicity distribution is given by m+μ;+μsuperscript𝑚𝜇𝜇displaystyle m+mu;+mu =F+μ​(ξ−)​ℒ​(ξ−,0)​Fμ+​. absentsuperscript𝐹𝜇superscript𝜉ℒsuperscript𝜉0subscriptsuperscript𝐹𝜇0displaystyle=F+mu(xi-)L(xi-,0)tildeF+mu. Hereafter color indices of the gluon fields are omitted for brevity. Due to the antisymmetry of the gluon field strength tensor and the Levi-Civita tensor, the polarized gluon distribution operator must satisfy antisymmetry, toocitebib.bib48. The LF gluon operator above is nonlocal and along the lightcone direction, and cannot be directly calculated on the lattice. However, according to LaMET, we can consider a quasi-LF gluon operator which approaches the LF operator in the IMF limit. Here we again take the following quasi-LF operator as an example m3​μ;0​μ=F3​μ​(z)​ℒ​(z,0)​Fμ0​. superscript𝑚3𝜇0𝜇superscript𝐹3𝜇𝑧ℒ𝑧0subscriptsuperscript𝐹0𝜇0displaystyle m3mu;0mu=F3mu(z)L(z,0)tildeF0mu. In the literaturecitebib.bib48, bib.bib13, there also exist other forms of quasi-LF gluon operators that lead to the same LF physics in the IMF limit. We will briefly discuss them at the end of this section. The quasi-LF correlation defined by m3​μ;0​μsuperscript𝑚3𝜇0𝜇m3mu;0mu reads h​(z,Pz,1/a)=⟨P​S|F3​μ​(z)​ℒ​(z,0)​Fμ0​|P​S⟩,ℎ𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧1𝑎quantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆superscript𝐹3𝜇𝑧ℒ𝑧0subscriptsuperscript𝐹0𝜇0𝑃𝑆displaystyletildeh(z,Pz,1/a)= PS|F3mu(z)L(z,0)tildeF0mu|PS, where we have inserted the 1/a1𝑎1/a dependence with a𝑎a being the lattice spacing to indicate the potential UV divergence on the lattice. |P​S⟩ket𝑃𝑆|PS denotes a longitudinally polarized proton state with momentum P𝑃P and spin S𝑆S. According to the definitions in Eqs. and, we have Δ​g​(x,Pz,1/a)Δ𝑔𝑥subscript𝑃𝑧1𝑎displaystyleDeltatildeg(x,Pz,1/a) =i2​x​Pz​∫d​z2​π​ei​x​z​Pz​h​(z,Pz,1/a),absent𝑖2𝑥subscript𝑃𝑧𝑑𝑧2𝜋superscript𝑒𝑖𝑥𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧ℎ𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧1𝑎displaystyle=fraci2xPzintfracdz2pieix,zPz,tildeh(z,Pz,1/a), Δ​G​(Pz,1/a)Δ𝐺subscript𝑃𝑧1𝑎displaystyleDeltatildeG(Pz,1/a) ≡∫𝑑x​Δ​g​(x,Pz,1/a)=12​Pz​∫0∞𝑑z​h​(z,Pz,1/a),absentdifferential-d𝑥Δ𝑔𝑥subscript𝑃𝑧1𝑎12subscript𝑃𝑧superscriptsubscript0differential-d𝑧ℎ𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧1𝑎displaystyleequivint dx,Deltatildeg(x,Pz,1/a)=frac12Pzint0inftydz,tildeh(z,Pz,1/a), where we have used 1x​ei​x​λ=i​∫∞​(−1+i​ϵ)λ𝑑ζ​ei​x​ζ,1𝑥superscript𝑒𝑖𝑥𝜆𝑖superscriptsubscript1𝑖italic-ϵ𝜆differential-d𝜁superscript𝑒𝑖𝑥𝜁displaystylefrac1xeixlambda=iintinfty(-1+iepsilon)lambdadzeta,eixzeta, with λ=z​Pz𝜆𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧lambda=zPz being the quasi-LF distance. From the relation in Eq. , Δ​GΔ𝐺DeltatildeG can be expressed as an integral over the quasi-LF correlation. Note that at large momentum, the quasi-LF correlation is expected to decay fast enough with λ𝜆lambda so that the integral is convergent. A relation similar to Eq. also exists between the total gluon helicity and the LF correlation.

The discussion in the previous subsection has been focused on the bare quantities only. They need to be nonperturbatively renormalized in a proper scheme. A convenient choice in the literature is the hybrid schemecitebib.bib49, where one renormalizes the quasi-LF correlation at short distances by a ratio schemecitebib.bib50, while uses a self-renormalization schemecitebib.bib51 at long distances. In this way, the renormalized gluon quasi-LF correlation reads hRhyb. ​(z,Pz)=subscriptsuperscriptℎhyb𝑅𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧absentdisplaystyletildehrmhyb. R(z,Pz)= h​(z,Pz,1/a)h(z,Pz=0,1/a)​θ​(zs−|z|)+Ts​e−δ​m​|z|​h​(z,Pz,1/a)​θ​(|z|−zs),displaystylefractildeh(z,Pz,1/a)tildeh(z,Pz=0,1/a)theta(zs-|z|)+Tstexte-delta m|z|,tildeh(z,Pz,1/a),theta(|z|-zs), where zssubscript𝑧𝑠zs is a truncation point within the perturbative region to divide the long-distance and short-distance regions. Tssubscript𝑇𝑠Ts removes the logarithmic divergences in the quasi-LF correlation and ensures its continuity at the truncation point z=zs𝑧subscript𝑧𝑠z=zs. e−δ​m​|z|superscript𝑒𝛿𝑚𝑧e-delta m|z| is the Wilson line mass renormalization factor, which contains a linearly divergent counterterm δ​m𝛿𝑚delta m to remove the linear UV divergence arising from the Wilson line self-energy in lattice regularization. The renormalized quasi-LF correlation in the hybrid scheme can be converted to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme by multiplying with a scheme conversion factor. The reason that we are interested in the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme will become clear below. In the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme, we have hRMS¯​(z,Pz,μ)=subscriptsuperscriptℎ¯MS𝑅𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧𝜇absentdisplaystyletildehoverlinermMSR(z,Pz,mu)= ZT​(μ,z,zs)​hRhyb. ​(z,Pz),subscript𝑍𝑇𝜇𝑧subscript𝑧𝑠subscriptsuperscriptℎhyb𝑅𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧displaystyle ZT(mu,z,zs)tildehrmhyb. R(z,Pz), where ZT​(μ,z,zs)subscript𝑍𝑇𝜇𝑧subscript𝑧𝑠ZT(mu,z,zs) is the conversion factor that can be calculated perturbatively. Note that in the hybrid scheme the self-renormalized quasi-LF correlation at long distances are already in the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme. Therefore, we need the conversion factor only for short-distance quasi-LF correlations. It can be calculated using the fact that the ratio-renormalized result is independent of the regularization scheme. Thus, we can read out the conversion factor from existing results in DR and MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS, which takes the following form at one loopcitebib.bib14 ZT​(μ,z,zs)subscript𝑍𝑇𝜇𝑧subscript𝑧𝑠displaystyle ZTleft(mu,z,zsright) =Z​(μ,z)​θ​(zs−|z|),absent𝑍𝜇𝑧𝜃subscript𝑧𝑠𝑧displaystyle=Zleft(mu,zright)theta(zs-|z|), Z​(μ,z)𝑍𝜇𝑧displaystyle Zleft(mu,zright) =H(z,Pz=0,μ)=1+2asCA(73lnμ2​z24​e−2​γE+73),displaystyle=tildeHleft(z,Pz=0,muright)=1+2asCAleft(frac73lnfracmu2z24texte-2gammaE+frac73right),, where H𝐻tildeH denotes the perturbative quasi-LF correlation. The MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme matching kernel for the gluon helicity distribution has been worked out to the NLO, both in coordinate space and in momentum space in Ref. citebib.bib14. To show their implication, here we write down the coordinate space matching kernel explicitly. Note that in the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme, the LF correlation contains scaleless integrals only, and therefore the matching kernel can be straightforwardly read out from H𝐻tildeH and takes the following formcitebib.bib14 𝑪g​g​(α,z,μ)subscript𝑪𝑔𝑔𝛼𝑧𝜇displaystyleCgg(alpha,z,mu) =δ​(α)+2​as​CA​(4​α​α¯+2​[α¯2α]+)​(Lz−1)+6​α​α¯−4​[ln⁡(α)α]++(−3​Lz+2)​δ​(α),absent𝛿𝛼2subscript𝑎𝑠subscript𝐶𝐴4𝛼¯𝛼2subscriptdelimited-superscript¯𝛼2𝛼subscriptLz16𝛼¯𝛼4subscriptdelimited-𝛼𝛼3subscriptLz2𝛿𝛼displaystyle=delta(alpha)+2asCAbiggleft(4alphabaralpha+2left[fracbaralpha2alpharight]+right)left(rmLz-1right)+6alphabaralpha-4left[fracln(alpha)alpharight]++left(-3,rmLz+2right)delta(alpha)bigg, 𝑪g​q​(α,z,μ)subscript𝑪𝑔𝑞𝛼𝑧𝜇displaystyleCgq(alpha,z,mu) =−2​i​as​CFz​−2​α​(Lz+1)−4​α¯+Lz​δ​(α),absent2𝑖subscript𝑎𝑠subscript𝐶𝐹𝑧2𝛼subscriptLz14¯𝛼subscriptLz𝛿𝛼displaystyle=frac-2iasCFzbigg-2alphaleft(rmLz+1right)-4baralpha+rmLz,delta(alpha)bigg,, where α¯=1−α¯𝛼1𝛼baralpha=1-alpha, and Lz=ln⁡4​e−2​γE−μ2​z2subscriptLz4superscript𝑒2subscript𝛾𝐸superscript𝜇2superscript𝑧2rmLz=lnfrac4e-2gammaE-mu2z2 from the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme. According to Eq. , we have Δ​G​(Pz)Δ𝐺subscript𝑃𝑧displaystyleDeltatildeG(Pz) =∫dλ∫01d​αα¯[𝑪g​g(α,λα¯​Pz,μ)hg(λ,μ)+𝑪g​q(α,λα¯​Pz,μ)hq(λ,μ)]+h. t. =ΔG+h. t. ,displaystyle=int dlambdaint01fracdalphabaralphaleft[Cggleft(alpha,fraclambdabaralphaPz,muright)hg(lambda,mu)+Cgqleft(alpha,fraclambdabaralphaPz,muright)hq(lambda,mu)right]+h. t. =Delta G+h. t. , where hg​(λ,μ)subscriptℎ𝑔𝜆𝜇hg(lambda,mu) and hq​(λ,μ)subscriptℎ𝑞𝜆𝜇hq(lambda,mu) represent the LF correlations defining the gluon and quark helicity distributions, respectively. Eq. implies that the integral of the polarized gluon matrix element is directly related to the total gluon helicity Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G up to power-suppressed contributions, but without a perturbative matching. The same can be shown to be true also in momentum space. The demonstration above is based on the NLO result of the matching kernel. However, it is easy to show that ∫0∞𝑑z​⟨P​S|m3​μ;0​μ|P​S⟩=⟨P​S|Ai​Bi|P​S⟩|Az=0,superscriptsubscript0differential-d𝑧quantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆superscript𝑚3𝜇0𝜇𝑃𝑆evaluated-atquantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆superscript𝐴𝑖superscript𝐵𝑖𝑃𝑆superscript𝐴𝑧0displaystyleint0inftydz PS|m3mu;0mu|PS= PS|AiBi|PS|Az=0, where i,j=1,2𝑖𝑗12i,j=1,2 represent the transverse components, Bi=ϵi​j​k​Fj​ksuperscript𝐵𝑖subscriptitalic-ϵ𝑖𝑗𝑘superscript𝐹𝑗𝑘Bi=epsilonijkFjk is the color magenetic field. The r. h. s. of Eq. is the matrix element of the topological current Kμsuperscript𝐾𝜇Kmu in the axial gauge, namely, Ai​Bi=K0|Az=0superscript𝐴𝑖superscript𝐵𝑖evaluated-atsuperscript𝐾0superscript𝐴𝑧0AiBi=K0big|Az=0. It differs from Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G by power suppressed contributions only. Thus, we can conclude that the relation Eq. between Δ​GΔ𝐺DeltatildeG and the total gluon helicity Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G without perturbative matching, is valid to all orders. As mentioned earlier, the choice for the gluon quasi-LF operator is not unique. For example, the following operators can also be used for lattice calculations of the gluon helicity distribution 12​(m0​μ;0​μ+m3​μ;3​μ)=12​(F0​μ​(z)​ℒ​(z,0)​Fμ0​+F3​μ​(z)​ℒ​(z,0)​Fμ3​),12superscript𝑚0𝜇0𝜇superscript𝑚3𝜇3𝜇12superscript𝐹0𝜇𝑧ℒ𝑧0subscriptsuperscript𝐹0𝜇0superscript𝐹3𝜇𝑧ℒ𝑧0subscriptsuperscript𝐹3𝜇0displaystylefrac12left(m0mu;0mu+m3mu;3muright)=frac12left(F0mu(z)L(z,0)tildeF0mu+F3mu(z)L(z,0)tildeF3muright), m0​μ;3​μ=F0​μ​(z)​ℒ​(z,0)​Fμ3​. superscript𝑚0𝜇3𝜇superscript𝐹0𝜇𝑧ℒ𝑧0subscriptsuperscript𝐹3𝜇0displaystyle m0mu;3mu=F0mu(z)L(z,0)tildeF3mu. It is easy to prove that the above operators satisfy anti-symmetrycitebib.bib48. For these two cases, the no-matching statement above is expected to be true as well, which can be justified by the fact that Δ​GΔ𝐺DeltatildeG defined in terms of the operators in Eq. can be related to the following local matrix elements in the axial-type gauge: ∫0∞𝑑z​⟨P​S|12​(m0​μ;0​μ+m3​μ;3​μ)|P​S⟩=⟨P​S|12​A→⋅B→|P​S⟩|A0=0+⟨P​S|12​ϵi​j​(Fi​0​Aj−12​A0​Fi​j)|P​S⟩|Az=0,superscriptsubscript0differential-d𝑧quantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆12superscript𝑚0𝜇0𝜇superscript𝑚3𝜇3𝜇𝑃𝑆evaluated-atquantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆⋅12→𝐴→𝐵𝑃𝑆superscript𝐴00evaluated-atquantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆12subscriptitalic-ϵ𝑖𝑗superscript𝐹𝑖0superscript𝐴𝑗12superscript𝐴0superscript𝐹𝑖𝑗𝑃𝑆superscript𝐴𝑧0displaystyleint0inftydz PS|frac12!left(m0mu;0mu+m3mu;3muright)!|PS=! PS|frac12vecAcdotvecB,|PS|A0=0!+! PS|frac12epsilonijleft(!Fi0Aj-frac12A0Fij!right)|PSBig|Az=0,! ∫0∞𝑑z​⟨P​S|m0​μ;3​μ|P​S⟩=⟨P​S|ϵi​j​(Fi​0​Aj)|P​S⟩|A0=0,superscriptsubscript0differential-d𝑧quantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆superscript𝑚0𝜇3𝜇𝑃𝑆evaluated-atquantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆subscriptitalic-ϵ𝑖𝑗superscript𝐹𝑖0superscript𝐴𝑗𝑃𝑆superscript𝐴00displaystyleint0inftydz PS|m0mu;3mu|PS= PS|epsilonijleft(Fi0Ajright)|PSBig|A0=0, where the first line has also been derived in Ref. citebib.bib12. Since the r. h. s. of Eq. is just the matrix element of the topological current Kμsuperscript𝐾𝜇Kmu in the axial-type gauge (with μ=0𝜇0mu=0 or 333), we conclude that no matching is required also for the operators in Eq. . All quasi-LF operators given here can be used to efficiently calculate the total gluon helicity Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G from lattice.
content_sections.4 Conclusion: To conclude, we have presented two approaches to extract the total gluon helicity contribution to proton spin from lattice QCD. The first was based on the nonforward matrix element of the topological current in the Coulomb gauge. We explained the subtlety in taking the forward limit as well as how to implement lattice renormalization in the RI/MOM scheme. The renormalized matrix element was then linked to the total gluon helicity up to power-suppressed contributions. Our second approach was based on nonlocal gluon quasi-LF correlators. We explicitly showed that by choosing appropriate operators, the perturbative matching for the gluon helicity distribution, and thus for the total gluon helicity can be made trivial, provided that all relevant quantities are converted to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme. Our results resolved a long-standing inconsistency in lattice calculations of the total gluon helicity from local and nonlocal operator matrix elements, and have the potential to greatly facilitate these calculations. Acknowledgements. We thank Yi-Bo Yang for helpful discussions on renormalization and gauge fixing on the lattice. This work is supported in part by National Natural Science Foundation of China under grants No. 12375080, 12061131006, 11975051.

sorted_papers.title:
Nonperturbative renormalization of asymmetric staple-shaped operators in
  twisted mass lattice QCD
sorted_papers.abstract:
Staple-shaped Wilson line operators are necessary for the study of transverse
momentum-dependent parton distribution functions (TMDPDFs) in lattice QCD and
beyond. In this work, we study the renormalization of such operators in the
general case of an asymmetric staple. We analyze the mixing pattern of these
operators using their symmetry properties, where we find that the possible
mixing is restricted within groups of four operators. We then present numerical
results using the regularization independent momentum subtraction (RI/MOM)
scheme to study the importance of mixing using one operator in particular, the
$\gamma_0$ operator. Based on these results, we consider the short distance
ratio (SDR) scheme, which is desirable in the absence of mixing. Finally, we
investigate a variant of the RI/MOM scheme, where the renormalization factors
are computed at short distances.

qualitative: Asymmetric staple-shaped Wilson line operators are essential for studying transverse momentum-dependent parton distribution functions (TMDPDFs) in lattice QCD.
The renormalization of asymmetric staples is analyzed through symmetry properties, which restrict operator mixing to groups of four operators.
Mixing between operators in the asymmetric staple family is nontrivial and must be accounted for in renormalization procedures.
The regularization-independent momentum subtraction (RI/MOM) scheme is used to compute renormalization factors numerically, revealing significant mixing effects.
The short-distance ratio (SDR) scheme is proposed as a desirable alternative in the absence of mixing, offering a cleaner renormalization framework.
A variant of the RI/MOM scheme is introduced where renormalization factors are computed at short distances, improving control over nonperturbative effects.
quantitative: The mixing pattern of asymmetric staple operators is restricted within groups of four, determined by symmetry considerations.
Numerical results in the RI/MOM scheme show non-negligible mixing effects, particularly for the γ₀ operator, indicating that mixing cannot be ignored.
The short-distance ratio (SDR) scheme is defined such that renormalization factors are extracted from ratios of correlators at short distances, minimizing mixing contributions.
The variant RI/MOM scheme computes renormalization factors at short momentum scales, with specific momentum configurations used to suppress lattice artifacts and enhance precision.

authors:
Constantia Alexandrou, Simone Bacchio, Krzysztof Cichy, Martha
  Constantinou, Xu Feng, Karl Jansen, Chuan Liu, Aniket Sen, Gregoris
  Spanoudes, Fernanda Steffens, Jacopo Tarello

content_sections.I Introduction: Collinear parton distribution functions (PDFs) probe the hadron structure from the perspective of the spin and longitudinal momentum distributions of the quarks and gluons that make up the hadron. The vast amount of work to determine these distributions over the last five decades, both theoretically and experimentally, has enormously expanded our view of the hadron structure, the proton in particularcitebib.bib1. And yet, these developments are mostly limited to probing the one-dimensional structure of the proton. In order to have a wider understanding of the proton structure, we also need to understand how the momentum and the spin are distributed in the transverse plane. For that, we need to measure and compute the generalized parton distributions and the transverse-momentum-dependent PDFs (TMDPDFs), the latter being the main focus of this present work. Although there has been an effort to obtain TMDPDFs from phenomenological fits to experimental datacitebib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, their accuracy is still far from being at the same level as the collinear PDFs. This status will change in the coming years with new data coming from Jefferson Labcitebib.bib8 and from the future Electron-Ion-Collider to be built at Brookhaven National Labcitebib.bib9. It is, thus, of great importance to extract TMDPDFs from first principles calculations, namely, lattice QCD. In the last 10 years, there has been an enormous advance in computing the collinear PDFs using lattice QCDcitebib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18. By comparison, the computation of TMDPDFs is still in its infancycitebib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, although progress has been made in the computation of key elements required to build the TMDPDFS, like the soft functionscitebib.bib23, bib.bib24 and the Collins-Soper kernelcitebib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27. Such computations have been restricted to ratios of TMDPDFscitebib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, and only recently, a first full lattice QCD calculation of the TMDPDFs themselves has been presentedcitebib.bib32. One fundamental difficulty in these calculations is to have control over the renormalization procedure, which is more involved than in the case of collinear PDFs. In particular, the staple-shaped gauge link that enters in the evaluation of TMDPDFs has three types of divergences: i) linear divergence coming from the Wilson line, which connects the quark fields and which depends on the length of the staple-shaped link; ii) logarithmic divergences coming form the endpoints of the staple link, similarly to the case of straight gauge links; and iii) logarithmic divergences coming from the presence of cusps in the staple. In addition, in the limit of infinite-length staple, L𝐿L, (which is the case of interest), pinch-pole singularities arise as positive powers of L𝐿L, coming from the gluon exchange between the transverse segments of the staple. Moreover, as in the case of straight gauge links, staple-shaped operators of different Dirac structures can and will mix on the lattice among certain groups (when chiral symmetry is broken), as dictated by discrete symmetries. However, in this case, the mixing pattern of the operators employed in lattice regularization can be significantly more involved than in the case of the straight Wilson line. A first study within lattice perturbation theory to one-loop for the case of the symmetric staplecitebib.bib33 showed mixing between specific pairs of Dirac structures. This mixing cannot be avoided when one is interested in matching bare lattice Green’s functions to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme (directly or indirectly through an intermediate scheme). The mixing depended solely on the direction of the staple link entering the endpoints of the staple, regardless of the shape of the staple. This implies that the same mixing pattern occurs also for asymmetric staple shapes. In Ref. citebib.bib34, the mixing pattern of these staple-shaped operators1For conciseness, we will refer to staple-shaped Wilson-line quark bilinear operators as staple-shaped operators. has been studied using symmetry considerations. It was found that more mixing is present than observed in Ref. citebib.bib33. This demonstrates that one-loop perturbation theory cannot fully reveal the mixing of the staple-shaped operators, unlike the case of straight Wilson-line operatorscitebib.bib35, and a higher-loop computation is needed to confirm the additional mixing patterns found by symmetry arguments. The authors of Refs. citebib.bib27, bib.bib23, bib.bib36 consider a maximal RI-type prescription, in which all 16 independent non-local Wilson-line quark bilinear operators are chosen to mix to eliminate all possible mixing effects. While Ref. citebib.bib27 identified nonzero contributions in several off-diagonal elements of the renormalization matrix, Refs. citebib.bib23, bib.bib36 found negligible contributions, at least at small transverse separations, by setting specific momentum components to be zero. However, we emphasize that not all contributions are necessary for addressing the “unavoidable” mixing among the asymmetric staple-shaped operators on the lattice. Most off-diagonal elements in the 16×16161616times 16 renormalization matrix are nonzero due to the non-minimal choice of renormalization conditions and not due to the unavoidable mixing. In this sense, it is preferable to construct a minimal intermediate scheme, keeping only the mixing sets that are needed for matching the bare lattice Green’s functions to the corresponding MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS-renormalized Green’s functions (as obtained in dimensional regularization). In our study, we consider such a minimal scheme by using symmetry arguments to restrict the operators allowed to mix. Improving renormalization schemes on the lattice eliminates finite lattice spacing errors, which can come from different Dirac structures in Green’s functions under consideration. A way of removing artifacts from all Dirac structures is to consider a wider mixing pattern, where higher dimensional operators multiplied by the appropriate power of the lattice spacing can also mix with the operators under study. This mixing is only present for finite values of the lattice spacing, while it vanishes when taking the continuum limit. The higher-dimensional operators will be higher twist since (by Lorentz invariance) they must have the same spin (with the operators under study), but their dimension will be higher. The unwanted effects of finite lattice spacing errors and higher-twist contributions are not considered in the present study; they will be addressed in a future publication. On the other hand, it has been showncitebib.bib37 that the linear divergence in the lattice spacing a𝑎a is not fully eliminated when the RI/MOM scheme is used to renormalize a straight Wilson line of length z𝑧z. Ref. citebib.bib36 has shown that this residual linear divergence remains in the case of the staple-shaped operator. In this scenario, an alternative approach that one can adopt is the so-called ratio scheme as proposed for the quasi-PDF casecitebib.bib11, bib.bib38, bib.bib39. In this approach, one subtracts the Ultra-Violet (UV) divergences by taking the ratio with a suitable object at a fixed short distance, where perturbation theory applies. Different choices of suitable objects to be used in the ratio have been proposed in Ref. citebib.bib40. The authors of Refs. citebib.bib36, bib.bib32 use ratios of the matrix elements of the operator under study. As the divergences of the staple-shaped operator are independent of the longitudinal momentum of the state, one can use the matrix elements computed at different values of the momentum in order to cancel the divergences. Usually, matrix elements at zero momentum are chosen for the denominator, and such scheme has been namedcitebib.bib32 short distance ratio (SDR) scheme. As for the remaining divergences associated with the asymmetric staple-shaped link, one can cancel them by taking an appropriate ratio with the vacuum expectation value of a rectangular Wilson loopcitebib.bib41. However, we would like to stress that the SDR scheme is valid when operator mixing is absent or negligible. We check that in the case under study the mixing is indeed negligible and thus, one can employ the ratio scheme. Another option, as proposed in Ref. citebib.bib34, is to employ RI/MOM in the spirit of the SDR scheme by fixing the dimensions of the staple at a short perturbative range (we will call this scheme RI-short). The paper is organized as follows. Section discusses how TMDs can be accessed on a Euclidean lattice using the quasi-distribution approach. In Section, we study operator mixing using symmetry arguments. Section presents our lattice setup. The size of the mixing is examined in Section. In Sections, and we employ RI/MOM, SDR and RI-short schemes, respectively, by following the procedures described in Ref. citebib.bib36, bib.bib32, bib.bib34. In Section, we present the renormalized beam functions in the three renormalization schemes discussed here, and then show the corresponding results in the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme. The conclusions are presented in Section.
content_sections.II Quasi-TMDPDFs: A first-principle computation of TMDPDFs in the context of LaMET is more involved than collinear PDFs. The main obstacle is to correctly subtract the so-called rapidity divergences, which are associated with the soft gluon radiation of the infinitely long Wilson lines present in the staple-shaped operator. This subtraction is made through the use of a soft functioncitebib.bib42, bib.bib43. Different ways how to perform this subtraction in the context of LaMET can be found in Ref. citebib.bib44. In general, the TMD soft function involves two opposite light-like directions, and this makes a lattice calculation significantly challenging. In Refs. citebib.bib21, bib.bib41, the authors define a rapidity-independent reduced soft function Srsubscript𝑆𝑟Sr and show that it can be extracted from a form factor and the quasi-TMD wave function of a light meson. Using Srsubscript𝑆𝑟Sr, the rapidity scheme-independent TMDPDF fT​M​D​(x,b,μ,ζ)superscript𝑓𝑇𝑀𝐷𝑥𝑏𝜇𝜁fTMD(x,b,mu,zeta) can be written ascitebib.bib41, bib.bib15 fT​M​D​(x,b,μ,ζ)=H​(ζzμ2)​e−ln⁡(ζzζ)​K​(b,μ)​f​(x,b,μ,ζz)​Sr12​(b,μ)+𝒪​(ΛQ​C​D2ζz,M2(Pz)2,1b2​ζz)superscript𝑓𝑇𝑀𝐷𝑥𝑏𝜇𝜁𝐻subscript𝜁𝑧superscript𝜇2superscript𝑒subscript𝜁𝑧𝜁𝐾𝑏𝜇𝑓𝑥𝑏𝜇subscript𝜁𝑧superscriptsubscript𝑆𝑟12𝑏𝜇𝒪superscriptsubscriptΛ𝑄𝐶𝐷2subscript𝜁𝑧superscript𝑀2superscriptsuperscript𝑃𝑧21superscript𝑏2subscript𝜁𝑧beginsplitfTMD(x,b,mu,zeta)=Hleft(fraczetazmu2right),e-lnleft(fraczetazzetaright)K(b,mu)tildef(x,b,mu,zetaz),Srfrac12(b,mu)+Oleft(fracLambdaQCD2zetaz,fracM2(Pz)2,frac1b2zetazright)endsplit where f​(x,b,μ,ζz)𝑓𝑥𝑏𝜇subscript𝜁𝑧tildef(x,b,mu,zetaz) is the so-called quasi-TMDPDF for a nucleon with mass M𝑀M, x𝑥x is the longitudinal momentum fraction, and b𝑏b is a separation transverse to direction of the momentum Pzsuperscript𝑃𝑧Pz carried by the nucleon. The scale μ𝜇mu defines the renormalization scale and ζz=(2​x​Pz)2subscript𝜁𝑧superscript2𝑥superscript𝑃𝑧2zetaz=(2xPz)2 is the Collins-Soper scale of the quasi-TMDPDF, with ζ𝜁zeta being the scale for the light-cone correlation. The factor H​(ζzμ2)𝐻subscript𝜁𝑧superscript𝜇2Hleft(fraczetazmu2right) is the perturbative matching kernel that connects the TMDPDFs to the quasi-TMDPDFs, and K​(b,μ)𝐾𝑏𝜇K(b,mu) is the Collins-Soper kernel. The renormalization scheme in Eq. is left unspecified; usually, it is computed in the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme. The quasi-TMDPDF on the lattice is defined ascitebib.bib44, bib.bib20 f​(x,b,μ,ζz)=∫d​z2​π​e−i​z​ζz​PzEP​BΓ​(z,b,μ,Pz),𝑓𝑥𝑏𝜇subscript𝜁𝑧𝑑𝑧2𝜋superscript𝑒𝑖𝑧subscript𝜁𝑧superscript𝑃𝑧subscript𝐸𝑃subscript𝐵Γ𝑧𝑏𝜇superscript𝑃𝑧tildef(x,b,mu,zetaz)=intfracdz2pie-izzetazfracPzEPBGamma(z,b,mu,Pz), with BΓ​(z,b,μ,Pz)subscript𝐵Γ𝑧𝑏𝜇superscript𝑃𝑧BGamma(z,b,mu,Pz) the renormalized beam function. It is obtained from the bare beam function, B0,Γ​(z,b,L,Pz;1/a)subscript𝐵0Γ𝑧𝑏𝐿superscript𝑃𝑧1𝑎B0,Gamma(z,b,L,Pz;1/a), defined as the matrix element of the non-local staple-shaped quark bilinear operator, 𝒪Γ​(z,b,L)superscript𝒪Γ𝑧𝑏𝐿OGamma(z,b,L), of length L𝐿L: B0,Γ​(z,b,L,Pz;1/a)subscript𝐵0Γ𝑧𝑏𝐿superscript𝑃𝑧1𝑎displaystyle B0,Gamma(z,b,L,Pz;1/a) =displaystyle= ⟨N​(Pz)|𝒪Γ​(z,b,L)|N​(Pz)⟩quantum-operator-product𝑁superscript𝑃𝑧superscript𝒪Γ𝑧𝑏𝐿𝑁superscript𝑃𝑧displaystyle N(Pz)|OGamma(z,b,L)|N(Pz) where N​(Pz)𝑁superscript𝑃𝑧N(Pz) is a nucleon state with momentum boost of (0,0,Pz)00superscript𝑃𝑧(0,0,Pz), a𝑎a is the lattice spacing, 𝒲​(z,b,L;1/a)𝒲𝑧𝑏𝐿1𝑎W(z,b,L;1/a) is the staple-shaped Wilson line, and ψ​(z)𝜓𝑧psi(z) is the standard up and down quark doublet. In practice, we compute the flavor non-singlet combination u−d𝑢𝑑u-d, and hence the operator also includes a Pauli τ3subscript𝜏3tau3 matrix in flavor space. For the unpolarized TMDPDF, ΓΓGamma can be either γ0subscript𝛾0gamma0 or γ3subscript𝛾3gamma3 or, in general, a combination of the two. In this work, we show results for Γ=γ0Γsubscript𝛾0Gamma=gamma0, since we observe a better signal for this operator compared to Γ=γ3Γsubscript𝛾3Gamma=gamma3. γ0subscript𝛾0gamma0 was also used for the quasi-PDF case, in which case mixing is absent, while γ3subscript𝛾3gamma3 mixed with 𝟏11. In general, 𝒲​(z,b,L;1/a)𝒲𝑧𝑏𝐿1𝑎W(z,b,L;1/a) is given by: 𝒲​(z,b,L)=Wz​(0→,−L)​W⟂​(−L​z,b)​Wz​(−L​z+b​y,L+z)𝒲𝑧𝑏𝐿subscript𝑊𝑧→0𝐿subscript𝑊perpendicular-to𝐿𝑧𝑏subscript𝑊𝑧𝐿𝑧𝑏𝑦𝐿𝑧W(z,b,L)=Wz(vec0,-L)Wperp(-Lhatz,b)Wz(-Lhatz+bhaty,L+z), where we dropped the dependence on 1/a1𝑎1/a to keep the notation compact. content_sections.III Operator mixing through symmetry: In order to use symmetry arguments to determine which operators mix, we first organize the staple-shaped operator using all possible ways to connect the fermion fields. We show in Fig. all the distinct possibilities of connecting a quark and an antiquark field located at a spatial separation of (0,b,z)0𝑏𝑧(0,b,z), using an asymmetric staple-shaped Wilson line. Since we build the staples only in the y​z𝑦𝑧yz-plane, in the following discussion and in Fig. , we denote the coordinate space (t,x,y,z)𝑡𝑥𝑦𝑧(t,x,y,z) as only (y,z)𝑦𝑧(y,z). Without loss of generality, we can fix the quark field (q𝑞q) at (0,0)00(0,0). Then, the only possibilities for the position of the antiquark field (q¯¯𝑞barq) are (b,z)𝑏𝑧(b,z), (b,−z)𝑏𝑧(b,-z), (−b,z)𝑏𝑧(-b,z) and (−b,−z)𝑏𝑧(-b,-z). Here, b𝑏b and z𝑧z are strictly non-negative. We define the staple-shaped Wilson line connecting q𝑞q at (0,0)00(0,0) and q¯¯𝑞barq at (b,z)𝑏𝑧(b,z) as O++​(Γ)superscript𝑂absentΓO++(Gamma). The two plus signs denote the direction of separation of q¯¯𝑞barq from q𝑞q. The first sign is for the transverse (y𝑦haty) and the second sign for the longitudinal (z𝑧hatz) axes. The ΓΓGamma denotes the insertion gamma matrix as defined in Eq. . In a similar fashion, the staple-shaped operator in the cases of q¯¯𝑞barq being located at (b,−z)𝑏𝑧(b,-z), (−b,z)𝑏𝑧(-b,z) and (−b,−z)𝑏𝑧(-b,-z) can be defined as O+−​(Γ)superscript𝑂absentΓO+-(Gamma), O−+​(Γ)superscript𝑂absentΓO-+(Gamma) and O−−​(Γ)superscript𝑂absentΓO--(Gamma) respectively. A visual representation of these operators is shown in Fig. in black. Due to the asymmetric nature of the staple-shaped Wilson line, there are four more operators that are obtained from the charge conjugation of the above defined four. These are also shown in Fig. , but in red, with the charge-conjugated version of O±±​(Γ)superscript𝑂plus-or-minusabsentplus-or-minusΓOpmpm(Gamma) denoted by Oc±±​(Γ)subscriptsuperscript𝑂plus-or-minusabsentplus-or-minus𝑐ΓOpmpmc(Gamma) and quark/antiquark fields having exchanged positions. For the symmetric case (z=0𝑧0z=0), the charge-conjugated operators are redundant, since Oc++≡O−+subscriptsuperscript𝑂absent𝑐superscript𝑂absentO++cequiv O-+, Oc+−≡O−−subscriptsuperscript𝑂absent𝑐superscript𝑂absentO+-cequiv O--, Oc−+≡O++subscriptsuperscript𝑂absent𝑐superscript𝑂absentO-+cequiv O++ and Oc−−≡O+−subscriptsuperscript𝑂absent𝑐superscript𝑂absentO--cequiv O+-. We analyze the symmetry properties using generalized parity (𝒫F​α1,2superscriptsubscript𝒫𝐹𝛼12PFalpha1,2) and time reversal (𝒯F​α1,2superscriptsubscript𝒯𝐹𝛼12TFalpha1,2) transformations with discrete flavor rotation, as well as charge conjugation (𝒞𝒞C), for the fermion fields in the twisted mass basis, χ​(xα,x)𝜒subscript𝑥𝛼xchi(xalpha,x), and with the gauge link, U​(xα,x;α)𝑈subscript𝑥𝛼x𝛼U(xalpha,x;alpha), in some direction α𝛼alpha. The symmetry transformations are defined in Appendix A. The operators O±±​(Γ)superscript𝑂plus-or-minusabsentplus-or-minusΓOpmpm(Gamma), Oc±±​(Γ)subscriptsuperscript𝑂plus-or-minusabsentplus-or-minus𝑐ΓOpmpmc(Gamma) do not have definite properties with respect to the symmetries of the lattice action. Instead, one needs to consider their linear combinations, defined by (i​j​k​l)c=i⋅O+++j⋅O−−+k⋅O+−+l⋅O−++c⋅(i⋅Oc+++j⋅Oc−−+k⋅Oc+−+l⋅Oc−+),subscript𝑖𝑗𝑘𝑙𝑐⋅𝑖superscript𝑂absent⋅𝑗superscript𝑂absent⋅𝑘superscript𝑂absent⋅𝑙superscript𝑂absent⋅𝑐⋅𝑖subscriptsuperscript𝑂absent𝑐⋅𝑗subscriptsuperscript𝑂absent𝑐⋅𝑘subscriptsuperscript𝑂absent𝑐⋅𝑙subscriptsuperscript𝑂absent𝑐(ijkl)c=icdot O+++jcdot O--+kcdot O+-+lcdot O-++ccdotleft(icdot O++c+jcdot O--c+kcdot O+-c+lcdot O-+cright), with i,j,k,l=±1𝑖𝑗𝑘𝑙plus-or-minus1i,j,k,l=pm 1 denoting the signs of O++,O−−,O+−,O−+superscript𝑂absentsuperscript𝑂absentsuperscript𝑂absentsuperscript𝑂absentO++,O--,O+-,O-+, and c=±1𝑐plus-or-minus1c=pm 1 representing the relative sign of the charge-conjugated versions of O±±superscript𝑂plus-or-minusabsentplus-or-minusOpmpm. The only combinations that have definite symmetry properties are: (++++)cdisplaystyle(++++)c ≡(−−−−)c,displaystyleequiv(----)c, (+−+−)cdisplaystyle(+-+-)c ≡(−+−+)c,displaystyleequiv(-+-+)c, (++−−)cdisplaystyle(++--)c ≡(−−++)c,displaystyleequiv(--++)c, (+−−+)cdisplaystyle(+--+)c ≡(−++−)c,displaystyleequiv(-++-)c, i. e. the combinations with all signs i,j,k,l𝑖𝑗𝑘𝑙i,j,k,l reversed are equivalent from the point of view of symmetry transformations, with irrelevant global phase. As an example, we look at the symmetry properties of γ0subscript𝛾0gamma0 and γ0​γ3subscript𝛾0subscript𝛾3gamma0gamma3 given in Tables and, respectively, restricting ourselves here to the flavor non-singlet case, u−d𝑢𝑑u-d (τ3subscript𝜏3tau3 matrix in flavor space). The combinations of operators that mix are those which have all signs equal in the nine rows of Tables and. For example, the symmetry properties of (++++)c(++++)c for Γ=γ0Γsubscript𝛾0Gamma=gamma0 (second column of Table) are identical to the ones of the combination (+−−+)c(+--+)c for Γ=γ0​γ3Γsubscript𝛾0subscript𝛾3Gamma=gamma0gamma3 (last column of Table). Thus, we conclude that the following mixings occur: (++++)cdisplaystyle(++++)c;; with(+−−+)c,displaystyletextwith;;(+--+)c, (+−+−)cdisplaystyle(+-+-)c;; with(++−−)c,displaystyletextwith;;(++--)c, (++−−)cdisplaystyle(++--)c;; with(+−+−)c,displaystyletextwith;;(+-+-)c, (+−−+)cdisplaystyle(+--+)c;; with(++++)c,displaystyletextwith;;(++++)c, where the first combination in a pair pertains to Γ=γ0Γsubscript𝛾0Gamma=gamma0 and the second one to Γ=γ0​γ3Γsubscript𝛾0subscript𝛾3Gamma=gamma0gamma3. Additional mixings appear with Γ=γ0​γ2Γsubscript𝛾0subscript𝛾2Gamma=gamma0gamma2 and Γ=γ5​γ1Γsubscript𝛾5subscript𝛾1Gamma=gamma5gamma1, thus forming a quadruple of operators that mix, γ0,γ0​γ2,γ0​γ3,γ5​γ1subscript𝛾0subscript𝛾0subscript𝛾2subscript𝛾0subscript𝛾3subscript𝛾5subscript𝛾1leftgamma0,gamma0gamma2,gamma0gamma3,gamma5gamma1right. These are the only relevant mixings for the present work, which concerns Γ=γ0Γsubscript𝛾0Gamma=gamma0 for unpolarized TMDs. For the general case, the symmetry properties for all ΓΓGamma’s are summarized in Table in Appendix A. They imply that the possible mixing is between ΓΓGamma and Γ​γ2,Γ​γ3,Γ​γ2​γ3Γsubscript𝛾2Γsubscript𝛾3Γsubscript𝛾2subscript𝛾3leftGammagamma2,Gammagamma3,Gammagamma2gamma3right in the asymmetric staples case. In turn, symmetric staples restrict the mixing by eliminating always one member of the mixing quadruple, i. e. Γ=γ0,1,5Γsubscript𝛾015Gamma=gamma0,1,5 mix with Γ​γ2Γsubscript𝛾2Gammagamma2 and Γ​γ3Γsubscript𝛾3Gammagamma3, while Γ=γ2,3Γsubscript𝛾23Gamma=gamma2,3 form a triple γ2,γ3,γ2​γ3subscript𝛾2subscript𝛾3subscript𝛾2subscript𝛾3leftgamma2,gamma3,gamma2gamma3right of operators that mix. We point out that different mixing patterns have been considered in recent studies of other groups, including mixing among all 16 operators of different Dirac structurescitebib.bib27, or at least mixing in pairs (Γ,Γ​γ3ΓΓsubscript𝛾3Gamma,Gammagamma3)citebib.bib34. In our work, we choose to consider the minimal set of staple-shaped operators (of the same dimension) that are allowed to mix by the above-mentioned C, P, and T symmetries. We have not considered mixing with higher-dimensional operators allowed by Lorentz symmetry (see, e. g. , Ref. citebib.bib45 for the straight Wilson-line case), since it is power suppressed and not relevant when one takes the continuum limit a→0→𝑎0arightarrow 0. Also, in contrast to Ref. citebib.bib34, in our analysis, the staple line has been chosen to be in a 2-dimensional (D), and not 3D, plane in Euclidean space formed by the transverse (y𝑦haty) and longitudinal (z𝑧hatz) directions. In this respect, we end up with a basis of 8 (instead of 16) operators, which are eigenstates of C, P and T transformations. We also note that calculations in one-loop lattice perturbation theorycitebib.bib33 show a smaller mixing pattern compared to the symmetries; however, this cannot guarantee a reduced mixing in higher loops.
content_sections.IV Lattice Setup: For the lattice simulation, we use an Nf=2+1+1subscript𝑁𝑓211Nf=2+1+1 clover-improved twisted mass fermion ensemble of size 243×48superscript24348243times 48, produced by the Extended Twisted Mass Collaboration (ETMC)citebib.bib46. Our study is done using Nc​o​n​fsubscript𝑁𝑐𝑜𝑛𝑓Nconf configurations with Ns​r​csubscript𝑁𝑠𝑟𝑐Nsrc source positions for each configuration. To increase statistics, the boost is taken in all three directions, and both positive and negative. For each such direction of boost, the staple is then constructed in both the remaining transverse directions. This gives us 12 measurements (666 boost directions ×times 222 transverse directions) for each source position. The details of the lattice simulation are summarized inTable.
