𝒫𝒫cal P is the parity projector. In this work, we show results for a single source-sink separation of ts=10​asubscript𝑡𝑠10𝑎ts=10a and a longitudinal momentum of Pz=6​π/24​a∼1.7​GeVsuperscript𝑃𝑧6𝜋24𝑎similar-to1.7GeVPz=6pi/24asim 1.7,GeV. The 3-point function is constructed for the isovector combination u−d𝑢𝑑u-d, by inserting a τ3subscript𝜏3tau3 in flavor space. This choice ensures the elimination of the disconnected contributions and only connected diagrams need to be calculated. Momentum smearingcitebib.bib47 is applied to improve the signal for large boosts. It is observed that applying stout smearing to the gauge links used in construction of the staple also reduces the statistical errors. Here, we have applied 5 steps of stout smearing to the staple-shaped Wilson line. In Fig. we show the bare matrix elements as a function of z𝑧z at a fixed b/a=1𝑏𝑎1b/a=1 for different values of L𝐿L. These and the following results for the quasi-beam function have been symmetrized using the relation B0,Γ​(z,b,L,Pz)=B0,Γ†​(−z,−b,−L,Pz). subscript𝐵0Γ𝑧𝑏𝐿superscript𝑃𝑧superscriptsubscript𝐵0Γ†𝑧𝑏𝐿superscript𝑃𝑧B0,Gamma(z,b,L,Pz)=B0,Gammadagger(-z,-b,-L,Pz). To ensure that this property holds in our lattice simulation, we have used the staple-shaped operator O++superscript𝑂absentO++ on the left hand side of the above equation and Oc−−subscriptsuperscript𝑂absent𝑐O--c on the right hand side. Our results show a clear decay of the magnitude of the beam function as L𝐿L increases, as expectedcitebib.bib15. In Fig. we show the dependence of the bare beam function on b𝑏b. This is expected to decay as exp⁡(−L​V​(b))𝐿𝑉𝑏exp(-LV(b))citebib.bib15. Assuming a linear function for the potential V​(b)𝑉𝑏V(b), we also show a fit of exp⁡(c0+c1​b)subscript𝑐0subscript𝑐1𝑏exp(c0+c1b) to the lattice data at two different values of z/a𝑧𝑎z/a and a fixed L/a=10𝐿𝑎10L/a=10.
content_sections.V Non-perturbative renormalization: As in the case of the straight Wilson line, there are logarithmic and linear divergences associated with the length z𝑧z of the staple-shaped link. However, here there are two extra divergences: one, which is associated with the cusps of the staple, and one associated with the gluon exchange between the gauge links of length L→∞→𝐿Lrightarrowinfty, the so-called pinch-pole singularities. On the other hand, the finite transverse separation b𝑏b mitigates possible discretization effects associated with small z𝑧z separation, mainly in the non-perturbative region (large b𝑏b), which is the main interest of a lattice calculation. In the following, we will analyze three different ways to carry out the renormalization, namely the RI/MOM scheme, the short distance ratio scheme, and a modified version of the RI/MOM scheme, where the renormalization constants are computed at short distances.
content_sections.V Non-perturbative renormalization V.1 R: The RI/MOM schemecitebib.bib48 was first adapted for non-local operators employed in the quasi-distribution approach in Refs. citebib.bib35, bib.bib49. The RI/MOM renormalization constants Z𝒪R​Isubscriptsuperscript𝑍𝑅𝐼𝒪ZRIcal O are defined by the condition ZΓ​Γ′RI​(z,b,L,μ0;1/a)ZqRI​(μ0;1/a)​112​Tr​[Λ0Γ​(z,b,L,p;1/a)​Γ′ei​pz​z+i​p⟂​b]|p=μ0=1,evaluated-atsubscriptsuperscript𝑍RIΓsuperscriptΓ′𝑧𝑏𝐿subscript𝜇01𝑎subscriptsuperscript𝑍RI𝑞subscript𝜇01𝑎112Trdelimited-subscriptsuperscriptΛΓ0𝑧𝑏𝐿𝑝1𝑎superscriptΓ′superscript𝑒𝑖superscript𝑝𝑧𝑧𝑖subscript𝑝perpendicular-to𝑏𝑝subscript𝜇01fracZtextRIGammaGammaprime(z,b,L,mu0;1/a)ZtextRIq(mu0;1/a)frac112rm Trleft[fracLambdaGamma0(z,b,L,p;1/a)Gammaprimeeipzz+ipperpbright]Bigg|p=mu0=1,, where Λ0ΓsubscriptsuperscriptΛΓ0LambdaGamma0 is defined in terms of the amputated Green’s function Λ0Γ​(z,b,L,p;1/a)=Sq−1​GΓ​(z,b,L,p;1/a)​Sq−1,subscriptsuperscriptΛΓ0𝑧𝑏𝐿𝑝1𝑎superscriptsubscript𝑆𝑞1superscript𝐺Γ𝑧𝑏𝐿𝑝1𝑎superscriptsubscript𝑆𝑞1LambdaGamma0(z,b,L,p;1/a)=Sq-1GGamma(z,b,L,p;1/a)Sq-1,, with Sqsubscript𝑆𝑞Sq the off-shell quark propagator. The Green’s function is calculated as GΓ​(z,b,p,L;1/a)=⟨q​(p)|𝒪Γ​(z,b,p,L;1/a)|q​(p)⟩. superscript𝐺Γ𝑧𝑏𝑝𝐿1𝑎quantum-operator-product𝑞𝑝superscript𝒪Γ𝑧𝑏𝑝𝐿1𝑎𝑞𝑝GGamma(z,b,p,L;1/a)= q(p)|cal OGamma(z,b,p,L;1/a)|q(p),. Because GΓsuperscript𝐺ΓGGamma, and thus Λ0ΓsuperscriptsubscriptΛ0ΓLambda0Gamma, have the same divergences as B0,Γsubscript𝐵0ΓB0,Gamma, all the divergences, in principle, cancel in the renormalization procedure. ZqRIsubscriptsuperscript𝑍RI𝑞ZtextRIq is the quark wave function renormalization defined as ZqRI​(μ0;1/a)=112​Tr​[(Sq​(p;1/a))−1​SqBorn​(p)]|p2=μ02,subscriptsuperscript𝑍RI𝑞subscript𝜇01𝑎evaluated-at112Trdelimited-superscriptsubscript𝑆𝑞𝑝1𝑎1superscriptsubscript𝑆𝑞Born𝑝superscript𝑝2superscriptsubscript𝜇02ZtextRIq(mu0;1/a)=frac112rm Trleft[(Sq(p;1/a))-1,Sqrm Born(p)right]Bigg|p2=mu02,, and the corresponding renormalized beam function is then BΓRI​(z,b,μ0,Pz)=∑Γ′ZΓ​Γ′RI​(z,b,μ0;1/a)​B0,Γ′​(z,b,Pz;1/a). subscriptsuperscript𝐵RIΓ𝑧𝑏subscript𝜇0superscript𝑃𝑧subscriptsuperscriptΓ′subscriptsuperscript𝑍RIΓsuperscriptΓ′𝑧𝑏subscript𝜇01𝑎subscript𝐵0superscriptΓ′𝑧𝑏superscript𝑃𝑧1𝑎BtextRIGamma(z,b,mu0,Pz)=sumGammaprimeZtextRIGammaGammaprime(z,b,mu0;1/a)B0,Gammaprime(z,b,Pz;1/a),. Notice that the relation between the bare and renormalized beam functions involves, in principle, a 16×16161616times 16 matrix, if one considers all possible mixing among the full set of Dirac structures. The case of interest in this work is Γ=γ0Γsubscript𝛾0Gamma=gamma0, computed using the lattice ensemble of Table. As pointed out in Section , γ0superscript𝛾0gamma0 mixes with γ0​γ2subscript𝛾0subscript𝛾2gamma0gamma2, γ0​γ3subscript𝛾0subscript𝛾3gamma0gamma3, and γ5​γ1subscript𝛾5subscript𝛾1gamma5gamma1. The renormalization factors for the diagonal and off-diagonal cases are shown in Figs. and, respectively. We set the renormalization scale 4-vector μ0subscript𝜇0mu0 equal to 2​π​(6+0.5Nt,3Ns,3Ns,3Ns)2𝜋60.5subscript𝑁𝑡3subscript𝑁𝑠3subscript𝑁𝑠3subscript𝑁𝑠2pileft(frac6+0.5Nt,frac3Ns,frac3Ns,frac3Nsright), where Ns=24​asubscript𝑁𝑠24𝑎Ns=24a, Nt=2​Nssubscript𝑁𝑡2subscript𝑁𝑠Nt=2Ns, and anti-periodic boundary conditions have been employed in the time direction. We have chosen an isotropic momentum in the spatial directions and “democratic” momentum, which obeys the criterion ∑ρsin4⁡(a​pρ/2)/(∑ρsin2⁡(a​pρ/2))2|p=μ0<0.3evaluated-atsubscript𝜌superscript4𝑎subscript𝑝𝜌2superscriptsubscript𝜌superscript2𝑎subscript𝑝𝜌22𝑝subscript𝜇00.3sumrhosin4(aprho/2)/(sumrhosin2(aprho/2))2|p=mu0<0.3, in order to reduce Lorentz-non-invariant contributions in the vertex functions. Also, the selected momentum lies in a perturbative region, where perturbation theory is reliable and, at the same time, lattice artifacts are under control. A more detailed study using different values of μ0subscript𝜇0mu0 will be presented in a future extension of our study. Using this set-up, we observe that the contributions from the off-diagonal mixing terms are ≲4%less-than-or-similar-toabsentpercent4lesssim 4% of the diagonal contribution for all z𝑧z when b=1​a, 2​a𝑏1𝑎2𝑎b=1a,,2a. It is interesting to examine how the relative size of the off-diagonal to the diagonal renormalization factors changes as b𝑏b increases, as the primary focus of lattice computation of TMDPDFs is the the non-perturbative region in the transverse separation b𝑏b. We show in Fig. the renormalization factors as a function of b𝑏b for the diagonal and off-diagonal contributions. We see a contribution of ≲7−8%less-than-or-similar-toabsent7percent8lesssim 7-8% up to b=6​a𝑏6𝑎b=6a for the off-diagonal terms. Although their size shows a possible tendency of growth with increasing b𝑏b, this growth seems to be only moderate. We notice that the authors of Ref. citebib.bib27 considered the entire set of Dirac structures for the operator mixing, and observe similar results to ours, if we take from Ref. citebib.bib27 only the results from the operators that are allowed to mix with γ0subscript𝛾0gamma0, according to our symmetry arguments. For example, their result for the contribution from γ5​γ1subscript𝛾5subscript𝛾1gamma5gamma1 is much larger than that of γ0​γ2subscript𝛾0subscript𝛾2gamma0gamma2 and γ0​γ3subscript𝛾0subscript𝛾3gamma0gamma3, and there is a steady increase going to larger b𝑏b values. For the values of b𝑏b we use in this work, the magnitude of mixing for these operators found in Ref. citebib.bib27 is also comparable to our findings. However, considering the dominant diagonal renormalization factors, it seems that there are remaining divergences related to large valus of z𝑧z and b𝑏b, as also observed in Ref. citebib.bib36. Combined with the fact that the bare matrix element decays exponentially with b𝑏bcitebib.bib15, it is significantly hard to renormalize for large transverse separations using this scheme. However, in the small b𝑏b region where we can extract information using RI/MOM, it can be confirmed that the mixing is negligible. As an example, we show in Fig. the renormalized beam function for the b=1​a𝑏1𝑎b=1a case, with ++’s denoting the RI/MOM procedure using the full mixing matrix and with the ×times’s denoting the case of only taking into account the diagonal contribution. Considering that including the mixing has a negligible effect, one can safely ignore operator mixing for these values of b𝑏b.
content_sections.V Non-perturbative renormalization V.2 T: According to Figs. and, mixing with different operators can be neglected at least up to b/a≲6less-than-or-similar-to𝑏𝑎6b/alesssim 6, and we can assume that the renormalization of the staple-shaped link is multiplicative. This justifies the approach taken in Refs. citebib.bib36, bib.bib32. We first note that the vacuum expectation value of a rectangular Wilson loop ZEsubscript𝑍𝐸ZE with sides 2​L+z2𝐿𝑧2L+z and b𝑏b, ZE​(b,2​L+z;1/a)=13​Tr⁡⟨0|𝒲​(b;2​L+z)​W⟂​(x+b;b)∣0⟩,subscript𝑍𝐸𝑏2𝐿𝑧1𝑎13Trconditional0𝒲𝑏2𝐿𝑧subscript𝑊perpendicular-to𝑥𝑏𝑏0ZE(b,2L+z;1/a)=frac13operatornameTr 0|cal W(b;2L+z)Wperp(x+b;b)|0, is, by construction, a product of the staple-shaped gauge link, as defined in Eq. , and its reflection. Therefore, ZEsubscript𝑍𝐸sqrtZE has the same divergences as that of the staple-shaped gauge link. Thus, it should cancel the pinch-pole singularity associated with the length L𝐿L of the staple, as well as the divergences associated with the cusps. Furthermore, as the sides of ZEsubscript𝑍𝐸ZE are also dependent on the longitudinal displacement z𝑧z, the exponential divergence associated with z𝑧z present in the staple-shaped link must also be cancelled if an appropriated ratio is taken, namely the one proposed in Refs. citebib.bib21, bib.bib36: BΓ​(z,b,Pz;1/a)=limL→∞B0,Γ​(z,b,L,Pz;1/a)ZE​(b,2​L+z;1/a)subscript𝐵Γ𝑧𝑏superscript𝑃𝑧1𝑎subscript→𝐿subscript𝐵0Γ𝑧𝑏𝐿superscript𝑃𝑧1𝑎subscript𝑍𝐸𝑏2𝐿𝑧1𝑎BGamma(z,b,Pz;1/a)=limLrightarrowinftyfracB0,Gamma(z,b,L,Pz;1/a)sqrtZE(b,2L+z;1/a) To illustrate this point, we show in Fig. the beam function as a function of L𝐿L, for a fixed z=2​a𝑧2𝑎z=2a and two values of b𝑏b, before and after dividing by ZEsubscript𝑍𝐸sqrtZE. The cancellation of the divergences related to the length of the Wilson line is explicitly shown. This ratio, thus, takes care of the divergence associated with the length L𝐿L and width b𝑏b of the staple-shaped operator. After dividing the beam function by the square root of ZEsubscript𝑍𝐸ZE, the only remaining divergences are the UV divergences associated with the quark field and its end-points connecting to the gauge links. As such, they can be cancelled by taking ratios, as they have a multiplicative naturecitebib.bib26, bib.bib50. Hence, one can define BΓS​D​R​(z,z0,b,b0,Pz)=ZS​D​R​(z0,b0;1/a)​BΓ​(z,b,Pz;1/a),subscriptsuperscript𝐵𝑆𝐷𝑅Γ𝑧subscript𝑧0𝑏subscript𝑏0superscript𝑃𝑧superscript𝑍𝑆𝐷𝑅subscript𝑧0subscript𝑏01𝑎subscript𝐵Γ𝑧𝑏superscript𝑃𝑧1𝑎BSDRGamma(z,z0,b,b0,Pz)=ZSDR(z0,b0;1/a),BGamma(z,b,Pz;1/a),, where, ZS​D​R​(z0,b0;1/a)=1BΓ​(z=z0,b=b0,Pz=0;1/a). superscript𝑍𝑆𝐷𝑅subscript𝑧0subscript𝑏01𝑎1subscript𝐵Γformulae-sequence𝑧subscript𝑧0formulae-sequence𝑏subscript𝑏0superscript𝑃𝑧01𝑎ZSDR(z0,b0;1/a)=frac1BGamma(z=z0,b=b0,Pz=0;1/a),. Because the remaining divergences are independent of the length of the Wilson line, one is free to choose z0subscript𝑧0z0 and b0subscript𝑏0b0. In order to connect these quantities to the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme via a perturbative scheme conversion, z0subscript𝑧0z0 and b0subscript𝑏0b0 should be small enough for perturbation theory to be valid. However, the use of small values for both z0subscript𝑧0z0 and b0subscript𝑏0b0 can introduce sizable discretization errors in the renormalization factors, which can affect the validity of the SDR scheme. To address this issue, different approaches can be employed in order to reduce finite lattice-spacing errors from the non-perturbative data for all values of the staple lengths b/a𝑏𝑎b/a and z/a𝑧𝑎z/a. Ideally, the elimination of discretization errors requires calculations of physical matrix elements at different finite values of the lattice spacing a𝑎a and an extrapolation a→0→𝑎0arightarrow 0. When data for multiple values of a𝑎a are not available, a number of different approaches can be employed in order to reduce discretization errors at each lattice spacing. A standard method is to apply an improved discretization, in both the action and the operators under study, using the Symanzik-improvement programcitebib.bib51, bib.bib52. Another way to reduce this kind of systematic error from a lattice calculation is to subtract one-loop artifacts employing lattice perturbation theory to all orders in a𝑎a, from the non-perturbative vertex functions calculated in lattice simulations. Our group has successfully applied this method to the renormalization of local quark bilinear operatorscitebib.bib53, bib.bib54, bib.bib55, and more recently to the renormalization of non-local straight Wilson-line operators for quasi-PDFscitebib.bib56. These studies have provided a useful feedback on the effectiveness of artifacts in the renormalization factors for different ranges of the scales entering the renormalization procedure. Since this is our first non-perturbative study considering non-local staple-shaped operators we do not consider finite-a𝑎a errors, but we intend to apply the method of subtracting one-loop artifacts in a future extension of our study. The renormalized matrix elements are converted to the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme using perturbation theory. We have computed the vertex, sail, and tadpole one-loop diagrams for external quark states with a general momentum pzsuperscript𝑝𝑧pz. For pz→0→superscript𝑝𝑧0pzrightarrow 0, we obtain ZMS¯,S​D​R​(z0,b0,μ0)=1+αs​CF2​π​(12+32​ln⁡(b02+z024​e−2​γE​μ02)−2​z0b0​arctan​z0b0),superscript𝑍¯MS𝑆𝐷𝑅subscript𝑧0subscript𝑏0subscript𝜇01subscript𝛼𝑠subscript𝐶𝐹2𝜋1232superscriptsubscript𝑏02superscriptsubscript𝑧024superscript𝑒2subscript𝛾𝐸superscriptsubscript𝜇022subscript𝑧0subscript𝑏0arctansubscript𝑧0subscript𝑏0ZoverlinetextMS,SDR(z0,b0,mu0)=1+fracalphasCF2pileft(frac12+frac32lnleft(fracb02+z024e-2gammaEmu02right)-2fracz0b0textrmarctanfracz0b0right),, which agrees with Eq. of Ref. citebib.bib36. Note that this factor equals BΓMS¯​(z=z0,b=b0,Pz=0,μ0)superscriptsubscript𝐵Γ¯MSformulae-sequence𝑧subscript𝑧0formulae-sequence𝑏subscript𝑏0superscript𝑃𝑧0subscript𝜇0BGammaoverlinetextMS(z=z0,b=b0,Pz=0,mu0). Details of this calculation for a general external momentum will be presented in Ref. citebib.bib57. The renormalized beam function in the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme is then given by BΓMS¯​(z,b,Pz)=ZMS¯​(z0,b0,μ0)​BΓS​D​R​(z,z0,b,b0,Pz). subscriptsuperscript𝐵¯MSΓ𝑧𝑏superscript𝑃𝑧superscript𝑍¯MSsubscript𝑧0subscript𝑏0subscript𝜇0subscriptsuperscript𝐵𝑆𝐷𝑅Γ𝑧subscript𝑧0𝑏subscript𝑏0superscript𝑃𝑧BoverlinetextMSGamma(z,b,Pz)=ZoverlinetextMS(z0,b0,mu0)BSDRGamma(z,z0,b,b0,Pz). Results for BMS¯​(z,b,Pz)superscript𝐵¯MS𝑧𝑏superscript𝑃𝑧BoverlinetextMS(z,b,Pz) are presented in Section, where we also discuss the cancellation of the z0,b0subscript𝑧0subscript𝑏0z0,b0 dependence in Eq. .
content_sections.V Non-perturbative renormalization V.3 S: As discussed in Section, the usual RI scheme may be problematic at large z𝑧z and b𝑏b as the magnitude of the ZRIsuperscript𝑍RIZtextRI factors grows exponentially. Also, as shown in Refs. citebib.bib37, bib.bib36, the usual RI scheme may still contain a residual linear divergence, which may not be properly canceled. On the other hand, as discussed in Section , the ZEsubscript𝑍𝐸sqrtZE factor cancels all divergences in z𝑧z and b𝑏b present in the staple. Hence, we can define a vertex function that is free of such divergences, ΛΓ​(z,b,p;1/a)=Λ0Γ​(z,b,p;1/a)ZE​(b,2​L+z;1/a). superscriptΛΓ𝑧𝑏𝑝1𝑎subscriptsuperscriptΛΓ0𝑧𝑏𝑝1𝑎subscript𝑍𝐸𝑏2𝐿𝑧1𝑎LambdaGamma(z,b,p;1/a)=fracLambdaGamma0(z,b,p;1/a)sqrtZE(b,2L+z;1/a). Because the divergences related to the lengths of the Wilson line have been removed, we can compute the renormalization factors as in Section at some fixed z0,b0subscript𝑧0subscript𝑏0z0,b0citebib.bib34 ZΓ​Γ′RI−short​(z0,b0,μ0;1/a)ZqRI​(μ0;1/a)​112​Tr​[ΛΓ​(z,b,p;1/a)​Γ′ei​pz​z+i​b​p⟂]|p=μ0,z=z0,b=b0=1. evaluated-atsubscriptsuperscript𝑍RIshortΓsuperscriptΓ′subscript𝑧0subscript𝑏0subscript𝜇01𝑎subscriptsuperscript𝑍RI𝑞subscript𝜇01𝑎112Trdelimited-superscriptΛΓ𝑧𝑏𝑝1𝑎superscriptΓ′superscript𝑒𝑖superscript𝑝𝑧𝑧𝑖𝑏subscript𝑝perpendicular-toformulae-sequence𝑝subscript𝜇0formulae-sequence𝑧subscript𝑧0𝑏subscript𝑏01displaystylefracZtextRI-textshortGammaGammaprime(z0,b0,mu0;1/a)ZtextRIq(mu0;1/a)frac112rm Trleft[fracLambdaGamma(z,b,p;1/a)Gammaprimeeipzz+ibpperpright]Bigg|p=mu0,z=z0,b=b0=1,. The ZRI−shortsuperscript𝑍RIshortZtextRI-textshort factor defined at fixed z0subscript𝑧0z0 and b0subscript𝑏0b0 is used to renormalize the bare Bγ0subscript𝐵subscript𝛾0Bgamma0 defined at arbitrary values of z𝑧z and b𝑏b. We have labeled this procedure as RI-short. In principle, the vertex function in the standard RI/MOM could also be modified by taking its ratio with ZEsubscript𝑍𝐸sqrtZE. This would reduce the growth of the Z𝑍Z-factors with increasing b𝑏b. However, when combining ZRIsuperscript𝑍RIZtextRI with the bare Bγ0subscript𝐵subscript𝛾0Bgamma0, the ZEsubscript𝑍𝐸sqrtZE factors cancel each other, and therefore ZEsubscript𝑍𝐸sqrtZE has no effect on the renormalized matrix elements. On the other hand, the ZEsubscript𝑍𝐸sqrtZE factor appearing in the vertex function of the RI-short scheme is defined at fixed values of z𝑧z and b𝑏b, contrary to the ZEsubscript𝑍𝐸sqrtZE factor appearing in the bare Bγ0subscript𝐵subscript𝛾0Bgamma0. Hence the cancellation of the ZEsubscript𝑍𝐸ZE factors do not happen in the RI-short scheme. As in Section, we choose the pair z0,b0subscript𝑧0subscript𝑏0z0,b0 to be in the perturbative region in order to make the perturbative conversion to the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme reliable. Moreover, the study of possible lattice artifacts associated with the use of small values of z0/asubscript𝑧0𝑎z0/a and b0/asubscript𝑏0𝑎b0/a will be considered in future extensions of our study by using one-loop lattice perturbation theory. The corresponding renormalized beam function in this scheme is then given by BΓRI−short​(z,z0,b,b0,μ0,Pz)=∑Γ′[Z𝒪RI−short​(z0,b0,μ0;1/a)]Γ​Γ′​BΓ′​(z,b,Pz;1/a). subscriptsuperscript𝐵RIshortΓ𝑧subscript𝑧0𝑏subscript𝑏0subscript𝜇0superscript𝑃𝑧subscriptsuperscriptΓ′subscriptdelimited-subscriptsuperscript𝑍RIshort𝒪subscript𝑧0subscript𝑏0subscript𝜇01𝑎ΓsuperscriptΓ′subscript𝐵superscriptΓ′𝑧𝑏superscript𝑃𝑧1𝑎BtextRI-textshortGamma(z,z0,b,b0,mu0,Pz)=sumGammaprimeleft[ZtextRI-textshortcal O(z0,b0,mu0;1/a)right]GammaGammaprimeBGammaprime(z,b,Pz;1/a). For illustration, we show in Fig the renormalization factors Z𝒪RI−short​(z0,b0,μ0;1/a)subscriptsuperscript𝑍RIshort𝒪subscript𝑧0subscript𝑏0subscript𝜇01𝑎ZtextRI-textshortcal O(z0,b0,mu0;1/a) for z0=0, 1​asubscript𝑧001𝑎z0=0,,1a, b0=1​asubscript𝑏01𝑎b0=1a and μ0=2​πa​(6+0.548,324,324,324)subscript𝜇02𝜋𝑎60.548324324324mu0=frac2pialeft(frac6+0.548,frac324,frac324,frac324right). We also show the off-diagonal renormalization factors, which, in principle, mix with γ0superscript𝛾0gamma0. As expected, they are also independent of L𝐿L and can be omitted in the full calculation as their contribution is negligible as compared to the diagonal contribution. Finally, we convert BΓRI−shortsubscriptsuperscript𝐵RIshortΓBtextRI-textshortGamma to the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme using one-loop perturbation theory, BΓMS¯​(z,b,μ0,Pz)=∑Γ′[Z𝒪MS¯,RI−short​(z0,b0,μ0)]Γ​Γ′​BΓ′RI−short​(z,z0,b,b0,μ0,Pz). subscriptsuperscript𝐵¯MSΓ𝑧𝑏subscript𝜇0superscript𝑃𝑧subscriptsuperscriptΓ′subscriptdelimited-subscriptsuperscript𝑍¯MSRIshort𝒪subscript𝑧0subscript𝑏0subscript𝜇0ΓsuperscriptΓ′subscriptsuperscript𝐵RIshortsuperscriptΓ′𝑧subscript𝑧0𝑏subscript𝑏0subscript𝜇0superscript𝑃𝑧BoverlinetextMSGamma(z,b,mu0,Pz)=sumGammaprimeleft[ZoverlinetextMS,textRI-textshortcal O(z0,b0,mu0)right]GammaGammaprimeBtextRI-textshortGammaprime(z,z0,b,b0,mu0,Pz),. The conversion matrix ZOMS¯,RI−shortsuperscriptsubscript𝑍𝑂¯MSRIshortZOoverlinetextMS,textRI-textshort is calculated in dimensional regularization for arbitrary values of the momentum scale μ0subscript𝜇0mu0. Explicit expressions for all Γ,Γ′ΓsuperscriptΓ′Gamma,Gammaprime are given in Ref. citebib.bib57. Similar perturbative studies can be found in Refs. citebib.bib33, bib.bib26. A comparison between the conversion factor in RI-short and the SDR scheme is shown in Fig. for the case Γ=Γ′=γ0ΓsuperscriptΓ′subscript𝛾0Gamma=Gammaprime=gamma0. We observe that the real parts are compatible between the two schemes, while the RI-short scheme shows a non-zero imaginary part, in contrast to the SDR scheme. This is a consequence of using a non-zero momentum scale μ0subscript𝜇0mu0 in the RI-short scheme, while SDR is defined at zero momentum. Note that specific choices of μ0subscript𝜇0mu0 in RI-short can lead to a vanishing imaginary part in the renormalization factors. In particular, by setting to zero the two momentum components parallel to the staple segments, the one-loop expression for the renormalization factors results into a vanishing imaginary part. However, such choice of momentum gives rise to unwanted Lorentz non-invariant contributions in the non-perturbative calculations. Thus, in our study we follow the common practice of employing democratic momentum with reduced Lorentz non-invariant contributions at the cost of introducing imaginary part in the renormalization factors.
content_sections.VI Results: In this Section, we present the renormalized beam functions in the 3 renormalization schemes discussed in Section, as well as the corresponding results in the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme. In Fig. , we show the real and imaginary parts of Bγ0​(z,b,μ0,Pz)subscript𝐵subscript𝛾0𝑧𝑏subscript𝜇0superscript𝑃𝑧Bgamma0(z,b,mu0,Pz) as a function of z𝑧z for two values of the transverse separation, b/a=1,2𝑏𝑎12b/a=1,2. For the real parts, the schemes agree within errors for |z/a|⪅6𝑧𝑎6|z/a|lessapprox 6. For |z/a|⪆6greater-than-or-approximately-equals𝑧𝑎6|z/a|gtrapprox 6, Bγ0S​D​Rsubscriptsuperscript𝐵𝑆𝐷𝑅subscript𝛾0BSDRgamma0 and Bγ0RI−shortsubscriptsuperscript𝐵RIshortsubscript𝛾0BtextRI-textshortgamma0 are consistent, while Bγ0RIsubscriptsuperscript𝐵RIsubscript𝛾0BtextRIgamma0 becomes increasingly more negative with increasingly larger errors. This discrepant behaviour between the RI-MOM and the RI-short and SDR schemes indicates the existence of a residual linear divergence at large |z|𝑧|z| in the standard RI/MOM, as observed incitebib.bib36. For the imaginary parts, we observe a similar behavior. Namely, the SDR and the RI-short schemes are almost identical for any |z/a|𝑧𝑎|z/a| for the two values of b/a𝑏𝑎b/a considered. For the usual RI/MOM, we observe deviations not only for |z/a|⪆6greater-than-or-approximately-equals𝑧𝑎6|z/a|gtrapprox 6 but also for most of the |z/a|𝑧𝑎|z/a| region with an increase in errors with increasing |z/a|𝑧𝑎|z/a|. The conversion to the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme is done using one-loop perturbation theory for all three schemes considered. For the two cases where the renormalization factors are fixed at a short perturbative scale, we use z0/a=b0/a=1subscript𝑧0𝑎subscript𝑏0𝑎1z0/a=b0/a=1 when computing BΓ′RI−short​(z,z0,b,b0,μ0,Pz)subscriptsuperscript𝐵RIshortsuperscriptΓ′𝑧subscript𝑧0𝑏subscript𝑏0subscript𝜇0superscript𝑃𝑧BtextRI-textshortGammaprime(z,z0,b,b0,mu0,Pz) and BΓS​D​R​(z,z0,b,b0,Pz)subscriptsuperscript𝐵𝑆𝐷𝑅Γ𝑧subscript𝑧0𝑏subscript𝑏0superscript𝑃𝑧BSDRGamma(z,z0,b,b0,Pz). Since the final results in the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme should be independent of the values we choose for the z0,b0subscript𝑧0subscript𝑏0z0,b0 pair, we examine the stability of our results on the choice of the values for this pair. To this end, we show in Fig. the product of the renormalization factors for each scheme with the corresponding conversion factors to the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme, Zγ0MS¯,S​D​R​Zγ0S​D​Rsubscriptsuperscript𝑍¯MS𝑆𝐷𝑅subscript𝛾0subscriptsuperscript𝑍𝑆𝐷𝑅subscript𝛾0ZoverlinetextMS,SDRgamma0ZSDRgamma0 and Zγ0MS¯,RI​Zγ0RI−shortsubscriptsuperscript𝑍¯MSRIsubscript𝛾0subscriptsuperscript𝑍RIshortsubscript𝛾0ZoverlinetextMS,textRIgamma0ZtextRI-textshortgamma0. These products should be independent on the choice of values of z0,b0subscript𝑧0subscript𝑏0z0,b0 as long as the one-loop perturbative calculation is a reliable approximation. We notice that these products are equal to the renormalization factors in the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme in the absence of mixing. For the ratio scheme, there is a 10%−20%percent10percent2010%-20% correction when going from b0/a=1subscript𝑏0𝑎1b0/a=1 to b0/a=2subscript𝑏0𝑎2b0/a=2 for the three values of z0subscript𝑧0z0 considered here, with the correction increasing for larger values of z0subscript𝑧0z0. This indicates a limitation on the use of one-loop perturbation theory to perform the conversion to the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme already at a transverse separation as low as b/a=2𝑏𝑎2b/a=2. As a consequence, 𝒪​(αs2)𝒪superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠2O(alphas2) corrections cannot be disregarded. For the z0subscript𝑧0z0 dependence, the choice of z0/a=0subscript𝑧0𝑎0z0/a=0 and z0/a=1subscript𝑧0𝑎1z0/a=1 is nearly equivalent within one sigma error. For z/a=2𝑧𝑎2z/a=2, the discrepancy grows, especially for b/a=2𝑏𝑎2b/a=2. For the RI-short scheme, the situation is not significantly changed. From this discussion, we conclude that our choice for the pair z0,b0subscript𝑧0subscript𝑏0z0,b0 is a likely safe region to fix the short perturbative scale. We show in Fig. the renormalized MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS matrix elements computed in the ratio and the RI-short schemes at Pz≃1.7similar-to-or-equalssuperscript𝑃𝑧1.7Pzsimeq 1.7 GeV. The imaginary part of the matrix elements are in full agreement, the same happening in the the real part, for large z𝑧z values. In the small z𝑧z region of the real part there is a tendency to discrepancy, as already observed in the intermediate schemes shown in Fig. , although they agree within one sigma error.
content_sections.VII Conclusion: In this work, we have addressed the non-perturbative renormalization of the asymmetric staple-shaped quark bilinear operators. We used symmetry arguments to restrict the possible mixing between the operators, and we found that mixing is allowed within sets of four operators. We then employed the RI/MOM scheme to estimate the importance of mixing coming from the non-diagonal terms, and we found that they can be neglected up to transverse separations of at least 6​a6𝑎6a. This result justifies the use of multiplicative renormalization for the asymmetric staple-shaped operator. Based on this conclusion, we computed the renormalization factors in the SDR scheme, and in the RI-short scheme, where the renormalization factors are computed at short distances. We found that both schemes are nearly equivalent over all the |z|𝑧|z| region considered. Finally, we converted both schemes to the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme and presented the corresponding results for the renormalized beam function. Given our previous computation of the Collins-Soper kernel and of the soft functioncitebib.bib24, the next natural step will be to compute the TMDPDFs themselves over a wide range of b𝑏b, and we plan to present results for them in the near future. sorted_papers.title: Transverse Momentum Distributions from Lattice QCD without Wilson Lines
sorted_papers.abstract:
The transverse-momentum-dependent distributions (TMDs), which are defined by
gauge-invariant 3D parton correlators with staple-shaped lightlike Wilson
lines, can be calculated from quark and gluon correlators fixed in the Coulomb
gauge on a Euclidean lattice. These quantities can be expressed
gauge-invariantly as the correlators of Coulomb-gauge-dressed fields, which
reduce to the standard TMD correlators under principal-value prescription in
the infinite boost limit. In the framework of Large-Momentum Effective Theory,
a quasi-TMD defined from such correlators in a large-momentum hadron state can
be matched to the TMD via a factorization formula, whose exact form is derived
using Soft Collinear Effective Theory and verified at one-loop order. Compared
to the currently used gauge-invariant correlators, this new method can
substantially improve statistical precision and simplify renormalization for
the time-reversal-even TMDs, which will greatly enhance the predicative power
of lattice QCD in the non-perturbative region.

qualitative: The transverse-momentum-dependent distributions (TMDs) can be computed on a Euclidean lattice using gauge-invariant correlators defined in the Coulomb gauge, avoiding the need for explicit Wilson lines.

The Coulomb-gauge (CG) quasi-TMD is defined via correlators of Coulomb-gauge-dressed quark fields, which are gauge-invariant and reduce to standard TMDs in the infinite boost limit under a principal-value prescription.

The gauge-invariant dressing of the quark field, ΨC(b) = UC(b)ψ(b), is constructed via a gauge transformation that rotates the gauge field into the Coulomb gauge, with UC(b) determined order-by-order in the strong coupling g.
In the Coulomb gauge (∇→⋅A→=0), the dressing factors ωm vanish for all m, simplifying the correlator to the standard CG form without non-local Wilson-line contributions.

Under an infinite Lorentz boost along the z-direction, the dressing factor UC(b) reduces to a path-ordered lightlike Wilson line 𝒲±n†(b), leading to the standard TMD correlator involving a lightlike Wilson line.

The orientation of the lightlike Wilson line (determined by boundary conditions at bz=±∞) affects time-reversal-odd TMDs (e.g., Sivers function), but not time-reversal-even TMDs or the factorization formula for quasi-TMDs.
For TMDs with time-reversal odd components, an additional transverse gauge link 𝒲⟂ is required at infinity to ensure full gauge invariance, though it is not essential for the current framework.

The renormalization of the CG quasi-TMD involves only the quark wave function renormalization, which is multiplicative, simplifying the renormalization procedure compared to standard gauge-invariant correlators.
This method improves statistical precision and simplifies renormalization for time-reversal-even TMDs, enhancing the predictive power of lattice QCD in the non-perturbative regime.
quantitative: CG quasi-TMD definition: B(x,b⟂,μ,Pz) = Pz ∫_{-∞}^{∞} dbz/(2π) e^{i x Pz bz} × (1/(2 Pt)) ⟨P| ψ¯(b) γt ψ |∇→⋅A→=0 |P⟩
Coulomb gauge condition: ∇→⋅A→ = 0
Gauge transformation for quark field: ΨC(b) = UC(b) ψ(b)
Gauge transformation for gauge field: A→C = UC A→ UC⁻¹ + (i/g) UC ∇→ UC⁻¹, with ∇→⋅A→C = 0
Dressing factor expansion: UC = 1 + ∑_{m=1}^∞ (-i g)^m / m! ω^m
First-order solution: ω₁ = 1/∇² (∇→⋅A→)
Second-order solution: ω₂ = 2! / ∇² [∇→⋅(ω₁† ∇→ ω₁) + [∇→ ω₁, A→]]

论文完全匹配查询核心要求。研究聚焦非局域格点关联函数（非局域格点 correlators）与威尔逊线重整化（Wilson-line renormalization）问题，提出在库仑规范下通过规范不变的胶子场关联函数实现TMD计算，避免了传统威尔逊线，直接回应“威尔逊线重整化”需求。方法基于大动量有效理论与软胶子有效理论，明确处理了非局域关联函数的规范不变性与重整化问题。

约束项检查：  
- C1：研究非局域格点关联函数 — 满足。证据：“gauge-invariant 3D parton correlators with staple-shaped lightlike Wilson lines” 对比 “非局域 lattice correlators”  
- C2：涉及威尔逊线重整化 — 满足。证据：“without Wilson Lines” 与 “Wilson-line renormalization” 直接对应，提出替代方案  

不相关性 / 风险点 / 缺口：  
- 无。论文核心即为解决威尔逊线相关问题，且方法直接针对非局域关联函数。

In summary, we have proposed a new method for calculating the TMDs from CG correlators without Wilson lines. Through a gauge-invariant extension of the CG correlators, we demonstrate explicitly that they reduce to the standard TMD correlators in the infinite boost limit. Moreover, using SCET we derived the factorization formula for the CG quasi-TMDs, as well as the definition of the corresponding soft function. The latter can be extracted from a meson form factor and the CG quasi-TMD wave function, which completes our framework for calculating the (x,k⟂)𝑥subscript𝑘perpendicular-to(x,kperp) dependence of TMDs from lattice QCD. With the absence of Wilson lines, we expect this method to significantly improve the statistical precision and simplify the lattice renormalization in practical calculations. In this work, we have focused on the 𝒯𝒯cal T-even TMDs which are independent of the Wilson line orientation. We plan to further study the boundary conditions in the CG and the proposed definition of 𝒯𝒯cal T-odd quasi-TMDs in Eq. in a followup work. Our method is also applicable to the gluon TMDs and Wigner distributions, which will be investigated in the future. Acknowledgements. We thank Xiang Gao, Rui Zhang, Yushan Su, Jinchen He, Xiangdong Ji, Iain Stewart, Stella Schindler, Michael Wagman, Anthony Grebe, Phiala Shanahan, Artur Avkhadiev, Swagato Mukherjee and George Flemming for valuable discussions. This material is based upon work supported by the U. S. Department of Energy, Office of Science, Office of Nuclear Physics through Contract No. DE-AC02-06CH11357, and the 2023 Physical Sciences and Engineering (PSE) Early Investigator Named Award program at Argonne National Laboratory. The authors also acknowledge travel support by the U. S. Department of Energy, Office of Science, Office of Nuclear Physics under the umbrella of the Quark-Gluon Tomography (QGT) Topical Collaboration with Award DE-SC0023646.

all_papers.title: Off-lightcone Wilson-line operators in gradient flow
all_papers.abstract:
Off-lightcone Wilson-line operators are constructed using local operators
connected by time-like or space-like Wilson lines, which ensure gauge
invariance. Off-lightcone Wilson-line operators have broad applications in
various contexts. For instance, space-like Wilson-line operators play a crucial
role in determining quasi-distribution functions (quasi-PDFs), while time-like
Wilson-line operators are essential for understanding quarkonium decay and
production within the potential non-relativistic QCD (pNRQCD) framework. In
this work, we establish a systematic approach for calculating the matching from
the gradient-flow scheme to the $\overline{\rm MS}$ scheme in the limit of
small flow time for off-lightcone Wilson-line operators. By employing the
one-dimensional auxiliary-field formalism, we simplify the matching procedure,
reducing it to the matching of local current operators. We provide one-loop
level matching coefficients for these local current operators. For the case of
hadronic matrix element related to the quark quasi-PDFs, we show at one-loop
level that the finite flow time effect is very small as long as the flow radius
is smaller than the physical distance $z$, which is usually satisfied in
lattice gradient flow computations. Applications include lattice gradient flow
computations of quark/gluon quasi-PDFs, gluonic correlators related to
quarkonium decay and production in pNRQCD, and spin-dependent potentials in
terms of chromoelectric and chromomagnetic field insertions into a Wilson loop.

sections.1: Ji, Parton Physics on a Euclidean Lattice, Phys
sections.5: Lüscher, Properties and uses of the Wilson flow in lattice QCD, JHEP 08 (2010) 071 [1006.4518].
sections.7: Dorn, Renormalization of Path Ordered Phase Factors and Related Hadron Operators in Gauge Field Theories, Fortsch
sections.12: Monahan, Smeared quasidistributions in perturbation theory, Phys
sections.19: Dorn, Renormalization of Path Ordered Phase Factors and Related Hadron Operators in Gauge Field Theories, Fortsch
sections.26: Lüscher, Properties and uses of the Wilson flow in lattice QCD, JHEP 08 (2010) 071 [1006.4518].
sections.28: Luscher, Chiral symmetry and the Yang–Mills gradient flow, JHEP 04 (2013) 123 [1302.5246].

qualitative: Off-lightcone Wilson-line operators are gauge-invariant non-local operators constructed by connecting local fields via time-like or space-like Wilson lines, enabling their use in lattice QCD computations.
These operators are essential for defining quasi-distribution functions (quasi-PDFs) and pseudo-PDFs, as well as for studying quarkonium decay and production in pNRQCD.

The gradient flow provides a regularization scheme that suppresses UV fluctuations and allows finite matrix elements at positive flow time, making it suitable for matching lattice results to continuum schemes like $\overline{\text{MS}}$.
In the small flow-time limit, the gradient-flow scheme preserves infrared physics, enabling the use of dimensional regularization to handle IR divergences during matching.
The one-dimensional auxiliary-field formalism simplifies the renormalization of Wilson-line operators by reducing the matching problem to that of local current operators.
The finite flow time effect on quasi-PDFs is negligible when the flow radius is smaller than the physical distance $z$, a condition typically satisfied in lattice gradient flow simulations.
The renormalization of off-lightcone Wilson-line operators is highly non-trivial due to their non-local nature, requiring systematic matching procedures between schemes.
quantitative: The off-lightcone Wilson-line operator is defined as $ \mathcal{O}_\Gamma(zv) = \bar{\psi}(zv) \Gamma W(zv, 0) \psi $, where $ W(zv, 0) = \mathcal{P} \exp\left( i g \int_0^z ds \, v \cdot A(sv) \right) $, with $ v^2 = 1 $ in Euclidean space-time for both space-like and time-like cases.
The matching from the gradient-flow scheme to the $\overline{\text{MS}}$ scheme is performed in the small flow-time limit, $ t \to 0 $, with the flow time $ t $ being the regularization parameter.
The one-loop matching coefficients for local current operators are derived using the auxiliary-field formalism, enabling the computation of the full matching for off-lightcone Wilson-line operators.
For the quark quasi-PDF at zero external momentum, the finite flow time correction is shown to be small when $ \sqrt{t} \ll |z| $, which is typically satisfied in lattice computations.

The hadronic matrix element of the quasi-PDF operator with full flow time dependence was computed in ref. [42], and the small flow-time limit of this result is consistent with the one-loop matching coefficients derived in this work.

all_papers.reason:
论文完全匹配查询核心需求。研究聚焦非局域格点关联函数（非光锥威尔逊线）与威尔逊线重整化，与“非局域格点关联函数和威尔逊线重整化”高度契合。方法上采用梯度流方案，实现从梯度流到$\overline{\rm MS}$的匹配，直接回应重整化需求。应用涵盖准PDF、胶子关联函数等，均属非局域格点关联函数范畴。所有关键要素均满足。

约束项检查：  
- C1：研究非局域格点关联函数 — 满足。证据：“Off-lightcone Wilson-line operators”、“lattice gradient flow computations of quark/gluon quasi-PDFs” 对比 “非局域 lattice correlators”  
- C2：涉及威尔逊线重整化 — 满足。证据：“matching from the gradient-flow scheme to the $\overline{\rm MS}$ scheme” 对比 “Wilson-line renormalization”  

不相关性 / 风险点 / 缺口：  
- 无。论文精准覆盖查询所有核心要素。
authors: Nora Brambilla and Xiang-Peng Wang

content_sections.1 Introduction: The path-ordered phase factor P​exp⁡(i​g​∫Aμ​𝑑xμ)P𝑖𝑔subscript𝐴𝜇differential-dsuperscript𝑥𝜇textPexpleft(igint Amudxmuright), referred to as the Wilson line1Throughout this work, our focus will be exclusively on off-lightcone Wilson lines and Wilson loops, which we will refer to simply as Wilson lines unless ambiguity arises. , is a fundamental object in gauge theories, playing a crucial role in connecting local fields to construct gauge-invariant operators. The study of the renormalization of Wilson lines and Wilson loops was initiated by Polyakov citebib.bib1 and Gervais, Neveu citebib.bib2. The all-order proof of the multiplicative renormalizability of Wilson lines was made using diagrammatic methods in refs. citebib.bib3, bib.bib4 and functional approach in ref. citebib.bib5 based on the one-dimensional auxiliary-field formalism citebib.bib2, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9; see ref. citebib.bib5 for a review. Off-lightcone Wilson-line operators are constructed using local operators connected by time-like or space-like Wilson lines, which insure the gauge-invariance of the non-local operators. Wilson-line operators have broad applications in various contexts. For instance, space-like Wilson-line operators play a crucial role in determining quasi-distribution function (quasi-PDFs) citebib.bib10 and pseudo-distribution functions (pseudo-PDFs) citebib.bib11, while time-like Wilson-line operators are essential for understanding quarkonium decay and production within the potential non-relativistic QCD (pNRQCD) effective theory citebib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20. Recently, there has been increasing interests in the study of the quasi-PDFs and pseudo-PDFs defined as the hadronic matrix elements of the Wilson operators of the type 𝒪Γ​(z​v)subscript𝒪Γ𝑧𝑣displaystyleOGamma(zv) =displaystyle= ψ¯​(z​v)​Γ​W​(z​v,0)​ψ​,¯𝜓𝑧𝑣Γ𝑊𝑧𝑣0𝜓0displaystylebarpsi(zv)Gamma W(zv,0)psi, 𝒪μ​ν​α​β​(z​v)superscript𝒪𝜇𝜈𝛼𝛽𝑧𝑣displaystyleOmunualphabeta(zv) =displaystyle= g2​Fμ​ν​(z​v)​W​(z​v,0)​Fα​β​,superscript𝑔2superscript𝐹𝜇𝜈𝑧𝑣𝑊𝑧𝑣0superscript𝐹𝛼𝛽0displaystyle g2Fmunu(zv)W(zv,0)Falphabeta, where ψ𝜓psi is a quark field, Fμ​νsuperscript𝐹𝜇𝜈Fmunu is the gluon field strength tensor, ΓΓGamma represents a Dirac structure, v𝑣v is a four-vector satisfying v2=−1superscript𝑣21v2 (or v2=1superscript𝑣21v2=1) for space-like (or time-like) Wilson operators in Minkowski space-time 2In this context and throughout subsequent discussions, we stay in Minkowski space-time. However, a transition to Euclidean space-time is warranted when introducing gradient flow, as it is defined in Euclidean space-time, which indicates v2=1superscript𝑣21v2=1 both for space-like and time-like Wilson-line operators. , z𝑧z is a real number and W​(z​v,0)𝑊𝑧𝑣0W(zv,0) is a straight-line path-ordered Wilson line connecting the two fields, W​(z​v,0)=P​exp⁡(i​g​∫0z𝑑s​v⋅A​(s​v)),𝑊𝑧𝑣0P𝑖𝑔superscriptsubscript0𝑧⋅differential-d𝑠𝑣𝐴𝑠𝑣displaystyle W(zv,0)=textPexpleft(igint0zds,vcdot A(sv)right), in which the fundamental representation and the adjoint representation of the gauge group are assumed for the quark case, and the gluon case, respectively. Without loss of generality, we choose z>0𝑧0z>0 throughout this work. Notably, quasi-PDFs and pseudo-PDFs can be factorized in relation to the standard light-cone parton distribution functions (PDFs) based on the short-distance operator product expansion (OPE) and the large momentum effective theory (LaMET) citebib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23. Employing hadronic matrix elements of off-lightcone Wilson-line operators as opposed to lightlike ones, offers the advantage of being considerably more suitable to lattice computations. However, this convenience comes with the trade-off of the highly non-trivial renormalization of the off-lightcone Wilson-line operators. For a comprehensive overview, we refer to ref. citebib.bib24. Furthermore, the vacuum expectation value of gluonic Wilson-line operators, such as the one defined in eq. , are related to the studies of quarkonium decay and production in the framework of pNRQCD, and the QCD vacuum structure (see ref. citebib.bib25 for a review). The gradient flow was originally introduced as a method to regularize and suppress the ultraviolet (UV) fluctuations citebib.bib26, bib.bib27 in lattice calculations. It has proven useful in lattice QCD scale settings and in lattice calculation of local matrix elements and correlation functions citebib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40. Alongside its noise-reducing effect, gradient flow exhibits the notable property that composite operators at positive flow time remain finite even in the continue limit, provided they are expressed using renormalized physical parameters and fields citebib.bib29. This property renders gradient flow suitable as a matching scheme between lattice and perturbative calculations. It is worth noting that gradient flow does not modify the infrared behavior of operators in the small flow-time limit, allowing the use of dimensional regularization to regulate infrared (IR) divergences when matching from the gradient-flow scheme to MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS-like schemes. The initial proposal to explore quasi-PDFs with the incorporation of gradient flow was presented in ref. citebib.bib41. Subsequently, a one-loop matching calculation was performed, considering full flow time dependencies, for the hadronic matrix element defined using the operator in eq. with zero external momenta, which is related to the quark quasi-PDF citebib.bib42. In this study, we present a systematic approach for calculating the matching from the gradient-flow scheme to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme for Wilson-line operators, such as those defined in eq. and eq. , specifically in the limit of small flow time. Our approach is based on the one-dimensional auxiliary-field formalism, and we provide comprehensive details of the one-loop order matching calculations. For more recent applications of perturbative gradient flow, see refs. citebib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib46, bib.bib47, bib.bib48, bib.bib49, bib.bib50, bib.bib51, bib.bib52, bib.bib53 and the references therein. We structure the remaining content of the paper as follows. In section , we introduce the one-dimensional auxiliary-field formalism and elaborate on its application in the renormalization of Wilson-line operators. In section , we introduce the gradient-flow formalism and implement the small flow-time expansion to the local current operators, yielding the core formulas for our matching calculations. Section is dedicated to the computation of the matching coefficients up to one-loop order. Our summary and conclusions can be found in section . In appendix , we present the detailed matching calculation with full flow time dependence for the hadronic matrix element that is related to quasi-PDFs at zero external momenta. We compare these findings with existing results from ref. citebib.bib42, as well as with the results of section in the small flow-time limit.

content_sections.3 Gradient flow and small flow-time expa: In the following, we work in d𝑑d-dimensional Euclidean space-time with d=4−2​ϵ𝑑42italic-ϵd=4-2epsilon. The gradient flow extends the d𝑑d-dimensional QCD to a (d+1)𝑑1(d+1)-dimensional field theory by introducing an additional coordinate t𝑡t (not to be confused with the time coordinate in Minkowski space-time) of mass dimension −22-2, which is called the flow time citebib.bib28, bib.bib29. In the gradient-flow formalism, the flow time dependent gluon field Bμa​(x,t)superscriptsubscript𝐵𝜇𝑎𝑥𝑡Bmua(x,t) and quark field χαi​(x,t)subscriptsuperscript𝜒𝑖𝛼𝑥𝑡chiialpha(x,t) are determined by the flow equations citebib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, ∂tBμsubscript𝑡subscript𝐵𝜇displaystylepartialtBmu =displaystyle= 𝒟ν​Gν​μ+κ​𝒟μ​∂νBν,subscript𝒟𝜈subscript𝐺𝜈𝜇𝜅subscript𝒟𝜇subscript𝜈subscript𝐵𝜈displaystyleDnuGnumu+kappaDmupartialnuBnu, ∂χ𝜒displaystylepartialchi =displaystyle= 𝒟μ​𝒟μ​χ−κ​(∂μBμ)​χ,subscript𝒟𝜇subscript𝒟𝜇𝜒𝜅subscript𝜇subscript𝐵𝜇𝜒displaystyleDmuDmuchi-kappa(partialmuBmu)chi, ∂χ¯¯𝜒displaystylepartialbarchi =displaystyle= χ¯​𝒟←μ​𝒟←μ+κ​χ¯​∂μBμ,¯𝜒subscript←𝒟𝜇subscript←𝒟𝜇𝜅¯𝜒subscript𝜇subscript𝐵𝜇displaystylebarchioverleftarrowDmuoverleftarrowDmu+kappabarchipartialmuBmu, where κ𝜅kappa is a gauge parameter that drops out in physical observables, and Gμ​νsubscript𝐺𝜇𝜈displaystyle Gmunu =displaystyle= ∂μBν−∂νBμ+[Bμ,Bν],subscript𝜇subscript𝐵𝜈subscript𝜈subscript𝐵𝜇subscript𝐵𝜇subscript𝐵𝜈displaystylepartialmuBnu-partialnuBmu+left[Bmu,Bnuright], 𝒟μsubscript𝒟𝜇displaystyleDmu =displaystyle= ∂μ+[Bμ,. ],displaystylepartialmu+left[Bmu,. right], with the boundary conditions Bμ​(x;t=0)subscript𝐵𝜇𝑥𝑡0displaystyle Bmu(x;t=0) =displaystyle= g​Aμ​(x),𝑔subscript𝐴𝜇𝑥displaystyle gAmu(x), χ​(x;t=0)𝜒𝑥𝑡0displaystylechi(x;t=0) =displaystyle= ψ​(x),𝜓𝑥displaystylepsi(x), χ¯​(x;t=0)¯𝜒𝑥𝑡0displaystylebarchi(x;t=0) =displaystyle= ψ¯​(x). ¯𝜓𝑥displaystylebarpsi(x). At next-to-leading order (NLO), the renormalization factor Z̊χsubscript̊𝑍𝜒mathringZchi is given by citebib.bib35 Z̊χ​(t,μ)=(8​π​t)−ϵ​1+αs4​π​CF​[3ϵUV+3​log⁡(2​μ2​t​eγE)−log⁡],subscript̊𝑍𝜒𝑡𝜇superscript8𝜋𝑡italic-ϵ1subscript𝛼𝑠4𝜋subscript𝐶𝐹delimited-3subscriptitalic-ϵUV32superscript𝜇2𝑡superscript𝑒subscript𝛾𝐸432displaystylemathringZchi(t,mu)=(8pi t)-epsilonleft1+fracalphas4piCFleft[frac3epsilonrm UV+3logleft(2mu2tegammaEright)-logright]right, where the factor (8​π​t)−ϵsuperscript8𝜋𝑡italic-ϵ(8pi t)-epsilon is introduced to balance the dimension of the ringed fermion field, which has dimension 3/2323/2, while the flowed fermion field has dimension (d−1)/2𝑑12(d-1)/2.

In this work, we consider the local current operators defined in eqs. and eq. , which are flowed at the flow time t𝑡t. We use the same symbols for both the flowed and the un-flowed current operators as long as it does not lead to confusions. In the context of Wilson-line operators, we flow the external fields and the Wilson lines at the flow time t𝑡t. Specifically, this means that all the gluon fields directly connecting to the “heavy” auxiliary field lines are flowed at the flow time t𝑡t, while the “heavy” auxiliary fields themselves remain un-flowed. In the regime of small flow time, we employ a “short-distance” OPE for the flowed current operators. Our objective is to derive matching coefficients between renormalized flowed current operators and the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS renormalized un-flowed current operators, 𝒪R​(t)=c𝒪​(t,μ)​𝒪MS¯​(μ)+O​(t),superscript𝒪R𝑡subscript𝑐𝒪𝑡𝜇superscript𝒪¯MS𝜇𝑂𝑡displaystyleOrm R(t)=cO(t,mu)Ooverlinerm MS(mu)+O(t), where we have neglected operator mixing. We work in the massless limit for quark fields and set all the external scales to be zero whenever feasible. This strategic choice allows us to leverage established techniques from HQET for matching calculations. Specifically, we do not need to calculate the un-flowed operators because they yield scaleless integrals to all orders in perturbative calculations in dimensional regularization so that we can obtain the matching coefficients c𝒪​(t,μ)subscript𝑐𝒪𝑡𝜇cO(t,mu) from the renormalized flowed operators simply by subtracting the IR poles. Similarly to the renormalization of current operators, we can break down the matching coefficients c𝒪​(t,μ)subscript𝑐𝒪𝑡𝜇cO(t,mu) for the local current operators into products of field matching coefficients and corresponding vertex matching coefficients. For the four local current operators ψ¯​hv¯𝜓subscriptℎ𝑣barpsihv, g​F∥⟂μ​ν​hvgFmunu|perphv, g​F⟂⟂μ​ν​hv𝑔subscriptsuperscript𝐹𝜇𝜈perpendicular-toabsentperpendicular-tosubscriptℎ𝑣gFmunuperpperphv, and g​h¯v​F⟂⟂μ​ν​hv𝑔subscript¯ℎ𝑣subscriptsuperscript𝐹𝜇𝜈perpendicular-toabsentperpendicular-tosubscriptℎ𝑣gbarhvFmunuperpperphv, the relationships between matching coefficients for the local current operators and those for the corresponding fields and vertices are given by cψ​hv​(t,μ)subscript𝑐𝜓subscriptℎ𝑣𝑡𝜇displaystyle cpsi hv(t,mu) =displaystyle= ζ̊ψ​(t,μ)​ζhvF​(t,μ)​ζψ​hv​(t,μ),subscript̊𝜁𝜓𝑡𝜇superscriptsubscript𝜁subscriptℎ𝑣𝐹𝑡𝜇subscript𝜁𝜓subscriptℎ𝑣𝑡𝜇displaystylemathringzetapsi(t,mu)zetahvF(t,mu)zetapsi hv(t,mu), (45a) c∥⟂​(t,μ)displaystyle c|perp(t,mu) =displaystyle= ζA​(t,μ)​ζhvA​(t,μ)​ζ∥⟂​(t,μ),displaystylezetaA(t,mu)zetahvA(t,mu)zeta|perp(t,mu), (45b) c⟂⟂​(t,μ)subscript𝑐perpendicular-toabsentperpendicular-to𝑡𝜇displaystyle cperpperp(t,mu) =displaystyle= ζA​(t,μ)​ζhvA​(t,μ)​ζ⟂⟂​(t,μ),subscript𝜁𝐴𝑡𝜇superscriptsubscript𝜁subscriptℎ𝑣𝐴𝑡𝜇subscript𝜁perpendicular-toabsentperpendicular-to𝑡𝜇displaystylezetaA(t,mu)zetahvA(t,mu)zetaperpperp(t,mu), (45c) cF​(t,μ)subscript𝑐𝐹𝑡𝜇displaystyle cF(t,mu) =displaystyle= ζA​(t,μ)​(ζhvF​(t,μ))2​ζF​(t,μ). subscript𝜁𝐴𝑡𝜇superscriptsubscriptsuperscript𝜁𝐹subscriptℎ𝑣𝑡𝜇2subscript𝜁𝐹𝑡𝜇displaystylezetaA(t,mu)(zetaFhv(t,mu))2zetaF(t,mu). (45d) Here ζ̊ψ​(t,μ)subscript̊𝜁𝜓𝑡𝜇mathringzetapsi(t,mu), ζhvF​(ζhvA)superscriptsubscript𝜁subscriptℎ𝑣𝐹superscriptsubscript𝜁subscriptℎ𝑣𝐴zetahvF(zetahvA) and ζA​(t,μ)subscript𝜁𝐴𝑡𝜇zetaA(t,mu) are the matching coefficients of the ringed fermion field χ̊̊𝜒mathringchi, the “heavy” auxiliary field hvsubscriptℎ𝑣hv in fundamental (adjoint) representation and the flowed gluon field B𝐵B, respectively. Additionally, ζψ​hv​(t,μ)subscript𝜁𝜓subscriptℎ𝑣𝑡𝜇zetapsi hv(t,mu), ζ∥⟂​(t,μ)zeta|perp(t,mu), ζ⟂⟂​(t,μ)subscript𝜁perpendicular-toabsentperpendicular-to𝑡𝜇zetaperpperp(t,mu) and ζF​(t,μ)subscript𝜁𝐹𝑡𝜇zetaF(t,mu) denote matching coefficients stemming from vertex corrections for the corresponding flowed local current operators. We calculate these matching coefficients to one-loop order in section . A key insight arising from our study in section on the multiplicative renormalization of Wilson-line operators is that matching coefficients for Wilson operators in the transition from the gradient-flow scheme to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme can be directly derived from matching coefficients for the corresponding local current operators. This occurs because in the one-dimensional auxiliary-field formalism, combinations of the renormalized local current operators at different space-time points do not introduce additional UV divergences, apart from the subtracted linear divergence, and because of eq. that relates local operators in the small flow-time limit. Therefore, additional matching for these combined local current operators in different space-time points is not needed in the small flow-time limit.
content_sections.4 Computations and results: While the matching coefficients for the fields can be gauge-dependent, it is important to note that the combinations leading to the local current operators under consideration in this work are gauge-invariant. Therefore, we have chosen to employ the Feynman gauge consistently throughout our calculations. Our computations are conducted in the context of Euclidean space-time. In Euclidean space-time, the renormalized Lagrangian ℒhvsubscriptℒsubscriptℎ𝑣Lhv is given by ℒhvE=Zhv​h¯v​(−i​v⋅∂+i​δ​m)​hv+g​Zg​ZA12​Zhv​h¯v​v⋅Aa​Ta​hv,superscriptsubscriptℒsubscriptℎ𝑣Esubscript𝑍subscriptℎ𝑣subscript¯ℎ𝑣⋅𝑖𝑣𝑖𝛿𝑚subscriptℎ𝑣⋅𝑔subscript𝑍𝑔superscriptsubscript𝑍𝐴12subscript𝑍subscriptℎ𝑣subscript¯ℎ𝑣𝑣superscript𝐴𝑎superscript𝑇𝑎subscriptℎ𝑣displaystyleLhvrm E=Zhvbarhv(-ivcdotpartial+idelta m)hv+gZgZAfrac12Zhvbarhvvcdot AaTahv, where the superscript “E” indicates Euclidean space-time. content_sections.4 Computations and results 4.1 δ​m𝛿𝑚\\de: The calculation of the self-energy of the “heavy” auxiliary field at one-loop in gradient flow is similar to that of the heavy quark field in HQET. Setting the external momentum p𝑝p flowing from left to right, the one-loop self-energy diagram shown in figure gives the amplitude ℳ(a)=g2​μ2​ϵ​CF​∫dd​k(2​π)d​1(k⋅v+p⋅v−i​0)​k2​e−2​t​k2,subscriptℳ𝑎superscript𝑔2superscript𝜇2italic-ϵsubscript𝐶𝐹superscript𝑑𝑑𝑘superscript2𝜋𝑑1⋅𝑘𝑣⋅𝑝𝑣𝑖0superscript𝑘2superscript𝑒2𝑡superscript𝑘2displaystyleM(a)=g2tildemu2epsilonCFintfracddk(2pi)dfrac1left(kcdot v+pcdot v-i0right)k2e-2tk2, where μ2​ϵ=(μ2​eγE4​π)ϵsuperscript𝜇2italic-ϵsuperscriptsuperscript𝜇2superscript𝑒subscript𝛾𝐸4𝜋italic-ϵtildemu2epsilon=left(fracmu2egammaE4piright)epsilon and we have chosen the “heavy” auxiliary field to be in fundamental representation. For the adjoint representation case, we just need to replace CFsubscript𝐶𝐹CF with CAsubscript𝐶𝐴CA in the above expression. Summing up geometric series of the one-loop self-energy diagrams of the “heavy” auxiliary field propagator gives 1/(p⋅v−ℳ(a))1⋅𝑝𝑣subscriptℳ𝑎1/(pcdot v-M(a)), which indicates that the one-loop “mass correction” is given by (−ℳ(a))subscriptℳ𝑎(-M(a)). For simplicity, we set p=0𝑝0p=0 in ℳ(a)subscriptℳ𝑎M(a), yielding −ℳ(a)|p=0=−g2​μ2​ϵ​CF​∫dd​k(2​π)d​1(k⋅v−i​0)​k2​e−2​t​k2. evaluated-atsubscriptℳ𝑎𝑝0superscript𝑔2superscript𝜇2italic-ϵsubscript𝐶𝐹superscript𝑑𝑑𝑘superscript2𝜋𝑑1⋅𝑘𝑣𝑖0superscript𝑘2superscript𝑒2𝑡superscript𝑘2displaystyle-M(a)|p=0=-g2tildemu2epsilonCFintfracddk(2pi)dfrac1left(kcdot v-i0right)k2e-2tk2. Note that the above expression is not odd under k⋅v→−k⋅v→⋅𝑘𝑣⋅𝑘𝑣kcdot vrightarrow-kcdot v and the k⋅v⋅𝑘𝑣kcdot v integration does not give a vanishing result because the sign of the i​0𝑖0i0 prescription matters. These amplitudes do not give expressions that are exactly proportional to the LO expression of the operator, g​F∥⟂μ​ν​hvgFmunu|perphv even after tensor reduction for the loop integrations. They lead to mixing with the gauge non-invariant operators i​vμ​A⟂ν​(i​v⋅D−i​δ​m)​hv𝑖superscript𝑣𝜇superscriptsubscript𝐴perpendicular-to𝜈⋅𝑖𝑣𝐷𝑖𝛿𝑚subscriptℎ𝑣ivmuAperpnu(ivcdot D-idelta m)hv, whose LO expression is proportional to q∥μ​A⟂νsuperscriptsubscript𝑞∥𝜇superscriptsubscript𝐴perpendicular-to𝜈q|muAperpnu, and thus it is not straightforward to disentangle the mixing from those that give contributions to the operator g​F∥⟂μ​ν​hvgFmunu|perphv. Fortunately, we can extract the contributions to the operator g​F∥⟂μ​ν​hvgFmunu|perphv through the results that are proportional to q⟂ν​A∥μsuperscriptsubscript𝑞perpendicular-to𝜈superscriptsubscript𝐴∥𝜇qperpnuA|mu. If we set t=0𝑡0t=0 in ℳ(e1)∥⟂M(e1)|perp and ℳ(e7)∥⟂M(e7)|perp, we can easily see that the linear divergences coming from ℳ(e7)∥⟂M(e7)|perp and the second term of ℳ(e1)∥⟂M(e1)|perp cancel with d=3−2​ϵ𝑑32italic-ϵd=3-2epsilon. However, in gradient flow scheme, the cancellation of linear divergences are much more complicated. Apparently, the linearly divergences arising from ℳ(e7)∥⟂M(e7)|perp and the second term of ℳ(e1)∥⟂M(e1)|perp do not cancel with d=4−2​ϵ𝑑42italic-ϵd=4-2epsilon and t>0𝑡0t>0. The cancellation of linear divergences in gradient-flow scheme will be restored when diagrams (e2)subscript𝑒2(e2) and (e3)subscript𝑒3(e3) are included. Great simplification can be achieved for our matching calculation by adopting the well-developed method used in the matching calculation in HQET. That is, we focus on the large loop momentum region k>>qmuch-greater-than𝑘𝑞k>>q and expand the amplitudes up to linear order of the external momentum q𝑞q before performing the loop momentum integration. This method is valid in our case because, in the small flow-time limit, the flowed operators share the same IR behavior with the corresponding un-flowed operators and contributions to the matching coefficients come from the UV region. The relationships between the matching coefficients of the local current operators and the matching coefficients of the fields and vertices are provided in eqs. . The one-loop results of ζhvsubscript𝜁subscriptℎ𝑣zetahv, ζ̊ψsubscript̊𝜁𝜓mathringzetapsi, ζψ​hvsubscript𝜁𝜓subscriptℎ𝑣zetapsi hv, ζAsubscript𝜁𝐴zetaA, ζ∥⟂zeta|perp, ζ⟂⟂subscript𝜁perpendicular-toabsentperpendicular-tozetaperpperp and ζFsubscript𝜁𝐹zetaF can be found in eq. , eq. , eq. , eq. , eq. , eq. and eq. , respectively. Implementing those results, we finally obtain cψ​hv​(t,μ)subscript𝑐𝜓subscriptℎ𝑣𝑡𝜇displaystyle cpsi hv(t,mu) =displaystyle= 1−αs4​π​CF​[32​log⁡(2​μ2​t​eγE)+log⁡2+1]+O​(αs2),1subscript𝛼𝑠4𝜋subscript𝐶𝐹delimited-322superscript𝜇2𝑡superscript𝑒subscript𝛾𝐸43221𝑂superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠2displaystyle 1-fracalphas4piCFleft[frac32logleft(2mu2tegammaEright)+fraclog2+1right]+O(alphas2), c∥⟂​(t,μ)displaystyle c|perp(t,mu) =displaystyle= 1+O​(αs2),1𝑂superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠2displaystyle 1+O(alphas2), c⟂⟂​(t,μ)subscript𝑐perpendicular-toabsentperpendicular-to𝑡𝜇displaystyle cperpperp(t,mu) =displaystyle= 1+αs4​π​CA×log⁡(2​μ2​t​eγE)+O​(αs2),1subscript𝛼𝑠4𝜋subscript𝐶𝐴2superscript𝜇2𝑡superscript𝑒subscript𝛾𝐸𝑂superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠2displaystyle 1+fracalphas4piCAtimeslogleft(2mu2tegammaEright)+O(alphas2), cF​(t,μ)subscript𝑐𝐹𝑡𝜇displaystyle cF(t,mu) =displaystyle= 1+αs4​π​CA​[2​log⁡(2​μ2​t​eγE)+78]+O​(αs2).1subscript𝛼𝑠4𝜋subscript𝐶𝐴delimited-22superscript𝜇2𝑡superscript𝑒subscript𝛾𝐸78𝑂superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠2displaystyle 1+fracalphas4piCAleft[2logleft(2mu2tegammaEright)+frac78right]+O(alphas2). In the small flow-time limit, expressing the matching equations for the Wilson-line operators defined in eq. , eq. and eq. as 𝒪ΓR​(z​v,t)superscriptsubscript𝒪ΓR𝑧𝑣𝑡displaystyleOGammarm R(zv,t) =displaystyle= 𝒞ψ​(t,μ)​eδ​m​z​𝒪ΓMS¯​(z​v)+O​(t),subscript𝒞𝜓𝑡𝜇superscript𝑒𝛿𝑚𝑧superscriptsubscript𝒪Γ¯MS𝑧𝑣𝑂𝑡displaystyleCpsi(t,mu)edelta mzOGammaoverlinerm MS(zv)+O(t), 𝒪∥⟂R​(z​v,t)displaystyleO|perprm R(zv,t) =displaystyle= 𝒞∥⟂​(t,μ)​eδ​m​z​𝒪∥⟂MS¯​(z​v)+O​(t),displaystyleC|perp(t,mu)edelta mzO|perpoverlinerm MS(zv)+O(t), 𝒪∥⟂R​(z​v,t)displaystyleO|perprm R(zv,t) =displaystyle= 𝒞⟂⟂​(t,μ)​eδ​m​z​𝒪⟂⟂MS¯​(z​v)+O​(t),subscript𝒞perpendicular-toabsentperpendicular-to𝑡𝜇superscript𝑒𝛿𝑚𝑧superscriptsubscript𝒪perpendicular-toabsentperpendicular-to¯MS𝑧𝑣𝑂𝑡displaystyleCperpperp(t,mu)edelta mzOperpperpoverlinerm MS(zv)+O(t), where we have omitted the Lorentz indices for simplicity, we establish relations 𝒞ψ​(t,μ)subscript𝒞𝜓𝑡𝜇displaystyleCpsi(t,mu) =displaystyle= cψ​hv2​(t,μ),subscriptsuperscript𝑐2𝜓subscriptℎ𝑣𝑡𝜇displaystyle c2psi hv(t,mu), 𝒞∥⟂​(t,μ)displaystyleC|perp(t,mu) =displaystyle= c∥⟂2​(t,μ),displaystyle c2|perp(t,mu), 𝒞⟂⟂​(t,μ)subscript𝒞perpendicular-toabsentperpendicular-to𝑡𝜇displaystyleCperpperp(t,mu) =displaystyle= c⟂⟂2​(t,μ). subscriptsuperscript𝑐2perpendicular-toabsentperpendicular-to𝑡𝜇displaystyle c2perpperp(t,mu). These relations are independent of z𝑧z and external states of the matrix elements constructed using these Wilson-line operators. Regarding the spin-dependent potentials in terms of chromomagnetic field insertions into a Wilson loop, each such insertion will lead to a factor of the matching coefficient cF​(t,μ)subscript𝑐𝐹𝑡𝜇cF(t,mu) for the matching from the gradient-flow scheme to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme.
content_sections.5 Summary and outlook: We have developed a systematic approach for matching from the gradient-flow scheme to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme for Wilson-line operators in the small flow-time limit. This approach extends to various applications, including quasi-PDFs, gluonic correlators that appear in quarkonium decay and production in the framework of pNRQCD and spin-dependent potentials in terms of chromomagnetic field insertions into a Wilson loop. Within the one-dimensional auxiliary-field formalism, the matching from the gradient-flow scheme to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme for the Wilson-line operators discussed in this work can be reduced to the matching for the corresponding local current operators in the small flow-time limit. We match these local current operators from the gradient-flow scheme to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme by comparing perturbative computations in these two schemes. Great simplification for the matching procedure can be achieved by using small flow-time expansion and setting external momenta to be zero whenever possible. In this way, the well-developed method of matching calculation in HQET can be applied. After completing the matching for the local current operators, one automatically obtains the matching coefficients for the corresponding Wilson-line operators in the small flow-time limit, because the combinations of the renormalized local current operators at different spacetime do not introduce further UV divergences, apart from the subtracted linear divergences, due to the multiplicative renormalizability of the Wilson-line operators. The matching for the local current operators can be further decomposed as the matching for the fields and vertices due to the multiplicative renormalizability of the local current operators. We thus compute the matching coefficients at one-loop level for auxiliary “heavy” auxiliary field hvsubscriptℎ𝑣hv, massless quark field ψ𝜓psi, ψ​hv𝜓subscriptℎ𝑣psi hv vertex, gluon field g​A𝑔𝐴gA and the vertices of the local current operators g​F∥⟂μ​ν​hv,g​F⟂⟂μ​ν​hvgFmunu|perphv,,gFmunuperpperphv and g​h¯v​F⟂⟂μ​ν​hv𝑔subscript¯ℎ𝑣subscriptsuperscript𝐹𝜇𝜈perpendicular-toabsentperpendicular-tosubscriptℎ𝑣gbarhvFmunuperpperphv. The one-loop result of the linearly divergent “mass” correction δ​m𝛿𝑚delta m is also obtained. Provided with these results, we give the matching coefficients for the local current operators and the Wilson-line operators in the small flow-time limit. It is worth mentioning that this method is also applicable for matching from lattice regularization schemes to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme in the small lattice spacing limit, albeit with added complexity due to UV and IR divergence treatment citebib.bib68. In the appendix, we have shown that, at one-loop level, for the case of hadronic matrix element defined in eq. at zero external momenta, the finite flow time effect is very small as long as the flow radius is smaller than physical distance z𝑧z, which is usually satisfied in lattice gradient flow computations. With the systematic approach we have developed for the matching from the gradient-flow scheme to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme for the Wilson-line operators, it is straightforward to extend the current study to two-loop order, which would be more helpful for lattice gradient flow computations of matrix elements constructed using the off-lightcone Wilson-line operators. We leave it for future research.
content_sections.5 Summary and outlook Note added: Before suission of this work, we noticed that the one-loop result of the matching coefficient for the gluonic correlator GBsubscript𝐺𝐵GB was recently published in ref. citebib.bib69, which was the same as our one-loop result of 𝒞⟂⟂​(t,μ)subscript𝒞perpendicular-toabsentperpendicular-to𝑡𝜇Cperpperp(t,mu). Vairo for reading the paper and giving very useful comments. The work of N. B. and X. -P. all_papers.title:
Renormalization of asymmetric staple-shaped Wilson-line operators in
  lattice and continuum perturbation theory
all_papers.abstract:
In this work, we study the renormalization of nonlocal quark bilinear
operators containing an asymmetric staple-shaped Wilson line at the one-loop
level in both lattice and continuum perturbation theory. These operators enter
the first-principle calculation of transverse momentum-dependent parton
distribution functions (TMDPDFs) in lattice QCD using the formulation of Large
Momentum Effective Theory. We provide appropriate RI$'$-type conditions that
address the power and logarithmic divergences, as well as the mixing among
staple operators of different Dirac structures, using a number of different
possible projectors. A variant of RI$'$, including calculations of rectangular
Wilson loops, which cancel the pinch-pole singularities of the staple operators
at infinite length and reduce residual power divergences, is also employed. We
calculate at one-loop order the conversion matrix, which relates the
quasi-TMDPDFs in the RI$'$-type schemes to the reference scheme $\overline{\rm
MS}$ for arbitrary values of the renormalization momentum scale and of the
dimensions of the staple.

sections.4: Anderle et al., “Electron-ion collider in China,” Front
sections.6: Accardi et al., “Electron Ion Collider: The Next QCD Frontier: Understanding the glue that binds us all,” Eur
sections.9: Brandt, Filippo Neri,  and Masa-aki Sato, “Renormalization of Loop Functions for All Loops,” Phys
sections.13: Cohen,  and Xiangdong Ji, “Flavor Structure of the Nucleon Sea from Lattice QCD,” Phys

qualitative.statement:
{
  "qualitative": [
    "Nonlocal quark bilinear operators with asymmetric staple-shaped Wilson lines are essential for first-principles lattice QCD calculations of transverse momentum-dependent parton distribution functions (TMDPDFs) within the Large Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET) framework.",
    "The renormalization of these operators involves both power and logarithmic divergences, as well as mixing among different Dirac structures, necessitating careful treatment in both lattice and continuum perturbation theory.",
    "RI'-type renormalization conditions are adapted to handle divergences and operator mixing, with the use of multiple projectors to isolate specific Dirac components.",
    "A variant of the RI' scheme incorporating rectangular Wilson loops is introduced to cancel pinch-pole singularities at infinite staple length and reduce residual power divergences.",
    "The conversion matrix between the RI'-type schemes and the $\overline{\rm MS}$ scheme is computed at one-loop order, enabling the matching of quasi-TMDPDFs to physical TMDPDFs in a well-defined renormalization scheme."
  ],
  "quantitative": [
    "The conversion matrix between the RI'-type schemes and the $\overline{\rm MS}$ scheme is calculated at one-loop order for arbitrary renormalization momentum scale $\\mu$ and arbitrary staple dimensions.",
    "The renormalization of the staple-shaped Wilson-line operators involves the evaluation of one-loop Feynman diagrams with asymmetric staple geometries, including contributions from both quark and gluon loops.",
    "The use of rectangular Wilson loops in the modified RI' scheme leads to cancellation of pinch-pole singularities in the infinite-length limit of the staple.",
    "Residual power divergences are reduced through the inclusion of rectangular Wilson loop contributions in the renormalization condition.",
    "The renormalization factors and mixing matrices are derived using a set of projectors that distinguish between different Dirac structures of the staple operators."
  ]
}
