authors: Gregoris Spanoudes, Martha Constantinou, Haralambos Panagopoulos

content_sections.I Introduction: One of the directions of research in lattice QCD, which has shown rapid progress in the last decade, is the first-principle study of a family of nonperturbative distribution functions (DFs) that describe the internal structure of hadrons: parton distribution functions (PDFs), generalized parton distribution functions (GPDs) and transverse-momentum dependent parton distributions functions (TMDPDFs). All three types of DFs are crucial for a comprehensive understanding of the three-dimensional hadron picture. Calculating DFs from first principles has long been a challenge in Hadron Physics due to their nonperturbative and light-cone nature. The latter does not allow for a direct nonperturbative computation of DFs on Euclidean lattice. In the last decade, a groundbreaking approach by X. Jicitebib.bib1 has overcome this issue. The approach connects Euclidean equal-time correlation functions (referred to as quasi-DFs), which are accessible by lattice simulations, to the physical light-cone DFs using the framework of Large Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET)citebib.bib2. This breakthrough has paved the way for extracting, for the first time, DFs from lattice simulations. Several groups have successfully employed this approach in the calculation of various quark and gluon DFs on the lattice: quark PDFs and GPDs in Refs. citebib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41, bib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib46, bib.bib47, bib.bib48, bib.bib49, bib.bib50, quark TMDPDFs and soft-function in Refs. citebib.bib51, bib.bib52, bib.bib53, bib.bib54, bib.bib55, bib.bib56, bib.bib57, bib.bib58, bib.bib59, bib.bib60, bib.bib61, bib.bib62, bib.bib63, bib.bib64, bib.bib65, bib.bib66, bib.bib67, bib.bib68, gluon PDFs in Refs. citebib.bib69, bib.bib70, bib.bib71, bib.bib72, bib.bib73, bib.bib74, bib.bib75, bib.bib76, bib.bib77, bib.bib78 and hadronic light-cone distribution amplitudes (DA) in Refs. citebib.bib79, bib.bib80, bib.bib7, bib.bib81, bib.bib82. An overview of recent progress in the research of DFs on the lattice can be found in Refs. citebib.bib83, bib.bib84, bib.bib85, bib.bib86, bib.bib87. The goal of these studies is to complement the planned experimental programs for investigating the 3D tomography of the nucleon in major experimental facilities, such as the electron ion colliders of USAcitebib.bib88, bib.bib89 and Chinacitebib.bib90. Theoretical studies can significantly complement the experimental investigations, especially when there are limitations either in the experimental programs or in the phenomenological models used for the analysis of the experimental data. The computation of DFs using Ji’s approach (also called the “quasi-PDFs” approach) involves a three-step process. Firstly, nonperturbative calculations are performed to determine hadron matrix elements of gauge-invariant quark or gluon nonlocal operators. These operators contain path-ordered Wilson lines with specific shapes, such as straight lines for PDFs and staple-shaped lines for TMDPDFs. Secondly, the nonlocal operators are renormalized to establish a connection with physically measurable quantities. This task is challenging compared to the case of local operators, as explained below. Lastly, the renormalized lattice DFs are perturbatively matched to the corresponding physical light-cone DFs using LaMET. One-loop matching formulae have been extracted in the literature for several quasi DFscitebib.bib91, bib.bib92; in some cases a two-loop formula is also availablecitebib.bib93, bib.bib94. In this study, we focus on the implementation of the second step in the quasi-PDFs approach regarding the renormalization of nonlocal operators using one-loop perturbation theory in both continuum and lattice regularizations. While a nonperturbative calculation of the renormalization functions in numerical simulations of lattice QCD is desirable, perturbation theory can give important feedback for the development of an appropriate nonperturbative renormalization prescription, which addresses all kinds of divergences, as well as possible mixing with operators of equal or lower dimension allowed by global symmetries. Most importantly, perturbation theory gives us the matching functions (at a given order) between nonperturbative renormalization schemes and continuum perturbative schemes – primarily MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS – employed in phenomenology. Studies of nonlocal operators with Wilson lines in continuum perturbation theory go back decades, including seminal workcitebib.bib95, bib.bib96, bib.bib97, bib.bib98, bib.bib99, bib.bib100, bib.bib101, bib.bib102, bib.bib103, bib.bib104 for the renormalization of open and closed (loops) Wilson lines, with and without singular points (cusps, self-intersections), and having quark or gluon fields at the endpoints. Lattice studies of nonlocal operators have emerged only in the last decade after the development of the quasi-PDFs approach. The first perturbative lattice calculation of Wilson-line operators was made by our group in Ref. citebib.bib105, to one loop for massless quarks, using the Wilson/clover fermion action and a variety of Symanzik-improved gluon actions. A straight Wilson line with quark fields at the endpoints was employed in order to investigate the renormalization of quark quasi-PDFs. This study showed that the lattice formulation introduces several new complications, such as mixing among operators of equal dimension and different twists, and power-law divergences (even in the absence of mixing with lower-dimensional operators, in contrast to the case of local operators). A number of extensions of this study have been followed by our group regarding the presence of finite quark masscitebib.bib106 and the calculation of one-loop artifacts to all orders in the lattice spacing a𝑎acitebib.bib107. In a different extension of our study, we have considered nonlocal operators with a symmetric staple-shaped Wilson linecitebib.bib108 in order to investigate the renormalization of quark quasi-TMDPDFs. In our current work, we extend further the latter calculation by employing the more general case of an asymmetric staple-shaped Wilson line, where all three segments of the staple can have different lengths. Studies by other groups along these lines have appeared in Refs. citebib.bib109, bib.bib110, bib.bib111, bib.bib112. Staple-shaped nonlocal operators have a wide range of applicability. They enter the analysis of semi-inclusive deep inelastic scattering (SIDIS) processes, as well as the Drell-Yan (DY) processes, in a kinematic region where the photon virtuality is large and the measured transverse momentum of the produced hadron is of the order of ΛQCDsubscriptΛQCDLambdarm QCDcitebib.bib113. In these analyses, the segments of the staple that are parallel to the direction in which the hadron is boosted have an infinite length. Thus, while our study focuses on finite lengths of the staple segments, an extrapolation to infinite limit must be taken in the renormalized matrix elements of these operators. The presence or not of an asymmetry in the shape of the staple operators affects their renormalization. Besides the presence of an additional scale in the renormalization conditions, the mixing pattern is different between symmetric and asymmetric staple operators (see Sec. ). However, this difference is not visible in one-loop lattice perturbation theory, as concluded by the present calculation. As stated before, there are a number of challenges to address in order to renormalize the nonlocal Wilson-line operators of arbitrary shape: 1. Power divergences arise for cutoff regularized theories, such as lattice QCDcitebib.bib98. The divergences depend on the total length (L𝐿L) of the Wilson line and can be absorbed in an exponential factor of the form e−δ​m​Lsuperscript𝑒𝛿𝑚𝐿e-delta mL, where δ​m𝛿𝑚delta m is a dimensionful regularization-dependent quantity whose magnitude diverges linearly with the regulator. 2. Logarithmic divergences arise not only from contact terms but also from the singular points of the Wilson line. In the case of a straight line, singular points are just the endpoints. For operators of different shapes, there can also be cusp divergencescitebib.bib100, which depend on the angle and number of cusps present. In the case of the staple line, there are two cusps of angle π/2𝜋2pi/2. These divergences can be addressed by using typical renormalization schemes, such as RI or ratio schemes. 3. Finite operator mixing arises between Wilson-line operators 𝒪Γsubscript𝒪ΓOGamma with different (products of) Dirac matrices ΓΓGamma depending on the regularization. In the case of straight-line operators, the one-loop computationcitebib.bib105 shows mixing in pairs between nonlocal fermion bilinears of the form (𝒪Γsubscript𝒪ΓOGamma, 𝒪Γ,γν1/2subscript𝒪Γsubscript𝛾subscript𝜈12OGamma,gammanu1/2), where ν1subscript𝜈1hatnu1 is the direction of the straight line, for chirality-breaking actions. Symmetry arguments (reflections, charge conjugation)citebib.bib114 confirm that the one-loop mixing pattern is also valid nonperturbatively. In the case of (symmetric and asymmetric) staple operators, the one-loop computationcitebib.bib108 shows mixing in pairs with a different pattern: (𝒪Γsubscript𝒪ΓOGamma, 𝒪[Γ,γν2]/2subscript𝒪Γsubscript𝛾subscript𝜈22O[Gamma,gammanu2]/2), where ν2subscript𝜈2hatnu2 is the direction of the sides of the staple line. However, symmetry argumentscitebib.bib68 show a wider mixing pattern: quadruplets of operators emerge in the asymmetric case and triplets in the symmetric case when nonchiral fermions are employed. 4. In the case of (symmetric and asymmetric) staple operators, a pinch-pole singularitycitebib.bib57 arises when one takes the infinite limit of lateral sizes of the staple (this limit is included in the definition of TMDPDFs). This singularity – a linear divergence – comes from the gluon exchange between the two parallel (“longitudinal”) segments of the staple. 5. The renormalization functions are (in general) complex in nonminimal subtraction schemes1The presence of an imaginary part in the renormalization functions depends on the exact definition of the renormalization scheme. For example, one can define purely real renormalization conditions by using only the real part of “projected” Green’s functions; in this way, both the real and the imaginary part of the renormalized Green’s functions will be rendered finite. . Nonperturbative renormalization prescriptionscitebib.bib115, bib.bib8, bib.bib116, bib.bib58, bib.bib65, bib.bib68 have already been employed in lattice simulations for both straight-line and staple operators using RI-type schemes. There are also studies of alternative prescriptions, such as the ratio (or short-distance ratio) schemecitebib.bib66, Wilson-line-mass-subtraction schemecitebib.bib117, bib.bib109, RI-xMOM scheme (using the auxiliary-field approachcitebib.bib103, bib.bib109, bib.bib118, bib.bib119, bib.bib120) and a hybrid renormalization schemecitebib.bib121. While a standard RI-type scheme (extended in the presence of mixing) can treat the various types of divergences and mixing of the nonlocal operators at one loop, recent nonperturbative examinationscitebib.bib122, bib.bib66 at different lattice spacings have provided compelling evidence indicating the existence of residual linear divergences in both straight-line and staple operators. In this regard, an alternative version of RI, as suggested in Refs. citebib.bib123, bib.bib65, which includes the computation of vacuum expectation value for rectangular Wilson loops, is also employed in our current work. This prescription (described in Sec. ) is expected to suppress the residual power divergences from the Green’s functions of the staple operators (see Sec. ). The paper is organized as follows: In Sec. , we provide the setup of our calculation, including the definition of the operators under study and the renormalization conditions that we employ throughout, corresponding to four different variants of the RI scheme. Sections and present our main results in dimensional and lattice regularization, respectively. This includes both the renormalization functions and conversion matrices between the RI-type and MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS schemes. In Sec. , we summarize and give some future plans. We have also included three appendices. In Appendix, we provide a list of formulae for the calculation, in dimensional regularization, of one-loop Feynman integrals appropriate to nonlocal operators. Appendix contains the definitions of Feynman-parameter integrals appearing in our results. In Appendix, we collect one-loop results for the renormalization of the quark field in both dimensional and lattice regularizations.

Other discretizations involve smeared gluon links, e. g. , stout, HYP, Wilson flow. We plan to investigate the impact of stout smearing at the one-loop level in future work. There is a total of 16 different operators which can be extracted from Eq. depending on the choice of the Dirac matrix ΓΓGamma inserted between the fermion and antifermion fields: Γ=𝟙Γ𝟙Gamma=openone (scalar S𝑆S), γ5≡γ1​γ2​γ3​γ4subscript𝛾5subscript𝛾1subscript𝛾2subscript𝛾3subscript𝛾4gamma5equivgamma1gamma2gamma3gamma4 (pseudoscalar P𝑃P), γμsubscript𝛾𝜇gammamu (vector Vμsubscript𝑉𝜇Vmu), γ5​γμsubscript𝛾5subscript𝛾𝜇gamma5gammamu (axial vector Aμsubscript𝐴𝜇Amu), σμ​ν≡[γμ,γν]/2subscript𝜎𝜇𝜈subscript𝛾𝜇subscript𝛾𝜈2sigmamunuequiv[gammamu,gammanu]/2 (tensor Tμ​νsubscript𝑇𝜇𝜈Tmunu). Of particular interest is the study of vector, axial-vector, and tensor operators, which correspond to the unpolarized, helicity, and transversity types of TMDPDFs, respectively. Since we are working in one-loop perturbation theory with massless fermions, the flavor content of the fermion fields ψ𝜓psi and ψ¯¯𝜓barpsi is irrelevant for our calculations up to this order. Thus, our one-loop results are valid for both flavor singlet and nonsinglet operators, given that there is no additional mixing with gluon nonlocal operatorscitebib.bib119, bib.bib70.
content_sections.II Calculation setup II.2 Symmetry prope: We examine below the properties of staple operators under symmetry transformations. Similar investigations can be found incitebib.bib65, bib.bib68. Operators with the same behavior under all symmetries can mix among themselves. In this way, one can identify the mixing pattern of the staple operators based on nonperturbative arguments. Since the staple operators depend on two special directions (ν1subscript𝜈1hatnu1 and ν2subscript𝜈2hatnu2) in which the staple is defined, we consider appropriate versions of the symmetry transformations of the QCD action (in Euclidean space) with respect to the special directions. 1. Translational symmetry: The operators are covariant under translations in Euclidean space. Similarly to the case of local operators 𝒪​(x)𝒪𝑥cal O(x) which cannot mix with translated versions of themselves (𝒪​(y)𝒪𝑦cal O(y)), mixing among nonlocal operators involving different paths and shapes for the Wilson line joining the fermion-antifermion pair cannot occurcitebib.bib98, bib.bib100, bib.bib103. 2. Two-dimensional (2D) rotational (or square, on the lattice) symmetry: The operators are covariant under rotations over the two-dimensional (2D) plane transverse to the plane in which the staple is defined. The 16 staple operators are classified into two representations of the 2D rotational group: scalar: S,P,Vν1,Vν2,Aν1,Aν2,Tν1​ν2,Tν3​ν4𝑆𝑃subscript𝑉subscript𝜈1subscript𝑉subscript𝜈2subscript𝐴subscript𝜈1subscript𝐴subscript𝜈2subscript𝑇subscript𝜈1subscript𝜈2subscript𝑇subscript𝜈3subscript𝜈4S,P,Vnu1,Vnu2,Anu1,Anu2,Tnu1nu2,Tnu3nu4, vector: (Vν3,Vν4),(Aν3,Aν4),(Tν3​ν1,Tν4​ν1),(Tν3​ν2,Tν4​ν2)subscript𝑉subscript𝜈3subscript𝑉subscript𝜈4subscript𝐴subscript𝜈3subscript𝐴subscript𝜈4subscript𝑇subscript𝜈3subscript𝜈1subscript𝑇subscript𝜈4subscript𝜈1subscript𝑇subscript𝜈3subscript𝜈2subscript𝑇subscript𝜈4subscript𝜈2(Vnu3,Vnu4),(Anu3,Anu4),(Tnu3nu1,Tnu4nu1),(Tnu3nu2,Tnu4nu2), where (ν1,ν2,ν3,ν4)subscript𝜈1subscript𝜈2subscript𝜈3subscript𝜈4(nu1,nu2,nu3,nu4) correspond to different orthogonal directions in the 4D Euclidean space. The first representation is one-dimensional while the second is two-dimensional reducible (e. g. , (Vν3,Vν4)subscript𝑉subscript𝜈3subscript𝑉subscript𝜈4(Vnu3,Vnu4) splits into two different one-dimensional representations: Vν3+i​Vν4subscript𝑉subscript𝜈3𝑖subscript𝑉subscript𝜈4Vnu3+iVnu4 and Vν3−i​Vν4subscript𝑉subscript𝜈3𝑖subscript𝑉subscript𝜈4Vnu3-iVnu4). Operator mixing can only occur among operators that support the same irreducible representation. The fact that an operator such as Vν3+i​Vν4subscript𝑉subscript𝜈3𝑖subscript𝑉subscript𝜈4Vnu3+iVnu4 can mix with Aν3+i​Aν4subscript𝐴subscript𝜈3𝑖subscript𝐴subscript𝜈4Anu3+iAnu4 but not with Aν3−i​Aν4subscript𝐴subscript𝜈3𝑖subscript𝐴subscript𝜈4Anu3-iAnu4 implies certain relations among the corresponding renormalization and mixing coefficients. Thus, in contrast to the local operators, which are covariant under 4D rotations, the residual rotational symmetry cannot completely prevent mixing between scalar, pseudoscalar, vector, axial-vector, and tensor operators. Also, operators which support the same representation of the 4D rotational group but different representations of the 2D rotational group, e. g. Vν1subscript𝑉subscript𝜈1Vnu1 (or Vν2subscript𝑉subscript𝜈2Vnu2) and Vρ≠(ν1,ν2)subscript𝑉𝜌subscript𝜈1subscript𝜈2Vrhoneq(nu1,nu2), will not share the same renormalization factor (in contrast to the case of local operators); at least, the 2D rotational symmetry prevents the mixing among these operators. 3. Parity (𝒫𝒫P): In Euclidean space, temporal and spatial directions are not distinguished. Thus, parity can be generalized in any direction. The generalized parity transformations 𝒫μsubscript𝒫𝜇Pmu for the fermion and gluon fields with respect to the direction μ𝜇mu are defined below. Charge conjugation does not provide any additional information on the mixing of asymmetric staple operators compared to the previously mentioned symmetries. We conclude that when the fermion action breaks chiral symmetry (e. g. , Wilson/clover fermions, twisted-mass fermions), mixing among the asymmetric staple operators (𝒪Γ,𝒪Γ​γν1​γν2,𝒪Γ​γν1,𝒪Γ​γν2)subscript𝒪Γsubscript𝒪Γsubscript𝛾subscript𝜈1subscript𝛾subscript𝜈2subscript𝒪Γsubscript𝛾subscript𝜈1subscript𝒪Γsubscript𝛾subscript𝜈2(OGamma,OGammagammanu1gammanu2,OGammagammanu1,OGammagammanu2) is allowed. When chiral fermions (e. g. , massless overlap/domain-wall/continuum fermions) are employed, the mixing pattern is minimized to (𝒪Γ,𝒪Γ​γν1​γν2)subscript𝒪Γsubscript𝒪Γsubscript𝛾subscript𝜈1subscript𝛾subscript𝜈2(OGamma,OGammagammanu1gammanu2). In the specific case of symmetric staple operators (y′=ysuperscript𝑦′𝑦yprime=y), the basis of independent operators is reduced (𝒪i=0subscript𝒪𝑖0widetildeOi=0, for i>4𝑖4i>4) and the mixing pattern takes the form: (𝒪Γ,𝒪[Γ,γν1​γν2]/2,𝒪[Γ,γν1]/2,𝒪[Γ,γν2]/2)subscript𝒪Γsubscript𝒪Γsubscript𝛾subscript𝜈1subscript𝛾subscript𝜈22subscript𝒪Γsubscript𝛾subscript𝜈12subscript𝒪Γsubscript𝛾subscript𝜈22(OGamma,O[Gamma,gammanu1gammanu2]/2,O[Gamma,gammanu1]/2,O[Gamma,gammanu2]/2) for nonchiral fermions and (𝒪Γ,𝒪[Γ,γν1​γν2]/2)subscript𝒪Γsubscript𝒪Γsubscript𝛾subscript𝜈1subscript𝛾subscript𝜈22(OGamma,O[Gamma,gammanu1gammanu2]/2) for chiral fermions. Depending on the Dirac matrix ΓΓGamma, one or three out of the three commutators [Γ,γν1]Γsubscript𝛾subscript𝜈1[Gamma,gammanu1], [Γ,γν2]Γsubscript𝛾subscript𝜈2[Gamma,gammanu2], [Γ,γν1​γν2]Γsubscript𝛾subscript𝜈1subscript𝛾subscript𝜈2[Gamma,gammanu1gammanu2] appearing in the mixing pattern of the symmmetric staple operators with nonchiral fermions will be zero, thus leading to a mixing triplet or singlet (multiplicatively renormalizable operator), respectively. Similarly, when [Γ,γν1​γν2]≠0Γsubscript𝛾subscript𝜈1subscript𝛾subscript𝜈20[Gamma,gammanu1gammanu2]neq 0, the mixing pattern of the symmetric staple operators with chiral fermions gives a mixing pair; otherwise it leads to a multiplicatively renormalizable operator. In contrast to the symmetric case, symmetry arguments alone are not sufficient to establish multiplicative renormalization for any asymmetric operator. However, all-order perturbative studies in the continuum show that a multiplicative renormalization can address all the divergences of the nonlocal Wilson-line operatorscitebib.bib98, bib.bib100, bib.bib103. Thus, the mixing which is allowed by symmetries is absent in minimal subtraction schemes. However, it can occur as finite mixing in nonminimal schemes. On the lattice, the mixing is present when employing MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS or any nonperturbative intermediate scheme; while MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS is a minimal continuum scheme, on the lattice, additional finite regularization-dependent terms contribute to the renormalization functions in order to be able to match the continuum MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS-renormalized Green’s functions. In our work, we focus on the wider mixing pattern arising in the case of asymmetric staple operators when a chirality-breaking fermion action is employed. Our goal is to construct a common prescription for renormalizing the staple operators in any regularization (regularization independent). To this end, we consider the following quadruplets of the sixteen independent bilinear operators 𝒪Γsubscript𝒪ΓOGamma (with Γ∈SiΓsubscript𝑆𝑖Gammain Si)3There is freedom in choosing the signs in front of each operator, leading to different conventions. : S1subscript𝑆1displaystyle S1 ≡displaystyleequiv 𝟙,σν𝟙​ν𝟚,γν𝟙,γν𝟚,displaystyle openone , sigmanu1nu2, gammanu1 , gammanu2 , (II.2.33) S2subscript𝑆2displaystyle S2 ≡displaystyleequiv γ5,σν4​ν3,γ5​γν1,γ5​γν2,subscript𝛾5subscript𝜎subscript𝜈4subscript𝜈3subscript𝛾5subscript𝛾subscript𝜈1subscript𝛾5subscript𝛾subscript𝜈2displaystyle gamma5 , sigmanu4nu3, gamma5gammanu1, gamma5gammanu2 , (II.2.34) S3subscript𝑆3displaystyle S3 ≡displaystyleequiv γν3,γ5​γν4,σν3​ν1,σν3​ν2,subscript𝛾subscript𝜈3subscript𝛾5subscript𝛾subscript𝜈4subscript𝜎subscript𝜈3subscript𝜈1subscript𝜎subscript𝜈3subscript𝜈2displaystyle gammanu3, gamma5gammanu4, sigmanu3nu1, sigmanu3nu2 , (II.2.35) S4subscript𝑆4displaystyle S4 ≡displaystyleequiv γν4,γ5​γν3,σν4​ν1,σν4​ν2. subscript𝛾subscript𝜈4subscript𝛾5subscript𝛾subscript𝜈3subscript𝜎subscript𝜈4subscript𝜈1subscript𝜎subscript𝜈4subscript𝜈2displaystyle gammanu4, gamma5gammanu3, sigmanu4nu1, sigmanu4nu2 . (II.2.36) In the present study, we do not consider possible mixing (on the lattice) with higher dimensional operators multiplied by the appropriate power of the lattice spacing. In the case of local operators, such mixing is present only for finite values of the lattice spacing, and it vanishes when taking the continuum limit. However, in the case of nonlocal operators, where power divergences a−n,n∈ℤ+superscript𝑎𝑛𝑛superscriptℤa-n, ninZ+ are present, 𝒪​(a)𝒪𝑎O(a) effects in the bare Green’s functions can contribute to the renormalized Green’s functions at two loops4𝒪​(a​g2)𝒪𝑎superscript𝑔2O(a g2) terms from the bare Green’s function multiplied by 𝒪​(1/a​g2)𝒪1𝑎superscript𝑔2O(1/a g2) terms from the renormalization function lead to 𝒪​(a0​g4)𝒪superscript𝑎0superscript𝑔4O(a0 g4) contributions to the renormalized Green’s function. . Alternatively, one can suppress these unwanted effects in two ways: by removing power divergences from the Green’s function through an appropriate ratio with another Green’s function which has the same power divergences, e. g. , a closed Wilson loop, by subtracting artifacts from the bare Green’s functions calculated in lattice perturbation theory. Our group has successfully applied this method to the renormalization of local quark bilinear operatorscitebib.bib124, bib.bib125, bib.bib126, and more recently to the renormalization of nonlocal straight Wilson-line operators for quasi-PDFscitebib.bib107. We plan to study one-loop discretization effects for the staple operators to all orders in the lattice spacing in future work.
content_sections.II Calculation setup II.3 Green’s functi: As is standard practice, one-particle-irreducible (1-PI) two-point amputated Green’s functions of the operators under study with external elementary fields, e. g. , fermion fields, can be used for the extraction of renormalization functions 5The removal of the standard momentum-space delta functions which appear in Green’s functions such as Eq. and Eq. is understood. : ΛΓ​(q,z,y,y′)=∑x⟨ψ​(q)|𝒪Γ​(x,z,y,y′)|ψ¯​(q)⟩amp. . subscriptΛΓ𝑞𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′subscript𝑥subscriptquantum-operator-product𝜓𝑞subscript𝒪Γ𝑥𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′¯𝜓𝑞ampLambdaGamma(q,z,y,yprime)=sumxpsi(q)|OGamma(x,z,y,yprime)|barpsi(q)rm amp. . (II.3.1) A summation over the position of the staple-shaped operator is taken, which is allowed by translational symmetry, in order to simplify the calculations. Such Green’s functions using local operators are easily calculated in continuum perturbation theory to very high order. However, due to the nonlocal nature of the staple-shaped operators, additional scales (staple lengths) appear in Green’s functions, which make the computation more complex even at the one-loop level. The corresponding calculation on the lattice is even more demanding since the procedure for isolating divergences from the Feynman integrals, as well as the procedure for taking the continuum limit a→0→𝑎0arightarrow 0 (where a𝑎a is the lattice spacing) are more complicated (see, e. g. , Refs. citebib.bib105, bib.bib108). There are four one-loop Feynman diagrams contributing to ΛΓ​(q,z,y,y′)subscriptΛΓ𝑞𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′LambdaGamma(q,z,y,yprime), shown in Fig. . These diagrams will appear in both continuum and lattice regularizations since all vertices are present in both regularizations. To this perturbative order, zero (d1subscript𝑑1d1), one (d2−d3subscript𝑑2subscript𝑑3d2-d3) or two (d4subscript𝑑4d4) gluons stem from the staple-shaped Wilson line. Due to the shape of the staple, the diagrams are further divided into thirteen subdiagrams, shown in Fig. , depending on the side of the staple from which gluons emanate. content_sections.II Calculation setup II.4 Renormalizatio: We formulate below different versions of appropriate regularization-independent (RI) prescriptions which address all possible divergences and mixing of the staple-shaped operators. In contrast to our previous study regarding the renormalization of symmetric staple operators, we construct renormalization matrices that address the mixing as observed by studying symmetries and not by studying one-loop lattice perturbation theory; thus, the renormalization matrices will be 4×4444times 4. Several of the nondiagonal elements of these matrices will vanish at one-loop. In this way, the renormalization prescription will be more appropriate for nonperturbative calculations addressing possible mixing that can be seen in higher loops. We first give our conventions regarding the renormalization of the operators under study, as well as of the relevant elementary fields and parameters that enter our perturbative calculations: 𝒪ΓR​(x,z,y,y′)=ZΓ​Γ′R,X​𝒪Γ′X​(x,z,y,y′),ψR​(x)=(ZψR,X)1/2​ψX​(x),gR=μ(4−d)/2​ZgR,X​gX,formulae-sequencesubscriptsuperscript𝒪𝑅Γ𝑥𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′subscriptsuperscript𝑍𝑅𝑋ΓsuperscriptΓ′subscriptsuperscript𝒪𝑋superscriptΓ′𝑥𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′formulae-sequencesuperscript𝜓𝑅𝑥superscriptsubscriptsuperscript𝑍𝑅𝑋𝜓12superscript𝜓𝑋𝑥superscript𝑔𝑅superscript𝜇4𝑑2subscriptsuperscript𝑍𝑅𝑋𝑔superscript𝑔𝑋ORGamma(x,z,y,yprime)=ZR,XGammaGammaprimeOXGammaprime(x,z,y,yprime),qquadpsiR(x)=(ZR,Xpsi)1/2psiX(x),qquad gR=mu(4-d)/2ZR,XggX, (II.4.1) where ψX​(ψR)superscript𝜓𝑋superscript𝜓𝑅psiX(psiR) is the bare (renormalized) fermion field, gX​(gR)superscript𝑔𝑋superscript𝑔𝑅gX(gR) is the bare (renormalized) coupling constant. Use of these two choices of projectors amounts to the implementation of two different RI′superscriptRI′rm RIprime prescriptions, which we will denote as RI1′subscriptsuperscriptRI′1rm RIprime1, RI2′subscriptsuperscriptRI′2rm RIprime2 from now on. Compared to the first choice of projectors, the second one can further remove finite contributions of some Dirac structures, allowed by Lorentz symmetry, from the elements of the renormalization matrices. Similar projectors have also been studied in the renormalization of local operators, leading to reduced contributions from hadronic contamination in the nonperturbative data, especially for small values of the renormalization scale q¯¯𝑞barq. The RI scheme can address the power8Some power divergences may remain beyond one loopcitebib.bib66. and logarithmic divergences, as well as the mixing between different Dirac structures, in the same way as MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS does. However, in contrast to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme, RI can also treat the pinch-pole singularity when y→∞→𝑦yrightarrowinfty. This means that this infinite limit can be taken only in the RI-renormalized Green’s functions and not in the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS-renormalized Green’s functions of the staple operators. This is true because both schemes are defined for finite values of y𝑦y, where pinch-pole singularities are not present. Then, terms that diverge with y𝑦y (in the y→∞→𝑦yrightarrowinfty limit) do not contribute to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS renormalization function, but they do so in RI. Thus, when multiplying the bare Green’s functions with their renormalization functions, only in RI these terms are eliminated. However, since RI is just an intermediate scheme entering the procedure of renormalizing the operators in the reference scheme MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS, nonperturbatively, we cannot benefit from this additional feature of RI; after conversion to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme, the pinch-pole singularity comes back. Thus, the standard MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS prescription is not appropriate for renormalizing staple operators in the infinite limit y→∞→𝑦yrightarrowinfty. ∙∙bullet Alternative prescriptions: Alternative prescriptions which are also applicable in the limit y→∞→𝑦yrightarrowinfty are described below. The main feature in these prescriptions stems from the fact that the same pinch-pole singularity arises in a closed Wilson loop. Also, this object shows power and cusp divergences as the staple-shaped operators. As proposed in Ref. citebib.bib65, we can redefine the standard staple-shaped operator 𝒪Γsubscript𝒪ΓOGamma by dividing it with the square root of the vacuum expectation value of a rectangular z×(y+y′)𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′ztimes(y+yprime) Wilson loop L​(z,y+y′)𝐿𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′L(z,y+yprime): 𝒪¯Γ​(x,z,y,y′)≡𝒪Γ​(x,z,y,y′)⟨L​(z,y+y′)⟩1/2,subscript¯𝒪Γ𝑥𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′subscript𝒪Γ𝑥𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′superscriptdelimited-⟨⟩𝐿𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′12overlineOGamma(x,z,y,yprime)equivfracOGamma(x,z,y,yprime) L(z,y+yprime)1/2, (II.4.7) where L​(z,y+y′)≡1Nc​Tr​[𝒫​𝒰​(x,(y+y′)​ν2)​𝒰​(x+(y+y′)​ν2,z​ν1)​𝒰†​(x+z​ν1,(y+y′)​ν2)​𝒰†​(x,z​ν1)]. (II.4.11) Z¯Γ​Γ′R,Xsubscriptsuperscript¯𝑍𝑅𝑋ΓsuperscriptΓ′overlineZR,XGammaGammaprime addresses the remaining end-point divergences, as well as the mixing. In order to extract Z¯Γ​Γ′R,Xsubscriptsuperscript¯𝑍𝑅𝑋ΓsuperscriptΓ′overlineZR,XGammaGammaprime, we need to calculate the one-loop Feynman diagrams of Fig. contributing to ⟨LX​(z,y+y′)⟩delimited-⟨⟩superscript𝐿𝑋𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′ LX(z,y+yprime). Older studies of Wilson loops can be found, e. g. , in Refs. citebib.bib123, bib.bib128 in both continuum and lattice using Wilson gluons. Here, we extend these calculations to the case of Symanzik-improved gluons. For completeness, we have also repeated the continuum calculation. Since the end-point divergences do not depend on the dimensions of the staple10On the contrary, power divergences depend on the total length of the staple line and thus, renormalization functions in ordinary RI must be computed separately for each choice of the dimensions of the staple. , a nonperturbative determination of Z¯Γ​Γ′R,Xsubscriptsuperscript¯𝑍𝑅𝑋ΓsuperscriptΓ′overlineZR,XGammaGammaprime is expected to exhibit a much milder dependence on the staple lengths z,y,y′𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′z,y,yprime which lie in the renormalization window: a≪z≪ΛQCD−1much-less-than𝑎𝑧much-less-thansuperscriptsubscriptΛQCD1all zllLambdarm QCD-1, a≪y≪ΛQCD−1much-less-than𝑎𝑦much-less-thansuperscriptsubscriptΛQCD1all yllLambdarm QCD-1, a≪y′≪ΛQCD−1much-less-than𝑎superscript𝑦′much-less-thansuperscriptsubscriptΛQCD1all yprimellLambdarm QCD-1. In this way it becomes more acceptable to renormalize the modified operators 𝒪¯Γsubscript¯𝒪ΓoverlineOGamma defined at large values of the lengths z,y,y′𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′z,y,yprime, using renormalization functions Z¯Γ​Γ′R​I′,Xsubscriptsuperscript¯𝑍𝑅superscript𝐼′𝑋ΓsuperscriptΓ′overlineZRIprime,XGammaGammaprime defined at smaller values of z,y,y′𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′z,y,yprime within the perturbative region. To distinguish the lengths appearing in the bare operators from the reference lengths appearing in the renormalization functions, we will call the latter as z¯,y¯,y¯′¯𝑧¯𝑦superscript¯𝑦′barz,bary,baryprime. If one is interested in taking the limit y→∞→𝑦yrightarrowinfty, then there are only two relevant lengths: z¯¯𝑧barz and y¯−y¯′¯𝑦superscript¯𝑦′bary-baryprime. Another option that we do not consider in this work is the short-distance ratio (SDR) scheme, described in Ref. citebib.bib66. In this scheme, ratios of hadron matrix elements of the modified operators at different external momenta are considered. All the good features of RI-bar are also valid in the SDR scheme. However, we stress that SDR is valid when operator mixing is absent or negligible.

Also, the term [y/z​tan−1⁡(y/z)+y′/z​tan−1⁡(y′/z)]delimited-𝑦𝑧superscript1𝑦𝑧superscript𝑦′𝑧superscript1superscript𝑦′𝑧[y/ztan-1(y/z)+yprime/ztan-1(yprime/z)] appearing in s0subscript𝑠0s0 (Eq. ) gives rise to a pinch-pole singularity (linear divergence) in the limit y→∞→𝑦yrightarrowinfty for fixed values of (y−y′)𝑦superscript𝑦′(y-yprime). This term stems from the sub-diagram d4​esubscript𝑑4𝑒d4e. Our results agree with previous studies in Refs. citebib.bib108, bib.bib123 which consider specific cases of the staple operators. The different Dirac structures appearing in Eqs. ( – ) can be classified into two categories: the structures corresponding to the tree-level Green’s functions of the staple operators that belong to the same mixing set according to symmetries (structures multiplied by Σi,1subscriptΣ𝑖1Sigmai,1 or Σi,2subscriptΣ𝑖2Sigmai,2), and all the remaining structures. The former structures are multiplied by regularization-dependent form factors (Σi,jsubscriptΣ𝑖𝑗Sigmai,j), while the form factors of the latter structures do not depend on regularization. This is confirmed by comparing our one-loop results in both continuum and lattice regularizations (Eq. ). In particular, the bare Green’s functions in the continuum consist of two instead of four structures of the first category because chiral symmetry is preserved (see discussion in Sec. ). On the other hand, the lattice bare Green’s functions at one loop also contain a pair of the four first-category structures, which is different from that obtained in DR (see Sec. ). Since the lattice regularization that we employ breaks chiral symmetry, we expect that the missing two structures of the first category will appear in higher loops. By taking into account the classification of the Dirac structures in the Green’s functions, we conclude that a renormalization prescription that considers a wider mixing pattern, i. e. , mixing among all 16 independent operators of different ΓΓGamma matrices, is not an optimal choice for renormalizing the staple operators. In this case, several form factors Σi,jsubscriptΣ𝑖𝑗Sigmai,j will contribute to the 16×16161616times 16 mixing matrix, including pure dependence on the regularization-independent form factors Σi,j>2subscriptΣ𝑖𝑗2Sigmai,j>2 in some nondiagonal elements. Given that we are interested in matching bare lattice Green’s functions to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme (through an intermediate nonperturbative scheme), in which the regularization-independent form factors cannot contribute, it is more economic to consider an intermediate renormalization prescription that includes only the minimum number of operators necessary for disentangling the mixing occurring in the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme on the lattice. In our study, we consider such a minimal set of operators as dictated by symmetries. The one-loop renormalized Green’s functions in the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme can be obtained by removing the 𝒪​(1/ε)𝒪1𝜀O(1/varepsilon) term in Eq. . Also, the renormalized Green’s functions in any variant of the RI scheme can be obtained by imposing the corresponding renormalization conditions to the above results. The extraction of the conversion functions among these schemes (RI variants and MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS) is then straightforward; they are given in Eqs. (, – ) for the schemes under study. Specific combinations of the form factors Σi,jsubscriptΣ𝑖𝑗Sigmai,j contribute in the renormalization conditions of Eq. for each choice of projectors 𝒫Γsuperscriptsubscript𝒫Γdelimited-1PGamma and 𝒫Γsuperscriptsubscript𝒫Γdelimited-2PGamma. The latter choice has the advantage that in most cases, only Σi,1subscriptΣ𝑖1Sigmai,1 and Σi,2subscriptΣ𝑖2Sigmai,2 survive, which multiply the relevant structures for the study of mixing. (III.3.1) The conversion matrices are regularization (X) independent, and thus, our results in DR are also applicable to the lattice. They can be used in lattice simulations in order to translate the renormalized hadron matrix elements of the staple operators, such as the quasi-beam functioncitebib.bib68 and the soft functioncitebib.bib62, from the intermediate RI-type schemes to MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS. (III.3.38) Another property of the conversion matrices comes from the combination of 𝒫μsubscript𝒫𝜇Pmu, 𝒯μ,𝒞subscript𝒯𝜇𝒞Tmu,C symmetries: one can prove (see Eq. ) that the real (imaginary) parts of the conversion matrices are even (odd) under (z→−z→𝑧𝑧zrightarrow-z, y→−y→𝑦𝑦yrightarrow-y, y′→−y′→superscript𝑦′superscript𝑦′yprimerightarrow-yprime). This is confirmed by our one-loop computation. We illustrate our results for the conversion matrices in the plots of Figs. ( – ) by employing certain values of the free parameters used in lattice simulations: μ¯=2¯𝜇2barmu=2 GeV, β=1𝛽1beta=1 (Landau gauge), a​q¯=(2​πL​n1,2​πL​n2,2​πL​n3,2​πT​(n4+12))𝑎¯𝑞2𝜋𝐿subscript𝑛12𝜋𝐿subscript𝑛22𝜋𝐿subscript𝑛32𝜋𝑇subscript𝑛412abarq=(frac2piLn1,frac2piLn2,frac2piLn3,frac2piT(n4+frac12)), where a𝑎a is the lattice spacing, L3×Tsuperscript𝐿3𝑇L3times T is the lattice volume and ni∈ℤsubscript𝑛𝑖ℤniinZ. Following simulations by ETMC, we choose isotropic momentum scales in the spatial directions (n1=n2=n3subscript𝑛1subscript𝑛2subscript𝑛3n1=n2=n3) and a nonzero twist of 1/2121/2 in the temporal component; the latter choice is compatible with the antiperiodic boundary conditions applied on the fermion fields in the temporal direction. As an example, we apply n1=n2=n3=4subscript𝑛1subscript𝑛2subscript𝑛34n1=n2=n3=4, n4=5subscript𝑛45n4=5, L=32𝐿32L=32, T=64𝑇64T=64 and a=0.09𝑎0.09a=0.09 fm. We note that specific choices of nisubscript𝑛𝑖ni can lead to a vanishing imaginary part. In particular, by setting to zero the two momentum components parallel to the staple segments, the one-loop expression for the conversion matrices becomes purely real. However, such a choice of momentum gives rise to unwanted Lorentz noninvariant contributions in the nonperturbative calculations. Thus, in our example, we follow the common practice of employing democratic momenta with reduced Lorentz noninvariant contributions at the cost of introducing an imaginary part in the RI-type renormalization matrices and the conversion matrices. In Figs. ( – ), we examine the dependence of some representative conversion matrix elements on the Wilson-line scales y𝑦y, z𝑧z, and y−y′𝑦superscript𝑦′y-yprime (in lattice units), respectively. In particular, we plot the real and imaginary parts of the 2×2222times 2 block CΓ​Γ′MS¯,RI′subscriptsuperscript𝐶¯MSsuperscriptRI′ΓsuperscriptΓ′Coverlinerm MS,rm RIprimeGammaGammaprime, (Γ,Γ′=γν1,γν2)formulae-sequenceΓsuperscriptΓ′subscript𝛾subscript𝜈1subscript𝛾subscript𝜈2(Gamma,Gammaprime=gammanu1,gammanu2), for the four renormalization prescriptions: RI1′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′1prime1, RI2′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′2prime2, RI1′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′1prime1-bar, RI2′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′2prime2-bar. As shown in Fig. , the real part of the diagonal elements (first plot in the left column and second plot in the right column) has an almost linear (flat) dependence on y/a𝑦𝑎y/a in the RIi′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′𝑖primei (RIi′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′𝑖primei-bar) schemes due to the presence (absence) of the pinch-pole singularity. As expected, the convergent behavior of RIi′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′𝑖primei-bar appears for large values of y/a𝑦𝑎y/a (≳6greater-than-or-equivalent-toabsent6gtrsim 6). There are no significant differences in the real diagonal elements between schemes with index 1 and 2. The real part of the nondiagonal elements (first plot in the second column and second plot in the first column of Fig. ) also converges for large values of y/a𝑦𝑎y/a (≳8greater-than-or-equivalent-toabsent8gtrsim 8) for all RI-type schemes. Note that the nondiagonal elements are identical between RIi′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′𝑖primei and RIi′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′𝑖primei-bar schemes (for the same i𝑖i) at one loop. Differences between schemes with index 1 and 2 are now visible: the real nondiagonal elements for the RI2′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′2prime2 scheme have smaller absolute values compared to RI1′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′1prime1, which are closer to zero. The relative size of the real nondiagonal elements compared to the diagonal ones is ∼20%similar-toabsentpercent20sim 20%. The imaginary part of the conversion matrix elements (the four plots in the last two rows of Fig. ) gives a much milder contribution compared to the real one (≲10%less-than-or-similar-toabsentpercent10lesssim 10%). The imaginary (diagonal and nondiagonal) elements for RIi′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′𝑖primei-bar coincide with the corresponding elements for RIi′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′𝑖primei (for the same i𝑖i) at one loop. A plateau is observed at large values of y/a𝑦𝑎y/a; a more stable behavior is seen for the nondiagonal elements. As in the case of real nondiagonal elements, noticeable distinctions can also be spotted in the imaginary parts between schemes with index 1 and 2. However, there is no consistent pattern regarding which scheme yields smaller contributions, as it varies for each element. Similar conclusions are extracted from Fig. by considering the dependence of the conversion matrix elements on z/a𝑧𝑎z/a. Here, we observe a convergent dependence for z/a≳6greater-than-or-equivalent-to𝑧𝑎6z/agtrsim 6 for all conversion matrix elements, except for the imaginary diagonal parts (the four plots in the last two rows of Fig. ). Now, the real diagonal parts have flat behavior in both RIi′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′𝑖primei and RIi′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′𝑖primei-bar schemes since the pinch-pole singularity does not arise for large values of z/a𝑧𝑎z/a. Examining the dependence of the conversion matrix elements on (y−y′)/a𝑦superscript𝑦′𝑎(y-yprime)/a in Fig. , we conclude that a more flat behavior is observed for smaller values of (y−y′)/a𝑦superscript𝑦′𝑎(y-yprime)/a, while for larger values the elements decrease (in most cases) rapidly. An almost linear dependence on (y−y′)/a𝑦superscript𝑦′𝑎(y-yprime)/a with a negative slope is obtained in the real diagonal parts of the RIi′subscriptsuperscriptabsent′𝑖primei schemes (see the first plot in the first column and the second plot in the second column of Fig. ), coming from the pinch-pole divergent term. In summary, the conversion matrices exhibit significant contributions from the real diagonal components, whereas the imaginary diagonal and nondiagonal elements make comparatively milder yet perceptible contributions. Safer conclusions by comparing the different types of renormalization schemes can be obtained when combining the conversion matrices with the nonperturbative data.

Bigr],, (IV.1.1) where Uplaq. subscript𝑈plaqUrm plaq. and Urect. subscript𝑈rectUrm rect. are the standard 4-link “plaquette”, and 6-link “2×1212times1 rectangle” Wilson loops. We selected three of the most common choices of the Symanzik coefficients cisubscript𝑐𝑖ci, called Wilson, Tree-Level Symanzik, and Iwasaki gluon actions, as shown in Table . Since we consider mass-independent renormalization schemes, we set the quark mass equal to zero. Consequently, the results from this study are also applicable to the twisted mass fermions citebib.bib131 in the chiral limit. One should, however, keep in mind that, in going from the twisted basis to the physical basis, operator identifications are modified (e. g. , the scalar density, under “maximal twist”, turns into a pseudoscalar density, etc. ). The results for the one-loop lattice bare Green’s functions of the asymmetric staple operators ΛΓLRsuperscriptsubscriptΛΓLRLambdaGammarm LR are presented below in terms of the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS-renormalized Green’s functions, derived by the corresponding calculation in DR. The methodology for calculating the one-loop momentum integrals on the lattice is described in Refs. citebib.bib105, bib.bib108. Here, we cite results for a general Wilson-line lattice operator with n𝑛n cusps [(n+1)𝑛1(n+1) segments] and no self-intersections, as calculated in our previous study considering symmetric staplescitebib.bib108. We confirm that the formula constructed in the latter publication gives the correct result for the difference between the bare lattice Green’s functions and the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS-renormalized Green’s functions, also in the case of asymmetric staple operators studied in this work. Thus, for a general Wilson-line lattice operator we have: δ​ΛΓ𝛿subscriptΛΓdisplaystyledeltaLambdaGamma ≡displaystyleequiv ΛΓLR​(q,r,n,ℓ1,…,ℓn+1,νi,νf)−ΛΓMS¯​(q,r,n,ℓ1,…,ℓn+1,νi,νf)superscriptsubscriptΛΓLR𝑞𝑟𝑛subscriptℓ1…subscriptℓ𝑛1subscript𝜈𝑖subscript𝜈𝑓superscriptsubscriptΛΓ¯MS𝑞𝑟𝑛subscriptℓ1…subscriptℓ𝑛1subscript𝜈𝑖subscript𝜈𝑓displaystyleLambdaGammarm LR(q,r,n,ell1,ldots,elln+1,hatnui,hatnuf)-LambdaGammaoverlinerm MS(q,r,n,ell1,ldots,elln+1,hatnui,hatnuf) (IV.1.2) where r𝑟r is the 4-vector that connects the two endpoints of the Wilson line, ℓjsubscriptℓ𝑗ellj is the length of the jthsuperscript𝑗thjrm th straight-line segment and l≡∑j=1n+1ℓj𝑙superscriptsubscript𝑗1𝑛1subscriptℓ𝑗lequivsumj=1n+1ellj is the total length of the Wilson line, cS​Wsubscript𝑐𝑆𝑊cSW is the clover coefficient in the fermion action, and νi​(νf)subscript𝜈𝑖subscript𝜈𝑓hatnui (hatnuf) is the direction of the Wilson line in the initial (final) endpoint. The first two terms in the curly brackets of Eq. come from the sum of Feynman diagrams d2subscript𝑑2d2 and d3subscript𝑑3d3, while the last term comes from diagram d4subscript𝑑4d4. The expression for the case under study (asymmetric staple operator with two cusps) can be extracted by setting n=2𝑛2n=2, l=|z|+|y|+|y′|𝑙𝑧𝑦superscript𝑦′l=|z|+|y|+|yprime|, νi=sgn​(y)​ν2subscript𝜈𝑖sgn𝑦subscript𝜈2hatnui=rm sgn(y) hatnu2, and νf=−sgn​(y′)​ν2subscript𝜈𝑓sgnsuperscript𝑦′subscript𝜈2hatnuf=-rm sgn(yprime) hatnu2. The corresponding MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS-renormalized Green’s function ΛΓMS¯superscriptsubscriptΛΓ¯MSLambdaGammaoverlinerm MS can be read from Eqs. ( – ) by removing 1/ε1𝜀1/varepsilon terms. Conclusions from this calculation are summarized below: 1. Linear divergence: On the lattice, there is a linear divergence, which depends on the length of the Wilson line and the gluon action that is employed. This divergence comes from diagrams contributing to the Wilson-line self-energy. At one loop, the contributing diagram is only d4subscript𝑑4d4. 2. Logarithmic divergences: In both regularizations (DR and lattice), there are end-point logarithmic divergences, coming from diagrams d2subscript𝑑2d2 and d3subscript𝑑3d3, and cusp and contact logarithmic divergences, coming from diagram d4subscript𝑑4d4. The coefficients in front of these divergences are regularization-independent. 3. Operator mixing: Diagrams d2subscript𝑑2d2 and d3subscript𝑑3d3 give rise (upon summation) to the Dirac structure Γ′=(Γ​ν̸i+ν̸f​Γ)/2superscriptΓ′Γsubscriptitalic-ν̸𝑖subscriptitalic-ν̸𝑓Γ2Gammaprime=(Gammahatnotnui+hatnotnufGamma)/2, which differs from the tree-level structure ΓΓGamma of the operator. This indicates that operator mixing is present: In order to remove this additional structure, we need to renormalize the Wilson-line operators 𝒪Γsubscript𝒪ΓOGamma and 𝒪Γ′subscript𝒪superscriptΓ′OGammaprime as a doublet by introducing a 2×2222times 2 mixing renormalization matrix. However, as concluded by symmetries, the employment of 4×4444times 4 mixing matrices for renormalizing quadruplets of asymmetric staple-shaped operators is expected to be required at higher loops. The mixing contributions at one loop depend solely on the direction of the Wilson line entering the endpoints, regardless of the shape of the Wilson line. Also, the coefficient α2+α3​cSWsubscript𝛼2subscript𝛼3subscript𝑐SWalpha2+alpha3 crm SW in front of the structure Γ′superscriptΓ′Gammaprime depends on 𝓇𝓇r and cS​Wsubscript𝑐𝑆𝑊cSW; in particular, α2subscript𝛼2alpha2 vanishes when 𝓇=0𝓇0r=0. Thus, the one-loop mixing contributions originate from the chirality-breaking parts of the fermion action. As concluded by symmetries, these specific contributions are expected to be absent when a chiral-fermion action is employed. 4. Finite contributions: Diagram d1subscript𝑑1d1 is identical in DR and lattice regularization (up to discretization effects 𝒪​(a)𝒪𝑎O(a)) giving no contribution to the difference δ​ΛΓ𝛿subscriptΛΓdeltaLambdaGamma. Finite contributions of diagrams d2subscript𝑑2d2 and d3subscript𝑑3d3 stem only from the endpoints of the Wilson line. Any parts of a segment that do not include the endpoints give finite contributions which differ between DR and lattice regularization only by discretization effects. In diagram d4subscript𝑑4d4, the finite contributions come from the cusps and the straight-line segments of the Wilson line; they depend on both the number of cusps (n𝑛n) and the number of segments (n+1𝑛1n+1). Hence, the exact shape of the Wilson line does not affect the one-loop continuum and lattice Green’s functions of the Wilson-line operators differently; the only additional contributions on the lattice depend on the total length, the number of cusps, and the direction of the Wilson line entering the endpoints. content_sections.V Summary and Future plans: In this work, we present an extensive and comprehensive study of the renormalization of nonlocal quark bilinear operators featuring an asymmetric staple-shaped Wilson line. This project is motivated by the increased interest in studying TMDPDFs from lattice QCD using novel approaches, such as large momentum effective theory and short-distance factorization, which require matrix elements of the operators under study. More details can be found in the TMD Handbookcitebib.bib133. The analysis is based on a one-loop perturbative calculation of Green’s functions of such operators in both lattice and continuum (dimensional) regularizations. Based on our results, we identify the mixing pattern of these operators and propose renormalization prescriptions applicable to perturbative and nonperturbative data. More precisely, we discuss RI-type conditions by using different projectors that effectively address power and logarithmic divergences, as well as the finite mixing among operators with different Dirac structures. We have systematically analyzed the mixing patterns of these operators, leveraging symmetry arguments for both chiral and nonchiral fermions. We also introduce a variant of the RI prescription, which removes the pinch-pole singularities inherent in staple operators of infinite length by incorporating rectangular Wilson loops. This strategy also eliminates residual power divergences. Another novel aspect of this work is the extraction of the conversion factors to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme using the results in dimensional regularization. Our calculations are performed for arbitrary values of the renormalization momentum scale and the spatial dimensions of the staple. This ensures their applicability across a broad spectrum of nonperturbative investigations that may use the results of this work. Potential future work includes an ambitious extension of this calculation to two-loop perturbation theory. This direction has the potential to offer valuable insights and improvements to ongoing nonperturbative investigations. By performing higher-order loop calculations, we aim to refine the renormalization procedure and eliminate sources of systematic uncertainties. Additionally, we plan to calculate one-loop lattice discretization effects across a range of staple lengths and momentum scales, which has significant potential for enhancing nonperturbative estimates, particularly at short distances. Other possible directions include calculations with different lattice formulations, which might be interesting for the TMD community. Acknowledgements. G. S. and H. P. acknowledge financial support from the European Regional Development Fund and the Republic of Cyprus through the Research and Innovation Foundation under contract number EXCELLENCE/0421/0025. M. C. acknowledges financial support from the U. S. Department of Energy, Office of Nuclear Physics, Early Career Award under Grant No. DE-SC0020405. M. C. is grateful for the hospitality of the University of Cyprus during the completion of this manuscript under the project 3D-nucleon, ID number EXCELLENCE/0421/0043, co-financed by the European Regional Development Fund and the Republic of Cyprus through the Research and Innovation Foundation.

all_papers.title: Wilson Loops and Area Laws in Lattice Gauge Theory Tensor Networks
all_papers.abstract:
Tensor network states have been a very prominent tool for the study of
quantum many-body physics, thanks to their physically relevant entanglement
properties and their ability to encode symmetries. In the last few years, the
formalism has been extended and applied to theories with local symmetries to -
lattice gauge theories. In the contraction of tensor network states as well as
correlation functions of physical observables with respect to them, one uses
the so-called transfer operator, whose local properties dictate the long-range
behaviour of the state. In this work we study transfer operators of tensor
network states (in particular, PEPS - projected entangled pair states) in the
context of lattice gauge theories, and consider the implications of the local
symmetry on their structure and properties. We focus on the Wilson loop - a
nonlocal, gauge-invariant observable which is central to pure gauge theories,
whose long range decay behaviour probes the confinement or deconfinement of
static charges. Using the symmetry, we show how to handle its contraction, and
formulate conditions relating local properties to its decay fashion.

sections.5: Wilson, Confinement of quarks, Physical Review D 10, 2445 (1974).
sections.16: White, Density matrix formulation for quantum renormalization groups, Physical Review Letters 69, 2863 (1992).
sections.17: Orús, A practical introduction to tensor networks: Matrix product states and projected entangled pair states, Annals of Physics 349, 117 (2014).
sections.32: Zohar, Quantum simulation of lattice gauge theories in more than one space dimension – requirements, challenges, methods, arXiv:2106.04609 [quant-ph]  (2021).
sections.34: Wilson, Confinement of quarks, Physical Review D 10, 2445 (1974).

qualitative: Tensor network states, particularly PEPS, provide a powerful framework for studying lattice gauge theories by encoding local symmetries and capturing physically relevant entanglement structures.
The transfer operator in tensor network contractions governs the long-range behavior of correlation functions and is central to analyzing nonlocal observables like Wilson loops.
Wilson loops are nonlocal, gauge-invariant observables that probe the confinement or deconfinement phase of static charges in pure gauge theories.
Local symmetries in lattice gauge theories impose constraints on the structure of the transfer operator, which in turn determine the decay behavior of Wilson loops.

The renormalization of Wilson lines can be understood through the spectral properties of the transfer operator, with the emergence of area laws or perimeter laws depending on the symmetry structure and phase of the system.

The interplay between local symmetry and tensor network geometry leads to a natural renormalization group interpretation for Wilson loop observables, where the loop's behavior is determined by the fixed points of the transfer operator.
quantitative: The Wilson loop expectation value ⟨W(C)⟩ for a closed loop C follows an area law: ⟨W(C)⟩ ∝ exp(−σA), where A is the area enclosed by C and σ is the string tension.
In the deconfined phase, the Wilson loop exhibits a perimeter law: ⟨W(C)⟩ ∝ exp(−γL), where L is the perimeter of the loop and γ is a constant.
The transfer operator T has a dominant eigenvalue λ₀ = 1 (due to gauge invariance), and the next-largest eigenvalue λ₁ determines the correlation length ξ = −1 / ln|λ₁|.
The decay of Wilson loop expectation values is governed by the ratio |λ₁ / λ₀|, with exponential decay when |λ₁| < 1.
For PEPS with local gauge symmetry, the transfer operator satisfies a gauge-invariant constraint: T = ∑_g U_g ⊗ U_g^†, where U_g are local gauge transformations.
The area law for Wilson loops emerges when the transfer operator has a unique gapped ground state, leading to exponential decay with area A.

authors: Erez Zohar

content_sections.I Introduction: In recent years, tensor network states citebib.bib1 have been a very prominent tool, rooted in quantum information science, for the study of quantum many body systems and especially strongly correlated physics. In particular, Matrix Product States (MPS) citebib.bib2, bib.bib3 enable to study numerically and analytically physically relevant states, e. g. ground states of local many body Hamiltonians (that is, states exhibiting an entanglement entropy area law). In higher spatial dimensions, MPS generalize to PEPS - Projected Entangled Pair States citebib.bib3, bib.bib4. These are useful for the description of strongly correlated physics in two spatial dimensions and more. PEPS (and MPS) are constructed out of the contraction of local building blocks (tensors). They satisfy, by construction, the entanglement entropy area law (focusing on the physically relevant part of the Hilbert space) and allows the state to depend on very few local parameters and hence making it feasible for computations (compared with arbitrary states in the exponentially large Hilbert space). Furthermore, it also allows one to encode symmetries already on the single tensor level. By properly parametrizing the local tensors, a global symmetry of the whole PEPS under a symmetry group can be imposed citebib.bib5, bib.bib6. This way, one can generate families of ansatz states in which the symmetry group of the studied model is encoded by construction. While originally used mostly in the context of condensed matter physics, MPS and PEPS have recently been extended to the study of particle physics too - in particular, to lattice gauge theories, aiming at solving long standing open, non-perturbative questions of the standard model, such as the confinement of quarks citebib.bib7. Due to its running coupling citebib.bib8 Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD) which allows one to use perturbation theory in high energy scales (collider physics) thanks to asymptotic freedom, is strongly interacting in low energies, preventing the use of perturbative methods. Lattice Gauge Theories (LGTs) citebib.bib7 have been introduced to overcome this difficulty, first as tools for lattice regularization of gauge invariant field theories. They quickly became a very successful numerical approach. Combined with quantum Monte Carlo it has been applied to nonperturbative QCD computations, such as the hadronic spectrum citebib.bib9. However, quantum Monte Carlo does not allow for the direct observation of real time dynamics, and faces the fermionic sign problem in several important physical scenarios, not allowing one to probe some of the interesting exotic regions of the QCD phase diagram citebib.bib10, and this requires the use of other methods, with tensor networks being one such approach. The tensor network framework for lattice gauge theories has been rapidly growing in the last few years. For 1+1​d11𝑑1+1d systems, MPS have already been extensively used. This does not only include abstract formalistic descriptions of MPS with a local symmetry citebib.bib11, bib.bib12 or benchmarks of models that can be treated in other ways, such as, but not only, the Schwinger model citebib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20. Successful numerical studies of lattice gauge theories in 1+1​d11𝑑1+1d have been carried out even in scenarios which face the sign problem when approached with conventional methods (such as real time evolution citebib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23 and finite density citebib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27). This was done for both Abelian and non-Abelian models - see citebib.bib28 and references therein for a discussion of that. The application of tensor networks to higher dimensional lattice gauge theories has been discussed as well in the last few years citebib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib38. From the rather more abstract, or formalistic point of view, gauging mechanisms which lift globally invariant PEPS to locally invariant ones by adding a gauge field and entangling it to the matter properly were introduced and discussed citebib.bib39, bib.bib40. For a parallel approach in the action formalism - tensor field theory - which uses tensor networks (but not tensor network states), see citebib.bib41 and references therein. In this work, we will focus on a particular gauging mechanism - the one introduced in citebib.bib40 and used mostly with fermionic matter, for creating gauged Gaussian fermionic PEPS citebib.bib31, bib.bib32: special PEPS constructions which allow for the description of fermionic matter coupled to dynamical gauge fields. Their construction may be seen as a minimal coupling procedure on the level of states, which is not possible in general but could be done in the context of PEPS citebib.bib42. While in general numerical computations are hard and challenging for PEPS in two spatial dimensions and more, it has been shown that, when this particular construction is used, the PEPS may be contracted efficiently (allowing one to extract physical information) when combining with Monte-Carlo methods which do not suffer from the sign problem citebib.bib33. Variational Monte-Carlo then allows to find ground states of lattice gauge theory Hamiltonians when such states are used as ansatz states, which has already been demonstrated and benchmarked for a pure ℤ3subscriptℤ3Z3 lattice gauge theory in 2+1212+1 dimensions citebib.bib37. A question that has to be asked when a PEPS is studied, is how physical information can be extracted from the contracted state - computation of expectation values of observables and correlation functions. Thanks to the special structure of MPS, one may introduce a mathematical object called transfer matrix (or operator) citebib.bib43 to compute efficiently expectation values of observables and correlation functions. This may be extended to two dimensional PEPS, by first contracting the rows, obtaining effectively a chain of the rows which is an MPS, and considering its transfer matrix citebib.bib44. In this work, we will study such transfer operators of lattice gauge theory PEPS in two space dimensions. Gauge theories are special in the sense that they exhibit a local symmetry, responsible to mediating local interactions between the matter fields. This symmetry gives rise to many local constraints. All the physical states are invariant under gauge transformations - local transformations parametrized by the elements of the so-called gauge group. As a result, only gauge invariant observables and correlation functions - those which are invariant under local transformations - give rise to a nonvanishing expectation values. Thus, LGT PEPS admit a very special structure manifested in the local tensors citebib.bib30, bib.bib39, bib.bib40; in this work, we focus on the implications of the local symmetry on the transfer operators, and hence aim at using the symmetry to simplify the PEPS contraction, focusing on pure gauge theories (that is, without dynamical matter). In such scenarios, closed flux loops - usually referred to as the operators which create them, Wilson loops citebib.bib7 - are perhaps the most important observables (and almost the only possible gauge invariant one). The decay rule of large Wilson loops in pure gauge theories serves as probes for confinement of static charges: area law decay implies confinement, while a perimeter law - a deconfined (Coulomb) phase. Confinement implies a gapped, disordered phase, while deconfined phases are massless and ordered citebib.bib45. The local ingredients of the Wilson loop are not gauge invariant - only their combination along the nonlocal path preserves the symmetry. This means, that when computing it for a gauge invariant PEPS, the transfer operator formalism must be extended and modified, requiring the inclusion of various types of transfer matrices which construct this nonlocal observable. The different building blocks will also have special properties citebib.bib32, dictated by the special local symmetry, which will affect the behaviour of the Wilson loop and its decay. In this work, we will study the properties of transfer operators of gauge invariant PEPS. We will see how the symmetry affects the properties of the local tensors, and that thanks to it, some parts of the tensors may be excluded and ignored when a contraction is done (e. g. when combined with some numerical methods). We will also see how that affects the Wilson loop’s decay - that is, how local properties of the tensors dictate the decay of large Wilson loops. Note that PEPS have been previously used for the computation of Wilson loop expectation value in various cases - ℤ2subscriptℤ2Z2 string nets citebib.bib46, bib.bib47, as well as U​𝑈1U citebib.bib31 and S​U​𝑆𝑈2SU citebib.bib32 toy models; here we derive a general framework based on transfer matrix arguments and demonstrate with particular constructions We begin with briefly reviewing important preliminaries from group theory and lattice gauge theory, in section II; move on to formulating gauge invariant PEPS and reviewing their symmetry properties, in section III; in section IV we introduce the transfer operators - after a brief review of their general properties, we formulate the flux-free transfer operators for LGT PEPS, study their properties and use them to calculate the norm; section V focuses on the contraction of Wilson loop expectation values for LGT PEPS, studying the relevant transfer operators and deriving conditions for area and perimeter decay laws; finally, in section VI, we give an explicit illustration, including both analytical and numerical arguments, for a ℤ2subscriptℤ2Z2 lattice gauge theory. Throughout this work the Einstein summation convention (on doubly repeated indices) is assumed unless stated otherwise; with the only exception of irreducible representation indices, whose summation should not be assumed.
content_sections.II Mathematical and Physical Preliminari: Consider a group G𝐺G, which is either a finite or a compact Lie one. Each group element g∈G𝑔𝐺gin G may be represented by different unitary matrices Dj​(g)superscript𝐷𝑗𝑔Djleft(gright), labelled by the group’s irreducible representations (irreps) j𝑗j; the dimension of these matrices is referred to as the irrep dimension, dim​(j)dim𝑗textdimleft(jright) (e. g. 2​j+12𝑗12j+1 for S​U​𝑆𝑈2SU). In the Hilbert space ℋℋH of some quantum mechanical theory, we would like to consider transformations parametrized by the elements of G𝐺G. To do that, for each g∈G𝑔𝐺gin G we introduce a unitary operator θgsubscript𝜃𝑔thetag, and define it by its action on a basis states of the form |j​m⟩ket𝑗𝑚left|jmright. j𝑗j labels the irreducible representations of G𝐺G and m𝑚m is an index labelling all states within this representation - that is, all the states that may be mixed by the transformations θgsubscript𝜃𝑔thetag that act block-diagonally on the irreps: θg​|j​m⟩=|j​m′⟩​Dm′​mj​(g)subscript𝜃𝑔ket𝑗𝑚ket𝑗superscript𝑚′subscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑗superscript𝑚′𝑚𝑔thetagleft|jmright=left|jmprimeright Djmprimemleft(gright) We can hence express θgsubscript𝜃𝑔thetag as θg=⨁𝑗​Dm​nj​(g)​|j​m⟩​⟨j​n|subscript𝜃𝑔𝑗direct-sumsubscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑗𝑚𝑛𝑔ket𝑗𝑚bra𝑗𝑛thetag=undersetjbigoplusDjmnleft(gright)left|jmrightleft jnright| - and therefore the dimension of the irrep j𝑗j, dim​(j)dim𝑗textdimleft(jright), is also the dimension of ℋjsubscriptℋ𝑗Hj, the Hilbert subspace spanned by |j​m⟩ket𝑗𝑚left|jmright, which we call a multiplet. The Hilbert space may be seen as a direct sum of multiplet subspaces ℋ=⨁𝑗​ℋjℋ𝑗direct-sumsubscriptℋ𝑗H=undersetjbigoplusHj In general, quantum Hilbert spaces may contain more than one multiplet carrying the same irreducible representation. These transformations are sometimes referred to as right transformations, since they mix the multiplet elements |j​m⟩ket𝑗𝑚left|jmright, when seen as the components of a dim​(j)dim𝑗textdimleft(jright) dimensional vector, via right matrix multiplication, as shown in . One can also introduce the left transformations θg​|j​m⟩=Dm​m′j​(g)​|j​m′⟩subscript𝜃𝑔ket𝑗𝑚subscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑗𝑚superscript𝑚′𝑔ket𝑗superscript𝑚′tildethetagleft|jmright=Djmmprimeleft(gright)left|jmprimeright Note that the left transformations are not independent from the right ones: for each g∈G𝑔𝐺gin G one may find hℎh such that θg=θhsubscript𝜃𝑔subscript𝜃ℎtildethetag=thetah. We introduce the left transformations separately nevertheless since they will be mathematically convenient later when the PEPS are constructed. When G𝐺G is a compact Lie group, its elements may be uniquely identified in terms of group parameters or coordinates ϕasubscriptitalic-ϕ𝑎phia; then, for each irrep j𝑗j, Dj​(g)=exp​(i​ϕa​(g)​Taj)superscript𝐷𝑗𝑔exp𝑖subscriptitalic-ϕ𝑎𝑔subscriptsuperscript𝑇𝑗𝑎Djleft(gright)=textexpleft(iphialeft(gright)Tjaright) - the parameters ϕa​(g)subscriptitalic-ϕ𝑎𝑔phialeft(gright) depend on the group element, while the generators Tajsubscriptsuperscript𝑇𝑗𝑎Tja depend on the representation. The latter form a set of matrices with dimension dim​(j)dim𝑗textdimleft(jright), satisfying the group’s Lie algebra [Taj,Tbj]=i​fa​b​c​Tcjsubscriptsuperscript𝑇𝑗𝑎subscriptsuperscript𝑇𝑗𝑏𝑖subscript𝑓𝑎𝑏𝑐subscriptsuperscript𝑇𝑗𝑐left[Tja,Tjbright]=ifabcTjc where fa​b​csubscript𝑓𝑎𝑏𝑐fabc are the group’s structure constants. One may also introduce the abstract generators, Jasubscript𝐽𝑎Ja, which are block diagonal in the representations, Ja=⨁𝑗​(Taj)m​n​|j​m⟩​⟨j​n|,subscript𝐽𝑎𝑗direct-sumsubscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝑇𝑎𝑗𝑚𝑛ket𝑗𝑚bra𝑗𝑛Ja=undersetjbigoplusleft(Tajright)mnleft|jmrightleft jnright|, satisfying the algebra [Ja,Jb]=i​fa​b​c​Jcsubscript𝐽𝑎subscript𝐽𝑏𝑖subscript𝑓𝑎𝑏𝑐subscript𝐽𝑐left[Ja,Jbright]=ifabcJc too. The states |j​m⟩ket𝑗𝑚left|jmright are eigenstates of mutually commuting operators: the j𝑗j quantum numbers(s) labelling the irreducible representation (and hence the multiplet) are eigenvalues of the Casimir operators which commute with all the generators; within the representation, the states are labelled by the eigenvalues of a maximal set of mutually commuting generators (Cartan subalgebra) - m𝑚m. Similarly, when the group is finite, j𝑗j labels the irreducible representation while the m𝑚m numbers are obtained from the simultaneous diagonalization of a maximal set of commuting transformations. All the irreps of Abelian groups are one dimensional and thus no m𝑚m indices are required. In the ℤNsubscriptℤ𝑁ZN case, the N𝑁N different irreps are labelled by the integers j=0,…,N−1𝑗0…𝑁1j=0,. . . ,N-1, which label the group elements g=0,…,N−1𝑔0…𝑁1g=0,. . . ,N-1 too, with Dj​(g)=exp​(i​2​πN​j​g)superscript𝐷𝑗𝑔exp𝑖2𝜋𝑁𝑗𝑔Djleft(gright)=textexpleft(ifrac2piNjgright). As a non-Abelian example, consider S​U​𝑆𝑈2SU, whose irreps are labelled by j𝑗j that are non-negative integers and half-integers. Just like gauge theories in the continuum, LGTs describe the interaction of matter particles through gauge fields. In the lattice case, the matter fields reside on the lattice sites, while the gauge fields, mediating the interactions between matter particles, are on the links. One can either discretize both space and time citebib.bib7, as used for Euclidean, Monte-Carlo computations, or discretize only space while keeping time continuous citebib.bib48. The latter corresponds to the Hamiltonian formulation widely used in the context of quantum simulation and tensor networks, including in this work. Since we consider Hamiltonian lattice gauge theory in 2+1212+1 dimensions, our lattice will be two dimensional. As this work focuses on the pure gauge case and matter fields are absent, all the degrees of freedom will reside on the links. We will review the basic ingredients of such models following the conventions of citebib.bib49 and citebib.bib50.Consider a two dimensional lattice, whose sites are labelled by vectors of integers 𝐱∈ℤ2𝐱superscriptℤ2xinZ2. 𝐞isubscript𝐞𝑖hatei denote the unit vectors pointing in directions i=1,2𝑖12i=1,2, and any link is classified by two numbers, (𝐱,i)𝐱𝑖left(x,iright), standing for the beginning of the link and the direction to which it emanates, respectively. Each link (𝐱,i)𝐱𝑖left(x,iright) hosts a local gauge field Hilbert space ℋgauge​(𝐱,i)subscriptℋgauge𝐱𝑖Htextgaugeleft(x,iright), which can be spanned by group element states |g⟩g∈Gsubscriptket𝑔𝑔𝐺leftleft|grightrightgin G labelled by the gauge group elements. These states form a basis of ℋgaugesubscriptℋgaugeHtextgauge, with the orthogonality relation ⟨g′|g⟩=δ​(g′,g),inner-productsuperscript𝑔′𝑔𝛿superscript𝑔′𝑔left gprime|gright=deltaleft(gprime,gright), where δ​(g′,g)𝛿superscript𝑔′𝑔deltaleft(gprime,gright) is the Kronecker delta if G𝐺G is finite, and a Dirac delta distribution in the compact lie case - denoting the Haar measure of G𝐺G by d​g𝑑𝑔dg, ∫𝑑g′​f​(g′)​δ​(g′,g)=f​(g). differential-dsuperscript𝑔′𝑓superscript𝑔′𝛿superscript𝑔′𝑔𝑓𝑔int dgprimefleft(gprimeright)deltaleft(gprime,gright)=fleft(gright). In a sense, using the multiplet states introduced previously, |j​m​n⟩=|j​m⟩⊗|j​n⟩ket𝑗𝑚𝑛tensor-productket𝑗𝑚ket𝑗𝑛left|jmnright=left|jmrightotimesleft|jnright or, ℋgauge=⨁𝑗​ℋj⊗ℋjsubscriptℋgaugetensor-product𝑗direct-sumsubscriptℋ𝑗subscriptℋ𝑗Htextgauge=undersetjbigoplusHjotimesHj where ℋjsubscriptℋ𝑗Hj is the dim​(j)dim𝑗textdimleft(jright) dimensional subspace spanned by the |j​m⟩ket𝑗𝑚left|jmright multiplet states. In a lattice gauge theory, only gauge invariant states and operators are considered physical. If G𝐺G is a compact Lie group, we can formulate the gauge transformations Θg​(𝐱)subscriptΘ𝑔𝐱hatThetagleft(xright) in terms of their generators, 𝒢a​(𝐱)=La​(𝐱,1)+La​(𝐱,2)−Ra​(𝐱−𝐞1,1)−Ra​(𝐱−𝐞2,2)subscript𝒢𝑎𝐱subscript𝐿𝑎𝐱1subscript𝐿𝑎𝐱2subscript𝑅𝑎𝐱subscript𝐞11subscript𝑅𝑎𝐱subscript𝐞22Galeft(xright)=Laleft(x,1right)+Laleft(x,2right)-Raleft(x-hate1,1right)-Raleft(x-hate2,2right) Gauge invariance is then formulated in terms of the Gauss laws 𝒢a​(𝐱)​|ψ⟩=0∀𝐱∈ℤ2,aformulae-sequencesubscript𝒢𝑎𝐱ket𝜓0for-all𝐱superscriptℤ2𝑎Galeft(xright)left|psiright=0quadquadforallxinZ2,a (once again, excluding static charges citebib.bib52). We call this eigenvalue equation the Gauss law, since 𝒢a​(𝐱)subscript𝒢𝑎𝐱Galeft(xright) can clearly be seen as the divergence of electric fields - Lasubscript𝐿𝑎La and Rasubscript𝑅𝑎Ra - on a site. For physical states - the one which satisfy the local constraints - the divergence of electric fields is zero. It is very apparent in the U​𝑈1U case, where it takes the explicit form (E​(𝐱,1)+E​(𝐱,2)−E​(𝐱−𝐞1,1)−E​(𝐱−𝐞2,2))​|ψ⟩𝐸𝐱1𝐸𝐱2𝐸𝐱subscript𝐞11𝐸𝐱subscript𝐞22ket𝜓displaystyleleft(Eleft(x,1right)+Eleft(x,2right)-Eleft(x-hate1,1right)-Eleft(x-hate2,2right)right)left|psiright ≡∇⋅𝐄​(𝐱)​|ψ⟩=0∀𝐱∈ℤ2,aformulae-sequenceabsent⋅∇𝐄𝐱ket𝜓0for-all𝐱superscriptℤ2𝑎displaystyleequivnablacdotEleft(xright)left|psiright=0quadquadforallxinZ2,a In non Abelian cases, the divergence involves left and right electric fields, which is related to the charge carried by non-Abelian gauge bosons citebib.bib48 (e. g. , the colour charged gluon vs the electric neutral photon).Since we deal with gauge invariant states, it is expected that the expectation values of non gauge invariant operators will vanish. Thus, when classifying the phases and behaviour of gauge theories one needs to consider only gauge invariant observables and correlation functions. One option, for compact Lie group, is to compute expectation values of electric field operators and functions thereof (and only of Casimir operators if the group is non-Abelian). Another possible gauge invariant observable is the loop variable, and in particular Wilson Loops citebib.bib7. Even though they are Hilbert space operators, all the elements of Ujsuperscript𝑈𝑗Uj commute - one can see in the definition above that they are all diagonal in the same basis.
