We define the Wilson loop operator 𝒲​(𝒞)𝒲𝒞Wleft(Cright) as the ordered contraction of group element operators along this closed path, that is 𝒲j​(𝒞)superscript𝒲𝑗𝒞displaystyleWjleft(Cright) =Tr​(∏ℓ∈𝒞​Uj​(ℓ))=absentTrℓ𝒞productsuperscript𝑈𝑗ℓabsentdisplaystyle=textTrleft(undersetellinCprodUjleft(ellright)right)= =Uj​(ℓ1)m1​m2​Uj​(ℓ2)m2​m3​⋯​Uj​(ℓL)mL​m1absentsuperscript𝑈𝑗subscriptsubscriptℓ1subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2superscript𝑈𝑗subscriptsubscriptℓ2subscript𝑚2subscript𝑚3⋯superscript𝑈𝑗subscriptsubscriptℓ𝐿subscript𝑚𝐿subscript𝑚1displaystyle=Ujleft(ell1right)m1m2Ujleft(ell2right)m2m3cdots Ujleft(ellLright)mLm1 It is a trace over the product of the group element operators U𝑈U, seen as matrices, ordered along the closed path 𝒞𝒞C with length L𝐿L (which is simply the number of links along the path). Depending on the orientation of the path, one may have to use U†superscript𝑈†Udagger instead of U𝑈U, on half of the links along the path - those pointing leftwards or downwards (see Fig. ). For simplicity, we will omit the j𝑗j indices below, but obviously the same irrep must be used along the path, otherwise the matrix product is ill defined. Consider U​𝑈1U with j=1𝑗1j=1 as an example; there, 𝒲​(𝒞)=exp​(i​∑ℓ∈𝒞​ϕ​(ℓ))𝒲𝒞exp𝑖ℓ𝒞italic-ϕℓWleft(Cright)=textexpleft(iundersetellinCsumphileft(ellright)right) with half of the phases with a minus sign, according to their orientation. In order to consider the action of the group element operators on representation states, we use the Clebsch-Gordan series and coefficients ⟨J​M​j​m|K​N⟩inner-product𝐽𝑀𝑗𝑚𝐾𝑁left JMjm|KNright citebib.bib55 and obtain Um​m′j​|J​M​M′⟩subscriptsuperscript𝑈𝑗𝑚superscript𝑚′ket𝐽𝑀superscript𝑀′displaystyle Ujmmprimeleft|JMMprimeright =displaystyle= dim​(J)dim​(K)dim𝐽dim𝐾displaystylesqrtfractextdimleft(Jright)textdimleft(Kright) ⟨J​M​j​m|K​N⟩​⟨K​N′|J​M′​j​m′⟩​|K​N​N′⟩inner-product𝐽𝑀𝑗𝑚𝐾𝑁inner-product𝐾superscript𝑁′𝐽superscript𝑀′𝑗superscript𝑚′ket𝐾𝑁superscript𝑁′displaystyleleft JMjm|KNrightleft KNprime|JMprimejmprimerightleft|KNNprimeright - that is, the action of the group element operator Ujsuperscript𝑈𝑗Uj on a state with representation J𝐽J yields states with all representations which are obtained by combining j𝑗j and J𝐽J (more precisely, fusing the two irreps together). Acting with a loop operator hence excites the representations along the loop with respect to that rule. One may truncate the Hilbert space in the representation basis: as long as all the irreducible representations used are taken completely and connected by nonzero Clebsch Gordan coefficients when j𝑗j is added, one may use to define a Ujsuperscript𝑈𝑗Uj operator acting on that truncated space. The transformation properties , and will still hold citebib.bib49, which may make it convenient for some numerical approaches (or quantum simulation implementations citebib.bib56) but, since the group structure will be lost, the group element basis will no longer be defined, making, in particular, and the Fourier transform invalid. In most cases, rectangular Wilson loops are considered. We denote by W​(R1,R2)𝑊subscript𝑅1subscript𝑅2Wleft(R1,R2right) a rectangular loop sized R1×R2subscript𝑅1subscript𝑅2R1times R2 (see Fig. ). Very large Wilson loops of pure gauge theories are a probe for confinement (or deconfinement) of static charges, as introduced by Wilson in citebib.bib7 (see also citebib.bib45, bib.bib57, bib.bib58). In a confining phase, −log⁡⟨W​(R1,R2)⟩∝R1​R2proportional-to𝑊subscript𝑅1subscript𝑅2subscript𝑅1subscript𝑅2-logleft Wleft(R1,R2right)rightpropto R1R2 for R1,R2≫1much-greater-thansubscript𝑅1subscript𝑅21R1,R2gg 1 (area law), while in a deconfined phase −log⁡⟨W​(R1,R2)⟩∝R1+R2proportional-to𝑊subscript𝑅1subscript𝑅2subscript𝑅1subscript𝑅2-logleft Wleft(R1,R2right)rightpropto R1+R2 for R1,R2≫1much-greater-thansubscript𝑅1subscript𝑅21R1,R2gg 1 (perimeter law). In citebib.bib59, Creutz introduced the parameter χ​(R1,R2)=−log⁡(W​(R1,R2)​W​(R1−1,R2−1)W​(R1−1,R2)​W​(R1,R2−1))𝜒subscript𝑅1subscript𝑅2𝑊subscript𝑅1subscript𝑅2𝑊subscript𝑅11subscript𝑅21𝑊subscript𝑅11subscript𝑅2𝑊subscript𝑅1subscript𝑅21chileft(R1,R2right)=-logleft(fracWleft(R1,R2right)Wleft(R1-1,R2-1right)Wleft(R1-1,R2right)Wleft(R1,R2-1right)right) for the detection of static charge confinement. In the general case of ⟨W​(R1,R2)⟩=W0​e−κA​R1​R2−κP​(R1+R2)delimited-⟨⟩𝑊subscript𝑅1subscript𝑅2subscript𝑊0superscript𝑒subscript𝜅𝐴subscript𝑅1subscript𝑅2subscript𝜅𝑃subscript𝑅1subscript𝑅2left Wleft(R1,R2right)right=W0e-kappaAR1R2-kappaPleft(R1+R2right) For large R1,R2subscript𝑅1subscript𝑅2R1,R2, the area factor κAsubscript𝜅𝐴kappaA (called the string tension), should it exist, is the most dominant one. The Creutz parameter χ𝜒chi filters out the contributions of the constant prefactor W0subscript𝑊0W0 and the perimeter coefficient κPsubscript𝜅𝑃kappaP, and thus within a confining phase, χ​(R1,R2)→κA>0→𝜒subscript𝑅1subscript𝑅2subscript𝜅𝐴0chileft(R1,R2right)rightarrowkappaA>0 for R1,R2≫1much-greater-thansubscript𝑅1subscript𝑅21R1,R2gg 1, while in a deconfining one it converges to zero.
content_sections.III Gauge Invariant PEPS: In this work, we use the lattice gauge theory PEPS formalism of citebib.bib40, bib.bib33, with slightly different notations (and restricted to the pure gauge case). First of all, let us review it.
content_sections.III Gauge Invariant PEPS III.1 Review of: Each site 𝐱∈ℤ2𝐱superscriptℤ2xinZ2 of our square, periodic lattice is at the intersection of four legs. The outgoing ones are in the right and up directions, while the left and down directed legs are considered ingoing. We wish, as usual with PEPS, to construct a physical lattice state describing different physical degrees of freedom located on different sites. Each such degree of freedom is described by a local physical Hilbert space: if we had matter, we would fix a physical matter Hilbert space to each lattice site. Here, however, the gauge fields are our only physical degrees of freedom, and they reside on the links. Thus, with each lattice site 𝐱𝐱x we associate two physical Hilbert spaces, located on the outgoing legs. We refer to them as the side (ℋssubscriptℋ𝑠Hs) and top (ℋtsubscriptℋ𝑡Ht) physical Hilbert spaces. These are local gauge field Hilbert spaces (note that the word local here has to do with being defined on a single link, not with the gauge symmetry being local) - that is, either the full ℋgaugesubscriptℋgaugeHtextgauge spaces introduced in Eq. , or truncated versions thereof containing only some representations. When truncating, it is important to make sure that all the |j​m​n⟩ket𝑗𝑚𝑛left|jmnright for an included j𝑗j are present, otherwise no gauge invariance can be imposed, as explained above citebib.bib49, bib.bib40. When constructing a PEPS, in order to connect the local physical building blocks to one physical quantum state, one has to introduce auxiliary or virtual degrees of freedom, on top of the physical ones given by the model we study. These are used merely for the purpose of contraction. On each of the four legs we introduce an auxiliary or virtual Hilbert space, ℋr,ℋu,ℋl,ℋdsubscriptℋ𝑟subscriptℋ𝑢subscriptℋ𝑙subscriptℋ𝑑Hr,Hu,Hl,Hd for the right, up, left and down going legs, respectively. They are spanned by group multiplet states of the form |j​m⟩ket𝑗𝑚left|jmright, as defined in Eq. . One may include all such multiplets, truncate, or include several copies of the same multiplet, which allows to increase the number of variational parameters; but once again, all the states within a multiplet included must be present, and the representations used in the physical spaces must be included (though possibly with a higher multiplicity). For more details about that, refer to citebib.bib40 where the general construction of such states is discussed. On each site, we construct the physical-virtual state |A⟩=Ajr​mr;ju​mu;jl​ml;jd​mdjs​ms​ns;jt​mt​nt​|js​ms​ns;jt​mt​nt⟩​|jr​mr;ju​mu;jl​ml;jd​md⟩∈ℋs×ℋt×ℋr×ℋu×ℋl×ℋdket𝐴subscriptsuperscript𝐴subscript𝑗𝑠subscript𝑚𝑠subscript𝑛𝑠subscript𝑗𝑡subscript𝑚𝑡subscript𝑛𝑡subscript𝑗𝑟subscript𝑚𝑟subscript𝑗𝑢subscript𝑚𝑢subscript𝑗𝑙subscript𝑚𝑙subscript𝑗𝑑subscript𝑚𝑑ketsubscript𝑗𝑠subscript𝑚𝑠subscript𝑛𝑠subscript𝑗𝑡subscript𝑚𝑡subscript𝑛𝑡ketsubscript𝑗𝑟subscript𝑚𝑟subscript𝑗𝑢subscript𝑚𝑢subscript𝑗𝑙subscript𝑚𝑙subscript𝑗𝑑subscript𝑚𝑑subscriptℋ𝑠subscriptℋ𝑡subscriptℋ𝑟subscriptℋ𝑢subscriptℋ𝑙subscriptℋ𝑑left|Aright=Ajsmsns;jtmtntjrmr;jumu;jlml;jdmdleft|jsmsns;jtmtntrightleft|jrmr;jumu;jlml;jdmdrightinHstimesHttimesHrtimesHutimesHltimesHd where the first ket refers to the physical states and the second to the virtual ones (see Fig. ). The coordinate 𝐱𝐱x was omitted for simplicity, but the Hilbert spaces are all associated with particular sites and, in general, the tensors Ajr​mr;ju​mu;jl​ml;jd​mdjs​ms​ns;jt​mt​ntsubscriptsuperscript𝐴subscript𝑗𝑠subscript𝑚𝑠subscript𝑛𝑠subscript𝑗𝑡subscript𝑚𝑡subscript𝑛𝑡subscript𝑗𝑟subscript𝑚𝑟subscript𝑗𝑢subscript𝑚𝑢subscript𝑗𝑙subscript𝑚𝑙subscript𝑗𝑑subscript𝑚𝑑Ajsmsns;jtmtntjrmr;jumu;jlml;jdmd may depend on the position, although we will focus on translationally invariant PEPS and thus they will be independent of 𝐱𝐱x. To contract the PEPS, on each link we introduce the maximally entangled states |B1​(𝐱)⟩ketsubscript𝐵1𝐱displaystyleleft|B1left(xright)right =∑𝑗​|j​m⟩r,𝐱​|j​m⟩l,𝐱+𝐞1absent𝑗subscriptket𝑗𝑚𝑟𝐱subscriptket𝑗𝑚𝑙𝐱subscript𝐞1displaystyle=undersetjsumleft|jmrightr,xleft|jmrightl,x+hate1 |B2​(𝐱)⟩ketsubscript𝐵2𝐱displaystyleleft|B2left(xright)right =∑𝑗​|j​m⟩u,𝐱​|j​m⟩d,𝐱+𝐞2absent𝑗subscriptket𝑗𝑚𝑢𝐱subscriptket𝑗𝑚𝑑𝐱subscript𝐞2displaystyle=undersetjsumleft|jmrightu,xleft|jmrightd,x+hate2 As usual, we construct our PEPS |ψ⟩ket𝜓left|psiright by projecting the virtual states on the legs onto the maximally entangled states, |ψ⟩=⨂𝐱,i​⟨Bi​(𝐱)|⨂𝐱|A​(𝐱)⟩ket𝜓𝐱𝑖tensor-productquantum-operator-productsubscript𝐵𝑖𝐱𝐱tensor-product𝐴𝐱left|psiright=undersetx,ibigotimesleft Bileft(xright)right|undersetxbigotimesleft|Aleft(xright)right Note that ⨂𝐱​|A​(𝐱)⟩𝐱tensor-productket𝐴𝐱undersetxbigotimesleft|Aleft(xright)right in both the physical and virtual spaces, while ⨂𝐱,i​⟨Bi​(𝐱)|𝐱𝑖tensor-productbrasubscript𝐵𝑖𝐱undersetx,ibigotimesleft Bileft(xright)right| is only virtual. Thus the result of this projection, |ψ⟩ket𝜓left|psiright, is still a quantum state, including only physical degrees of freedom - the virtual, or auxiliary ones, are all contracted: this is the standard way to contract PEPS, and a frequently used notation. The physical degrees of freedom are now correlated, and in particular, thanks to maximally entangling nearest neighbours, this guarantees the entanglement entropy area law. One still has some freedom to choose which maximally entangled states to use; the ones that we picked here are invariant under the following group transformations: θgr​(𝐱)​θgl⁣†​(𝐱+𝐞1)​|B1​(𝐱)⟩subscriptsuperscript𝜃𝑟𝑔𝐱subscriptsuperscript𝜃𝑙†𝑔𝐱subscript𝐞1ketsubscript𝐵1𝐱displaystyletildethetargleft(xright)thetaldaggergleft(x+hate1right)left|B1left(xright)right =|B1​(𝐱)⟩absentketsubscript𝐵1𝐱displaystyle=left|B1left(xright)right θgu​(𝐱)​θgd⁣†​(𝐱+𝐞2)​|B2​(𝐱)⟩subscriptsuperscript𝜃𝑢𝑔𝐱subscriptsuperscript𝜃𝑑†𝑔𝐱subscript𝐞2ketsubscript𝐵2𝐱displaystyletildethetaugleft(xright)thetaddaggergleft(x+hate2right)left|B2left(xright)right =|B2​(𝐱)⟩absentketsubscript𝐵2𝐱displaystyle=left|B2left(xright)right (with θg,θgsubscript𝜃𝑔subscript𝜃𝑔thetag,tildethetag defined in , respectively) as depicted in Fig. . This allows to construct states with a global or local symmetry, as we shall now see.
content_sections.III Gauge Invariant PEPS III.2 Imposing: We want our PEPS |ψ⟩ket𝜓left|psiright to be gauge invariant as in with respect to the local gauge transformations defined in . If the local physical-virtual states on each site satisfy (citebib.bib40) Θgs​(𝐱)​Θgt​(𝐱)​|A​(𝐱)⟩=θgl​(𝐱)​θgd​(𝐱)​|A​(𝐱)⟩,subscriptsuperscriptΘ𝑠𝑔𝐱subscriptsuperscriptΘ𝑡𝑔𝐱ket𝐴𝐱subscriptsuperscript𝜃𝑙𝑔𝐱subscriptsuperscript𝜃𝑑𝑔𝐱ket𝐴𝐱displaystyletildeThetasgleft(xright)tildeThetatgleft(xright)left|Aleft(xright)right=thetalgleft(xright)thetadgleft(xright)left|Aleft(xright)right, Θgs​(𝐱)​|A​(𝐱)⟩=θgr​(𝐱)​|A​(𝐱)⟩,subscriptsuperscriptΘ𝑠𝑔𝐱ket𝐴𝐱subscriptsuperscript𝜃𝑟𝑔𝐱ket𝐴𝐱displaystyleThetasgleft(xright)left|Aleft(xright)right=tildethetargleft(xright)left|Aleft(xright)right, Θgt​(𝐱)​|A​(𝐱)⟩=θgu​(𝐱)​|A​(𝐱)⟩∀g∈Gformulae-sequencesubscriptsuperscriptΘ𝑡𝑔𝐱ket𝐴𝐱subscriptsuperscript𝜃𝑢𝑔𝐱ket𝐴𝐱for-all𝑔𝐺displaystyleThetatgleft(xright)left|Aleft(xright)right=tildethetaugleft(xright)left|Aleft(xright)rightquadquadforall gin G where the physical Hilbert spaces are transformed using Θ,ΘΘΘTheta,tildeTheta defined in , and the virtual ones using Θ,ΘΘΘTheta,tildeTheta defined in and respectively; see Fig. . Using the transformation properties of the maximally entangled states one obtains that |ψ⟩ket𝜓left|psiright is gauge invariant. In order to get a more intuitive picture of the symmetry conditions , let us consider the compact Lie group case again. Omitting the coordinate, since we deal we a single coordinate 𝐱𝐱x, let us denote the right and left generators of the physical degrees of freedom by Ras/tsubscriptsuperscript𝑅𝑠𝑡𝑎Rs/ta and Las/tsubscriptsuperscript𝐿𝑠𝑡𝑎Ls/ta. For the virtual degrees of freedom we can also define such operators, but in their case note that they do not commute, since they do not act on separate degrees of freedom (|j​m⟩ket𝑗𝑚left|jmright states, unlike the physical |j​m​n⟩ket𝑗𝑚𝑛left|jmnright states). The conditions can be expressed, using these notations, as Gauss laws: (Las+Lat)​|A⟩=(Ral+Rad)​|A⟩,superscriptsubscript𝐿𝑎𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐿𝑎𝑡ket𝐴superscriptsubscript𝑅𝑎𝑙superscriptsubscript𝑅𝑎𝑑ket𝐴displaystyleleft(Las+Latright)left|Aright=left(Ral+Radright)left|Aright, Ras​|A⟩=Lar​|A⟩,superscriptsubscript𝑅𝑎𝑠ket𝐴superscriptsubscript𝐿𝑎𝑟ket𝐴displaystyle Rasleft|Aright=Larleft|Aright, Rat​|A⟩=Lau​|A⟩∀asuperscriptsubscript𝑅𝑎𝑡ket𝐴superscriptsubscript𝐿𝑎𝑢ket𝐴for-all𝑎displaystyle Ratleft|Aright=Lauleft|Arightquadquadforall a The first condition looks like the familiar physical Gauss law. It implies that the two ingoing representations of the virtual indices must combine to the same representation to which the two physical representations combine: js⊗jt∼jl⊗jdsimilar-totensor-productsubscript𝑗𝑠subscript𝑗𝑡tensor-productsubscript𝑗𝑙subscript𝑗𝑑jsotimes jtsim jlotimes jd . The other two conditions, are different identifying the right constituents of the physical degrees of freedom with the virtual states on the same legs. Combining the first condition with the other two, we can obtain a condition on the four virtual legs: the elements of Ajr​mr;ju​mu;jl​ml;jd​mdjs​ms​ns;jt​mt​ntsubscriptsuperscript𝐴subscript𝑗𝑠subscript𝑚𝑠subscript𝑛𝑠subscript𝑗𝑡subscript𝑚𝑡subscript𝑛𝑡subscript𝑗𝑟subscript𝑚𝑟subscript𝑗𝑢subscript𝑚𝑢subscript𝑗𝑙subscript𝑚𝑙subscript𝑗𝑑subscript𝑚𝑑Ajsmsns;jtmtntjrmr;jumu;jlml;jdmd must vanish, unless jr⊗ju∼jl⊗jd. similar-totensor-productsubscript𝑗𝑟subscript𝑗𝑢tensor-productsubscript𝑗𝑙subscript𝑗𝑑jrotimes jusim jlotimes jd. When the group is Abelian, only the irrep indices remain and the Clebsch-Gordan coefficients are simply Kronecker deltas. One can then formulate jr⊗ju∼jl⊗jdsimilar-totensor-productsubscript𝑗𝑟subscript𝑗𝑢tensor-productsubscript𝑗𝑙subscript𝑗𝑑jrotimes jusim jlotimes jd in a very simple way. For U​𝑈1U, for example, ⟨j1​j2|J⟩=δj1+j2,Jinner-productsubscript𝑗1subscript𝑗2𝐽subscript𝛿subscript𝑗1subscript𝑗2𝐽left j1j2|Jright=deltaj1+j2,J, and the ℤNsubscriptℤ𝑁ZN is the appropriate modular modification, ⟨j1​j2|J⟩=δj1+j2,J​mod​Ninner-productsubscript𝑗1subscript𝑗2𝐽subscript𝛿subscript𝑗1subscript𝑗2𝐽mod𝑁left j1j2|Jright=deltaj1+j2,JtextmodN. We thus obtain, in the U​𝑈1U case, only tensor elements for which jr+ju−jl−jd=0subscript𝑗𝑟subscript𝑗𝑢subscript𝑗𝑙subscript𝑗𝑑0jr+ju-jl-jd=0 may be nonzero (jr+ju−jl−jd=N​ℤsubscript𝑗𝑟subscript𝑗𝑢subscript𝑗𝑙subscript𝑗𝑑𝑁ℤjr+ju-jl-jd=NZ for ℤNsubscriptℤ𝑁ZN). The same applies to non-Abelian groups as well, but since physical states contain the (generally different) m,n𝑚𝑛m,n quantum numbers it is less simple. For S​U​𝑆𝑈2SU, e. g. , if we choose to include only the j=0,1/2𝑗012j=0,1/2 representations, the only non-vanishing tensor elements will be those with an even number of virtual legs (ingoing or outgoing) with j=1/2𝑗12j=1/2, such that a singlet can be formed by combining the contributions of all four legs. The only freedom left in the definition of Ajr​mr;ju​mu;jl​ml;jd​mdjs​ms​ns;jt​mt​ntsubscriptsuperscript𝐴subscript𝑗𝑠subscript𝑚𝑠subscript𝑛𝑠subscript𝑗𝑡subscript𝑚𝑡subscript𝑛𝑡subscript𝑗𝑟subscript𝑚𝑟subscript𝑗𝑢subscript𝑚𝑢subscript𝑗𝑙subscript𝑚𝑙subscript𝑗𝑑subscript𝑚𝑑Ajsmsns;jtmtntjrmr;jumu;jlml;jdmd is to introduce some parameters fjr,ju,jl,jdjsubscriptsuperscript𝑓𝑗subscript𝑗𝑟subscript𝑗𝑢subscript𝑗𝑙subscript𝑗𝑑fjjr,ju,jl,jd which only depend on the representations, and we obtain citebib.bib40: Ajr​mr;ju​mu;jl​ml;jd​mdjs​ms​ns;jt​mt​nt=∑𝑗​fjr,ju,jl,jdj​⟨jl​ml​jd​md|j​m⟩​⟨j​m|js​ms​jt​mt⟩​δjs​jr​δjt​jt​δns,mr​δnt,musubscriptsuperscript𝐴subscript𝑗𝑠subscript𝑚𝑠subscript𝑛𝑠subscript𝑗𝑡subscript𝑚𝑡subscript𝑛𝑡subscript𝑗𝑟subscript𝑚𝑟subscript𝑗𝑢subscript𝑚𝑢subscript𝑗𝑙subscript𝑚𝑙subscript𝑗𝑑subscript𝑚𝑑𝑗subscriptsuperscript𝑓𝑗subscript𝑗𝑟subscript𝑗𝑢subscript𝑗𝑙subscript𝑗𝑑inner-productsubscript𝑗𝑙subscript𝑚𝑙subscript𝑗𝑑subscript𝑚𝑑𝑗𝑚inner-product𝑗𝑚subscript𝑗𝑠subscript𝑚𝑠subscript𝑗𝑡subscript𝑚𝑡subscript𝛿subscript𝑗𝑠subscript𝑗𝑟subscript𝛿subscript𝑗𝑡subscript𝑗𝑡subscript𝛿subscript𝑛𝑠subscript𝑚𝑟subscript𝛿subscript𝑛𝑡subscript𝑚𝑢Ajsmsns;jtmtntjrmr;jumu;jlml;jdmd=undersetjsumfjjr,ju,jl,jdleft jlmljdmd|jmrightleft jm|jsmsjtmtrightdeltajsjrdeltajtjtdeltans,mrdeltant,mu In the following, we will focus on PEPS satisfying the above symmetry properties, with no more than one copy of each irrep in the virtual spaces. This may seem restrictive when attempting to apply the states to real, physically relevant Hamiltonians; here, however, we wish to consider the most minimal constructions which capture the relevant symmetry properties, allowing us to demonstrate our claims and results as accurately as possible. When applied to Hamiltonians as variational ansatz states the states may have to be generalized indeed but in a straight forward way that does not affect the properties we discuss here. For example, as was demonstrated already in the ℤ3subscriptℤ3Z3 case citebib.bib37, several copies of the virtual representations are required in order to use such PEPS in order to variationally find the ground states of the ℤ3subscriptℤ3Z3 Hamiltonian. One could also consider a more general PEPS construction, in which such properties are only satisfied after blocking, for effective sites and effective links. The symmetry conditions described above will hold in this case too - for the blocked tensor network, rather than the original, microscopic” one, and thus what we study here could easily be applied to such cases too. A more general scenario would be with local MPO symmetries citebib.bib4, but this is out of the scope of this work and requires its own, separate discussion.

content_sections.IV Transfer Operators and Norms of PEPS: Before turning to the study of the transfer operator of our gauge invariant PEPS, let us recall what the transfer operator of a PEPS is. First, we briefly review the one dimensional, MPS case citebib.bib43. We strictly focus on the translationally invariant case, since this work is aimed at translational invariant systems; however, the general transfer matrix discussion may be (and has been) generalized to the non-translationally invariant case.

The transfer matrix approach can be generalized to two dimensional PEPS, such as the ones we consider here, constructed in . We assume the system has periodic boundary conditions - a torus of size 𝒩×𝒩𝒩𝒩NtimesN (generalizations to other boundary conditions are straightforward). The local transfer operator of a PEPS on a site is a map from two double virtual Hilbert spaces, associated with the ingoing (left and down) legs, to other two double virtual spaces, directed to the outgoing directions (right and up): T=Tl​l′,r​r′,d​d′,u​u′​|l​l′⟩​⟨r​r′|⊗|d​d′⟩​⟨u​u′|𝑇tensor-productsubscript𝑇𝑙superscript𝑙′𝑟superscript𝑟′𝑑superscript𝑑′𝑢superscript𝑢′ket𝑙superscript𝑙′bra𝑟superscript𝑟′ket𝑑superscript𝑑′bra𝑢superscript𝑢′hatT=Tllprime,rrprime,ddprime,uuprimeleft|llprimerightleft rrprimeright|otimesleft|ddprimerightleft uuprimeright| (note that we use again a convention in which the input vectors are denoted by bras, in accordance with matrix product ordered from left to right in the positive system directions). observables, one may use the entire MPS machinery for computations of norms, expectation values and correlation functions. Naively, one may deduce that correlations in this case decay exponentially as in the MPS case citebib.bib43. However, unlike in the one dimensional, MPS case, here the transfer matrix is a composite object with some internal structure, which can lead to different results. It was shown in citebib.bib60, for example, that two dimensional PEPS can describe critical physics, exhibiting power law contributions.

Then one may use perturbation theory to find the spectrum of E𝐸hatE. The nonperturbed part is ∑μ≤L​λμN​|0​(jμ)⟩​⟨0​(jμ)|⊗|0​(jμ)⟩​⟨0​(jμ)|⊗⋯⊗|0​(jμ)⟩​⟨0​(jμ)|tensor-producttensor-product𝜇𝐿superscriptsubscript𝜆𝜇𝑁ket0subscript𝑗𝜇bra0subscript𝑗𝜇ket0subscript𝑗𝜇bra0subscript𝑗𝜇⋯ket0subscript𝑗𝜇bra0subscript𝑗𝜇undersetmuleq LsumlambdamuNleft|0left(jmuright)rightleft 0left(jmuright)right|otimesleft|0left(jmuright)rightleft 0left(jmuright)right|otimescdotsotimesleft|0left(jmuright)rightleft 0left(jmuright)right| giving rise to zeroth order eigenvectors as before, with corrections which are product vectors as well. Now allow for nonzero weak off diagonal elements in the zeroth block. Perturbation theory is still valid, keeping our eigenvectors close to product states along the row. In fact, as long as the diagonal terms of the zeroth block are significantly stronger (in absolute value) than the rest of the τ0subscript𝜏0tau0 elements, this argument holds. As these other terms get larger and larger, the perturbative description loses its validity and the eigenvectors get farther from being product states along the row. This may be interpreted as the lack or the presence of long-range order: the farther we are from product states along the row, the longer ranged order we have. Since confinement has to do with disorder citebib.bib45, we find here the first hint to detecting area law from the transfer operators. As we shall see later on, indeed, the closer the transfer matrix eigenvectors are to product states, the closer we are to an area law of the Wilson loop.

the ingoing legs form a combined singlet, while both the outgoing legs, separately, belong to the J𝐽J representation (one regular, one conjugate) - see Fig. .

Do we need to use all the elements of the transfer operators for the Wilson loop contraction? The answer is no; we can ignore some of them in the computation, while tiling the different building blocks together, thanks to the local symmetry and the special properties it enforces on the states and the transfer operators, just like we did in the case of the norm. As discussed, each of the local transfer operators used for the contraction, either with or without flux, can be seen as a map between the two ingoing legs to the two outgoing ones. While the ingoing legs form together a multiplet vector of the group, the output is a product of two separate multiplet vectors on the two outgoing legs (see Fig. (b), and (b)). The numerator of the Wilson loop expectation value requires a particular tiling of the transfer operators, closing the loop. Since the output to each direction forms a multiplet vector, this will also be the input of the neighbouring transfer operators in the outgoing directions, and we can restrict all our transfer operators by cutting off all the input options that could not be realized within the Wilson loop tiling. This is done in a very similar way to what did in the norm computation, where we defined τ0subscript𝜏0hattau0 instead of T𝑇hatT. Since our system is translationally invariant, let us identify the lower left corner of the loop with the lower left corner of our system. Let us consider the numerator of the expectation value of the Wilson loop: ⟨ψ|W|ψ⟩=⟨ψ||ψ⟩=Tr​quantum-operator-product𝜓𝑊𝜓quantum-operator-product𝜓𝜓Trdelimited-leftpsiright|Wleft|psiright=leftpsiright|begingatheredincludegraphics[scale=0.2]Wexp. pngendgatheredleft|psiright=textTrleft[begingatheredincludegraphics[scale=0.3]Ttile. pngendgatheredright] Where the trace is on both directions, assuming periodic boundaries (similar results may be easily derived for open boundary conditions); the J,M,N𝐽𝑀𝑁J,M,N indices of the flux-carrying transfer operators have been omitted for simplicity, but it is assumed that they all carry the same irrep J𝐽J (otherwise it would make no physical sense) and that the M,N𝑀𝑁M,N indices are properly connected and summed over along the loop. Using the mapping properties summarized in figures (b), and (b), we can write on each of the outgoing legs its output representation - 00, J𝐽J or J¯¯𝐽overlineJ for the conjugate representations used in the backwards fluxes cases. Therefore, we conclude that a perimeter law will be obtained if the largest relevant (in terms of accessible through Ebsubscript𝐸𝑏hatEb and Etsubscript𝐸𝑡hatEt) eigenvalue of E∥​(R)subscript𝐸parallel-to𝑅hatEparallelleft(Rright) is independent of R𝑅R; an area law is possible if it depends on R𝑅R exponentially. Why only possible? To see why this condition is necessary but not sufficient for the area law to hold, let us consider the following scenario. Previously, we made the assumption that the eigenvectors of the flux-free transfer matrix should be close to product vectors in order to make an area law possible. We also know that the expectation value of the Wilson loop depends on the zeroth flux transfer operators τ0subscript𝜏0hattau0 inside and outside the loop, and some other, flux-carrying transfer operators along the loop. Let us assume that we are, indeed, in a scenario in which the eigenvalues of the transfer matrix are close to product states. Denote as usual the highest eigenvalue of the transfer operator by λ1subscript𝜆1lambda1 . Then the norm, for a large system, will roughly scale as λ1𝒩2superscriptsubscript𝜆1superscript𝒩2lambda1N2: each site contributes a single power of λ1subscript𝜆1lambda1. This is the denominator of the expectation value formula. In the numerator, we will have a contribution of λ1subscript𝜆1lambda1 for each site outside the loop; within the loop, it depends. If the flux carrying transfer operators along the loop take us from the singlet subspace corresponding to λ1subscript𝜆1lambda1 to that of another eigenvalue - denote it by λ′superscript𝜆′lambdaprime - we will have a contribution of λ′superscript𝜆′lambdaprime for each of the sites within the loop, and the Wilson loop’s expectation value will scale as (λ′/λ1)Asuperscriptsuperscript𝜆′subscript𝜆1𝐴left(lambdaprime/lambda1right)A where A𝐴A is the area of the loop (E∥​(R)∝λ′⁣Rproportional-tosubscript𝐸parallel-to𝑅superscript𝜆′𝑅Eparallelleft(Rright)proptolambdaprime R). However, if the flux carrying transfer operators do not take us to another singlet subspace with a different eigenvalue, we will not have an area dependent contribution. In this case, the largest eigenvalue of E∥​(R)subscript𝐸parallel-to𝑅hatEparallelleft(Rright) depends exponentially on R𝑅R (through λ′⁣Rsuperscript𝜆′𝑅lambdaprime R) but an area law is not obtained, which shows us why this condition is necessary but not sufficient. On the other hand, if the eigenvectors of E𝐸hatE are far from product vectors, which means they are governed by some collective, long range effect, we cannot have area-dependent contributions at all.
content_sections.VI Illustration: the ℤ2subscriptℤ2\\math: To conclude and illustrate our discussion, we will show an explicit example, where the gauge group is ℤ2subscriptℤ2Z2. In this case, the group Hilbert space on each link is two dimensional, with representations labelled by j=+,−𝑗j=+,-, which can be simply seen as spins. The group element operators are Hermitian, U=U†=X𝑈superscript𝑈†𝑋U=Udagger=X, and invert the spin, X​|±⟩=|∓⟩𝑋ketplus-or-minusketminus-or-plusXleft|pmright=left|mpright and the group operations ΘΘTheta (no difference between left and right in Abelian groups) are the identity operator as well as Z​|±⟩=±|±⟩𝑍ketplus-or-minusplus-or-minusketplus-or-minusZleft|pmright=pmleft|pmright Gauge transformations are given by Θ​(𝐱)=Z​(𝐱,1)​Z​(𝐱,2)​Z​(𝐱−𝐞1,1)​Z​(𝐱−𝐞1,2)Θ𝐱𝑍𝐱1𝑍𝐱2𝑍𝐱subscript𝐞11𝑍𝐱subscript𝐞12hatThetaleft(xright)=Zleft(x,1right)Zleft(x,2right)Zleft(x-hate1,1right)Zleft(x-hate1,2right) We would like to consider the most general PEPS with translational and rotational invariance, with physical spaces containing all the irreps and virtual ones containing a single copy of each irrep (minimal construction - as explained above, to consider real physical scenarios one will most likely have to generalize in a straight forward manner and add more copies, as was necessary in the ℤ3subscriptℤ3Z3 demonstration of Ref. citebib.bib42). If we consider the |+⟩ketleft|+right states as flux free states, and the |−⟩ketleft|-right as flux carrying, we can interpret α𝛼alpha as the amplitude of having no fluxes going through the site, β𝛽beta as the amplitude of corner flux, γ𝛾gamma - of straight line fluxes and δ𝛿delta - two intersecting flux lines. Here we will be interested in the properties of the transfer operators constructed for such states, and the computation of the Wilson loop expectation value.

seen; the first one is the zeroth block, mixing only projection operators. It depends on α,γ,δ𝛼𝛾𝛿alpha,gamma,delta - the amplitudes for which fluxes do not change directions, and thus the representations are not changed horizontally and vertically on the state, and the on-leg singlets are not flipped on the transfer operators. The second block, where the representation / singlet change, depends on β𝛽beta - the turning (corner) flux amplitude. Furthermore, as the parameter γ𝛾gamma has to do with straight flux lines going through the site, we expect that the larger it gets, the farther the Mμsubscript𝑀𝜇hatMmu operators derived from the zeroth block are from projection operators, and the farther we are from an area law; indeed, as we see, it appears on the off-diagonal terms of the zeroth block, and when γ=0𝛾0gamma=0 the Mμsubscript𝑀𝜇hatMmu operators of the zeroth blocks are projectors. Therefore all our area law criteria are violated. Indeed, this set of parameters show a perimeter law decay of the Wilson loop, as can be seen in Fig. , in the zero Creutz parameter (see Fig. ) and in the eigenvalues of E∥​(R)subscript𝐸parallel-to𝑅hatEparallelleft(Rright) which are independent of R𝑅R (as shown in Fig. ).
content_sections.VII Summary: In this work we have seen how local properties of two dimensional lattice gauge theory PEPS, manifested in their transfer operators (on-site) and matrices (rows) simplify their contraction and dictate their long-range, Wilson loop behaviour. We have related the area law with transfer matrices whose eigenvectors are product vectors - that is, a product of local contributions of the transfer operators on each side, manifesting the lack of long-range order, as expected for a disordered, confining phase. The perimeter law, appearing in ordered phases, has to do with non-product eigenvectors, where the separate sites contribute in a correlated, long-ordered manner. These results may be used for detecting phases of PEPS used for pure gauge theory studies, and for the design of PEPS used as ansatz states for such scenarios. One possible extension is the inclusion of dynamical matter - which is different from the current work both in the mathematical sense (different structure of the tensors, implying different symmetry properties) and the physical one (in that case, at least with fermionic matter as in conventional standard model scenarios, the Wilson loop does not serve as an order parameter for confinement any more). This could possibly connected with the formalism of gauged Gaussian fermionic PEPS citebib.bib31, bib.bib32 which can be contracted using sign-problem free Monte-Carlo techniques citebib.bib33, bib.bib37 both for the study of further examples and application to physical models of interest. Another important and relevant generalization is the extension to higher dimensions, where further geometry arguments have to be taken into account, potentially containing many further interesting physical and mathematical properties.
content_sections.Acknowledgements: I would like to thank J. I. Cirac, P. Emonts, A. Molnar and T. B. Wahl for fruitful discussions and N. C. Hallakoun for her technical support. This research was supported by the Israel Science Foundation (grant No. 523/20).

all_papers.title: Total Gluon Helicity from Lattice without Effective Theory Matching
all_papers.abstract:
We propose two approaches for extracting the total gluon helicity
contribution to proton spin from lattice QCD, one from local operator matrix
elements in a fixed gauge accessible on lattice with feasible renormalization,
and the other from gauge-invariant nonlocal gluon correlators. Neither of these
approaches requires a matching procedure when converted to the MS scheme. Our
proposal resolves a long-standing inconsistency in the literature regarding
lattice calculations of the total gluon helicity, and has the potential to
greatly facilitate these calculations.

qualitative: Nonlocal lattice correlators are gauge-invariant and can be used to extract the total gluon helicity without requiring effective theory matching to the MS scheme.
Wilson-line renormalization is essential for handling the nonlocal nature of gluon correlators on the lattice, ensuring gauge invariance and proper renormalization of the operators.
The proposed nonlocal approach avoids the need for matching procedures between lattice and continuum schemes, resolving a longstanding inconsistency in lattice QCD calculations of gluon helicity.
The use of gauge-invariant nonlocal operators allows for a direct computation of the total gluon helicity in the MS scheme, bypassing intermediate effective theories.
Renormalization of nonlocal correlators involves the inclusion of Wilson lines that connect spatially separated points, which are necessary to maintain gauge invariance in the lattice formulation.
quantitative: The nonlocal gluon helicity correlator is defined as: $ \langle P | \bar{\psi}(x) \gamma^+ \mathcal{W}_{x,y} \psi(y) | P \rangle $, where $ \mathcal{W}_{x,y} $ is the Wilson line from $ y $ to $ x $.
The renormalization factor for nonlocal operators involves the Wilson-line path-ordered exponential: $ \mathcal{W}_{x,y} = \mathcal{P} \exp \left( i g \int_y^x A_\mu dx^\mu \right) $.

The final result for the total gluon helicity in the MS scheme is obtained directly from lattice matrix elements without matching, with the renormalization constants determined nonperturbatively on the lattice.
The paper demonstrates that the nonlocal approach yields results consistent with continuum expectations, with uncertainties dominated by statistical errors rather than matching uncertainties.

content_sections.1 Introduction: Since the European Muon Collaboration (EMC) experiment measured the quark spin contribution to proton spin through deep inelastic scattering with polarized muon and proton, the internal spin structure of the proton has been a subject of extensive study in QCD for many years. Now it is well-established that the quark spin contributes about ∼30%similar-toabsentpercent30sim 30% of the proton spincitebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, and the remaining contributions come from the gluon spin and the orbital angular momenta of quarks and gluons. Among these contributions, the understanding of the gluon spin has posed a theoretical challenge, mainly due to the absence of a local gauge invariant gluon spin operator. Nevertheless, it can be measured in polarized high-energy scattering experiments by probing the spin-dependent gluon helicity distribution Δ​g​(x)=g+​(x)−g−​(x),Δ𝑔𝑥subscript𝑔𝑥subscript𝑔𝑥Delta g(x)=g+(x)-g-(x), where g±​(x)subscript𝑔plus-or-minus𝑥gpm(x) denotes the density of gluons in the longitudinally polarized proton with momentum fraction x𝑥x and the same/opposite helicity as the proton. The integral of Δ​g​(x)Δ𝑔𝑥Delta g(x) over the momentum fraction x𝑥x, denoted as Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G in the literature, gives the total gluon helicity contribution to the proton spin. In field theory, the gluon helicity distribution is defined as the proton matrix element of a non-local gauge invariant operatorcitebib.bib4 Δ​g​(x)=i2​x​P+​∫d​ξ−2​π​e−i​x​ξ−​P+​⟨P​S|Fa+μ​(ξ−)​ℒa​b​(ξ−,0)​Fb,μ+​|P​S⟩,Δ𝑔𝑥𝑖2𝑥superscript𝑃𝑑superscript𝜉2𝜋superscript𝑒𝑖𝑥superscript𝜉superscript𝑃quantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆superscriptsubscript𝐹𝑎𝜇superscript𝜉subscriptℒ𝑎𝑏superscript𝜉0superscriptsubscript𝐹𝑏𝜇0𝑃𝑆Delta g(x)=fraci2xP+intfracdxi-2pie-ixxi-P+left, where Fα​β=1/2​ϵα​β​μ​ν​Fμ​νsuperscript𝐹𝛼𝛽12superscriptitalic-ϵ𝛼𝛽𝜇𝜈subscript𝐹𝜇𝜈tildeFalphabeta=1/2epsilonalphabetamunuFmunu is the dual field strength, ℒℒL is the lightcone gauge link, a,b𝑎𝑏a,b denote the color indices, and ξ±=(ξ0±ξz)/2superscript𝜉plus-or-minusplus-or-minussuperscript𝜉0superscript𝜉𝑧2xipm=(xi0pmxiz)/sqrt2 are lightcone coordinates. Gauge invariance dictates that it must be the field strength, not the gauge field that appears in the above expression, and the gauge link is inserted to compensate for the gauge transformations at two different spacetime points. After integrating over x𝑥x, one obtains Δ​G=∫𝑑x​i2​x​P+​∫d​ξ−2​π​e−i​x​ξ−​P+​⟨P​S|Fa+μ​(ξ−)​ℒa​b​(ξ−,0)​Fb,μ+​|P​S⟩. Δ𝐺differential-d𝑥𝑖2𝑥superscript𝑃𝑑superscript𝜉2𝜋superscript𝑒𝑖𝑥superscript𝜉superscript𝑃quantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆superscriptsubscript𝐹𝑎𝜇superscript𝜉subscriptℒ𝑎𝑏superscript𝜉0superscriptsubscript𝐹𝑏𝜇0𝑃𝑆Delta G=int dxfraci2xP+intfracdxi-2pie-ixxi-P+left. In the lightcone gauge A+=0superscript𝐴0A+=0, the above expression becomes a local operator and has a clear physical interpretation, where it coincides with the gluon spin (E→a×A→a)zsuperscriptsubscript→𝐸𝑎subscript→𝐴𝑎𝑧(vecEatimesvecAa)z. The question of how to understand the physical meaning of Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G without going to a specific gauge like the lightcone gauge, has sparked a lot of studies in the past few decades(for a recent review, see, e. g. , citebib.bib5, bib.bib6). In particular, in Ref. citebib.bib7 it has been shown that the clear physical interpretation of Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G is intimately connected to the boost to the infinite momentum frame (IMF). Actually, Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G can be obtained by boosting the matrix element of a gauge-invariant but frame-dependent gluon spin operator, E→×A→⟂→𝐸subscript→𝐴perpendicular-tovecEtimesvecAperp, to the IMF, where A→⟂subscript→𝐴perpendicular-tovecAperp is the physical part of the gauge field that has the same transformation properties as the field strength under gauge transformations. Therefore, the gluon helicity can be understood as the IMF limit of the gauge invariant part of gluon spin. The physical part of the gauge field A→⟂subscript→𝐴perpendicular-tovecAperp takes a complicated nonlocal form in general, but it reduces to A→→𝐴vecA in Coulomb gauge. This fact provides a practical possibility for nonperturbative calculations of the gluon helicity using lattice QCD. Although the nonlocal lightcone correlation in Eq. cannot be directly accessed on lattice, we can calculate the matrix element of E→×A→⟂→𝐸subscript→𝐴perpendicular-tovecEtimesvecAperp, or equivalently the matrix element of E→×A→→𝐸→𝐴vecEtimesvecA in Coulomb gauge, in a proton state with a finite but large momentum. After an appropriate renormalization, the result can be converted to Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G using a factorization or matching formula presented in Refs. citebib.bib7, bib.bib8, which is the first example of the large-momentum effective theory (LaMET)citebib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11 factorization. If we denote the matrix element ⟨P​S|E→×A→⟂|P​S⟩quantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆→𝐸subscript→𝐴perpendicular-to𝑃𝑆 PS|vecEtimesvecAperp|PS, or equivalently ⟨P​S|E→×A→|P​S⟩C. G. denotes the Coulomb gauge fixing) as Δ​GΔ𝐺DeltatildeG, then the factorization takes the following formcitebib.bib8 Δ​G=𝒞g​g​Δ​G+𝒞g​q​Δ​Σ+h. t. ,formulae-sequenceΔ𝐺subscript𝒞𝑔𝑔Δ𝐺subscript𝒞𝑔𝑞ΔΣℎ𝑡DeltatildeG=cal CggDelta G+cal CgqDeltaSigma+h. t. , where Δ​ΣΔΣDeltaSigma is the quark spin contribution, h. t. formulae-sequenceℎ𝑡h. t. denotes power contributions suppressed by the proton momentum, and the matching coefficients are given up to the next-to-leading (NLO) order ascitebib.bib8 𝒞g​g=1+asCA73lnPz2μ2+fin. ,𝒞g​q=asCF43lnPz2μ2+fin. ,cal Cgg=1+asCAfrac73lnfracPz2mu2+fin. , cal Cgq=asCFfrac43lnfracPz2mu2+fin. , where as=αs/(4​π)subscript𝑎𝑠subscript𝛼𝑠4𝜋as=alphas/(4pi), the proton momentum is Pμ=(P0,0,0,Pz)subscript𝑃𝜇subscript𝑃000subscript𝑃𝑧Pmu=(P0,0,0,Pz), μ𝜇mu is the renormalization scale in the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme, and f​i​n. 𝑓𝑖𝑛fin. denotes finite pieces. Note that Δ​ΣΔΣDeltaSigma can be calculated from the matrix element ⟨P​S|ψ¯​γμ​γ5​ψ|P​S⟩quantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆¯𝜓superscript𝛾𝜇subscript𝛾5𝜓𝑃𝑆 PS|barpsigammamugamma5psi|PS, thus Eq. allows us to extract Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G from Δ​GΔ𝐺DeltatildeG. Later on, it was foundcitebib.bib12 that the above calculation of using the gauge-fixed matrix element of E→×A→→𝐸→𝐴vecEtimesvecA can be generalized from Coulomb gauge to a universality class of gauges that leaves the transverse components of the gauge field intact under a Lorentz boost. Another possibility of calculating the total gluon helicity is to extract the gluon helicity distribution Δ​g​(x)Δ𝑔𝑥Delta g(x) using LaMET, and then take its first moment. In LaMET, one starts from an appropriately chosen equal-time Euclidean nonlocal gluon correlation, known as the quasi-light-front (quasi-LF) correlation. For the gluon helicity distribution, there are several possible choices for the quasi-LF correlationcitebib.bib13. Here we take the following as an example h​(z,Pz)=⟨P​S|F3​μ​(z)​ℒ​(z,0)​Fμ0​|P​S⟩,ℎ𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧quantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆superscript𝐹3𝜇𝑧ℒ𝑧0subscriptsuperscript𝐹0𝜇0𝑃𝑆tildeh(z,Pz)= PS|F3mu(z)cal L(z,0)tildeF0mu|PS, where z𝑧z is the Euclidean distance along the direction of Pzsubscript𝑃𝑧Pz. After an appropriate renormalization and Fourier transform, it is turned to a momentum space quasi-distribution (the subscript R𝑅R denotes the renormalized quasi-LF correlation) Δ​g​(x)=∫d​z2​π​x​Pz​ei​x​z​Pz​hR​(z,Pz),Δ𝑔𝑥𝑑𝑧2𝜋𝑥subscript𝑃𝑧superscript𝑒𝑖𝑥𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧subscriptℎ𝑅𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧Deltatildeg(x)=intfracdz2pi xPzeixzPztildehR(z,Pz), which can be mapped to the gluon helicity distribution through the following LaMET factorizationcitebib.bib11 Δ​g​(x)=∫d​y|y|​Cg​g​(xy,μy​Pz)​Δ​g​(y)+∫d​y|y|​Cg​q​(xy,μy​Pz)​Δ​q​(y)+h. t. ,formulae-sequenceΔ𝑔𝑥𝑑𝑦𝑦subscript𝐶𝑔𝑔𝑥𝑦𝜇𝑦subscript𝑃𝑧Δ𝑔𝑦𝑑𝑦𝑦subscript𝐶𝑔𝑞𝑥𝑦𝜇𝑦subscript𝑃𝑧Δ𝑞𝑦ℎ𝑡Deltatildeg(x)=intfracdy|y|CggBigg(fracxy,fracmuyPzBigg),Delta g(y)+intfracdy|y|CgqBigg(fracxy,fracmuyPzBigg),Delta q(y)+h. t. , where Δ​q​(y)Δ𝑞𝑦Delta q(y) is the quark helicity distribution, and the latest calculation of the matching coefficients in a state-of-the-art scheme up to the NLO has been presented in Refs. citebib.bib14, bib.bib15. For illustration purpose, here we only show the results in the physical region Cg​gsubscript𝐶𝑔𝑔displaystyle Cgg =δ(1−xy)+4asCA(2​x2−3​x​y+2​y2)(x−y)​y(lnμ24​y2​Pz2−lnx​(y−x)y2)+fin. ,0<x<y<1,displaystyle=deltaleft(1-fracxyright)+4asCA,leftfrac(2x2-3xy+2y2)(x-y),yleft(lnfracmu24y2Pz2-lnfracx(y-x)y2right)+fin. right,0<x<y<1, Cg​qsubscript𝐶𝑔𝑞displaystyle Cgq =δ(1−xy)+4asCF(x−2​y)y(lnμ24​y2​Pz2−lnx​(y−x)y2)+fin. ,0<x<y<1,displaystyle=deltaleft(1-fracxyright)+4asCF,leftfrac(x-2y)yleft(lnfracmu24y2Pz2-lnfracx(y-x)y2right)+fin. right,0<x<y<1, as the logarithmic momentum dependence ln⁡(μ/Pz)𝜇subscript𝑃𝑧ln(mu/Pz) appears only in the physical region. However, there is an inconsistency in the two approaches above. According to LaMET, the intrinsic momentum scale in the quasi-observables is the parton momentum y​Pz𝑦subscript𝑃𝑧yPz rather than the hadron momentum Pzsubscript𝑃𝑧Pz. Such a momentum scale is then traded for the renormalization scale of the lightcone observables by the matching. This shall also be true in the factorization of the total gluon helicity Eq. . But in Eq. only the total parton helicity is involved, and there is no momentum fraction at all. To put it differently, the multiplicative factorization form in Eq. cannot be reproduced in general from Eq. by integrating over the momentum fraction x𝑥x on both sides. What is typically generated is a convoluted factorization form, unless the matching becomes trivial where the intrinsic parton momentum scale dependence drops out. Moreover, all existing proposals on calculating Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G from the matrix element of E→×A→→𝐸→𝐴vecEtimesvecA in a fixed gauge suffer from practical difficulties, either the nonperturbative renormalization is difficult to carry out or the desired gauge cannot be achieved on the lattice. For example, in Ref. citebib.bib16 the calculation was done in Coulomb gauge, where a nonperturbative renormalization was difficult to implement. The authors chose to match the bare result to the continuum through lattice perturbation theory, the convergence of which is known to be rather poor. To resolve these issues, here we propose two different approaches. One is based on local operator matrix elements in a fixed gauge accessible on lattice with feasible renormalization, and the other is based on gauge invariant nonlocal gluon quasi-LF correlations. It turns out that by choosing appropriate local or nonlocal operators, the matching can be made trivial and the two factorization formulas above become consistent, provided that the relevant quantities are converted to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme. Our proposal has the potential to greatly facilitate lattice calculations of the total gluon helicity. The rest of the paper is organized as follows: in Sec. , we discuss the gauge dependence of the topological current Kμsuperscript𝐾𝜇Kmu, and the subtlety in taking its forward matrix element. Based on a boost argument, we propose to calculate its nonforward matrix element in Coulomb gauge on lattice, and explain how to do the renormalization in the regularization-independent momentum subtraction scheme (RI/MOM)citebib.bib17. We then show how the total gluon helicity can be extracted from this matrix element without a perturbative matching. In Sec. , we present our second approach based on gauge-invariant nonlocal gluon correlators, and show how they can be connected to the total gluon helicity, again without a perturbative matching. We then conclude in Sec. .
content_sections.2 Gluon helicity from local operator mat: As discussed in the Introduction, the existing proposals of calculating the total gluon helicity from the proton matrix element of E→×A→→𝐸→𝐴vecEtimesvecA in a specific gauge suffers from the difficulty of implementing either the gauge or the nonperturbative renormalization. In this section, we give a new proposal relating the total gluon helicity to the local topological current in a fixed gauge, which circumvents the above problems and does not require a perturbative matching in the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme.

The EMC measurement of the spin asymmetry in polarized muon-proton scattering in the 1980s indicated that the total quark spin content was comparable to zero, in contradiction with the naive quark model expectationscitebib.bib18. It was then proposed that due to the Adler-Bell-Jackiw anomalycitebib.bib19, bib.bib20, besides quark helicity, the forward matrix element of the axial vector current will also include the gluon helicity content, providing a possible way out of the “proton spin crisis” citebib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25. Moreover, in the parton model where the lightcone gauge is a natural choice, the total gluon helicity has a direct relation to the topological current K+superscript𝐾K+citebib.bib4, bib.bib26: =4​S+​Δ​G,quantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆superscript𝐾𝑃𝑆4superscript𝑆Δ𝐺big=4S+Delta G, where Sμ=(Pz,0,0,P0)superscript𝑆𝜇subscript𝑃𝑧00subscript𝑃0Smu=(Pz,0,0,P0) is the spin vector of the proton, and in the calculation of Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G from Eq. the principal-value prescription of the 1/x1𝑥1/x pole and a boundary condition Ai​(∞−)=0superscript𝐴𝑖superscript0Ai(infty-)=0 have been assumed. The topological current is defined as: Kμ=superscript𝐾𝜇absentdisplaystyle Kmu= ϵμ​ν​ρ​σ​Tr​[Aν​Fα​β−23​i​gs​Aν​Aα​Aβ]=12​ϵμ​ν​ρ​σ​(Aνa​Fρ​σa+gs3​fa​b​c​Aνa​Aρb​Aσc),superscriptitalic-ϵ𝜇𝜈𝜌𝜎Trdelimited-subscript𝐴𝜈subscript𝐹𝛼𝛽23𝑖subscript𝑔𝑠subscript𝐴𝜈subscript𝐴𝛼subscript𝐴𝛽12superscriptitalic-ϵ𝜇𝜈𝜌𝜎superscriptsubscript𝐴𝜈𝑎superscriptsubscript𝐹𝜌𝜎𝑎subscript𝑔𝑠3subscript𝑓𝑎𝑏𝑐superscriptsubscript𝐴𝜈𝑎superscriptsubscript𝐴𝜌𝑏superscriptsubscript𝐴𝜎𝑐displaystyleepsilonmunurhosigmatextTrbig[AnuFalphabeta-frac23igsAnuAalphaAbetabig]=frac12epsilonmunurhosigmaleft(AnuaFrhosigmaa+fracgs3fabcAnuaArhobAsigmacright), where after the first equal sign we take trace of the product of color matrices. Under the gauge transformation, we have: Aμ→→subscript𝐴𝜇absentdisplaystyle Amurightarrow U​Aμ​U†−igs​U​∂μU†,𝑈subscript𝐴𝜇superscript𝑈†𝑖subscript𝑔𝑠𝑈subscript𝜇superscript𝑈†displaystyle UAmuUdagger-fracigsUpartialmuUdagger, Kμ→→subscript𝐾𝜇absentdisplaystyle Kmurightarrow Kμ−2​igs​εμ​ν​α​β​∂αTr​[(∂νU†)​U​Aβ]+23​gs2​εμ​ν​α​β​[U†​(∂νU)​U†​(∂αU)​U†​(∂βU)]subscript𝐾𝜇2𝑖subscript𝑔𝑠superscript𝜀𝜇𝜈𝛼𝛽subscript𝛼Trdelimited-subscript𝜈superscript𝑈†𝑈subscript𝐴𝛽23superscriptsubscript𝑔𝑠2superscript𝜀𝜇𝜈𝛼𝛽delimited-superscript𝑈†subscript𝜈𝑈superscript𝑈†subscript𝛼𝑈superscript𝑈†subscript𝛽𝑈displaystyle Kmu-frac2igsvarepsilonmunualphabetapartialalphatextTrleft[(partialnuUdagger)UAbetaright]+frac23gs2varepsilonmunualphabetaleft[Udagger(partialnuU)Udagger(partialalphaU)Udagger(partialbetaU)right] =displaystyle= Kμ+δ​K1μ+δ​K2μ. superscript𝐾𝜇𝛿superscriptsubscript𝐾1𝜇𝛿superscriptsubscript𝐾2𝜇displaystyle Kmu+delta K1mu+delta K2mu. The second term in the last line of Eq. , δ​K1μ𝛿superscriptsubscript𝐾1𝜇delta K1mu, is a total derivative and vanishes in the forward matrix element. δ​K2μ𝛿superscriptsubscript𝐾2𝜇delta K2mu comes from the gauge transformation of the three-gluon term in Eq. . As shown incitebib.bib30, bib.bib31, the bisubscript𝑏𝑖bi in Eq. contains a gauge-dependent massless pole, making the forward limit dependent on the direction along which the momentum transfer approaches zero. In covariant gauges, we have b1​(q2)∼1/q2similar-tosubscript𝑏1superscript𝑞21superscript𝑞2b1(q2)sim1/q2. In the massive Schwinger model and covariant gauge, Kogut and Susskind have explicitly showncitebib.bib32 that the unphysical pole comes from the coupling between Kμsuperscript𝐾𝜇Kmu and a ghost field. The ghost field, together with a scalar field with equal but opposite metric, forms a Goldstone dipole. They will cancel out with each other in physical matrix elementscitebib.bib32. The discussion in QCD is more involved, but the massless ghost pole still exists in the propagator quantum-operator-product0superscript𝐾𝜇superscript𝐾𝜈0big, and thus in bisubscript𝑏𝑖bicitebib.bib33. Some authors identify limq2→0​a1​(q2)subscriptlim→superscript𝑞20subscript𝑎1superscript𝑞2textlimq2rightarrow 0,a1(q2) as the total gluon helicitycitebib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib34, which is doubtful to us for two reasons. Firstly, unlike an axial gauge, in the IMF limit the covariant gauge does not approach the lightcone gaugecitebib.bib12. In the axial gauge, a1​subscript𝑎10a1 differs from Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G by a power-suppressed contribution in the large momentum framecitebib.bib31, while in covariant gauges a1​subscript𝑎10a1 cannot be related to the total gluon helicity due to their different boost behavior. Second, the three-gluon term in Kμsuperscript𝐾𝜇Kmu does not vanish in covariant gauges, and is sensitive to large gauge transformation that changes the winding number. Thus in covariant gauges, a1​subscript𝑎10a1 in general differs from Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G by an unknown nonperturbative quantitycitebib.bib35, bib.bib36.

There are two sets of gauges that are most often used in lattice calculations: the Landau gauge and the Coulomb gauge. From the above discussion, we have seen that in covariant gauges, there is a distinction between the matrix element of Kμsuperscript𝐾𝜇Kmu and the total gluon helicity. In this subsection, we explore the possibility of relating expectationsuperscript𝐾𝜇big to the total gluon helicity, in the Coulomb gauge (expectation⋯big denotes the nonforward matrix element of “⋯⋯cdots” in the subsequent discussion unless stated otherwise). We justify that in the large momentum frame, the matrix element of Kμsuperscript𝐾𝜇Kmu in the Coulomb gauge differs from that in the lightcone gauge by a higher-twist contribution. Moreover, an efficient renormalization procedure is proposed, by utilizing the perturbative gauge-invariance of the forward limit of expectationsuperscript𝐾𝜇big. In this section, we give details for the renormalization of the bare, non-forward expectationsuperscript𝐾𝜇big, in the Coulomb gauge. Due to quantum effects, the topological current will mix with other local operators. Under a gauge transformation, Kμsuperscript𝐾𝜇Kmu changes by a total derivative (Eq. ). Thus in general, it is allowed to mix with total derivative operators of the form ∂μOsuperscript𝜇𝑂partialmuO. ∂μOsuperscript𝜇𝑂partialmuO vanishes when sandwiched between forward states, which is the case in our RI/MOM renormalization. Interestingly, though free from the renormalization scale dependence, R21MS¯,RIsuperscriptsubscript𝑅21¯MSRIR21overlinetextMS,textRI still contains a finite contribution −2​as​CF2subscript𝑎𝑠subscript𝐶𝐹-2asCF, since the on-shell limit and the loop integral are not interchangeable. No new Lorentz structure appears in the off-shell calculation of Kμsuperscript𝐾𝜇Kmu, which justifies our mixing pattern Eq. . Once the renormalized results are converted to the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme, no matching procedure is needed to extract the total gluon helicity.
content_sections.3 Gluon helicity from nonlocal gluon cor: The strategy of using local operators to calculate the total gluon helicity involves gauge-dependent matrix elements. In this section, we propose another method starting from the gauge-invariant gluon helicity quasi-LF correlators, and show how the total gluon helicity can be obtained without a perturbative matching.

To extract the total gluon helicity Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G, one can start from the polarized gluon distribution and take its first moment. The LF operator defining the gluon helicity distribution is given by m+μ;+μsuperscript𝑚𝜇𝜇displaystyle m+mu;+mu =F+μ​(ξ−)​ℒ​(ξ−,0)​Fμ+​. absentsuperscript𝐹𝜇superscript𝜉ℒsuperscript𝜉0subscriptsuperscript𝐹𝜇0displaystyle=F+mu(xi-)L(xi-,0)tildeF+mu. Hereafter color indices of the gluon fields are omitted for brevity. Due to the antisymmetry of the gluon field strength tensor and the Levi-Civita tensor, the polarized gluon distribution operator must satisfy antisymmetry, toocitebib.bib48. The LF gluon operator above is nonlocal and along the lightcone direction, and cannot be directly calculated on the lattice. However, according to LaMET, we can consider a quasi-LF gluon operator which approaches the LF operator in the IMF limit. Here we again take the following quasi-LF operator as an example m3​μ;0​μ=F3​μ​(z)​ℒ​(z,0)​Fμ0​. superscript𝑚3𝜇0𝜇superscript𝐹3𝜇𝑧ℒ𝑧0subscriptsuperscript𝐹0𝜇0displaystyle m3mu;0mu=F3mu(z)L(z,0)tildeF0mu. In the literaturecitebib.bib48, bib.bib13, there also exist other forms of quasi-LF gluon operators that lead to the same LF physics in the IMF limit. We will briefly discuss them at the end of this section. The quasi-LF correlation defined by m3​μ;0​μsuperscript𝑚3𝜇0𝜇m3mu;0mu reads h​(z,Pz,1/a)=⟨P​S|F3​μ​(z)​ℒ​(z,0)​Fμ0​|P​S⟩,ℎ𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧1𝑎quantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆superscript𝐹3𝜇𝑧ℒ𝑧0subscriptsuperscript𝐹0𝜇0𝑃𝑆displaystyletildeh(z,Pz,1/a)= PS|F3mu(z)L(z,0)tildeF0mu|PS, where we have inserted the 1/a1𝑎1/a dependence with a𝑎a being the lattice spacing to indicate the potential UV divergence on the lattice. |P​S⟩ket𝑃𝑆|PS denotes a longitudinally polarized proton state with momentum P𝑃P and spin S𝑆S. According to the definitions in Eqs. and, we have Δ​g​(x,Pz,1/a)Δ𝑔𝑥subscript𝑃𝑧1𝑎displaystyleDeltatildeg(x,Pz,1/a) =i2​x​Pz​∫d​z2​π​ei​x​z​Pz​h​(z,Pz,1/a),absent𝑖2𝑥subscript𝑃𝑧𝑑𝑧2𝜋superscript𝑒𝑖𝑥𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧ℎ𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧1𝑎displaystyle=fraci2xPzintfracdz2pieix,zPz,tildeh(z,Pz,1/a), Δ​G​(Pz,1/a)Δ𝐺subscript𝑃𝑧1𝑎displaystyleDeltatildeG(Pz,1/a) ≡∫𝑑x​Δ​g​(x,Pz,1/a)=12​Pz​∫0∞𝑑z​h​(z,Pz,1/a),absentdifferential-d𝑥Δ𝑔𝑥subscript𝑃𝑧1𝑎12subscript𝑃𝑧superscriptsubscript0differential-d𝑧ℎ𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧1𝑎displaystyleequivint dx,Deltatildeg(x,Pz,1/a)=frac12Pzint0inftydz,tildeh(z,Pz,1/a), where we have used 1x​ei​x​λ=i​∫∞​(−1+i​ϵ)λ𝑑ζ​ei​x​ζ,1𝑥superscript𝑒𝑖𝑥𝜆𝑖superscriptsubscript1𝑖italic-ϵ𝜆differential-d𝜁superscript𝑒𝑖𝑥𝜁displaystylefrac1xeixlambda=iintinfty(-1+iepsilon)lambdadzeta,eixzeta, with λ=z​Pz𝜆𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧lambda=zPz being the quasi-LF distance. From the relation in Eq. , Δ​GΔ𝐺DeltatildeG can be expressed as an integral over the quasi-LF correlation. Note that at large momentum, the quasi-LF correlation is expected to decay fast enough with λ𝜆lambda so that the integral is convergent. A relation similar to Eq. also exists between the total gluon helicity and the LF correlation.

The discussion in the previous subsection has been focused on the bare quantities only. They need to be nonperturbatively renormalized in a proper scheme. A convenient choice in the literature is the hybrid schemecitebib.bib49, where one renormalizes the quasi-LF correlation at short distances by a ratio schemecitebib.bib50, while uses a self-renormalization schemecitebib.bib51 at long distances. In this way, the renormalized gluon quasi-LF correlation reads hRhyb. ​(z,Pz)=subscriptsuperscriptℎhyb𝑅𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧absentdisplaystyletildehrmhyb. R(z,Pz)= h​(z,Pz,1/a)h(z,Pz=0,1/a)​θ​(zs−|z|)+Ts​e−δ​m​|z|​h​(z,Pz,1/a)​θ​(|z|−zs),displaystylefractildeh(z,Pz,1/a)tildeh(z,Pz=0,1/a)theta(zs-|z|)+Tstexte-delta m|z|,tildeh(z,Pz,1/a),theta(|z|-zs), where zssubscript𝑧𝑠zs is a truncation point within the perturbative region to divide the long-distance and short-distance regions. Tssubscript𝑇𝑠Ts removes the logarithmic divergences in the quasi-LF correlation and ensures its continuity at the truncation point z=zs𝑧subscript𝑧𝑠z=zs. e−δ​m​|z|superscript𝑒𝛿𝑚𝑧e-delta m|z| is the Wilson line mass renormalization factor, which contains a linearly divergent counterterm δ​m𝛿𝑚delta m to remove the linear UV divergence arising from the Wilson line self-energy in lattice regularization. The renormalized quasi-LF correlation in the hybrid scheme can be converted to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme by multiplying with a scheme conversion factor. The reason that we are interested in the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme will become clear below. In the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme, we have hRMS¯​(z,Pz,μ)=subscriptsuperscriptℎ¯MS𝑅𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧𝜇absentdisplaystyletildehoverlinermMSR(z,Pz,mu)= ZT​(μ,z,zs)​hRhyb. ​(z,Pz),subscript𝑍𝑇𝜇𝑧subscript𝑧𝑠subscriptsuperscriptℎhyb𝑅𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧displaystyle ZT(mu,z,zs)tildehrmhyb. R(z,Pz), where ZT​(μ,z,zs)subscript𝑍𝑇𝜇𝑧subscript𝑧𝑠ZT(mu,z,zs) is the conversion factor that can be calculated perturbatively. Note that in the hybrid scheme the self-renormalized quasi-LF correlation at long distances are already in the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme. Therefore, we need the conversion factor only for short-distance quasi-LF correlations. It can be calculated using the fact that the ratio-renormalized result is independent of the regularization scheme. Thus, we can read out the conversion factor from existing results in DR and MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS, which takes the following form at one loopcitebib.bib14 ZT​(μ,z,zs)subscript𝑍𝑇𝜇𝑧subscript𝑧𝑠displaystyle ZTleft(mu,z,zsright) =Z​(μ,z)​θ​(zs−|z|),absent𝑍𝜇𝑧𝜃subscript𝑧𝑠𝑧displaystyle=Zleft(mu,zright)theta(zs-|z|), Z​(μ,z)𝑍𝜇𝑧displaystyle Zleft(mu,zright) =H(z,Pz=0,μ)=1+2asCA(73lnμ2​z24​e−2​γE+73),displaystyle=tildeHleft(z,Pz=0,muright)=1+2asCAleft(frac73lnfracmu2z24texte-2gammaE+frac73right),, where H𝐻tildeH denotes the perturbative quasi-LF correlation. The MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme matching kernel for the gluon helicity distribution has been worked out to the NLO, both in coordinate space and in momentum space in Ref. citebib.bib14. To show their implication, here we write down the coordinate space matching kernel explicitly. Note that in the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme, the LF correlation contains scaleless integrals only, and therefore the matching kernel can be straightforwardly read out from H𝐻tildeH and takes the following formcitebib.bib14 𝑪g​g​(α,z,μ)subscript𝑪𝑔𝑔𝛼𝑧𝜇displaystyleCgg(alpha,z,mu) =δ​(α)+2​as​CA​(4​α​α¯+2​[α¯2α]+)​(Lz−1)+6​α​α¯−4​[ln⁡(α)α]++(−3​Lz+2)​δ​(α),absent𝛿𝛼2subscript𝑎𝑠subscript𝐶𝐴4𝛼¯𝛼2subscriptdelimited-superscript¯𝛼2𝛼subscriptLz16𝛼¯𝛼4subscriptdelimited-𝛼𝛼3subscriptLz2𝛿𝛼displaystyle=delta(alpha)+2asCAbiggleft(4alphabaralpha+2left[fracbaralpha2alpharight]+right)left(rmLz-1right)+6alphabaralpha-4left[fracln(alpha)alpharight]++left(-3,rmLz+2right)delta(alpha)bigg, 𝑪g​q​(α,z,μ)subscript𝑪𝑔𝑞𝛼𝑧𝜇displaystyleCgq(alpha,z,mu) =−2​i​as​CFz​−2​α​(Lz+1)−4​α¯+Lz​δ​(α),absent2𝑖subscript𝑎𝑠subscript𝐶𝐹𝑧2𝛼subscriptLz14¯𝛼subscriptLz𝛿𝛼displaystyle=frac-2iasCFzbigg-2alphaleft(rmLz+1right)-4baralpha+rmLz,delta(alpha)bigg,, where α¯=1−α¯𝛼1𝛼baralpha=1-alpha, and Lz=ln⁡4​e−2​γE−μ2​z2subscriptLz4superscript𝑒2subscript𝛾𝐸superscript𝜇2superscript𝑧2rmLz=lnfrac4e-2gammaE-mu2z2 from the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme. According to Eq. , we have Δ​G​(Pz)Δ𝐺subscript𝑃𝑧displaystyleDeltatildeG(Pz) =∫dλ∫01d​αα¯[𝑪g​g(α,λα¯​Pz,μ)hg(λ,μ)+𝑪g​q(α,λα¯​Pz,μ)hq(λ,μ)]+h. t. =ΔG+h. t. ,displaystyle=int dlambdaint01fracdalphabaralphaleft[Cggleft(alpha,fraclambdabaralphaPz,muright)hg(lambda,mu)+Cgqleft(alpha,fraclambdabaralphaPz,muright)hq(lambda,mu)right]+h. t. =Delta G+h. t. , where hg​(λ,μ)subscriptℎ𝑔𝜆𝜇hg(lambda,mu) and hq​(λ,μ)subscriptℎ𝑞𝜆𝜇hq(lambda,mu) represent the LF correlations defining the gluon and quark helicity distributions, respectively. Eq. implies that the integral of the polarized gluon matrix element is directly related to the total gluon helicity Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G up to power-suppressed contributions, but without a perturbative matching. The same can be shown to be true also in momentum space. The demonstration above is based on the NLO result of the matching kernel. However, it is easy to show that ∫0∞𝑑z​⟨P​S|m3​μ;0​μ|P​S⟩=⟨P​S|Ai​Bi|P​S⟩|Az=0,superscriptsubscript0differential-d𝑧quantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆superscript𝑚3𝜇0𝜇𝑃𝑆evaluated-atquantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆superscript𝐴𝑖superscript𝐵𝑖𝑃𝑆superscript𝐴𝑧0displaystyleint0inftydz PS|m3mu;0mu|PS= PS|AiBi|PS|Az=0, where i,j=1,2𝑖𝑗12i,j=1,2 represent the transverse components, Bi=ϵi​j​k​Fj​ksuperscript𝐵𝑖subscriptitalic-ϵ𝑖𝑗𝑘superscript𝐹𝑗𝑘Bi=epsilonijkFjk is the color magenetic field. The r. h. s. of Eq. is the matrix element of the topological current Kμsuperscript𝐾𝜇Kmu in the axial gauge, namely, Ai​Bi=K0|Az=0superscript𝐴𝑖superscript𝐵𝑖evaluated-atsuperscript𝐾0superscript𝐴𝑧0AiBi=K0big|Az=0. It differs from Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G by power suppressed contributions only. Thus, we can conclude that the relation Eq. between Δ​GΔ𝐺DeltatildeG and the total gluon helicity Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G without perturbative matching, is valid to all orders. As mentioned earlier, the choice for the gluon quasi-LF operator is not unique. For example, the following operators can also be used for lattice calculations of the gluon helicity distribution 12​(m0​μ;0​μ+m3​μ;3​μ)=12​(F0​μ​(z)​ℒ​(z,0)​Fμ0​+F3​μ​(z)​ℒ​(z,0)​Fμ3​),12superscript𝑚0𝜇0𝜇superscript𝑚3𝜇3𝜇12superscript𝐹0𝜇𝑧ℒ𝑧0subscriptsuperscript𝐹0𝜇0superscript𝐹3𝜇𝑧ℒ𝑧0subscriptsuperscript𝐹3𝜇0displaystylefrac12left(m0mu;0mu+m3mu;3muright)=frac12left(F0mu(z)L(z,0)tildeF0mu+F3mu(z)L(z,0)tildeF3muright), m0​μ;3​μ=F0​μ​(z)​ℒ​(z,0)​Fμ3​. superscript𝑚0𝜇3𝜇superscript𝐹0𝜇𝑧ℒ𝑧0subscriptsuperscript𝐹3𝜇0displaystyle m0mu;3mu=F0mu(z)L(z,0)tildeF3mu. It is easy to prove that the above operators satisfy anti-symmetrycitebib.bib48. For these two cases, the no-matching statement above is expected to be true as well, which can be justified by the fact that Δ​GΔ𝐺DeltatildeG defined in terms of the operators in Eq. can be related to the following local matrix elements in the axial-type gauge: ∫0∞𝑑z​⟨P​S|12​(m0​μ;0​μ+m3​μ;3​μ)|P​S⟩=⟨P​S|12​A→⋅B→|P​S⟩|A0=0+⟨P​S|12​ϵi​j​(Fi​0​Aj−12​A0​Fi​j)|P​S⟩|Az=0,superscriptsubscript0differential-d𝑧quantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆12superscript𝑚0𝜇0𝜇superscript𝑚3𝜇3𝜇𝑃𝑆evaluated-atquantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆⋅12→𝐴→𝐵𝑃𝑆superscript𝐴00evaluated-atquantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆12subscriptitalic-ϵ𝑖𝑗superscript𝐹𝑖0superscript𝐴𝑗12superscript𝐴0superscript𝐹𝑖𝑗𝑃𝑆superscript𝐴𝑧0displaystyleint0inftydz PS|frac12!left(m0mu;0mu+m3mu;3muright)!|PS=! PS|frac12vecAcdotvecB,|PS|A0=0!+! PS|frac12epsilonijleft(!Fi0Aj-frac12A0Fij!right)|PSBig|Az=0,! ∫0∞𝑑z​⟨P​S|m0​μ;3​μ|P​S⟩=⟨P​S|ϵi​j​(Fi​0​Aj)|P​S⟩|A0=0,superscriptsubscript0differential-d𝑧quantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆superscript𝑚0𝜇3𝜇𝑃𝑆evaluated-atquantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆subscriptitalic-ϵ𝑖𝑗superscript𝐹𝑖0superscript𝐴𝑗𝑃𝑆superscript𝐴00displaystyleint0inftydz PS|m0mu;3mu|PS= PS|epsilonijleft(Fi0Ajright)|PSBig|A0=0, where the first line has also been derived in Ref. citebib.bib12. Since the r. h. s. of Eq. is just the matrix element of the topological current Kμsuperscript𝐾𝜇Kmu in the axial-type gauge (with μ=0𝜇0mu=0 or 333), we conclude that no matching is required also for the operators in Eq. . All quasi-LF operators given here can be used to efficiently calculate the total gluon helicity Δ​GΔ𝐺Delta G from lattice.
content_sections.4 Conclusion: To conclude, we have presented two approaches to extract the total gluon helicity contribution to proton spin from lattice QCD. The first was based on the nonforward matrix element of the topological current in the Coulomb gauge. We explained the subtlety in taking the forward limit as well as how to implement lattice renormalization in the RI/MOM scheme. The renormalized matrix element was then linked to the total gluon helicity up to power-suppressed contributions. Our second approach was based on nonlocal gluon quasi-LF correlators. We explicitly showed that by choosing appropriate operators, the perturbative matching for the gluon helicity distribution, and thus for the total gluon helicity can be made trivial, provided that all relevant quantities are converted to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme. Our results resolved a long-standing inconsistency in lattice calculations of the total gluon helicity from local and nonlocal operator matrix elements, and have the potential to greatly facilitate these calculations. Acknowledgements. We thank Yi-Bo Yang for helpful discussions on renormalization and gauge fixing on the lattice. This work is supported in part by National Natural Science Foundation of China under grants No. 12375080, 12061131006, 11975051.

all_papers.title:
Nonperturbative renormalization of asymmetric staple-shaped operators in
  twisted mass lattice QCD
all_papers.abstract:
Staple-shaped Wilson line operators are necessary for the study of transverse
momentum-dependent parton distribution functions (TMDPDFs) in lattice QCD and
beyond. In this work, we study the renormalization of such operators in the
general case of an asymmetric staple. We analyze the mixing pattern of these
operators using their symmetry properties, where we find that the possible
mixing is restricted within groups of four operators. We then present numerical
results using the regularization independent momentum subtraction (RI/MOM)
scheme to study the importance of mixing using one operator in particular, the
$\gamma_0$ operator. Based on these results, we consider the short distance
ratio (SDR) scheme, which is desirable in the absence of mixing. Finally, we
investigate a variant of the RI/MOM scheme, where the renormalization factors
are computed at short distances.

qualitative: Asymmetric staple-shaped Wilson line operators are essential for studying transverse momentum-dependent parton distribution functions (TMDPDFs) in lattice QCD.
The renormalization of asymmetric staples is analyzed through symmetry properties, which restrict operator mixing to groups of four operators.
Mixing between operators in the asymmetric staple family is nontrivial and must be accounted for in renormalization procedures.
The regularization-independent momentum subtraction (RI/MOM) scheme is used to compute renormalization factors numerically, revealing significant mixing effects.
The short-distance ratio (SDR) scheme is proposed as a desirable alternative in the absence of mixing, offering a cleaner renormalization framework.
A variant of the RI/MOM scheme is introduced where renormalization factors are computed at short distances, improving control over nonperturbative effects.
quantitative: The mixing pattern of asymmetric staple operators is restricted within groups of four, determined by symmetry considerations.
Numerical results in the RI/MOM scheme show non-negligible mixing effects, particularly for the γ₀ operator, indicating that mixing cannot be ignored.
The short-distance ratio (SDR) scheme is defined such that renormalization factors are extracted from ratios of correlators at short distances, minimizing mixing contributions.
The variant RI/MOM scheme computes renormalization factors at short momentum scales, with specific momentum configurations used to suppress lattice artifacts and enhance precision.
