authors:
Constantia Alexandrou, Simone Bacchio, Krzysztof Cichy, Martha
  Constantinou, Xu Feng, Karl Jansen, Chuan Liu, Aniket Sen, Gregoris
  Spanoudes, Fernanda Steffens, Jacopo Tarello

content_sections.I Introduction: Collinear parton distribution functions (PDFs) probe the hadron structure from the perspective of the spin and longitudinal momentum distributions of the quarks and gluons that make up the hadron. The vast amount of work to determine these distributions over the last five decades, both theoretically and experimentally, has enormously expanded our view of the hadron structure, the proton in particularcitebib.bib1. And yet, these developments are mostly limited to probing the one-dimensional structure of the proton. In order to have a wider understanding of the proton structure, we also need to understand how the momentum and the spin are distributed in the transverse plane. For that, we need to measure and compute the generalized parton distributions and the transverse-momentum-dependent PDFs (TMDPDFs), the latter being the main focus of this present work. Although there has been an effort to obtain TMDPDFs from phenomenological fits to experimental datacitebib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, their accuracy is still far from being at the same level as the collinear PDFs. This status will change in the coming years with new data coming from Jefferson Labcitebib.bib8 and from the future Electron-Ion-Collider to be built at Brookhaven National Labcitebib.bib9. It is, thus, of great importance to extract TMDPDFs from first principles calculations, namely, lattice QCD. In the last 10 years, there has been an enormous advance in computing the collinear PDFs using lattice QCDcitebib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18. By comparison, the computation of TMDPDFs is still in its infancycitebib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, although progress has been made in the computation of key elements required to build the TMDPDFS, like the soft functionscitebib.bib23, bib.bib24 and the Collins-Soper kernelcitebib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27. Such computations have been restricted to ratios of TMDPDFscitebib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, and only recently, a first full lattice QCD calculation of the TMDPDFs themselves has been presentedcitebib.bib32. One fundamental difficulty in these calculations is to have control over the renormalization procedure, which is more involved than in the case of collinear PDFs. In particular, the staple-shaped gauge link that enters in the evaluation of TMDPDFs has three types of divergences: i) linear divergence coming from the Wilson line, which connects the quark fields and which depends on the length of the staple-shaped link; ii) logarithmic divergences coming form the endpoints of the staple link, similarly to the case of straight gauge links; and iii) logarithmic divergences coming from the presence of cusps in the staple. In addition, in the limit of infinite-length staple, L𝐿L, (which is the case of interest), pinch-pole singularities arise as positive powers of L𝐿L, coming from the gluon exchange between the transverse segments of the staple. Moreover, as in the case of straight gauge links, staple-shaped operators of different Dirac structures can and will mix on the lattice among certain groups (when chiral symmetry is broken), as dictated by discrete symmetries. However, in this case, the mixing pattern of the operators employed in lattice regularization can be significantly more involved than in the case of the straight Wilson line. A first study within lattice perturbation theory to one-loop for the case of the symmetric staplecitebib.bib33 showed mixing between specific pairs of Dirac structures. This mixing cannot be avoided when one is interested in matching bare lattice Green’s functions to the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme (directly or indirectly through an intermediate scheme). The mixing depended solely on the direction of the staple link entering the endpoints of the staple, regardless of the shape of the staple. This implies that the same mixing pattern occurs also for asymmetric staple shapes. In Ref. citebib.bib34, the mixing pattern of these staple-shaped operators1For conciseness, we will refer to staple-shaped Wilson-line quark bilinear operators as staple-shaped operators. has been studied using symmetry considerations. It was found that more mixing is present than observed in Ref. citebib.bib33. This demonstrates that one-loop perturbation theory cannot fully reveal the mixing of the staple-shaped operators, unlike the case of straight Wilson-line operatorscitebib.bib35, and a higher-loop computation is needed to confirm the additional mixing patterns found by symmetry arguments. The authors of Refs. citebib.bib27, bib.bib23, bib.bib36 consider a maximal RI-type prescription, in which all 16 independent non-local Wilson-line quark bilinear operators are chosen to mix to eliminate all possible mixing effects. While Ref. citebib.bib27 identified nonzero contributions in several off-diagonal elements of the renormalization matrix, Refs. citebib.bib23, bib.bib36 found negligible contributions, at least at small transverse separations, by setting specific momentum components to be zero. However, we emphasize that not all contributions are necessary for addressing the “unavoidable” mixing among the asymmetric staple-shaped operators on the lattice. Most off-diagonal elements in the 16×16161616times 16 renormalization matrix are nonzero due to the non-minimal choice of renormalization conditions and not due to the unavoidable mixing. In this sense, it is preferable to construct a minimal intermediate scheme, keeping only the mixing sets that are needed for matching the bare lattice Green’s functions to the corresponding MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS-renormalized Green’s functions (as obtained in dimensional regularization). In our study, we consider such a minimal scheme by using symmetry arguments to restrict the operators allowed to mix. Improving renormalization schemes on the lattice eliminates finite lattice spacing errors, which can come from different Dirac structures in Green’s functions under consideration. A way of removing artifacts from all Dirac structures is to consider a wider mixing pattern, where higher dimensional operators multiplied by the appropriate power of the lattice spacing can also mix with the operators under study. This mixing is only present for finite values of the lattice spacing, while it vanishes when taking the continuum limit. The higher-dimensional operators will be higher twist since (by Lorentz invariance) they must have the same spin (with the operators under study), but their dimension will be higher. The unwanted effects of finite lattice spacing errors and higher-twist contributions are not considered in the present study; they will be addressed in a future publication. On the other hand, it has been showncitebib.bib37 that the linear divergence in the lattice spacing a𝑎a is not fully eliminated when the RI/MOM scheme is used to renormalize a straight Wilson line of length z𝑧z. Ref. citebib.bib36 has shown that this residual linear divergence remains in the case of the staple-shaped operator. In this scenario, an alternative approach that one can adopt is the so-called ratio scheme as proposed for the quasi-PDF casecitebib.bib11, bib.bib38, bib.bib39. In this approach, one subtracts the Ultra-Violet (UV) divergences by taking the ratio with a suitable object at a fixed short distance, where perturbation theory applies. Different choices of suitable objects to be used in the ratio have been proposed in Ref. citebib.bib40. The authors of Refs. citebib.bib36, bib.bib32 use ratios of the matrix elements of the operator under study. As the divergences of the staple-shaped operator are independent of the longitudinal momentum of the state, one can use the matrix elements computed at different values of the momentum in order to cancel the divergences. Usually, matrix elements at zero momentum are chosen for the denominator, and such scheme has been namedcitebib.bib32 short distance ratio (SDR) scheme. As for the remaining divergences associated with the asymmetric staple-shaped link, one can cancel them by taking an appropriate ratio with the vacuum expectation value of a rectangular Wilson loopcitebib.bib41. However, we would like to stress that the SDR scheme is valid when operator mixing is absent or negligible. We check that in the case under study the mixing is indeed negligible and thus, one can employ the ratio scheme. Another option, as proposed in Ref. citebib.bib34, is to employ RI/MOM in the spirit of the SDR scheme by fixing the dimensions of the staple at a short perturbative range (we will call this scheme RI-short). The paper is organized as follows. Section discusses how TMDs can be accessed on a Euclidean lattice using the quasi-distribution approach. In Section, we study operator mixing using symmetry arguments. Section presents our lattice setup. The size of the mixing is examined in Section. In Sections, and we employ RI/MOM, SDR and RI-short schemes, respectively, by following the procedures described in Ref. citebib.bib36, bib.bib32, bib.bib34. In Section, we present the renormalized beam functions in the three renormalization schemes discussed here, and then show the corresponding results in the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme. The conclusions are presented in Section.
content_sections.II Quasi-TMDPDFs: A first-principle computation of TMDPDFs in the context of LaMET is more involved than collinear PDFs. The main obstacle is to correctly subtract the so-called rapidity divergences, which are associated with the soft gluon radiation of the infinitely long Wilson lines present in the staple-shaped operator. This subtraction is made through the use of a soft functioncitebib.bib42, bib.bib43. Different ways how to perform this subtraction in the context of LaMET can be found in Ref. citebib.bib44. In general, the TMD soft function involves two opposite light-like directions, and this makes a lattice calculation significantly challenging. In Refs. citebib.bib21, bib.bib41, the authors define a rapidity-independent reduced soft function Srsubscript𝑆𝑟Sr and show that it can be extracted from a form factor and the quasi-TMD wave function of a light meson. Using Srsubscript𝑆𝑟Sr, the rapidity scheme-independent TMDPDF fT​M​D​(x,b,μ,ζ)superscript𝑓𝑇𝑀𝐷𝑥𝑏𝜇𝜁fTMD(x,b,mu,zeta) can be written ascitebib.bib41, bib.bib15 fT​M​D​(x,b,μ,ζ)=H​(ζzμ2)​e−ln⁡(ζzζ)​K​(b,μ)​f​(x,b,μ,ζz)​Sr12​(b,μ)+𝒪​(ΛQ​C​D2ζz,M2(Pz)2,1b2​ζz)superscript𝑓𝑇𝑀𝐷𝑥𝑏𝜇𝜁𝐻subscript𝜁𝑧superscript𝜇2superscript𝑒subscript𝜁𝑧𝜁𝐾𝑏𝜇𝑓𝑥𝑏𝜇subscript𝜁𝑧superscriptsubscript𝑆𝑟12𝑏𝜇𝒪superscriptsubscriptΛ𝑄𝐶𝐷2subscript𝜁𝑧superscript𝑀2superscriptsuperscript𝑃𝑧21superscript𝑏2subscript𝜁𝑧beginsplitfTMD(x,b,mu,zeta)=Hleft(fraczetazmu2right),e-lnleft(fraczetazzetaright)K(b,mu)tildef(x,b,mu,zetaz),Srfrac12(b,mu)+Oleft(fracLambdaQCD2zetaz,fracM2(Pz)2,frac1b2zetazright)endsplit where f​(x,b,μ,ζz)𝑓𝑥𝑏𝜇subscript𝜁𝑧tildef(x,b,mu,zetaz) is the so-called quasi-TMDPDF for a nucleon with mass M𝑀M, x𝑥x is the longitudinal momentum fraction, and b𝑏b is a separation transverse to direction of the momentum Pzsuperscript𝑃𝑧Pz carried by the nucleon. The scale μ𝜇mu defines the renormalization scale and ζz=(2​x​Pz)2subscript𝜁𝑧superscript2𝑥superscript𝑃𝑧2zetaz=(2xPz)2 is the Collins-Soper scale of the quasi-TMDPDF, with ζ𝜁zeta being the scale for the light-cone correlation. The factor H​(ζzμ2)𝐻subscript𝜁𝑧superscript𝜇2Hleft(fraczetazmu2right) is the perturbative matching kernel that connects the TMDPDFs to the quasi-TMDPDFs, and K​(b,μ)𝐾𝑏𝜇K(b,mu) is the Collins-Soper kernel. The renormalization scheme in Eq. is left unspecified; usually, it is computed in the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme. The quasi-TMDPDF on the lattice is defined ascitebib.bib44, bib.bib20 f​(x,b,μ,ζz)=∫d​z2​π​e−i​z​ζz​PzEP​BΓ​(z,b,μ,Pz),𝑓𝑥𝑏𝜇subscript𝜁𝑧𝑑𝑧2𝜋superscript𝑒𝑖𝑧subscript𝜁𝑧superscript𝑃𝑧subscript𝐸𝑃subscript𝐵Γ𝑧𝑏𝜇superscript𝑃𝑧tildef(x,b,mu,zetaz)=intfracdz2pie-izzetazfracPzEPBGamma(z,b,mu,Pz), with BΓ​(z,b,μ,Pz)subscript𝐵Γ𝑧𝑏𝜇superscript𝑃𝑧BGamma(z,b,mu,Pz) the renormalized beam function. It is obtained from the bare beam function, B0,Γ​(z,b,L,Pz;1/a)subscript𝐵0Γ𝑧𝑏𝐿superscript𝑃𝑧1𝑎B0,Gamma(z,b,L,Pz;1/a), defined as the matrix element of the non-local staple-shaped quark bilinear operator, 𝒪Γ​(z,b,L)superscript𝒪Γ𝑧𝑏𝐿OGamma(z,b,L), of length L𝐿L: B0,Γ​(z,b,L,Pz;1/a)subscript𝐵0Γ𝑧𝑏𝐿superscript𝑃𝑧1𝑎displaystyle B0,Gamma(z,b,L,Pz;1/a) =displaystyle= ⟨N​(Pz)|𝒪Γ​(z,b,L)|N​(Pz)⟩quantum-operator-product𝑁superscript𝑃𝑧superscript𝒪Γ𝑧𝑏𝐿𝑁superscript𝑃𝑧displaystyle N(Pz)|OGamma(z,b,L)|N(Pz) where N​(Pz)𝑁superscript𝑃𝑧N(Pz) is a nucleon state with momentum boost of (0,0,Pz)00superscript𝑃𝑧(0,0,Pz), a𝑎a is the lattice spacing, 𝒲​(z,b,L;1/a)𝒲𝑧𝑏𝐿1𝑎W(z,b,L;1/a) is the staple-shaped Wilson line, and ψ​(z)𝜓𝑧psi(z) is the standard up and down quark doublet. In practice, we compute the flavor non-singlet combination u−d𝑢𝑑u-d, and hence the operator also includes a Pauli τ3subscript𝜏3tau3 matrix in flavor space. For the unpolarized TMDPDF, ΓΓGamma can be either γ0subscript𝛾0gamma0 or γ3subscript𝛾3gamma3 or, in general, a combination of the two. In this work, we show results for Γ=γ0Γsubscript𝛾0Gamma=gamma0, since we observe a better signal for this operator compared to Γ=γ3Γsubscript𝛾3Gamma=gamma3. γ0subscript𝛾0gamma0 was also used for the quasi-PDF case, in which case mixing is absent, while γ3subscript𝛾3gamma3 mixed with 𝟏11. content_sections.III Operator mixing through symmetry: In order to use symmetry arguments to determine which operators mix, we first organize the staple-shaped operator using all possible ways to connect the fermion fields. We show in Fig. all the distinct possibilities of connecting a quark and an antiquark field located at a spatial separation of (0,b,z)0𝑏𝑧(0,b,z), using an asymmetric staple-shaped Wilson line. Since we build the staples only in the y​z𝑦𝑧yz-plane, in the following discussion and in Fig. , we denote the coordinate space (t,x,y,z)𝑡𝑥𝑦𝑧(t,x,y,z) as only (y,z)𝑦𝑧(y,z). Without loss of generality, we can fix the quark field (q𝑞q) at (0,0)00(0,0). Then, the only possibilities for the position of the antiquark field (q¯¯𝑞barq) are (b,z)𝑏𝑧(b,z), (b,−z)𝑏𝑧(b,-z), (−b,z)𝑏𝑧(-b,z) and (−b,−z)𝑏𝑧(-b,-z). Here, b𝑏b and z𝑧z are strictly non-negative. We define the staple-shaped Wilson line connecting q𝑞q at (0,0)00(0,0) and q¯¯𝑞barq at (b,z)𝑏𝑧(b,z) as O++​(Γ)superscript𝑂absentΓO++(Gamma). The two plus signs denote the direction of separation of q¯¯𝑞barq from q𝑞q. The first sign is for the transverse (y𝑦haty) and the second sign for the longitudinal (z𝑧hatz) axes. The ΓΓGamma denotes the insertion gamma matrix as defined in Eq. . In a similar fashion, the staple-shaped operator in the cases of q¯¯𝑞barq being located at (b,−z)𝑏𝑧(b,-z), (−b,z)𝑏𝑧(-b,z) and (−b,−z)𝑏𝑧(-b,-z) can be defined as O+−​(Γ)superscript𝑂absentΓO+-(Gamma), O−+​(Γ)superscript𝑂absentΓO-+(Gamma) and O−−​(Γ)superscript𝑂absentΓO--(Gamma) respectively. A visual representation of these operators is shown in Fig. in black. Due to the asymmetric nature of the staple-shaped Wilson line, there are four more operators that are obtained from the charge conjugation of the above defined four. These are also shown in Fig. , but in red, with the charge-conjugated version of O±±​(Γ)superscript𝑂plus-or-minusabsentplus-or-minusΓOpmpm(Gamma) denoted by Oc±±​(Γ)subscriptsuperscript𝑂plus-or-minusabsentplus-or-minus𝑐ΓOpmpmc(Gamma) and quark/antiquark fields having exchanged positions. For the symmetric case (z=0𝑧0z=0), the charge-conjugated operators are redundant, since Oc++≡O−+subscriptsuperscript𝑂absent𝑐superscript𝑂absentO++cequiv O-+, Oc+−≡O−−subscriptsuperscript𝑂absent𝑐superscript𝑂absentO+-cequiv O--, Oc−+≡O++subscriptsuperscript𝑂absent𝑐superscript𝑂absentO-+cequiv O++ and Oc−−≡O+−subscriptsuperscript𝑂absent𝑐superscript𝑂absentO--cequiv O+-. We analyze the symmetry properties using generalized parity (𝒫F​α1,2superscriptsubscript𝒫𝐹𝛼12PFalpha1,2) and time reversal (𝒯F​α1,2superscriptsubscript𝒯𝐹𝛼12TFalpha1,2) transformations with discrete flavor rotation, as well as charge conjugation (𝒞𝒞C), for the fermion fields in the twisted mass basis, χ​(xα,x)𝜒subscript𝑥𝛼xchi(xalpha,x), and with the gauge link, U​(xα,x;α)𝑈subscript𝑥𝛼x𝛼U(xalpha,x;alpha), in some direction α𝛼alpha. The symmetry transformations are defined in Appendix A. The operators O±±​(Γ)superscript𝑂plus-or-minusabsentplus-or-minusΓOpmpm(Gamma), Oc±±​(Γ)subscriptsuperscript𝑂plus-or-minusabsentplus-or-minus𝑐ΓOpmpmc(Gamma) do not have definite properties with respect to the symmetries of the lattice action. Instead, one needs to consider their linear combinations, defined by (i​j​k​l)c=i⋅O+++j⋅O−−+k⋅O+−+l⋅O−++c⋅(i⋅Oc+++j⋅Oc−−+k⋅Oc+−+l⋅Oc−+),subscript𝑖𝑗𝑘𝑙𝑐⋅𝑖superscript𝑂absent⋅𝑗superscript𝑂absent⋅𝑘superscript𝑂absent⋅𝑙superscript𝑂absent⋅𝑐⋅𝑖subscriptsuperscript𝑂absent𝑐⋅𝑗subscriptsuperscript𝑂absent𝑐⋅𝑘subscriptsuperscript𝑂absent𝑐⋅𝑙subscriptsuperscript𝑂absent𝑐(ijkl)c=icdot O+++jcdot O--+kcdot O+-+lcdot O-++ccdotleft(icdot O++c+jcdot O--c+kcdot O+-c+lcdot O-+cright), with i,j,k,l=±1𝑖𝑗𝑘𝑙plus-or-minus1i,j,k,l=pm 1 denoting the signs of O++,O−−,O+−,O−+superscript𝑂absentsuperscript𝑂absentsuperscript𝑂absentsuperscript𝑂absentO++,O--,O+-,O-+, and c=±1𝑐plus-or-minus1c=pm 1 representing the relative sign of the charge-conjugated versions of O±±superscript𝑂plus-or-minusabsentplus-or-minusOpmpm. The only combinations that have definite symmetry properties are: (++++)cdisplaystyle(++++)c ≡(−−−−)c,displaystyleequiv(----)c, (+−+−)cdisplaystyle(+-+-)c ≡(−+−+)c,displaystyleequiv(-+-+)c, (++−−)cdisplaystyle(++--)c ≡(−−++)c,displaystyleequiv(--++)c, (+−−+)cdisplaystyle(+--+)c ≡(−++−)c,displaystyleequiv(-++-)c, i. e. the combinations with all signs i,j,k,l𝑖𝑗𝑘𝑙i,j,k,l reversed are equivalent from the point of view of symmetry transformations, with irrelevant global phase. As an example, we look at the symmetry properties of γ0subscript𝛾0gamma0 and γ0​γ3subscript𝛾0subscript𝛾3gamma0gamma3 given in Tables and, respectively, restricting ourselves here to the flavor non-singlet case, u−d𝑢𝑑u-d (τ3subscript𝜏3tau3 matrix in flavor space). The combinations of operators that mix are those which have all signs equal in the nine rows of Tables and. For example, the symmetry properties of (++++)c(++++)c for Γ=γ0Γsubscript𝛾0Gamma=gamma0 (second column of Table) are identical to the ones of the combination (+−−+)c(+--+)c for Γ=γ0​γ3Γsubscript𝛾0subscript𝛾3Gamma=gamma0gamma3 (last column of Table). Thus, we conclude that the following mixings occur: (++++)cdisplaystyle(++++)c;; with(+−−+)c,displaystyletextwith;;(+--+)c, (+−+−)cdisplaystyle(+-+-)c;; with(++−−)c,displaystyletextwith;;(++--)c, (++−−)cdisplaystyle(++--)c;; with(+−+−)c,displaystyletextwith;;(+-+-)c, (+−−+)cdisplaystyle(+--+)c;; with(++++)c,displaystyletextwith;;(++++)c, where the first combination in a pair pertains to Γ=γ0Γsubscript𝛾0Gamma=gamma0 and the second one to Γ=γ0​γ3Γsubscript𝛾0subscript𝛾3Gamma=gamma0gamma3. Additional mixings appear with Γ=γ0​γ2Γsubscript𝛾0subscript𝛾2Gamma=gamma0gamma2 and Γ=γ5​γ1Γsubscript𝛾5subscript𝛾1Gamma=gamma5gamma1, thus forming a quadruple of operators that mix, γ0,γ0​γ2,γ0​γ3,γ5​γ1subscript𝛾0subscript𝛾0subscript𝛾2subscript𝛾0subscript𝛾3subscript𝛾5subscript𝛾1leftgamma0,gamma0gamma2,gamma0gamma3,gamma5gamma1right. These are the only relevant mixings for the present work, which concerns Γ=γ0Γsubscript𝛾0Gamma=gamma0 for unpolarized TMDs. For the general case, the symmetry properties for all ΓΓGamma’s are summarized in Table in Appendix A. They imply that the possible mixing is between ΓΓGamma and Γ​γ2,Γ​γ3,Γ​γ2​γ3Γsubscript𝛾2Γsubscript𝛾3Γsubscript𝛾2subscript𝛾3leftGammagamma2,Gammagamma3,Gammagamma2gamma3right in the asymmetric staples case. In turn, symmetric staples restrict the mixing by eliminating always one member of the mixing quadruple, i. e. Γ=γ0,1,5Γsubscript𝛾015Gamma=gamma0,1,5 mix with Γ​γ2Γsubscript𝛾2Gammagamma2 and Γ​γ3Γsubscript𝛾3Gammagamma3, while Γ=γ2,3Γsubscript𝛾23Gamma=gamma2,3 form a triple γ2,γ3,γ2​γ3subscript𝛾2subscript𝛾3subscript𝛾2subscript𝛾3leftgamma2,gamma3,gamma2gamma3right of operators that mix. We point out that different mixing patterns have been considered in recent studies of other groups, including mixing among all 16 operators of different Dirac structurescitebib.bib27, or at least mixing in pairs (Γ,Γ​γ3ΓΓsubscript𝛾3Gamma,Gammagamma3)citebib.bib34. In our work, we choose to consider the minimal set of staple-shaped operators (of the same dimension) that are allowed to mix by the above-mentioned C, P, and T symmetries. We have not considered mixing with higher-dimensional operators allowed by Lorentz symmetry (see, e. g. , Ref. citebib.bib45 for the straight Wilson-line case), since it is power suppressed and not relevant when one takes the continuum limit a→0→𝑎0arightarrow 0. Also, in contrast to Ref. citebib.bib34, in our analysis, the staple line has been chosen to be in a 2-dimensional (D), and not 3D, plane in Euclidean space formed by the transverse (y𝑦haty) and longitudinal (z𝑧hatz) directions. In this respect, we end up with a basis of 8 (instead of 16) operators, which are eigenstates of C, P and T transformations. We also note that calculations in one-loop lattice perturbation theorycitebib.bib33 show a smaller mixing pattern compared to the symmetries; however, this cannot guarantee a reduced mixing in higher loops.
content_sections.IV Lattice Setup: For the lattice simulation, we use an Nf=2+1+1subscript𝑁𝑓211Nf=2+1+1 clover-improved twisted mass fermion ensemble of size 243×48superscript24348243times 48, produced by the Extended Twisted Mass Collaboration (ETMC)citebib.bib46. Our study is done using Nc​o​n​fsubscript𝑁𝑐𝑜𝑛𝑓Nconf configurations with Ns​r​csubscript𝑁𝑠𝑟𝑐Nsrc source positions for each configuration. To increase statistics, the boost is taken in all three directions, and both positive and negative. For each such direction of boost, the staple is then constructed in both the remaining transverse directions. This gives us 12 measurements (666 boost directions ×times 222 transverse directions) for each source position. The details of the lattice simulation are summarized inTable. 𝒫𝒫cal P is the parity projector. In this work, we show results for a single source-sink separation of ts=10​asubscript𝑡𝑠10𝑎ts=10a and a longitudinal momentum of Pz=6​π/24​a∼1.7​GeVsuperscript𝑃𝑧6𝜋24𝑎similar-to1.7GeVPz=6pi/24asim 1.7,GeV. The 3-point function is constructed for the isovector combination u−d𝑢𝑑u-d, by inserting a τ3subscript𝜏3tau3 in flavor space. This choice ensures the elimination of the disconnected contributions and only connected diagrams need to be calculated. Momentum smearingcitebib.bib47 is applied to improve the signal for large boosts. It is observed that applying stout smearing to the gauge links used in construction of the staple also reduces the statistical errors. Here, we have applied 5 steps of stout smearing to the staple-shaped Wilson line. In Fig. we show the bare matrix elements as a function of z𝑧z at a fixed b/a=1𝑏𝑎1b/a=1 for different values of L𝐿L. These and the following results for the quasi-beam function have been symmetrized using the relation B0,Γ​(z,b,L,Pz)=B0,Γ†​(−z,−b,−L,Pz). subscript𝐵0Γ𝑧𝑏𝐿superscript𝑃𝑧superscriptsubscript𝐵0Γ†𝑧𝑏𝐿superscript𝑃𝑧B0,Gamma(z,b,L,Pz)=B0,Gammadagger(-z,-b,-L,Pz). To ensure that this property holds in our lattice simulation, we have used the staple-shaped operator O++superscript𝑂absentO++ on the left hand side of the above equation and Oc−−subscriptsuperscript𝑂absent𝑐O--c on the right hand side. Our results show a clear decay of the magnitude of the beam function as L𝐿L increases, as expectedcitebib.bib15. In Fig. we show the dependence of the bare beam function on b𝑏b. This is expected to decay as exp⁡(−L​V​(b))𝐿𝑉𝑏exp(-LV(b))citebib.bib15. Assuming a linear function for the potential V​(b)𝑉𝑏V(b), we also show a fit of exp⁡(c0+c1​b)subscript𝑐0subscript𝑐1𝑏exp(c0+c1b) to the lattice data at two different values of z/a𝑧𝑎z/a and a fixed L/a=10𝐿𝑎10L/a=10.
content_sections.V Non-perturbative renormalization: As in the case of the straight Wilson line, there are logarithmic and linear divergences associated with the length z𝑧z of the staple-shaped link. However, here there are two extra divergences: one, which is associated with the cusps of the staple, and one associated with the gluon exchange between the gauge links of length L→∞→𝐿Lrightarrowinfty, the so-called pinch-pole singularities. On the other hand, the finite transverse separation b𝑏b mitigates possible discretization effects associated with small z𝑧z separation, mainly in the non-perturbative region (large b𝑏b), which is the main interest of a lattice calculation. In the following, we will analyze three different ways to carry out the renormalization, namely the RI/MOM scheme, the short distance ratio scheme, and a modified version of the RI/MOM scheme, where the renormalization constants are computed at short distances.
content_sections.V Non-perturbative renormalization V.1 R: The RI/MOM schemecitebib.bib48 was first adapted for non-local operators employed in the quasi-distribution approach in Refs. citebib.bib35, bib.bib49. The RI/MOM renormalization constants Z𝒪R​Isubscriptsuperscript𝑍𝑅𝐼𝒪ZRIcal O are defined by the condition ZΓ​Γ′RI​(z,b,L,μ0;1/a)ZqRI​(μ0;1/a)​112​Tr​[Λ0Γ​(z,b,L,p;1/a)​Γ′ei​pz​z+i​p⟂​b]|p=μ0=1,evaluated-atsubscriptsuperscript𝑍RIΓsuperscriptΓ′𝑧𝑏𝐿subscript𝜇01𝑎subscriptsuperscript𝑍RI𝑞subscript𝜇01𝑎112Trdelimited-subscriptsuperscriptΛΓ0𝑧𝑏𝐿𝑝1𝑎superscriptΓ′superscript𝑒𝑖superscript𝑝𝑧𝑧𝑖subscript𝑝perpendicular-to𝑏𝑝subscript𝜇01fracZtextRIGammaGammaprime(z,b,L,mu0;1/a)ZtextRIq(mu0;1/a)frac112rm Trleft[fracLambdaGamma0(z,b,L,p;1/a)Gammaprimeeipzz+ipperpbright]Bigg|p=mu0=1,, where Λ0ΓsubscriptsuperscriptΛΓ0LambdaGamma0 is defined in terms of the amputated Green’s function Λ0Γ​(z,b,L,p;1/a)=Sq−1​GΓ​(z,b,L,p;1/a)​Sq−1,subscriptsuperscriptΛΓ0𝑧𝑏𝐿𝑝1𝑎superscriptsubscript𝑆𝑞1superscript𝐺Γ𝑧𝑏𝐿𝑝1𝑎superscriptsubscript𝑆𝑞1LambdaGamma0(z,b,L,p;1/a)=Sq-1GGamma(z,b,L,p;1/a)Sq-1,, with Sqsubscript𝑆𝑞Sq the off-shell quark propagator. The Green’s function is calculated as GΓ​(z,b,p,L;1/a)=⟨q​(p)|𝒪Γ​(z,b,p,L;1/a)|q​(p)⟩. superscript𝐺Γ𝑧𝑏𝑝𝐿1𝑎quantum-operator-product𝑞𝑝superscript𝒪Γ𝑧𝑏𝑝𝐿1𝑎𝑞𝑝GGamma(z,b,p,L;1/a)= q(p)|cal OGamma(z,b,p,L;1/a)|q(p),. Because GΓsuperscript𝐺ΓGGamma, and thus Λ0ΓsuperscriptsubscriptΛ0ΓLambda0Gamma, have the same divergences as B0,Γsubscript𝐵0ΓB0,Gamma, all the divergences, in principle, cancel in the renormalization procedure. ZqRIsubscriptsuperscript𝑍RI𝑞ZtextRIq is the quark wave function renormalization defined as ZqRI​(μ0;1/a)=112​Tr​[(Sq​(p;1/a))−1​SqBorn​(p)]|p2=μ02,subscriptsuperscript𝑍RI𝑞subscript𝜇01𝑎evaluated-at112Trdelimited-superscriptsubscript𝑆𝑞𝑝1𝑎1superscriptsubscript𝑆𝑞Born𝑝superscript𝑝2superscriptsubscript𝜇02ZtextRIq(mu0;1/a)=frac112rm Trleft[(Sq(p;1/a))-1,Sqrm Born(p)right]Bigg|p2=mu02,, and the corresponding renormalized beam function is then BΓRI​(z,b,μ0,Pz)=∑Γ′ZΓ​Γ′RI​(z,b,μ0;1/a)​B0,Γ′​(z,b,Pz;1/a). subscriptsuperscript𝐵RIΓ𝑧𝑏subscript𝜇0superscript𝑃𝑧subscriptsuperscriptΓ′subscriptsuperscript𝑍RIΓsuperscriptΓ′𝑧𝑏subscript𝜇01𝑎subscript𝐵0superscriptΓ′𝑧𝑏superscript𝑃𝑧1𝑎BtextRIGamma(z,b,mu0,Pz)=sumGammaprimeZtextRIGammaGammaprime(z,b,mu0;1/a)B0,Gammaprime(z,b,Pz;1/a),. Notice that the relation between the bare and renormalized beam functions involves, in principle, a 16×16161616times 16 matrix, if one considers all possible mixing among the full set of Dirac structures. The case of interest in this work is Γ=γ0Γsubscript𝛾0Gamma=gamma0, computed using the lattice ensemble of Table. As pointed out in Section , γ0superscript𝛾0gamma0 mixes with γ0​γ2subscript𝛾0subscript𝛾2gamma0gamma2, γ0​γ3subscript𝛾0subscript𝛾3gamma0gamma3, and γ5​γ1subscript𝛾5subscript𝛾1gamma5gamma1. The renormalization factors for the diagonal and off-diagonal cases are shown in Figs. and, respectively. We set the renormalization scale 4-vector μ0subscript𝜇0mu0 equal to 2​π​(6+0.5Nt,3Ns,3Ns,3Ns)2𝜋60.5subscript𝑁𝑡3subscript𝑁𝑠3subscript𝑁𝑠3subscript𝑁𝑠2pileft(frac6+0.5Nt,frac3Ns,frac3Ns,frac3Nsright), where Ns=24​asubscript𝑁𝑠24𝑎Ns=24a, Nt=2​Nssubscript𝑁𝑡2subscript𝑁𝑠Nt=2Ns, and anti-periodic boundary conditions have been employed in the time direction. We have chosen an isotropic momentum in the spatial directions and “democratic” momentum, which obeys the criterion ∑ρsin4⁡(a​pρ/2)/(∑ρsin2⁡(a​pρ/2))2|p=μ0<0.3evaluated-atsubscript𝜌superscript4𝑎subscript𝑝𝜌2superscriptsubscript𝜌superscript2𝑎subscript𝑝𝜌22𝑝subscript𝜇00.3sumrhosin4(aprho/2)/(sumrhosin2(aprho/2))2|p=mu0<0.3, in order to reduce Lorentz-non-invariant contributions in the vertex functions. Also, the selected momentum lies in a perturbative region, where perturbation theory is reliable and, at the same time, lattice artifacts are under control. A more detailed study using different values of μ0subscript𝜇0mu0 will be presented in a future extension of our study. Using this set-up, we observe that the contributions from the off-diagonal mixing terms are ≲4%less-than-or-similar-toabsentpercent4lesssim 4% of the diagonal contribution for all z𝑧z when b=1​a, 2​a𝑏1𝑎2𝑎b=1a,,2a. It is interesting to examine how the relative size of the off-diagonal to the diagonal renormalization factors changes as b𝑏b increases, as the primary focus of lattice computation of TMDPDFs is the the non-perturbative region in the transverse separation b𝑏b. We show in Fig. the renormalization factors as a function of b𝑏b for the diagonal and off-diagonal contributions. We see a contribution of ≲7−8%less-than-or-similar-toabsent7percent8lesssim 7-8% up to b=6​a𝑏6𝑎b=6a for the off-diagonal terms. Although their size shows a possible tendency of growth with increasing b𝑏b, this growth seems to be only moderate. We notice that the authors of Ref. citebib.bib27 considered the entire set of Dirac structures for the operator mixing, and observe similar results to ours, if we take from Ref. citebib.bib27 only the results from the operators that are allowed to mix with γ0subscript𝛾0gamma0, according to our symmetry arguments. For example, their result for the contribution from γ5​γ1subscript𝛾5subscript𝛾1gamma5gamma1 is much larger than that of γ0​γ2subscript𝛾0subscript𝛾2gamma0gamma2 and γ0​γ3subscript𝛾0subscript𝛾3gamma0gamma3, and there is a steady increase going to larger b𝑏b values. For the values of b𝑏b we use in this work, the magnitude of mixing for these operators found in Ref. citebib.bib27 is also comparable to our findings. However, considering the dominant diagonal renormalization factors, it seems that there are remaining divergences related to large valus of z𝑧z and b𝑏b, as also observed in Ref. citebib.bib36. Combined with the fact that the bare matrix element decays exponentially with b𝑏bcitebib.bib15, it is significantly hard to renormalize for large transverse separations using this scheme. However, in the small b𝑏b region where we can extract information using RI/MOM, it can be confirmed that the mixing is negligible. As an example, we show in Fig. the renormalized beam function for the b=1​a𝑏1𝑎b=1a case, with ++’s denoting the RI/MOM procedure using the full mixing matrix and with the ×times’s denoting the case of only taking into account the diagonal contribution. Considering that including the mixing has a negligible effect, one can safely ignore operator mixing for these values of b𝑏b.
content_sections.V Non-perturbative renormalization V.2 T: According to Figs. and, mixing with different operators can be neglected at least up to b/a≲6less-than-or-similar-to𝑏𝑎6b/alesssim 6, and we can assume that the renormalization of the staple-shaped link is multiplicative. This justifies the approach taken in Refs. citebib.bib36, bib.bib32. We first note that the vacuum expectation value of a rectangular Wilson loop ZEsubscript𝑍𝐸ZE with sides 2​L+z2𝐿𝑧2L+z and b𝑏b, ZE​(b,2​L+z;1/a)=13​Tr⁡⟨0|𝒲​(b;2​L+z)​W⟂​(x+b;b)∣0⟩,subscript𝑍𝐸𝑏2𝐿𝑧1𝑎13Trconditional0𝒲𝑏2𝐿𝑧subscript𝑊perpendicular-to𝑥𝑏𝑏0ZE(b,2L+z;1/a)=frac13operatornameTr 0|cal W(b;2L+z)Wperp(x+b;b)|0, is, by construction, a product of the staple-shaped gauge link, as defined in Eq. , and its reflection. Therefore, ZEsubscript𝑍𝐸sqrtZE has the same divergences as that of the staple-shaped gauge link. Thus, it should cancel the pinch-pole singularity associated with the length L𝐿L of the staple, as well as the divergences associated with the cusps. Furthermore, as the sides of ZEsubscript𝑍𝐸ZE are also dependent on the longitudinal displacement z𝑧z, the exponential divergence associated with z𝑧z present in the staple-shaped link must also be cancelled if an appropriated ratio is taken, namely the one proposed in Refs. citebib.bib21, bib.bib36: BΓ​(z,b,Pz;1/a)=limL→∞B0,Γ​(z,b,L,Pz;1/a)ZE​(b,2​L+z;1/a)subscript𝐵Γ𝑧𝑏superscript𝑃𝑧1𝑎subscript→𝐿subscript𝐵0Γ𝑧𝑏𝐿superscript𝑃𝑧1𝑎subscript𝑍𝐸𝑏2𝐿𝑧1𝑎BGamma(z,b,Pz;1/a)=limLrightarrowinftyfracB0,Gamma(z,b,L,Pz;1/a)sqrtZE(b,2L+z;1/a) To illustrate this point, we show in Fig. the beam function as a function of L𝐿L, for a fixed z=2​a𝑧2𝑎z=2a and two values of b𝑏b, before and after dividing by ZEsubscript𝑍𝐸sqrtZE. The cancellation of the divergences related to the length of the Wilson line is explicitly shown. This ratio, thus, takes care of the divergence associated with the length L𝐿L and width b𝑏b of the staple-shaped operator. After dividing the beam function by the square root of ZEsubscript𝑍𝐸ZE, the only remaining divergences are the UV divergences associated with the quark field and its end-points connecting to the gauge links. As such, they can be cancelled by taking ratios, as they have a multiplicative naturecitebib.bib26, bib.bib50. Hence, one can define BΓS​D​R​(z,z0,b,b0,Pz)=ZS​D​R​(z0,b0;1/a)​BΓ​(z,b,Pz;1/a),subscriptsuperscript𝐵𝑆𝐷𝑅Γ𝑧subscript𝑧0𝑏subscript𝑏0superscript𝑃𝑧superscript𝑍𝑆𝐷𝑅subscript𝑧0subscript𝑏01𝑎subscript𝐵Γ𝑧𝑏superscript𝑃𝑧1𝑎BSDRGamma(z,z0,b,b0,Pz)=ZSDR(z0,b0;1/a),BGamma(z,b,Pz;1/a),, where, ZS​D​R​(z0,b0;1/a)=1BΓ​(z=z0,b=b0,Pz=0;1/a). superscript𝑍𝑆𝐷𝑅subscript𝑧0subscript𝑏01𝑎1subscript𝐵Γformulae-sequence𝑧subscript𝑧0formulae-sequence𝑏subscript𝑏0superscript𝑃𝑧01𝑎ZSDR(z0,b0;1/a)=frac1BGamma(z=z0,b=b0,Pz=0;1/a),. Because the remaining divergences are independent of the length of the Wilson line, one is free to choose z0subscript𝑧0z0 and b0subscript𝑏0b0. In order to connect these quantities to the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme via a perturbative scheme conversion, z0subscript𝑧0z0 and b0subscript𝑏0b0 should be small enough for perturbation theory to be valid. However, the use of small values for both z0subscript𝑧0z0 and b0subscript𝑏0b0 can introduce sizable discretization errors in the renormalization factors, which can affect the validity of the SDR scheme. To address this issue, different approaches can be employed in order to reduce finite lattice-spacing errors from the non-perturbative data for all values of the staple lengths b/a𝑏𝑎b/a and z/a𝑧𝑎z/a. Ideally, the elimination of discretization errors requires calculations of physical matrix elements at different finite values of the lattice spacing a𝑎a and an extrapolation a→0→𝑎0arightarrow 0. When data for multiple values of a𝑎a are not available, a number of different approaches can be employed in order to reduce discretization errors at each lattice spacing. A standard method is to apply an improved discretization, in both the action and the operators under study, using the Symanzik-improvement programcitebib.bib51, bib.bib52. Another way to reduce this kind of systematic error from a lattice calculation is to subtract one-loop artifacts employing lattice perturbation theory to all orders in a𝑎a, from the non-perturbative vertex functions calculated in lattice simulations. Our group has successfully applied this method to the renormalization of local quark bilinear operatorscitebib.bib53, bib.bib54, bib.bib55, and more recently to the renormalization of non-local straight Wilson-line operators for quasi-PDFscitebib.bib56. These studies have provided a useful feedback on the effectiveness of artifacts in the renormalization factors for different ranges of the scales entering the renormalization procedure. Since this is our first non-perturbative study considering non-local staple-shaped operators we do not consider finite-a𝑎a errors, but we intend to apply the method of subtracting one-loop artifacts in a future extension of our study. The renormalized matrix elements are converted to the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme using perturbation theory. We have computed the vertex, sail, and tadpole one-loop diagrams for external quark states with a general momentum pzsuperscript𝑝𝑧pz. For pz→0→superscript𝑝𝑧0pzrightarrow 0, we obtain ZMS¯,S​D​R​(z0,b0,μ0)=1+αs​CF2​π​(12+32​ln⁡(b02+z024​e−2​γE​μ02)−2​z0b0​arctan​z0b0),superscript𝑍¯MS𝑆𝐷𝑅subscript𝑧0subscript𝑏0subscript𝜇01subscript𝛼𝑠subscript𝐶𝐹2𝜋1232superscriptsubscript𝑏02superscriptsubscript𝑧024superscript𝑒2subscript𝛾𝐸superscriptsubscript𝜇022subscript𝑧0subscript𝑏0arctansubscript𝑧0subscript𝑏0ZoverlinetextMS,SDR(z0,b0,mu0)=1+fracalphasCF2pileft(frac12+frac32lnleft(fracb02+z024e-2gammaEmu02right)-2fracz0b0textrmarctanfracz0b0right),, which agrees with Eq. of Ref. citebib.bib36. Note that this factor equals BΓMS¯​(z=z0,b=b0,Pz=0,μ0)superscriptsubscript𝐵Γ¯MSformulae-sequence𝑧subscript𝑧0formulae-sequence𝑏subscript𝑏0superscript𝑃𝑧0subscript𝜇0BGammaoverlinetextMS(z=z0,b=b0,Pz=0,mu0). Details of this calculation for a general external momentum will be presented in Ref. citebib.bib57. The renormalized beam function in the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme is then given by BΓMS¯​(z,b,Pz)=ZMS¯​(z0,b0,μ0)​BΓS​D​R​(z,z0,b,b0,Pz). subscriptsuperscript𝐵¯MSΓ𝑧𝑏superscript𝑃𝑧superscript𝑍¯MSsubscript𝑧0subscript𝑏0subscript𝜇0subscriptsuperscript𝐵𝑆𝐷𝑅Γ𝑧subscript𝑧0𝑏subscript𝑏0superscript𝑃𝑧BoverlinetextMSGamma(z,b,Pz)=ZoverlinetextMS(z0,b0,mu0)BSDRGamma(z,z0,b,b0,Pz). Results for BMS¯​(z,b,Pz)superscript𝐵¯MS𝑧𝑏superscript𝑃𝑧BoverlinetextMS(z,b,Pz) are presented in Section, where we also discuss the cancellation of the z0,b0subscript𝑧0subscript𝑏0z0,b0 dependence in Eq. .
content_sections.V Non-perturbative renormalization V.3 S: As discussed in Section, the usual RI scheme may be problematic at large z𝑧z and b𝑏b as the magnitude of the ZRIsuperscript𝑍RIZtextRI factors grows exponentially. Also, as shown in Refs. citebib.bib37, bib.bib36, the usual RI scheme may still contain a residual linear divergence, which may not be properly canceled. On the other hand, as discussed in Section , the ZEsubscript𝑍𝐸sqrtZE factor cancels all divergences in z𝑧z and b𝑏b present in the staple. Hence, we can define a vertex function that is free of such divergences, ΛΓ​(z,b,p;1/a)=Λ0Γ​(z,b,p;1/a)ZE​(b,2​L+z;1/a). superscriptΛΓ𝑧𝑏𝑝1𝑎subscriptsuperscriptΛΓ0𝑧𝑏𝑝1𝑎subscript𝑍𝐸𝑏2𝐿𝑧1𝑎LambdaGamma(z,b,p;1/a)=fracLambdaGamma0(z,b,p;1/a)sqrtZE(b,2L+z;1/a). Because the divergences related to the lengths of the Wilson line have been removed, we can compute the renormalization factors as in Section at some fixed z0,b0subscript𝑧0subscript𝑏0z0,b0citebib.bib34 ZΓ​Γ′RI−short​(z0,b0,μ0;1/a)ZqRI​(μ0;1/a)​112​Tr​[ΛΓ​(z,b,p;1/a)​Γ′ei​pz​z+i​b​p⟂]|p=μ0,z=z0,b=b0=1. evaluated-atsubscriptsuperscript𝑍RIshortΓsuperscriptΓ′subscript𝑧0subscript𝑏0subscript𝜇01𝑎subscriptsuperscript𝑍RI𝑞subscript𝜇01𝑎112Trdelimited-superscriptΛΓ𝑧𝑏𝑝1𝑎superscriptΓ′superscript𝑒𝑖superscript𝑝𝑧𝑧𝑖𝑏subscript𝑝perpendicular-toformulae-sequence𝑝subscript𝜇0formulae-sequence𝑧subscript𝑧0𝑏subscript𝑏01displaystylefracZtextRI-textshortGammaGammaprime(z0,b0,mu0;1/a)ZtextRIq(mu0;1/a)frac112rm Trleft[fracLambdaGamma(z,b,p;1/a)Gammaprimeeipzz+ibpperpright]Bigg|p=mu0,z=z0,b=b0=1,. The ZRI−shortsuperscript𝑍RIshortZtextRI-textshort factor defined at fixed z0subscript𝑧0z0 and b0subscript𝑏0b0 is used to renormalize the bare Bγ0subscript𝐵subscript𝛾0Bgamma0 defined at arbitrary values of z𝑧z and b𝑏b. We have labeled this procedure as RI-short. In principle, the vertex function in the standard RI/MOM could also be modified by taking its ratio with ZEsubscript𝑍𝐸sqrtZE. This would reduce the growth of the Z𝑍Z-factors with increasing b𝑏b. However, when combining ZRIsuperscript𝑍RIZtextRI with the bare Bγ0subscript𝐵subscript𝛾0Bgamma0, the ZEsubscript𝑍𝐸sqrtZE factors cancel each other, and therefore ZEsubscript𝑍𝐸sqrtZE has no effect on the renormalized matrix elements. On the other hand, the ZEsubscript𝑍𝐸sqrtZE factor appearing in the vertex function of the RI-short scheme is defined at fixed values of z𝑧z and b𝑏b, contrary to the ZEsubscript𝑍𝐸sqrtZE factor appearing in the bare Bγ0subscript𝐵subscript𝛾0Bgamma0. Hence the cancellation of the ZEsubscript𝑍𝐸ZE factors do not happen in the RI-short scheme. As in Section, we choose the pair z0,b0subscript𝑧0subscript𝑏0z0,b0 to be in the perturbative region in order to make the perturbative conversion to the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme reliable. Moreover, the study of possible lattice artifacts associated with the use of small values of z0/asubscript𝑧0𝑎z0/a and b0/asubscript𝑏0𝑎b0/a will be considered in future extensions of our study by using one-loop lattice perturbation theory. The corresponding renormalized beam function in this scheme is then given by BΓRI−short​(z,z0,b,b0,μ0,Pz)=∑Γ′[Z𝒪RI−short​(z0,b0,μ0;1/a)]Γ​Γ′​BΓ′​(z,b,Pz;1/a). subscriptsuperscript𝐵RIshortΓ𝑧subscript𝑧0𝑏subscript𝑏0subscript𝜇0superscript𝑃𝑧subscriptsuperscriptΓ′subscriptdelimited-subscriptsuperscript𝑍RIshort𝒪subscript𝑧0subscript𝑏0subscript𝜇01𝑎ΓsuperscriptΓ′subscript𝐵superscriptΓ′𝑧𝑏superscript𝑃𝑧1𝑎BtextRI-textshortGamma(z,z0,b,b0,mu0,Pz)=sumGammaprimeleft[ZtextRI-textshortcal O(z0,b0,mu0;1/a)right]GammaGammaprimeBGammaprime(z,b,Pz;1/a). For illustration, we show in Fig the renormalization factors Z𝒪RI−short​(z0,b0,μ0;1/a)subscriptsuperscript𝑍RIshort𝒪subscript𝑧0subscript𝑏0subscript𝜇01𝑎ZtextRI-textshortcal O(z0,b0,mu0;1/a) for z0=0, 1​asubscript𝑧001𝑎z0=0,,1a, b0=1​asubscript𝑏01𝑎b0=1a and μ0=2​πa​(6+0.548,324,324,324)subscript𝜇02𝜋𝑎60.548324324324mu0=frac2pialeft(frac6+0.548,frac324,frac324,frac324right). We also show the off-diagonal renormalization factors, which, in principle, mix with γ0superscript𝛾0gamma0. As expected, they are also independent of L𝐿L and can be omitted in the full calculation as their contribution is negligible as compared to the diagonal contribution. Finally, we convert BΓRI−shortsubscriptsuperscript𝐵RIshortΓBtextRI-textshortGamma to the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme using one-loop perturbation theory, BΓMS¯​(z,b,μ0,Pz)=∑Γ′[Z𝒪MS¯,RI−short​(z0,b0,μ0)]Γ​Γ′​BΓ′RI−short​(z,z0,b,b0,μ0,Pz). subscriptsuperscript𝐵¯MSΓ𝑧𝑏subscript𝜇0superscript𝑃𝑧subscriptsuperscriptΓ′subscriptdelimited-subscriptsuperscript𝑍¯MSRIshort𝒪subscript𝑧0subscript𝑏0subscript𝜇0ΓsuperscriptΓ′subscriptsuperscript𝐵RIshortsuperscriptΓ′𝑧subscript𝑧0𝑏subscript𝑏0subscript𝜇0superscript𝑃𝑧BoverlinetextMSGamma(z,b,mu0,Pz)=sumGammaprimeleft[ZoverlinetextMS,textRI-textshortcal O(z0,b0,mu0)right]GammaGammaprimeBtextRI-textshortGammaprime(z,z0,b,b0,mu0,Pz),. The conversion matrix ZOMS¯,RI−shortsuperscriptsubscript𝑍𝑂¯MSRIshortZOoverlinetextMS,textRI-textshort is calculated in dimensional regularization for arbitrary values of the momentum scale μ0subscript𝜇0mu0. Explicit expressions for all Γ,Γ′ΓsuperscriptΓ′Gamma,Gammaprime are given in Ref. citebib.bib57. Similar perturbative studies can be found in Refs. citebib.bib33, bib.bib26. A comparison between the conversion factor in RI-short and the SDR scheme is shown in Fig. for the case Γ=Γ′=γ0ΓsuperscriptΓ′subscript𝛾0Gamma=Gammaprime=gamma0. We observe that the real parts are compatible between the two schemes, while the RI-short scheme shows a non-zero imaginary part, in contrast to the SDR scheme. This is a consequence of using a non-zero momentum scale μ0subscript𝜇0mu0 in the RI-short scheme, while SDR is defined at zero momentum. Note that specific choices of μ0subscript𝜇0mu0 in RI-short can lead to a vanishing imaginary part in the renormalization factors. In particular, by setting to zero the two momentum components parallel to the staple segments, the one-loop expression for the renormalization factors results into a vanishing imaginary part. However, such choice of momentum gives rise to unwanted Lorentz non-invariant contributions in the non-perturbative calculations. Thus, in our study we follow the common practice of employing democratic momentum with reduced Lorentz non-invariant contributions at the cost of introducing imaginary part in the renormalization factors.
content_sections.VI Results: In this Section, we present the renormalized beam functions in the 3 renormalization schemes discussed in Section, as well as the corresponding results in the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme. In Fig. , we show the real and imaginary parts of Bγ0​(z,b,μ0,Pz)subscript𝐵subscript𝛾0𝑧𝑏subscript𝜇0superscript𝑃𝑧Bgamma0(z,b,mu0,Pz) as a function of z𝑧z for two values of the transverse separation, b/a=1,2𝑏𝑎12b/a=1,2. For the real parts, the schemes agree within errors for |z/a|⪅6𝑧𝑎6|z/a|lessapprox 6. For |z/a|⪆6greater-than-or-approximately-equals𝑧𝑎6|z/a|gtrapprox 6, Bγ0S​D​Rsubscriptsuperscript𝐵𝑆𝐷𝑅subscript𝛾0BSDRgamma0 and Bγ0RI−shortsubscriptsuperscript𝐵RIshortsubscript𝛾0BtextRI-textshortgamma0 are consistent, while Bγ0RIsubscriptsuperscript𝐵RIsubscript𝛾0BtextRIgamma0 becomes increasingly more negative with increasingly larger errors. This discrepant behaviour between the RI-MOM and the RI-short and SDR schemes indicates the existence of a residual linear divergence at large |z|𝑧|z| in the standard RI/MOM, as observed incitebib.bib36. For the imaginary parts, we observe a similar behavior. Namely, the SDR and the RI-short schemes are almost identical for any |z/a|𝑧𝑎|z/a| for the two values of b/a𝑏𝑎b/a considered. For the usual RI/MOM, we observe deviations not only for |z/a|⪆6greater-than-or-approximately-equals𝑧𝑎6|z/a|gtrapprox 6 but also for most of the |z/a|𝑧𝑎|z/a| region with an increase in errors with increasing |z/a|𝑧𝑎|z/a|. The conversion to the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme is done using one-loop perturbation theory for all three schemes considered. For the two cases where the renormalization factors are fixed at a short perturbative scale, we use z0/a=b0/a=1subscript𝑧0𝑎subscript𝑏0𝑎1z0/a=b0/a=1 when computing BΓ′RI−short​(z,z0,b,b0,μ0,Pz)subscriptsuperscript𝐵RIshortsuperscriptΓ′𝑧subscript𝑧0𝑏subscript𝑏0subscript𝜇0superscript𝑃𝑧BtextRI-textshortGammaprime(z,z0,b,b0,mu0,Pz) and BΓS​D​R​(z,z0,b,b0,Pz)subscriptsuperscript𝐵𝑆𝐷𝑅Γ𝑧subscript𝑧0𝑏subscript𝑏0superscript𝑃𝑧BSDRGamma(z,z0,b,b0,Pz). Since the final results in the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme should be independent of the values we choose for the z0,b0subscript𝑧0subscript𝑏0z0,b0 pair, we examine the stability of our results on the choice of the values for this pair. To this end, we show in Fig. the product of the renormalization factors for each scheme with the corresponding conversion factors to the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme, Zγ0MS¯,S​D​R​Zγ0S​D​Rsubscriptsuperscript𝑍¯MS𝑆𝐷𝑅subscript𝛾0subscriptsuperscript𝑍𝑆𝐷𝑅subscript𝛾0ZoverlinetextMS,SDRgamma0ZSDRgamma0 and Zγ0MS¯,RI​Zγ0RI−shortsubscriptsuperscript𝑍¯MSRIsubscript𝛾0subscriptsuperscript𝑍RIshortsubscript𝛾0ZoverlinetextMS,textRIgamma0ZtextRI-textshortgamma0. These products should be independent on the choice of values of z0,b0subscript𝑧0subscript𝑏0z0,b0 as long as the one-loop perturbative calculation is a reliable approximation. We notice that these products are equal to the renormalization factors in the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme in the absence of mixing. For the ratio scheme, there is a 10%−20%percent10percent2010%-20% correction when going from b0/a=1subscript𝑏0𝑎1b0/a=1 to b0/a=2subscript𝑏0𝑎2b0/a=2 for the three values of z0subscript𝑧0z0 considered here, with the correction increasing for larger values of z0subscript𝑧0z0. This indicates a limitation on the use of one-loop perturbation theory to perform the conversion to the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme already at a transverse separation as low as b/a=2𝑏𝑎2b/a=2. As a consequence, 𝒪​(αs2)𝒪superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠2O(alphas2) corrections cannot be disregarded. For the z0subscript𝑧0z0 dependence, the choice of z0/a=0subscript𝑧0𝑎0z0/a=0 and z0/a=1subscript𝑧0𝑎1z0/a=1 is nearly equivalent within one sigma error. For z/a=2𝑧𝑎2z/a=2, the discrepancy grows, especially for b/a=2𝑏𝑎2b/a=2. For the RI-short scheme, the situation is not significantly changed. From this discussion, we conclude that our choice for the pair z0,b0subscript𝑧0subscript𝑏0z0,b0 is a likely safe region to fix the short perturbative scale. We show in Fig. the renormalized MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS matrix elements computed in the ratio and the RI-short schemes at Pz≃1.7similar-to-or-equalssuperscript𝑃𝑧1.7Pzsimeq 1.7 GeV. The imaginary part of the matrix elements are in full agreement, the same happening in the the real part, for large z𝑧z values. In the small z𝑧z region of the real part there is a tendency to discrepancy, as already observed in the intermediate schemes shown in Fig. , although they agree within one sigma error.
content_sections.VII Conclusion: In this work, we have addressed the non-perturbative renormalization of the asymmetric staple-shaped quark bilinear operators. We used symmetry arguments to restrict the possible mixing between the operators, and we found that mixing is allowed within sets of four operators. We then employed the RI/MOM scheme to estimate the importance of mixing coming from the non-diagonal terms, and we found that they can be neglected up to transverse separations of at least 6​a6𝑎6a. This result justifies the use of multiplicative renormalization for the asymmetric staple-shaped operator. Based on this conclusion, we computed the renormalization factors in the SDR scheme, and in the RI-short scheme, where the renormalization factors are computed at short distances. We found that both schemes are nearly equivalent over all the |z|𝑧|z| region considered. Finally, we converted both schemes to the MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS scheme and presented the corresponding results for the renormalized beam function. Given our previous computation of the Collins-Soper kernel and of the soft functioncitebib.bib24, the next natural step will be to compute the TMDPDFs themselves over a wide range of b𝑏b, and we plan to present results for them in the near future. all_papers.title: Transverse Momentum Distributions from Lattice QCD without Wilson Lines
all_papers.abstract:
The transverse-momentum-dependent distributions (TMDs), which are defined by
gauge-invariant 3D parton correlators with staple-shaped lightlike Wilson
lines, can be calculated from quark and gluon correlators fixed in the Coulomb
gauge on a Euclidean lattice. These quantities can be expressed
gauge-invariantly as the correlators of Coulomb-gauge-dressed fields, which
reduce to the standard TMD correlators under principal-value prescription in
the infinite boost limit. In the framework of Large-Momentum Effective Theory,
a quasi-TMD defined from such correlators in a large-momentum hadron state can
be matched to the TMD via a factorization formula, whose exact form is derived
using Soft Collinear Effective Theory and verified at one-loop order. Compared
to the currently used gauge-invariant correlators, this new method can
substantially improve statistical precision and simplify renormalization for
the time-reversal-even TMDs, which will greatly enhance the predicative power
of lattice QCD in the non-perturbative region.

qualitative: The transverse-momentum-dependent distributions (TMDs) can be computed on a Euclidean lattice using gauge-invariant correlators defined in the Coulomb gauge, avoiding the need for explicit Wilson lines.

The Coulomb-gauge (CG) quasi-TMD is defined via correlators of Coulomb-gauge-dressed quark fields, which are gauge-invariant and reduce to standard TMDs in the infinite boost limit under a principal-value prescription.

The gauge-invariant dressing of the quark field, ΨC(b) = UC(b)ψ(b), is constructed via a gauge transformation that rotates the gauge field into the Coulomb gauge, with UC(b) determined order-by-order in the strong coupling g.
In the Coulomb gauge (∇→⋅A→=0), the dressing factors ωm vanish for all m, simplifying the correlator to the standard CG form without non-local Wilson-line contributions.

Under an infinite Lorentz boost along the z-direction, the dressing factor UC(b) reduces to a path-ordered lightlike Wilson line 𝒲±n†(b), leading to the standard TMD correlator involving a lightlike Wilson line.

The orientation of the lightlike Wilson line (determined by boundary conditions at bz=±∞) affects time-reversal-odd TMDs (e.g., Sivers function), but not time-reversal-even TMDs or the factorization formula for quasi-TMDs.
For TMDs with time-reversal odd components, an additional transverse gauge link 𝒲⟂ is required at infinity to ensure full gauge invariance, though it is not essential for the current framework.

The renormalization of the CG quasi-TMD involves only the quark wave function renormalization, which is multiplicative, simplifying the renormalization procedure compared to standard gauge-invariant correlators.
This method improves statistical precision and simplifies renormalization for time-reversal-even TMDs, enhancing the predictive power of lattice QCD in the non-perturbative regime.
quantitative: CG quasi-TMD definition: B(x,b⟂,μ,Pz) = Pz ∫_{-∞}^{∞} dbz/(2π) e^{i x Pz bz} × (1/(2 Pt)) ⟨P| ψ¯(b) γt ψ |∇→⋅A→=0 |P⟩
Coulomb gauge condition: ∇→⋅A→ = 0
Gauge transformation for quark field: ΨC(b) = UC(b) ψ(b)
Gauge transformation for gauge field: A→C = UC A→ UC⁻¹ + (i/g) UC ∇→ UC⁻¹, with ∇→⋅A→C = 0
Dressing factor expansion: UC = 1 + ∑_{m=1}^∞ (-i g)^m / m! ω^m
First-order solution: ω₁ = 1/∇² (∇→⋅A→)
Second-order solution: ω₂ = 2! / ∇² [∇→⋅(ω₁† ∇→ ω₁) + [∇→ ω₁, A→]]

论文完全匹配查询核心要求。研究聚焦非局域格点关联函数（非局域格点 correlators）与威尔逊线重整化（Wilson-line renormalization）问题，提出在库仑规范下通过规范不变的胶子场关联函数实现TMD计算，避免了传统威尔逊线，直接回应“威尔逊线重整化”需求。方法基于大动量有效理论与软胶子有效理论，明确处理了非局域关联函数的规范不变性与重整化问题。

约束项检查：  
- C1：研究非局域格点关联函数 — 满足。证据：“gauge-invariant 3D parton correlators with staple-shaped lightlike Wilson lines” 对比 “非局域 lattice correlators”  
- C2：涉及威尔逊线重整化 — 满足。证据：“without Wilson Lines” 与 “Wilson-line renormalization” 直接对应，提出替代方案  

不相关性 / 风险点 / 缺口：  
- 无。论文核心即为解决威尔逊线相关问题，且方法直接针对非局域关联函数。

Note that if we do not insert the transverse gauge link as suggested in Eq. , then the quasi-TMD wave function is under the principle-value prescriptioncitebib.bib66 and must be as real as C𝐶C, which provides another simplification in lattice calculationscitebib.bib36, bib.bib38, bib.bib39.
In summary, we have proposed a new method for calculating the TMDs from CG correlators without Wilson lines. Through a gauge-invariant extension of the CG correlators, we demonstrate explicitly that they reduce to the standard TMD correlators in the infinite boost limit. Moreover, using SCET we derived the factorization formula for the CG quasi-TMDs, as well as the definition of the corresponding soft function. The latter can be extracted from a meson form factor and the CG quasi-TMD wave function, which completes our framework for calculating the (x,k⟂)𝑥subscript𝑘perpendicular-to(x,kperp) dependence of TMDs from lattice QCD. With the absence of Wilson lines, we expect this method to significantly improve the statistical precision and simplify the lattice renormalization in practical calculations. In this work, we have focused on the 𝒯𝒯cal T-even TMDs which are independent of the Wilson line orientation. We plan to further study the boundary conditions in the CG and the proposed definition of 𝒯𝒯cal T-odd quasi-TMDs in Eq. in a followup work. Our method is also applicable to the gluon TMDs and Wigner distributions, which will be investigated in the future. Acknowledgements. all_papers.title: Double Parton Distributions from Euclidean Lattice
all_papers.abstract:
We show that double parton distributions, which are important in describing
double parton scattering processes in hadron collisions, can be directly
computed from correlations of equal-time nonlocal Euclidean operators on the
lattice in the large hadron momentum limit. We demonstrate this by taking the
unpolarized color singlet quark double parton distribution as an example, and
present a factorization formula connecting the corresponding lightcone and
Euclidean correlations. This opens a new possibility of studying multiparton
interactions on the Euclidean lattice.
all_papers.reason:
论文涉及非局域格点关联（nonlocal lattice correlators）与威尔逊线重整化（Wilson-line renormalization）的核心主题，明确讨论欧几里得格点上双部分子分布的计算，且提出连接轻锥与欧几里得关联的因子化公式，契合查询核心。  

约束项检查：  
- C1：非局域格点关联 — 满足。证据：“equal-time nonlocal Euclidean operators on the lattice” 对比 “nonlocal lattice correlators”  
- C2：威尔逊线重整化 — 满足。证据：“factorization formula connecting lightcone and Euclidean correlations” 暗含威尔逊线结构，且格点计算需重整化处理  

不相关性 / 风险点 / 缺口：  
- 未明确提及“威尔逊线重整化”术语，但隐含在非局域算符与因子化中，属合理推断  
- 未直接讨论重整化群演化或具体重整化方案，但非核心缺失
authors: Jian-Hui Zhang

all_papers.title: Dynamics of a lattice 2-group gauge theory model
all_papers.abstract:
We study a simple lattice model with local symmetry, whose construction is
based on a crossed module of finite groups. Its dynamical degrees of freedom
are associated both to links and faces of a four-dimensional lattice. In
special limits the discussed model reduces to certain known topological quantum
field theories. In this work we focus on its dynamics, which we study both
analytically and using Monte Carlo simulations. We prove a factorization
theorem which reduces computation of correlation functions of local observables
to known, simpler models. This, combined with standard Krammers-Wannier type
dualities, allows us to propose a detailed phase diagram, which form is then
confirmed in numerical simulations. We describe also topological charges
present in the model, its symmetries and symmetry breaking patterns. The
corresponding order parameters are the Polyakov loop and its generalization,
which we call a Polyakov surface. The latter is particularly interesting, as it
is beyond the scope of the factorization theorem. As shown by the numerical
results, expectation value of Polyakov surface may serve to detects all phase
transitions and is sensitive to a value of the topological charge.

sections.13: Savit, Topological excitations in U(1) invariant theories, Phys
sections.22: Creutz, Monte Carlo study of quantized S​U​(2)𝑆𝑈2SU(2) gauge theory, Phys

all_papers.reason:
论文研究了四维格点上基于有限群交叉模的2-群规范理论，涉及非局域关联（如Polyakov面）与对偶性，与查询中“非局域格点关联”高度契合。其对Polyakov面的分析及数值模拟支持其作为相变探测器，符合“威尔逊线重整化”中对非局域算符行为的关注。但未明确讨论“重整化群”或“重整化过程”本身，仅间接涉及。  

约束项检查：  
- C1：非局域格点关联 — 满足。证据：“Polyakov surface”、“correlation functions of local observables” 对比 “非局域 lattice correlators”  
- C2：威尔逊线重整化 — 部分。证据：提及Polyakov环与面，但未讨论重整化群变换或重整化流程。  

不相关性 / 风险点 / 缺口：  
- 未明确处理“重整化”过程，仅分析可观测量期望值。  
- 未涉及重整化群方法或尺度变换机制。
authors: Arkadiusz Bochniak, Leszek Hadasz, Piotr Korcyl, B{\l}a\.zej Ruba

all_papers.title: Gauging C on the Lattice
all_papers.abstract:
We discuss general aspects of charge conjugation symmetry in Euclidean
lattice field theories, including its dynamical gauging. Our main focus is
$O(2) = U(1)\rtimes \mathbb{Z}_2 $ gauge theory, which we construct using a
non-abelian generalization of the Villain formulation via gauging the charge
conjugation symmetry of pure $U(1)$ gauge theory. We describe how to construct
gauge-invariant non-local operators in a theory with gauged charge conjugation
symmetry, and use it to define Wilson and 't Hooft lines as well as
non-invertible symmetry operators. Our lattice discretization preserves the
higher-group and non-invertible symmetries of $O(2)$ gauge theory, which we
explore in detail. In particular, these symmetries give rise to selection rules
for extended operators and their junctions, and constrain the properties of the
worldvolume degrees of freedom on twist vortices (also known as Alice or
Cheshire strings). We propose a phase diagram of the theory coupled to
dynamical magnetic monopoles and twist vortices, where the various generalized
symmetries are typically only emergent.

all_papers.title: Phases of Wilson Lines: Conformality and Screening
all_papers.abstract:
We study the rich dynamics resulting from introducing static charged
particles (Wilson lines) in 2+1 and 3+1 dimensional gauge theories. Depending
on the charges of the external particles, there may be multiple defect fixed
points with interesting renormalization group flows connecting them, or an
exponentially large screening cloud can develop (defining a new emergent length
scale), screening the bare charge entirely or partially. We investigate several
examples where the dynamics can be solved in various weak coupling or double
scaling limits. Sometimes even the elementary Wilson lines, corresponding to
the lowest nontrivial charge, are screened. We consider Wilson lines in 3+1
dimensional gauge theories including massless scalar and fermionic QED$_4$, and
also in the ${\mathcal N}=4$ supersymmetric Yang-Mills theory. We also consider
Wilson lines in 2+1 dimensional conformal gauge theories such as QED$_3$ with
bosons or fermions, Chern-Simons-Matter theories, and the effective theory of
graphene. Our results in 2+1 dimensions have potential implications for
graphene, second-order superconducting phase transitions, etc. Finally, we
comment on magnetic line operators in 3+1 dimensions ('t Hooft lines) and argue
that our results for the infrared dynamics of electric and magnetic lines are
consistent with non-Abelian electric-magnetic duality.

all_papers.title:
Nonperturbative Collins-Soper Kernel from Chiral Quarks with Physical
  Masses
all_papers.abstract:
We present a lattice QCD calculation of the rapidity anomalous dimension of
quark transverse-momentum-dependent distributions, i.e., the Collins-Soper (CS)
kernel, up to transverse separations of about 1 fm. This unitary lattice
calculation is conducted, for the first time, employing the
chiral-symmetry-preserving domain wall fermion discretization and physical
values of light and strange quark masses. The CS kernel is extracted from the
ratios of pion quasi-transverse-momentum-dependent wave functions
(quasi-TMDWFs) at next-to-leading logarithmic perturbative accuracy. Also for
the first time, we utilize the recently proposed Coulomb-gauge-fixed
quasi-TMDWF correlator without a Wilson line. We observe significantly slower
signal decay with increasing quark separations compared to the established
gauge-invariant method with a staple-shaped Wilson line. This enables us to
determine the CS kernel at large nonperturbative transverse separations and
find its near-linear dependence on the latter. Our result is consistent with
the recent lattice calculation using gauge-invariant quasi-TMDWFs, and agrees
with various recent phenomenological parametrizations of experimental data.

all_papers.title: Factorization connecting continuum and lattice TMDs
all_papers.abstract:
Transverse-momentum-dependent parton distribution functions (TMDs) can be
studied from first principles by a perturbative matching onto
lattice-calculable quantities: so-called lattice TMDs, which are a class of
equal-time correlators that includes quasi-TMDs and TMDs in the
Lorentz-invariant approach. We introduce a general correlator that includes as
special cases these two Lattice TMDs and continuum TMDs, like the Collins
scheme. Then, to facilitate the derivation of a factorization relation between
lattice and continuum TMDs, we construct a new scheme, the Large Rapidity (LR)
scheme, intermediate between the Collins and quasi-TMDs. The LR and Collins
schemes differ only by an order of limits, and can be matched onto one another
by a multiplicative kernel. We show that this same matching also holds between
quasi and Collins TMDs, which enables us to prove a factorization relation
between these quantities to all orders in $\alpha_s$. Our results imply that
there is no mixing between various quark flavors or gluons when matching
Collins and quasi TMDs, making the lattice calculation of individual flavors
and gluon TMDs easier than anticipated. We cross-check these results explicitly
at one loop and discuss implications for other physical-to-lattice scheme
factorizations.

all_papers.title: One-loop matching for gluon lattice TMDs
all_papers.abstract:
Transverse-momentum-dependent parton distributions (TMDs) can be calculated
from first principles by computing a related set of Euclidean lattice
observables and connecting them via a factorization formula. This work focuses
on the leading-power factorization formula connecting the lattice quasi-TMD and
continuum Collins TMD for gluons. We calculate the one-loop gluon matching
coefficient, which is known to be independent of spin and exhibits no mixing
with quarks. We demonstrate that this coefficient satisfies Casimir scaling
with respect to the quark matching coefficient at one-loop order. Our result
facilitates reliable lattice QCD calculations of gluon TMDs.

all_papers.title:
Gluon Transverse-Momentum-Dependent Distributions from Large-Momentum
  Effective Theory
all_papers.abstract:
We demonstrate that gluon transverse-momentum-dependent parton distribution
functions (TMDPDFs) can be extracted from lattice calculations of appropriate
Euclidean correlations in large-momentum effective theory (LaMET). Based on
perturbative calculations of gluon unpolarized and helicity TMDPDFs, we present
a matching formula connecting them and their LaMET counterparts, where the
latter are renormalized in a scheme facilitating lattice calculations and
converted to the $\overline{\rm MS}$ scheme. The hard matching kernel is given
up to one-loop level. We also show that the perturbative result is independent
of the prescription used for the pinch-pole singularity in the relevant
correlations. Our results offer a guidance for the extraction of gluon TMDPDFs
from lattice simulations, and have the potential to greatly facilitate
perturbative calculations of the hard matching kernel.

all_papers.title: Bootstrapping the Abelian Lattice Gauge Theories
all_papers.abstract:
We study the $\mathbb{Z}_2$ and $U(1)$ Abelian lattice gauge theories using a
bootstrap method, in which the loop equations and positivity conditions are
employed for Wilson loops with lengths $L\leqslant L_{\textrm{max}}$ to derive
two-sided bounds on the Wilson loop averages. We address a fundamental question
that whether the constraints from loop equations and positivity are strong
enough to solve lattice gauge theories. We answer this question by
bootstrapping the 2D $U(1)$ lattice gauge theory. We show that with
sufficiently large $L_{\textrm{max}}=60$, the two-sided bounds provide
estimates for the plaquette averages with precision near $10^{-8}$ or even
higher, suggesting the bootstrap constraints are sufficient to numerically pin
down this theory. We compute the bootstrap bounds on the plaquette averages in
the 3D $\mathbb{Z}_2$ and $U(1)$ lattice gauge theories with
$L_{\textrm{max}}=16$. In the regions with weak or strong coupling, the
two-sided bootstrap bounds converge quickly and coincide with the perturbative
results to high precision. The bootstrap bounds are well consistent with the
Monte Carlo results in the nonperturbative region. We observe interesting
connections between the bounds generated by the bootstrap computations and the
Griffiths' inequalities. We present results towards bootstrapping the string
tension and glueball mass in Abelian lattice gauge theories.

all_papers.title: Phases of Wilson Lines in Conformal Field Theories
all_papers.abstract:
We study the low-energy limit of Wilson lines (charged impurities) in
conformal gauge theories in 2+1 and 3+1 dimensions. As a function of the
representation of the Wilson line, certain defect operators can become
marginal, leading to interesting renormalization group flows and for
sufficiently large representations to complete or partial screening by charged
fields. This result is universal: in large enough representations, Wilson lines
are screened by the charged matter fields. We observe that the onset of the
screening instability is associated with fixed-point mergers. We study this
phenomenon in a variety of applications. In some cases, the screening of the
Wilson lines takes place by dimensional transmutation and the generation of an
exponentially large scale. We identify the space of infrared conformal Wilson
lines in weakly coupled gauge theories in 3+1 dimensions and determine the
screening cloud due to bosons or fermions. We also study QED in 2+1 dimensions
in the large $N_f$ limit and identify the nontrivial conformal Wilson lines. We
briefly discuss 't Hooft lines in 3+1-dimensional gauge theories and find that
they are screened in weakly coupled gauge theories with simply connected gauge
groups. In non-Abelian gauge theories with S-duality, this together with our
analysis of the Wilson lines gives a compelling picture for the screening of
the line operators as a function of the coupling.

all_papers.title: Renormalization Group Flows on Line Defects
all_papers.abstract:
We consider line defects in d-dimensional Conformal Field Theories (CFTs).
The ambient CFT places nontrivial constraints on Renormalization Group (RG)
flows on such line defects. We show that the flow on line defects is
consequently irreversible and furthermore a canonical decreasing entropy
function exists. This construction generalizes the g theorem to line defects in
arbitrary dimensions. We demonstrate our results in a flow between Wilson loops
in 4 dimensions.

all_papers.title: Correlators on the Wilson Line Defect CFT
all_papers.abstract:
Conformal field theory (CFT) plays a key role in modern theoretical physics.
Through CFT we describe real physical systems at criticality and fixed points
of the renormalization group flow. It is also central in the study of quantum
gravity, thanks to the AdS/CFT correspondence. This thesis originates in the
context of the N=4 supersymmetric Yang-Mills (SYM) theory, which represents the
CFT side of this correspondence. This work mainly revolves around the
supersymmetric Wilson line and its interpretation as a conformal defect in N=4
SYM. Particularly, we focus on excitations localized on the defect called
insertions, whose correlators are described by a one-dimensional CFT. The first
main result of this work is an efficient algorithm for computing multipoint
correlation functions of scalar insertions on the Wilson line, consisting of
recursion relations up to next-to-leading order at weak coupling. We show
various computations of such four-, five- and six-point correlators, and
discuss their properties. Moreover, we use the four-point function case to
illustrate the power of the Ward identities, which are crucial in deriving a
next-to-next-to-leading order result. Thanks to these perturbative results, we
find a family of differential operators annihilating our correlation functions,
which we conjecture to be a multipoint extension of the Ward identities
satisfied by the four-point functions. These non-perturbative constraints are
shown to be fundamental ingredients in the bootstrap of a five-point function
at strong coupling. To conclude, we define an inherently one-dimensional Mellin
amplitude at the non-perturbative level with appropriate subtractions and
analytical continuations. The efficiency of the 1d Mellin formalism is manifest
at the perturbative level. We find a closed-form expression for the Mellin
transform of leading order contact interactions and use it to extract CFT data.

all_papers.title: Wilson loops in the Hamiltonian formalism
all_papers.abstract:
In a gauge theory, the gauge invariant Hilbert space is unchanged by the
coupling to arbitrary local operators. In the presence of Wilson loops, though,
the physical Hilbert space must be enlarged by adding test electric charges
along the loop. I discuss how at nonzero temperature Polyakov loops are
naturally related to the propagator of a test charge. 't Hooft loops represent
the propagation of a test magnetic charge, and so do not alter the physical
Hilbert space.

all_papers.title: Lattice QCD calculation of the Collins-Soper kernel from quasi TMDPDFs
all_papers.abstract:
This work presents a lattice quantum chromodynamics (QCD) calculation of the
nonperturbative Collins-Soper kernel, which describes the rapidity evolution of
quark transverse-momentum-dependent parton distribution functions. The kernel
is extracted at transverse momentum scales in the range 400 MeV $< q_T < 1.7$
GeV in a calculation with dynamical fermions and quark masses corresponding to
a larger-than-physical pion mass, $m_\pi=538(1)$ MeV. It is found that
different approaches to extract the Collins-Soper kernel from the same
underlying lattice QCD matrix elements yield significantly different results
and uncertainty estimates, revealing that power corrections, such as those
associated with higher-twist effects, and perturbative matching between quasi
and light-cone beam functions, cannot be neglected.

sections.28: Akaike, IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control 19, 716 (1974).

all_papers.title: Confinement and screening via holographic Wilson loops
all_papers.abstract:
We present the holographic dual to a family of ${\cal N}=1$ SCFTs in four
dimensions, deformed by a VEV leading to a gapped system. We calculate Wilson
loops in this system containing adjoint, bifundamental and fundamental matter.
We calculate the quark-antiquark energy $E$ in terms of their separation $L$,
finding an approximate analytic expression for $E(L)$. This expression shows
the transition between conformal, confining and screened behaviours.
Interesting phenomenology is discussed in a variety of examples. The tool used
is the minimization of the F1 string action, for which the code used is made
publicly available.

all_papers.title: Factorization for quasi-TMD distributions of sub-leading power
all_papers.abstract:
The quasi-transverse-momentum dependent (qTMD) distributions are equal-time
correlators that can be computed within the lattice QCD approach. In the regime
of large hadron's momentum, qTMD distributions are expressed in terms of
standard TMD distributions via the factorization theorem. We derive the
corresponding factorization theorem at the next-leading power (NLP), and, for
the first time, we present the factorized expressions for a large class of qTMD
distributions of sub-leading power. The NLP expression contains TMD
distributions of twist-two, twist-three, and a new lattice-specific
nonperturbative function. We point out that some of the qTMD distributions
considered in this work can be employed to extract the Collins-Soper kernel
using the standard techniques of different-momenta-ratio. We provide NLO
expressions for all the elements of the factorization theorem. Also, for the
first time, we explicitly demonstrate the restoration of boost invariance in
NLP TMD factorization.

all_papers.title: Collins-Soper kernel from lattice QCD at the physical pion mass
all_papers.abstract:
This work presents a determination of the quark Collins-Soper kernel, which
relates transverse-momentum-dependent parton distributions (TMDs) at different
rapidity scales, using lattice Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD). This is the first
lattice QCD calculation of the kernel at quark masses corresponding to a
close-to-physical value of the pion mass, with next-to-next-leading logarithmic
matching to TMDs from the corresponding lattice-calculable distributions, and
includes a complete analysis of systematic uncertainties arising from operator
mixing. The kernel is extracted at transverse momentum scales
$240\,\text{MeV}\lesssim q_{T}\lesssim 1.6\,\text{GeV}$ with a precision
sufficient to begin to discriminate between different phenomenological models
in the non-perturbative region.

all_papers.title: Light baryon spatial correlators at short distances
all_papers.abstract:
To study the light baryon light-cone distribution amplitudes (LCDAs), the
spatial correlator of the light baryon has been calculated up to one-loop order
in coordinate space. They reveal certain identities that do not appear in the
study of mesons DAs and PDFs. Subsequently, it was renormalized using the ratio
renormalization scheme involving division by a 0-momentum matrix element. Then
through the matching in the coordinate space, the light baryon light-cone
correlator can be extracted out from the spatial correlator. %By choosing a
specific type of spatial correlator for light baryons, the LCDA is determined
from first principles. These results provide insights into both the ultraviolet
(UV) and infrared (IR) structures of the light baryon spatial correlator, which
is valuable for further exploration in this field. Furthermore, the employed
ratio scheme is an efficient and lattice-friendly renormalization approach
suitable for short-distance applications. These can be used for studying the
light baryon LCDAs using lattice techniques.

all_papers.title: Determination of the Collins-Soper Kernel from Lattice QCD
all_papers.abstract:
We present lattice results for the non-perturbative Collins-Soper (CS)
kernel, which describes the energy-dependence of transverse momentum-dependent
parton distributions (TMDs). The CS kernel is extracted from the ratios of
first Mellin moments of quasi-TMDs evaluated at different nucleon momenta.The
analysis is done with dynamical $N_f=2+1$ clover fermions for the CLS ensemble
H101 ($a=0.0854\,\mathrm{fm}$, $m_{\pi}=m_K=422\,\mathrm{MeV}$). The computed
CS kernel is in good agreement with experimental extractions and previous
lattice studies.

all_papers.title:
Systematic Uncertainties from Gribov Copies in Lattice Calculation of
  Parton Distributions in the Coulomb gauge
all_papers.abstract:
Recently, it has been proposed to compute parton distributions from boosted
correlators fixed in the Coulomb gauge within the framework of Large-Momentum
Effective Theory. This method does not involve Wilson lines and could greatly
improve the efficiency and precision of lattice QCD calculations. However,
there are concerns about whether the systematic uncertainties from Gribov
copies, which correspond to the ambiguity in lattice gauge-fixing, are under
control. This work gives an assessment of the Gribov copies' effect in the
Coulomb-gauge-fixed quark correlators. We utilize different strategies for the
Coulomb-gauge fixing, selecting two different groups of Gribov copies based on
the lattice gauge configurations. We test the difference in the resulted
spatial quark correlators in the vacuum and a pion state. Our findings indicate
that the statistical errors of the matrix elements from both Gribov copies,
regardless of the correlation range, decrease proportionally to the square root
of the number of gauge configurations. The difference between the strategies
does not show statistical significance compared to the gauge noise. This
demonstrates that the effect of the Gribov copies can be neglected in the
practical lattice calculation of the quark parton distributions.
